### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the inputs and outputs of Quality Planning. How does the Quality Plan differ from a Project Management Plan? (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Inputs to Quality Planning (3 Marks):**
1. **Quality Policy** ŌĆö The organization's overarching quality philosophy and commitments.
2. **Project Scope Statement** ŌĆö Defines project boundaries and deliverables.
3. **Product Description** ŌĆö Technical description of the product's features and functions.
4. **Standards and Regulations** ŌĆö External benchmarks like ISO 9001, BIS, ASTM, or industry-specific codes.
5. **Outputs from Other Processes** ŌĆö Risk register, stakeholder register, and procurement documents that influence quality.

**Outputs of Quality Planning (2 Marks):**
1. **Project Quality Plan** ŌĆö A formal document outlining quality practices, standards, and procedures.
2. **Quality Metrics** ŌĆö Measurable indicators (e.g., Defects Per Million Opportunities ŌĆö DPMO).
3. **Quality Checklists** ŌĆö Step-by-step verification lists for processes and deliverables.
4. **Process Improvement Plans** ŌĆö Action plans for continuous quality enhancement.

**Difference Between Quality Plan and Project Management Plan (2 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Quality Plan** | **Project Management Plan** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Focused exclusively on quality standards and procedures | Comprehensive; covers scope, schedule, cost, risk, etc. |
| **Purpose** | Ensures deliverables meet quality requirements | Integrates and coordinates all project management knowledge areas |
| **Hierarchy** | A **subsidiary plan** of the Project Management Plan | The **master document** that may contain the Quality Plan as a component |
| **Audience** | Quality teams, inspectors, auditors | All project stakeholders |

---

**(b) Explain the concept of the House of Quality (HOQ) in Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Describe any five components with a suitable illustration. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Conceptual Overview (2 Marks):**
Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured planning tool that translates customer requirements (the "Whats") into specific engineering characteristics (the "Hows"). The matrix used to capture this translation is called the **House of Quality (HOQ)** because its grid-like structure resembles the shape of a house.

**Five Components of the House of Quality (5 Marks ŌĆö 1 each):**

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats) ŌĆö Left Wall:**
   Lists the Voice of Customer (VOC) items such as *"low fuel consumption," "comfortable seating," "affordable price."* Each requirement is assigned an **importance weight** (typically 1ŌĆō5) based on customer survey data.

2. **Technical Descriptors (Hows) ŌĆö Ceiling:**
   Lists the engineering specifications that can address customer requirements. Example: *"engine displacement," "seat cushion density," "production cost per unit."*

3. **Relationship Matrix ŌĆö Central Room:**
   The core of the HOQ. It shows the **strength of relationship** between each customer requirement (Whats) and each technical descriptor (Hows). Standard symbols are:
   - ŌŚÅ Strong relationship (9 points)
   - ŌŚŗ Moderate relationship (3 points)
   - Ō¢│ Weak relationship (1 point)

4. **Roof Matrix ŌĆö Top of the House:**
   Shows the **correlations between the technical Hows themselves** ŌĆö indicating trade-offs. For example, increasing engine displacement (How 1) may negatively correlate with reducing fuel consumption (How 2).

5. **Competitive Benchmarking ŌĆö Right Wall:**
   Compares the company's product against **competitors' products** on each customer requirement. This identifies strengths, weaknesses, and market positioning opportunities.

> [!TIP]
> **For the exam:** Sketch a simple 3├Ś3 matrix with Whats on the left, Hows on top, and the relationship matrix in the center. Even a hand-drawn schematic earns full marks if labeled correctly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain Juran's Cost of Quality model with a neat diagram. Discuss how an organization can optimize its quality costs strategically. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Juran's COQ Model (3 Marks):**
J.M. Juran proposed that the total Cost of Quality (COQ) consists of two components:
- **Cost of Conformance** = Prevention Costs + Appraisal Costs
- **Cost of Non-Conformance** = Internal Failure Costs + External Failure Costs

As an organization invests more in **Prevention and Appraisal**, the **Failure Costs decrease**. The total COQ curve is **U-shaped (or convex)**, with a clear minimum point ŌĆö the **Optimal Quality Investment Level**.

**Neat Diagram Description (2 Marks):**

```
   Cost Ōöé
        Ōöé\                            /
        Ōöé \                          /
        Ōöé  \                        /
        Ōöé   \                      /
        Ōöé    \_______________     /   ŌåÉ Total COQ Curve (U-shaped)
        Ōöé                 /   \
        Ōöé                / Optimum Point (Minimum Total Cost)
        Ōöé               /     \
        Ōöé   Prevention/Appraisal      Failure
        Ōöé   Costs Rise ŌåÆ              Costs Fall ŌåÆ
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Quality Investment
```

**Strategic Optimization (2 Marks):**
1. **Invest in Prevention First:** Training, mistake-proofing, supplier development, and process design yield the highest ROI.
2. **Use Appraisal Judiciously:** Avoid over-inspection (which raises costs without value addition).
3. **Shift the Cost Mix:** Aim to convert **failure costs into prevention costs** over time.
4. **Continuous Monitoring:** Track COQ quarterly to ensure the organization operates near the optimum point.

---

**(b) A manufacturing firm is evaluating a Quality Planning proposal with the following data: Initial Investment = Ōé╣ 8,00,000; Annual Savings = Ōé╣ 3,00,000; Project Life = 4 years; Discount Rate = 12%. Calculate the Net Present Value (NPV) and Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR). Should the project be accepted? (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Present Value of Costs (1 Mark):**
$$
PV_{\text{Costs}} = \mathbf{8{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}} \quad (\text{incurred at Year 0})
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Present Worth Factor at 12% for 4 Years (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PW \text{ Factor}_{(12\%, 4)} &= \frac{(1.12)^4 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^4} \\
&= \frac{1.5735 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 1.5735} \\
&= \frac{0.5735}{0.18882} \\
&\approx \mathbf{3.0373}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Present Value of Benefits (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{Benefits}} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.0373 \\
&= \mathbf{9{,}11{,}190 \; \text{INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Net Present Value (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{\text{Benefits}} - PV_{\text{Costs}} \\
&= 9{,}11{,}190 - 8{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{+1{,}11{,}190 \; \text{INR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Benefit-Cost Ratio (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
BCR &= \frac{PV_{\text{Benefits}}}{PV_{\text{Costs}}} = \frac{9{,}11{,}190}{8{,}00{,}000} \approx \mathbf{1.14}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Decision (1 Mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $NPV = +1{,}11{,}190 > 0$ and $BCR = 1.14 > 1$, the Quality Planning proposal **is economically justified** and **should be accepted**. However, the margin is thin ŌĆö the company should look for ways to increase annual savings or reduce initial capital outlay.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Students Easy Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Quality Planning with Quality Control:** Students often write "Quality Planning involves inspecting products." This is **WRONG**. Planning is *preventive and design-oriented*; Control is *detective and corrective-oriented*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Label the Quality Plan as a Subsidiary Plan:** When comparing the Quality Plan with the Project Management Plan, students frequently state they are "independent." Always clarify that the **Quality Plan is a component** of the larger Project Management Plan.
>
> 3. **Skipping Diagrams in HOQ Questions:** Even a rough sketch of the House of Quality earns 2ŌĆō3 bonus marks. Examiners reward visual learners.
>
> 4. **Wrong Cost Classification in COQ Questions:** A common error is placing **"warranty claims"** under Prevention Costs. Warranty claims are **External Failure Costs**. Memorize the four COQ categories thoroughly.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Decision Rule in CBA:** Always conclude with a clear *Accept/Reject* statement based on NPV and BCR values. Examiners deduct marks when the final decision is missing.
>
> 6. **Missing the "Voice of Customer" Step:** Many students jump directly to tools like Flowcharts without first identifying VOC. Always begin with VOC ŌĆö this is the **entry point** of Quality Planning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Core Definitions to Memorize:
- [ ] Quality Planning = Identifying standards + Determining how to meet them.
- [ ] Quality Plan = Formal document of quality standards, practices, and procedures.
- [ ] VOC = Voice of Customer ŌĆö explicit and implicit customer needs.
- [ ] CTQ = Critical-to-Quality ŌĆö measurable parameters derived from VOC.
- [ ] COQ = Cost of Quality = Cost of Conformance + Cost of Non-Conformance.
- [ ] QFD / HOQ = Quality Function Deployment / House of Quality ŌĆö translates Whats into Hows.
- [ ] DFQ = Design for Quality ŌĆö engineering quality into the product at the design stage.

### The Four COQ Categories (with one example each):
- [ ] **Prevention** ŌĆö Quality training programs.
- [ ] **Appraisal** ŌĆö Incoming material inspection.
- [ ] **Internal Failure** ŌĆö Rework and scrap.
- [ ] **External Failure** ŌĆö Warranty claims and product recalls.

### Key Tools of Quality Planning:
- [ ] Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)
- [ ] Benchmarking
- [ ] Flowcharting
- [ ] Affinity Diagrams
- [ ] Matrix Diagrams
- [ ] Prioritization Matrices
- [ ] Brainstorming
- [ ] House of Quality (QFD)

### Formula Sheet (For Numerical Problems):
- [ ] $NPV = PV_{\text{Benefits}} - PV_{\text{Costs}}$
- [ ] $BCR = \frac{PV_{\text{Benefits}}}{PV_{\text{Costs}}}$
- [ ] $PW \text{ Factor} = \frac{(1 + r)^n - 1}{r \cdot (1 + r)^n}$
- [ ] Decision Rule: Accept if $NPV > 0$ AND $BCR > 1$.

### Strategic Principles to Quote in Long Answers:
- [ ] **Juran's COQ Optimization:** Prevention costs are an investment, not an expense.
- [ ] **Deming's PDCA:** Quality Planning is the "Plan" stage ŌĆö without it, execution is blind.
- [ ] **Crosby's "Do It Right the First Time":** Prevention is cheaper than correction.
- [ ] **Customer-Centricity:** Every quality plan must begin and end with the customer.

### One-Line Exam Trigger Phrases:
- [ ] *"Define Quality Planning"* ŌåÆ Mention PDCA + Preventive approach + Quality Plan output.
- [ ] *"Differentiate..."* ŌåÆ Use a comparison table with at least 4 differentiating points.
- [ ] *"Explain with diagram"* ŌåÆ ALWAYS include a labeled schematic or Mermaid-style flow.
- [ ] *"Calculate NPV/BCR"* ŌåÆ Show PV Factor calculation, then NPV and BCR, then decision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational, day-to-day, shop-floor-level set of activities and techniques that a manufacturing or service organization uses to **fulfill quality requirements** by monitoring processes, identifying deviations, and removing causes of non-conformance. It is the *inspection-driven, corrective, reactive* arm of quality management.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Quality Control is one of the three concentric layers of quality philosophy:

| Layer | Focus | Question Answered | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | System & Policy | *What is our quality strategy?* | Strategic |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Process & System | *Are we building quality in?* | Preventive / Proactive |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Product & Output | *Is the product meeting specs?* | Detective / Reactive |

The widely cited ISO 9000:2015 definition describes QC as *"part of quality management focused on fulfilling quality requirements."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a chef preparing 100 plates of biryani.
> - **Quality Management** = The restaurant owner deciding "we serve only premium biryani."
> - **Quality Assurance** = Writing down the standard recipe, training cooks, maintaining the kitchen, and ensuring oil temperature is checked *before* cooking.
> - **Quality Control** = The head chef *tasting* each plate as it comes out, using a weighing scale to check portion size, and **rejecting** plates that are under-cooked or have too little salt.
>
> QC is the **"taste test"** ŌĆö it happens *after* (or *during*) production. It catches the bad ones.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** ŌĆö Six Sigma core metric
- **Parts Per Million (PPM) defective** ŌĆö common automotive metric
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** ŌĆö % of units passing inspection without rework
- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** ŌĆö product of stage yields
- **3-Sigma Control Limits** ŌĆö UCL/LCL boundaries on Shewhart charts
- **Process Capability Indices** ŌĆö **Cp** and **Cpk** (industry acceptance: Cpk Ōēź 1.33)
- **Acceptance Number (c)** ŌĆö maximum defectives allowed in sampling plans

## 1.4 The Quality Gurus ŌĆö Historical Foundation

| Guru | Decade | Core Contribution to QC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1920s | Father of Statistical Quality Control; invented the **Control Chart** (Bell Labs, 1924) |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 1940s | 14 Points for Management; PDCA Cycle |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | 1950s | Juran Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956 | Coined **"Total Quality Control (TQC)"** |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1960s | **7 QC Tools**; Quality Circles; Fishbone Diagram |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979 | "Quality is Free"; **Zero Defects** concept |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | 1980s | **Taguchi Loss Function**; Robust Design |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked pair is *Shewhart ŌåÆ Control Chart* and *Ishikawa ŌåÆ 7 QC Tools / Fishbone*. Memorize these mappings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Quality Control

The operational objectives, in KTU Board Exam order:

1. **Detect** defects in products/processes
2. **Identify** causes of variation
3. **Correct** deviations from standards
4. **Ensure** conformance to specifications
5. **Provide** feedback for continuous improvement
6. **Reduce** cost of poor quality (scrap, rework, warranty)

## 2.2 Classification of Variation (The "Why" Behind Control Charts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All processes vary. The trick is to know whether the variation is NORMAL or ABNORMAL.**

| Type of Variation | Synonym | Caused By | Detected By | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Common Cause** | Chance / Random / Noise | Inherent to the process (machine wear, ambient temp) | Control chart points stay **inside** limits | Improve the **system** (Management) |
| **Special Cause** | Assignable / Signal | Specific identifiable factor (broken tool, operator error, bad batch) | Control chart points fall **outside** limits or show patterns | Remove the **specific cause** (Worker/Engineer) |

**Deming's Insight:** *94% of quality problems belong to the system (management's responsibility); only 6% are special causes.*

## 2.3 The 7 QC Tools (Ishikawa's Arsenal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These 7 tools are **mandatory** for KTU Module 1. Expect a 7-mark question on enumeration + application.

| # | Tool | Purpose | Output Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** | Find root causes of a problem | Fishbone / Ishikawa diagram |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Collect data systematically at source | Tally / frequency table |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor process variation over time | Time-series with UCL/LCL |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Show frequency distribution of data | Bar chart of bins |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Separate vital few from trivial many | Sorted bar + cumulative % curve |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Show relationship between 2 variables | X-Y plot |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Map a process step by step | Process map |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Statistical Quality Control)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Safety Rule:** The vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking tables. Render these in LaTeX.

| # | Chart / Metric | Center Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **$\bar{X}$ (X-bar)** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 2 | **R (Range)** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 3 | **$\sigma$ (Std Dev)** | $\bar{s}$ | $B_4 \bar{s}$ | $B_3 \bar{s}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 4 | **p (Proportion Defective)** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 5 | **np (Number Defective)** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 6 | **c (Defects per Unit)** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| 7 | **u (Defects per Sample)** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

### 2.4.1 Control Chart Constants (Sample Size n = 2 to 10)

| n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 |

### 2.4.2 Process Capability Formulas

| Index | Formula | What it Means | KTU Stars |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cp** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process *potential* (centered) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Cpk** | $\min\!\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Process *performance* (off-center correction) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Pp / Ppk** | Same as Cp/Cpk using $\sigma_{overall}$ | Long-term / "actual" capability | ŌŁÉŌŁÉ |
| **Sigma Level** | $\dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Distance from mean to nearest spec in Žā | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Acceptance Benchmarks for Cpk:**
> - Cpk Ōēź 1.67 ŌåÆ World-class (Six Sigma territory, 3.4 DPMO)
> - Cpk Ōēź 1.33 ŌåÆ Industry standard (4-Sigma, ~6,210 PPM)
> - Cpk Ōēź 1.00 ŌåÆ Marginal (3-Sigma, ~66,807 PPM)
> - Cpk < 1.00 ŌåÆ Process NOT capable; 100% inspection required

### 2.4.3 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve ŌĆö Sampling Plans

Two risks govern every acceptance-sampling plan:

| Risk | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Producer's Risk** | $\alpha$ | Good lot rejected | 0.05 (5%) |
| **Consumer's Risk** | $\beta$ | Bad lot accepted | 0.10 (10%) |
| **Acceptable Quality Level** | AQL | Worst tolerable % defective (producer's view) | Set by contract |
| **Lot Tolerance % Defective** | LTPD | Worst lot consumer will accept (╬▓) | Set by consumer |

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

This is the **binomial probability of acceptance** ŌĆö KTU expects you to compute $P_a$ for a given (n, c, p) triple.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 3-Sigma Control Limits?

**Starting Principle (Shewhart's Insight):** For a process whose quality characteristic $X$ follows a Normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ under stable (in-control) conditions, we apply the empirical rule.

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = 0.9973$$

Therefore, the probability of a point falling **outside** these limits purely by chance is:

$$P(\vert X - \mu \vert > 3\sigma) = 1 - 0.9973 = 0.0027$$

This is roughly **3 chances in 1000** (1 in 370). Shewhart judged this an "economical" balance:

- **Too tight (e.g., 2Žā):** UCL = $\mu + 2\sigma$ ŌåÆ $\alpha = 0.0455$. Too many false alarms ŌåÆ operator loses faith in chart.
- **Too loose (e.g., 4Žā):** UCL = $\mu + 4\sigma$ ŌåÆ $\alpha = 0.0063$. Too few signals ŌåÆ defective products slip through undetected.
- **3Žā is the sweet spot** between false alarms and missed detection.

Hence the canonical KTU result:

$$\boxed{\text{UCL} = \mu + 3\sigma \quad ; \quad \text{LCL} = \mu - 3\sigma \quad ; \quad \text{CL} = \mu}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö X-bar and R Chart Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A machine produces bolts. Five samples of size n = 4 are drawn. The means ($\bar{X}$) and ranges (R) for each sample are tabulated. Construct the X-bar and R control charts.

**Given Data Table:**

| Sample (i) | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 50.2 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 49.8 | 1.4 |
| 3 | 50.5 | 0.9 |
| 4 | 50.0 | 1.2 |
| 5 | 49.5 | 1.1 |
| **Sum** | **250.0** | **5.6** |

### Step 1: Compute Grand Mean and Average Range

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{250.0}{5} = 50.0$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{5.6}{5} = 1.12$$

### Step 2: Select Control Chart Constants for n = 4

From the KTU table:
$$A_2 = 0.729 \quad ; \quad D_3 = 0 \quad ; \quad D_4 = 2.282$$

### Step 3: Compute X-bar Chart Control Limits

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.0 + (0.729)(1.12)$$

$$= 50.0 + 0.81648 = 50.816 \approx 50.82$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.0 - 0.81648 = 49.184 \approx 49.18$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.0$$

### Step 4: Compute R Chart Control Limits

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(1.12) = 2.5558 \approx 2.56$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(1.12) = 0.00$$

$$\text{CL}_R = 1.12$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

All five $\bar{X}_i$ values (50.2, 49.8, 50.5, 50.0, 49.5) lie between 49.18 and 50.82 ŌåÆ **X-bar chart in control.**

All five $R_i$ values (1.0, 1.4, 0.9, 1.2, 1.1) lie between 0.00 and 2.56 ŌåÆ **R chart in control.**

Process is **statistically stable.** However, stability ŌēĀ capability. We must still check Cp and Cpk against specification limits.

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Process Capability (Cp and Cpk)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A shaft diameter has specification USL = 25.02 mm and LSL = 24.98 mm. The process mean is $\mu = 25.005$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.005$ mm. Compute Cp and Cpk. Is the process capable?

### Step 1: Compute Cp (Potential Capability)

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 24.98}{6 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.04}{0.03} = 1.333$$

### Step 2: Compute Cpk (Actual Capability)

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 25.005}{3 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.015}{0.015} = 1.000$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.005 - 24.98}{3 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.025}{0.015} = 1.667$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.000,\ 1.667) = 1.000$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cp | 1.333 | Process *potential* is good (Ōēź 1.33) |
| Cpk | 1.000 | Process is **off-center**; only marginally capable |
| Gap (Cp ŌłÆ Cpk) | 0.333 | Indicates the process mean is shifted |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students compute Cp and stop. The Board **deducts 2-3 marks** if Cpk is missing. Always compute **both** Cp and Cpk and **comment on centering**. A Cp = 1.33 with Cpk = 0.5 is a "capable but decentered" process ŌĆö Dr. Taguchi would call this a "loss to society."

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö p-Chart (Proportion Defective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Typical):** 20 samples of size n = 100 are inspected. The number of defectives in each sample ranges such that $\sum d_i = 80$. Construct the p-chart.

### Step 1: Compute the Average Proportion Defective

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{k \cdot n} = \frac{80}{20 \times 100} = 0.04 \quad (4\%)$$

### Step 2: Compute Standard Error

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.04 \times 0.96}{100}} = \sqrt{0.000384} = 0.0196$$

### Step 3: Compute Control Limits

$$\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.04 + 3(0.0196) = 0.04 + 0.0588 = 0.0988$$

$$\text{LCL}_p = \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p = 0.04 - 0.0588 = -0.0188$$

Since proportion cannot be negative, **set LCL = 0.**

$$\text{CL}_p = 0.04$$

### Step 4: Plot and Interpret

If any sample's $p_i = d_i / n$ exceeds 0.0988 or falls below 0, the process is **out of control** at that point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Quality Pyramid (QM ŌåÆ QA ŌåÆ QC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["QUALITY MANAGEMENT (QM) - Strategic"]:::top
    B["QUALITY ASSURANCE (QA) - Preventive"]:::mid
    C["QUALITY CONTROL (QC) - Detective"]:::bot
    D["Customer Satisfaction, Vision, Policy"]:::top
    E["Process Design, SOPs, Training, Audits"]:::mid
    F["Inspection, Testing, Control Charts, Sampling"]:::bot
    A --> B --> C
    A -.- D
    B -.- E
    C -.- F

    classDef top fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#3a7ca5,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bot fill:#d4a017,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Statistical Process Control Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Process Operation"]:::node --> P2["Sample Collection (n items)"]:::node
    P2 --> P3["Compute Statistic (Xbar, R, p, c)"]:::node
    P3 --> P4{"Inside Control Limits?"}:::dec
    P4 -- Yes --> P5["Process In-Control: Continue"]:::ok
    P4 -- No --> P6["Out-of-Control: INVESTIGATE"]:::warn
    P6 --> P7["Identify Assignable Cause"]:::warn
    P7 --> P8["Remove Cause & Document"]:::warn
    P8 --> P9["Recalculate Limits if Needed"]:::warn
    P9 --> P1
    P5 --> P1

    classDef node fill:#e8f0fe,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000
    classDef dec fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000
    classDef ok fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    classDef warn fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Ishikawa Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OUT["PROBLEM: High Defect Rate"]:::head
    SP["4M + 1E + 1P Framework"]:::frame

    SP --- M1["MAN (Workforce)"]:::bone
    SP --- M2["MACHINE"]:::bone
    SP --- M3["MATERIAL"]:::bone
    SP --- M4["METHOD"]:::bone
    SP --- M5["MEASUREMENT"]:::bone
    SP --- M6["ENVIRONMENT (Mother Nature)"]:::bone

    M1 --- M1a["Lack of training"]
    M1 --- M1b["Operator fatigue"]
    M2 --- M2a["Machine wear"]
    M2 --- M2b["Improper calibration"]
    M3 --- M3a["Supplier quality"]
    M3 --- M3b["Storage damage"]
    M4 --- M4a["No SOP"]
    M4 --- M4b["Wrong process params"]
    M5 --- M5a["Faulty gauge"]
    M5 --- M5b["Rounding errors"]
    M6 --- M6a["Temperature"]
    M6 --- M6b["Humidity"]

    M1a & M1b & M2a & M2b & M3a & M3b & M4a & M4b & M5a & M5b & M6a & M6b --> OUT

    classDef head fill:#c0392b,stroke:#641e16,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef frame fill:#fdf2e9,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000
    classDef bone fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Selection Logic ŌĆö Which Control Chart?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"What type of data?"}:::dec
    Q -- "Continuous (variable) ŌĆö length, weight, time" --> V{"Subgroup size n?"}:::dec
    Q -- "Attribute (discrete) ŌĆö pass/fail, defect count" --> A{"What to count?"}:::dec

    V -- "n is small (2 to 10)" --> V1["X-bar and R Charts"]:::node
    V -- "n is large (greater than 10) or one-by-one" --> V2["X-bar and S Chart, or I-MR (Individuals) Chart"]:::node

    A -- "Number of defective items (varying n)" --> A1["p-chart"]:::node
    A -- "Number of defectives (constant n)" --> A2["np-chart"]:::node
    A -- "Number of defects (constant sample)" --> A3["c-chart"]:::node
    A -- "Number of defects (varying sample area)" --> A4["u-chart"]:::node

    classDef dec fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000
    classDef node fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Focus | Process & System | Product & Output |
| Approach | Preventive, proactive | Detective, reactive |
| Stage | Built into the process | Performed after production |
| Tools | Audits, SOPs, FMEA, training | Inspection, control charts, sampling |
| Objective | Prevent defects from occurring | Detect and remove defective items |
| Responsibility | Entire organization | QC department / inspectors |
| Question | "Are we doing things right?" | "Did we make the right thing?" |

QA is the *strategic system*; QC is the *tactical execution*. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model]
**List the Seven Tools of Quality Control proposed by Ishikawa. State the purpose of the Pareto Chart.**

**Model Answer:**

The Seven QC Tools (Kaoru Ishikawa) are:
1. Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone)
2. Check Sheet
3. Control Chart
4. Histogram
5. Pareto Chart
6. Scatter Diagram
7. Flowchart

**Purpose of Pareto Chart:** Based on the **80/20 rule (Pareto Principle)**, it separates the *"vital few"* causes (responsible for ~80% of the problem) from the *"trivial many"* (responsible for ~20%). It helps management prioritize corrective action on the highest-impact issues first. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module 1 Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Statistical Process Control

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Statistical Process Control (SPC). With a neat diagram, describe the construction of an $\bar{X}$ (X-bar) control chart. State the role of the Central Limit Theorem in its application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data represent the measurements of shaft diameter (in mm) taken from 6 samples of size 4. Compute the control limits for the $\bar{X}$ and R charts and comment on the state of control of the process. (Use n = 4: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$) **(7 marks)**

| Sample | Observations (mm) | Mean $\bar{X}$ | Range R |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 24.9, 25.1, 25.0, 25.2 | 25.05 | 0.30 |
| 2 | 25.0, 25.1, 25.2, 24.9 | 25.05 | 0.30 |
| 3 | 25.1, 25.2, 25.0, 25.3 | 25.15 | 0.30 |
| 4 | 25.0, 24.9, 25.1, 25.2 | 25.05 | 0.30 |
| 5 | 25.2, 25.0, 25.1, 24.8 | 25.025 | 0.40 |
| 6 | 25.1, 25.0, 25.2, 24.9 | 25.05 | 0.30 |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Concept of SPC & X-bar Chart Construction:**

Statistical Process Control (SPC) is the application of statistical techniques ŌĆö primarily control charts ŌĆö to monitor and control a process over time. It distinguishes between *common cause* (inherent) and *special cause* (assignable) variation, enabling operators to take corrective action only when warranted.

**Construction of $\bar{X}$ Chart ŌĆö Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Collect k preliminary samples, each of size n, from the process under stable conditions. Record each observation $X_{ij}$ (i = sample, j = observation within sample).

**Step 2:** Compute the sample mean $\bar{X}_i = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}$ and sample range $R_i = X_{i,\max} - X_{i,\min}$ for each sample.

**Step 3:** Compute the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i$ and the average range $\bar{R} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$.

**Step 4:** Compute control limits using the factor $A_2$ from the standard table (depends on n):

$$\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}} \quad ; \quad \text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \quad ; \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

**Step 5:** Plot the sample means $\bar{X}_i$ sequentially as a time series, with the three horizontal lines (UCL, CL, LCL) drawn across the plot.

**Role of Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The CLT states that even if individual observations $X$ are not normally distributed, the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ approaches a normal distribution as n increases (typically n Ōēź 4 or 5 is sufficient). This justifies the use of the **3-sigma rule** (UCL/LCL at $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$) on the $\bar{X}$ chart, even when the underlying raw data is non-normal. The standard error is $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. Since $\sigma$ is not directly known, it is estimated using $\bar{R}/d_2$, leading to the $A_2 \bar{R}$ formulation.

**Schematic:**

```
  UCL = ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ  (X╠┐ + A2┬ĘR╠ä)
        ┬Ę   ┬Ę   ┬Ę
   CL = ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ ┬Ę ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ ┬Ę ┬Ę ŌöĆ  (X╠┐)
        ┬Ę       ┬Ę
  LCL = ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ  (X╠┐ ŌłÆ A2┬ĘR╠ä)
       S1  S2  S3  S4  S5  S6   (Sample index)
```

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** Compute Grand Mean and Average Range.

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{25.05 + 25.05 + 25.15 + 25.05 + 25.025 + 25.05}{6} = \frac{150.375}{6} = 25.0625 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.30 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.30}{6} = \frac{1.90}{6} = 0.3167 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** For n = 4, $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

**X-bar Chart Limits:**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 25.0625 + (0.729)(0.3167) = 25.0625 + 0.2309 = 25.293 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 25.0625 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 25.0625 - 0.2309 = 24.832 \text{ mm}$$

**R Chart Limits:**

$$\text{UCL}_R = (2.282)(0.3167) = 0.7227 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = 0.3167 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = (0)(0.3167) = 0.000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Comment on State of Control:**

| Sample | $\bar{X}_i$ | Within [24.832, 25.293]? | $R_i$ | Within [0.000, 0.7227]? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 25.05 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.30 | Ō£ō In-control |
| 2 | 25.05 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.30 | Ō£ō In-control |
| 3 | 25.15 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.30 | Ō£ō In-control |
| 4 | 25.05 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.30 | Ō£ō In-control |
| 5 | 25.025 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.40 | Ō£ō In-control |
| 6 | 25.05 | Ō£ō In-control | 0.30 | Ō£ō In-control |

**Conclusion:** All sample means and ranges lie within the respective control limits. The process is **statistically in-control** under both charts. No assignable cause is evident. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Process Capability & Pareto Analysis

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the process capability indices Cp and Cpk. With suitable formulas, explain their significance. Why is Cpk alone not sufficient to declare a process as "capable"? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packaging line records the following defect types over a week: *Seal Failure (45), Label Misprint (20), Box Damage (15), Color Mismatch (12), Wrong Quantity (8)*. Construct a Pareto chart. Identify the "vital few" and recommend a quality improvement action plan. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Process Capability Indices:**

**Cp (Process Capability Index ŌĆö Potential):**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

It measures the *spread* of the process variation relative to the width of the specification limits. It assumes the process is **perfectly centered** at the midpoint of the specifications. A higher Cp indicates a more capable process *if centered*.

**Cpk (Process Capability Index ŌĆö Actual):**
$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

It measures the *actual* capability, accounting for the fact that the process mean $\mu$ may not be centered between USL and LSL. It is the **minimum of the upper and lower one-sided capability** values, giving the worst-case distance from mean to spec in units of 3Žā.

**Significance:** Industry benchmarks (Cpk Ōēź 1.33) help engineers decide whether a process can meet customer requirements without 100% inspection.

**Why Cpk alone is insufficient:** A single Cpk value does not tell you *why* a process is incapable. If $C_p = 1.33$ and $C_{pk} = 0.5$, the issue is **centering** ŌĆö easily fixed by re-adjusting the mean. If $C_p = 0.7$ and $C_{pk} = 0.7$, the issue is **excess variation** ŌĆö requires fundamental process redesign. Reporting only Cpk hides this distinction. Hence Cp and Cpk must be **reported together**, along with the gap $(C_p - C_{pk})$ which quantifies the centering shift. (7 marks)

**Part (b) ŌĆö Pareto Chart Construction:**

**Step 1:** Tabulate defects in descending order of frequency and compute cumulative percentage.

| # | Defect Type | Frequency | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Seal Failure | 45 | 45 | 45.0 % |
| 2 | Label Misprint | 20 | 65 | 65.0 % |
| 3 | Box Damage | 15 | 80 | 80.0 % |
| 4 | Color Mismatch | 12 | 92 | 92.0 % |
| 5 | Wrong Quantity | 8 | 100 | 100.0 % |
| | **Total** | **100** | | |

**Step 2:** Identify the "Vital Few."

Pareto's 80/20 rule: The top **3 defect types** (Seal Failure, Label Misprint, Box Damage) account for **80%** of all defects. These are the **"vital few"** that warrant immediate attention. The remaining 2 (Color Mismatch, Wrong Quantity) are the *"trivial many"* accounting for only 20%.

**Step 3:** Pareto Chart Schematic.

```mermaid
%%{init: {"xyChart": {"xAxis": {"labelFontSize": 12}, "yAxis": {"labelFontSize": 12}}}}%%
xychart-beta
    title "Pareto Chart: Packaging Defects"
    x-axis ["Seal Fail", "Label Print", "Box Damage", "Color", "Qty"]
    y-axis "Frequency" 0 --> 50
    bar [45, 20, 15, 12, 8]
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Quality Improvement Action Plan:**

| Priority | Defect | Recommended Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (Vital) | **Seal Failure** (45) | Investigate sealing temperature, dwell time, and pressure. Conduct a Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) analysis on the sealing station. Check machine calibration. |
| 2 (Vital) | **Label Misprint** (20) | Audit printer ribbon/ink supplier; check label alignment sensor; implement poka-yoke (error-proofing) on the print head. |
| 3 (Vital) | **Box Damage** (15) | Inspect material handling process; replace worn conveyor belts; train operators on stacking technique. |
| 4 (Trivial) | Color Mismatch (12) | Monitor trend; address only if frequency increases. |
| 5 (Trivial) | Wrong Quantity (8) | Check counting sensor; verify check-weigher settings. |

**Conclusion:** By focusing on the vital 3, the line can eliminate up to 80% of defects. The remaining 20% are monitored but do not require immediate engineering intervention. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** in the first question. Use the "process vs product" mnemonic.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3Žā justification** in SPC derivations. Always show *why 3Žā and not 2Žā or 4Žā* (probability = 0.0027; balance of Type I/II error).
> 3. **Reporting only Cp without Cpk** in capability questions. Board deducts 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong chart selection** (using X-bar for attribute data or p-chart for variable data). Always check the data type first.
> 5. **Skipping the control chart constants** in tables. The constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ must be *quoted* from the standard table, not invented.
> 6. **Not setting negative LCL to zero** in p and c charts. A negative proportion or defect count is physically impossible.
> 7. **Pareto chart missing cumulative % curve** ŌĆö it is *not* a Pareto chart without the second axis showing cumulative percentage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Quality Control (Module 1)**

- **QC vs QA vs QM:** QC is the *detective / inspection* layer; QA is *process-oriented*; QM is *strategic*.
- **Shewhart (1924)** invented the control chart at Bell Labs ŌĆö 3Žā limits are his legacy.
- **Two variations:** *Common cause* (inherent ŌåÆ fix the system) vs *Special cause* (assignable ŌåÆ fix the specific event).
- **Deming's 94/6 rule:** ~94% of quality problems are system-caused (management's job).
- **Seven QC Tools (Ishikawa):** Fishbone, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- **Pareto 80/20:** Vital few cause 80% of the problem; trivial many cause 20%.
- **Chart selection logic:**
  - Variable data, small n ŌåÆ **$\bar{X}$ & R** chart
  - Variable data, large n ŌåÆ **$\bar{X}$ & S** chart
  - Variable data, n = 1 ŌåÆ **I-MR** (Individuals) chart
  - Attribute, variable n ŌåÆ **p-chart**
  - Attribute, constant n, defective count ŌåÆ **np-chart**
  - Attribute, constant n, defect count ŌåÆ **c-chart**
  - Attribute, variable area, defect count ŌåÆ **u-chart**
- **3Žā limits** = UCL = $\mu + 3\sigma$, LCL = $\mu - 3\sigma$, $P = 0.0027$ outside.
- **Control chart constants** for n = 4: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, $d_2 = 2.059$.
- **Cp** = potential capability (centered); **Cpk** = actual capability (off-center); $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$.
- **Cpk Ōēź 1.33** = industry-acceptable; **Cpk Ōēź 1.67** = world-class (Six Sigma).
- **Process in control ŌēĀ Process capable.** Stability is a prerequisite, not a guarantee of capability.
- **Acceptance Sampling risks:** Producer's risk = $\alpha$ (5%); Consumer's risk = $\beta$ (10%).
- **AQL** is from the producer's view; **LTPD** is from the consumer's view. They are *not* the same.
- **OC curve formula:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ ŌĆö probability of accepting a lot of quality p.
- **Inspection types:** 100% (expensive but thorough), Sampling (statistical), No inspection (when cost of inspection > cost of defect).
- **PDCA cycle (Deming):** Plan ŌåÆ Do ŌåÆ Check ŌåÆ Act. "Check" is where QC operates.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** Quality loss begins the moment the characteristic deviates from target, even within specs. Society pays.
- **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning ŌåÆ Quality Control ŌåÆ Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's Four Absolutes:** Quality = conformance to requirements; Prevention, not inspection; Zero defects performance standard; Measure by price of non-conformance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance

# 1. Quality Assurance ŌĆö Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA)** is a systematic, planned set of activities embedded within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that ensures that a product, process, or service conforms to specified **quality requirements**, standards, and customer expectations. It is a **proactive, process-oriented** discipline focused on preventing defects *before* they occur, rather than detecting them after.

According to **ISO 9000:2015**, Quality Assurance is formally defined as:

> *"Part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."*

The four pivotal pillars that define QA in the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework are:

| Pillar | Meaning | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Planned Activities** | Documented procedures, SOPs | Prevention |
| **Process-Centric** | Built into the workflow | Design |
| **Systematic** | Follows standards (ISO, CMMI) | Compliance |
| **Confidence-Building** | Reassures stakeholders | Trust |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Inspection**
>
> Imagine you own a restaurant. Instead of tasting every dish *after* it is cooked (which is **Quality Control** ŌĆö inspection *after* the fact), you instead:
> - Hire trained chefs (**competence**),
> - Standardize every recipe (**documented process**),
> - Calibrate ovens every morning (**process control**),
> - Check ingredient freshness *before* cooking (**incoming verification**),
> - Train the waiters properly (**skill development**).
>
> This **entire system** is **Quality Assurance**. You trust that *good processes* will *consistently* produce *good food* ŌĆö even if you never inspect a single plate!

That is the essence of QA ŌĆö **building quality into the process**, not inspecting it into the product.

## 1.3 Quality Assurance vs. Quality Control vs. Quality Management

This distinction is **the single most-examined concept** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** | **Quality Management (QM)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process | Product | Entire System |
| **Approach** | Proactive (Prevention) | Reactive (Detection) | Strategic (Both) |
| **Timing** | Before & during production | After production | Throughout lifecycle |
| **Tool** | Audits, process reviews | Inspection, testing | QA + QC + Policy |
| **Goal** | Build quality in | Find defects | Customer satisfaction |
| **Responsibility** | Everyone in the org | QC team | Top Management |

## 1.4 Quality Assurance Principles (ISO 9000 / Deming Foundations)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The QA framework rests on **7 Principles of Quality Management** defined by ISO:

1. **Customer Focus** ŌĆö Primary objective is meeting customer needs.
2. **Leadership** ŌĆö Top management establishes unity of purpose.
3. **Engagement of People** ŌĆö Competent, empowered workforce.
4. **Process Approach** ŌĆö Activities understood as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** ŌĆö Continuous, ongoing effort.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** ŌĆö Data and analysis drive choices.
7. **Relationship Management** ŌĆö Manage supplier and stakeholder relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Assurance Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

The QA lifecycle can be broken into **five sequential, iterative phases**:

- **Phase 1 ŌĆö Plan (Quality Planning):**
  - Identify quality standards relevant to the product/service.
  - Define acceptance criteria (e.g., tolerances, defect thresholds).
  - Document the **Quality Plan** ŌĆö a project-specific roadmap.

- **Phase 2 ŌĆö Do (Process Definition & Documentation):**
  - Create **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
  - Define work instructions, checklists, and process maps.
  - Assign responsibilities (RACI matrix).

- **Phase 3 ŌĆö Implement (Process Execution):**
  - Train personnel in documented processes.
  - Calibrate equipment, verify raw materials.
  - Execute the production/service delivery following the SOP.

- **Phase 4 ŌĆö Check (Audits & Reviews):**
  - Conduct **internal audits** (1st party), **supplier audits** (2nd party), **certification audits** (3rd party).
  - Measure process capability indices like **Cpk** and **Ppk**.

- **Phase 5 ŌĆö Act (Corrective & Preventive Action ŌĆö CAPA):**
  - Address non-conformities.
  - Implement **preventive actions** so defects never recur.
  - Feed lessons learned back into Phase 1 (closed-loop feedback).

## 2.2 Cost of Quality (CoQ) ŌĆö The Engineering Metric

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is a financial framework used to quantify the monetary impact of quality-related activities. This is **the most numerical concept** in QA and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

### Cost of Quality Formula

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} \;+\; C_{a} \;+\; C_{i} \;+\; C_{e}
$$

Where each component is defined below.

| Symbol | Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{p}$ | **Prevention Cost** | Money spent to *prevent* defects | Training, SOP design, quality planning |
| $C_{a}$ | **Appraisal Cost** | Money spent to *detect* defects | Inspection, testing, audits |
| $C_{i}$ | **Internal Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| $C_{e}$ | **External Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, recalls, legal liability |

### Optimal Quality Cost Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem:** As investment in **Prevention + Appraisal (P+A)** increases, **Failure costs (I+E)** decrease faster than the increase in P+A. There exists an **optimum point** where **Total Cost of Quality is minimized**.

$$
C_{oQ}^{*}(P) \;=\; \min_{P} \left[\, C_{p}(P) + C_{a}(P) + C_{i}(P) + C_{e}(P) \,\right]
$$

This is illustrated conceptually below:

| Prevention Spending | Internal Failure | External Failure | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low | High | Very High | **Very High** |
| Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |
| High (Optimal) | Low | Very Low | **Lowest** |
| Excessive | Very Low | Very Low | Rising again |

## 2.3 Process Capability Indices ŌĆö The Statistical Backbone

Process capability measures how well a process output meets specifications.

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

| Index | Meaning | Ideal Value (KTU reference) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p}$ | Process potential (centered) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| $C_{pk}$ | Process capability (actual, accounts for shift) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of process | Smaller = better |

## 2.4 Six Sigma ŌĆö The Modern QA Benchmark

$$
\text{Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO)} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}
$$

Where $D$ = number of defects, $N$ = sample size, $O$ = opportunities per unit.

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 3Žā | 66,807 | 93.32 |
| 4Žā | 621 | 99.38 |
| **5Žā** | **233** | **99.977** |
| **6Žā** | **3.4** | **99.9997** |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of QA

- **Manufacturing:** Prevents defective units from reaching customers, reducing warranty claims.
- **Software Engineering:** Defect prevention in SDLC (e.g., code reviews, unit testing, CI/CD pipelines).
- **Healthcare:** Standardized procedures (SOPs) reduce surgical errors.
- **Service Sector:** Airlines, banks, and IT services use QA frameworks (ISO 9001, CMMI) for consistent service delivery.
- **Construction:** Ensures structural safety via material testing and process audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: Cost of Quality Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
> - Prevention Cost ($C_{p}$) = Ōé╣ 2,00,000
> - Appraisal Cost ($C_{a}$) = Ōé╣ 3,00,000
> - Internal Failure Cost ($C_{i}$) = Ōé╣ 4,00,000
> - External Failure Cost ($C_{e}$) = Ōé╣ 6,00,000
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Compute the Total Cost of Quality (CoQ).
> 2. The firm plans to increase prevention spending by 50%, which is expected to reduce internal failure cost by 40% and external failure cost by 60%. Appraisal cost remains unchanged. Compute the **new** CoQ and comment.

### Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Compute Initial CoQ

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; \boxed{15{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}}
$$

### Solution ŌĆö Step 2: Compute Revised CoQ After Investment

New Prevention Cost:

$$
C_{p}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.50) \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000
$$

New Internal Failure Cost (40% reduction):

$$
C_{i}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) \;=\; 2{,}40{,}000
$$

New External Failure Cost (60% reduction):

$$
C_{e}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.60) \;=\; 2{,}40{,}000
$$

Appraisal Cost unchanged:

$$
C_{a}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000
$$

Revised Total CoQ:

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; \boxed{10{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR}}
$$

### Solution ŌĆö Step 3: Savings Analysis

$$
\Delta C_{oQ} \;=\; 15{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}80{,}000 \;=\; 4{,}20{,}000 \text{ INR saved}
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings} \;=\; \dfrac{4{,}20{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 28\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A **50% increase in prevention spending** yielded a **28% net reduction** in total CoQ. This empirically validates the **KTU theorem** that *investment in prevention reduces failure costs more than proportionally*, achieving optimal QA economics.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö QA Tools and Their Application

| Tool / Technique | Type | Used In QA Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pareto Chart** | Statistical | Check | Prioritize vital few defects (80/20 rule) |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** | Cause-Effect | Check / Act | Identify root causes of defects |
| **Control Charts (SPC)** | Statistical | Check | Monitor process stability over time |
| **Flowcharts** | Process | Plan / Do | Document processes for clarity |
| **Check Sheets** | Data Collection | Check | Record frequency of defects |
| **Histogram** | Statistical | Check | Visualize variation distribution |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Statistical | Check | Study correlation between variables |
| **FMEA** | Risk | Plan | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis |

## 3.3 Quality Assurance Documentation Hierarchy

The QA documentation structure follows a strict top-down hierarchy:

$$
\text{Quality Manual} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procedures} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Work Instructions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Records}
$$

| Level | Document | Audience |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Top) | **Quality Manual** | Top Management, Auditors |
| 2 | **Procedures (SOPs)** | Department Heads |
| 3 | **Work Instructions** | Operators / Engineers |
| 4 (Base) | **Records / Forms** | Operational Evidence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The Deming **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle is the engine that drives QA. The diagram below maps it to QA-specific activities.


```mermaid
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Implement Process Controls SPC]

    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Analyze Data and Identify Improvements]
```
