# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Quality Assurance. How is it different from Quality Control?**

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Assurance (QA):** QA is a systematic, planned set of activities that ensures that products, services, or processes conform to established quality standards. It is **process-oriented** and **prevention-driven**, aiming to build quality into the system itself rather than inspecting defects after they occur. As per ISO 9000:2015, QA is the part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled.

**Difference from Quality Control (QC):**

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Process | Product |
| Approach | Prevention | Detection |
| Timing | Before & during production | After production |
| Responsibility | Entire organization | QC team |

QA builds confidence in the **process**, while QC verifies the **product**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List and briefly explain any three tools used in Quality Assurance.**

**Model Answer:**

The three widely-used QA tools are:

1. **Pareto Chart:** A bar chart combined with a line graph that prioritizes defects based on frequency. Based on the **80/20 rule** (Pareto Principle), it helps focus efforts on the *vital few* causes responsible for the majority of defects.

2. **Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa):** A cause-and-effect diagram used to identify the **root causes** of a quality problem. Categories typically include Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Environment (the **6M** framework).

3. **Control Charts (SPC):** A statistical tool that plots process data over time against upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) to monitor whether a process is in a state of **statistical control**.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the difference table** between QA and QC ŌĆö losing up to **2 marks**.
> - In numerical CoQ problems, students often **forget to compute the savings percentage** ŌĆö losing **1 mark**.
> - Students confuse **Cpk with Cp** in process capability questions ŌĆö examiners deduct **0.5ŌĆō1 mark** for this.
> - Always **label diagrams** (PDCA, QA pyramid) ŌĆö unlabelled diagrams lose **up to 2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> - Use **bold keywords** (prevention, process, ISO 9001) ŌĆö examiners scan for these terms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** Define Quality Assurance. Explain the various **principles of Quality Assurance** as outlined in ISO 9000. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A company spends Ōé╣ 5,00,000 on prevention, Ōé╣ 4,00,000 on appraisal, Ōé╣ 3,00,000 on internal failure, and Ōé╣ 8,00,000 on external failure. If prevention spending is doubled, internal failure drops by 50% and external failure drops by 70%. Compute the new Cost of Quality and percentage savings. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q (a)

**Definition:**
QA is a part of quality management focused on **providing confidence** that quality requirements will be fulfilled. It is a planned, systematic, process-oriented set of activities that prevents defects and ensures consistent conformance to standards.

**ISO 9000 Principles of QA:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Understanding and meeting customer needs is the primary goal. Organizations must research, capture, and act on customer requirements.

2. **Leadership:** Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and an environment in which people can engage in achieving quality objectives.

3. **Engagement of People:** Competent, empowered, and motivated employees at all levels are essential to enhance the organization's capability to create value.

4. **Process Approach:** Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.

5. **Improvement:** Successful organizations maintain a continual focus on improvement ŌĆö this is the **PDCA** cycle in action.

6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making:** Effective decisions are based on the analysis of data, facts, and evidence rather than intuition alone.

7. **Relationship Management:** For sustained success, organizations must manage relationships with **interested parties**, particularly suppliers and partners.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial CoQ:**

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Revised values after doubled prevention:**

- $C_{p}^{\text{new}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = 10{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{a}^{\text{new}} = 4{,}00{,}000$ (unchanged)
- $C_{i}^{\text{new}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 1{,}50{,}000$
- $C_{e}^{\text{new}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 2{,}40{,}000$

**Step 3 ŌĆö New CoQ:**

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 \;=\; 17{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Savings:**

$$
\Delta C_{oQ} \;=\; 20{,}00{,}000 - 17{,}90{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}10{,}000
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings} \;=\; \dfrac{2{,}10{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 10.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Despite prevention spending doubling, the net CoQ reduced by 10.5%, demonstrating that **prevention is more cost-effective than failure correction**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle** in detail and its role in Quality Assurance. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of **Cost of Quality**. Discuss its four components with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q (a)

**PDCA Cycle (Deming Cycle / Shewhart Cycle):**

The PDCA cycle is a four-stage iterative management method used for the **continuous improvement** of processes and products. It is the **operational engine of QA**.

**Plan:**
- Identify the problem or opportunity.
- Analyze the current process using data.
- Define objectives, acceptance criteria, and the quality plan.
- *QA Activity:* Define quality standards and SOPs.

**Do:**
- Execute the planned activities on a small/pilot scale.
- Implement the documented procedures and work instructions.
- Train staff, calibrate equipment.
- *QA Activity:* Carry out process execution per SOPs.

**Check:**
- Monitor and measure process outputs against the plan.
- Conduct audits, collect data, use control charts.
- Compare actual vs. expected performance.
- *QA Activity:* Audit and verify conformance.

**Act:**
- Standardize successful changes.
- Implement Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA).
- Feed improvements back into the next cycle.
- *QA Activity:* Ensure continuous improvement.

**Role in QA:** PDCA is the **foundation** of any effective Quality Assurance system. It transforms QA from a static checklist into a **dynamic, self-improving loop**, ensuring that quality is not a one-time achievement but a sustained organizational capability.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q (b)

**Cost of Quality (CoQ):** A financial model that quantifies the **total cost** of achieving (or failing to achieve) product/service quality. It helps management decide **how much to invest in prevention versus correction**.

**Four Components:**

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_{p}$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.
   - *Examples:* Quality training, SOP preparation, supplier capability assessment, quality planning.

2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_{a}$):** Costs incurred to *detect* defects during production.
   - *Examples:* Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, calibration of test equipment.

3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{i}$):** Costs of defects discovered *before* delivery to the customer.
   - *Examples:* Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis.

4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{e}$):** Costs of defects discovered *after* delivery.
   - *Examples:* Warranty claims, product recalls, legal liability, customer complaints, loss of reputation.

**Formula:**

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}
$$

**Insight:** Increasing $C_{p}$ and $C_{a}$ leads to a *proportionately larger* reduction in $C_{i}$ and $C_{e}$, yielding a **lower total CoQ** at the optimum point ŌĆö this is the **strategic foundation** for QA investment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for Quality Assurance:**

- **QA is process-oriented; QC is product-oriented.** (Most-tested distinction.)
- **ISO 9000:2015** defines QA as *"part of QM focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."*
- **QA = Prevention; QC = Detection.**
- The **7 ISO Principles** are: Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decisions, Relationship Management.
- **PDCA Cycle** = Plan ŌåÆ Do ŌåÆ Check ŌåÆ Act (Deming Cycle).
- **Cost of Quality = $C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}$**.
- **Optimal QA investment** minimizes total CoQ by increasing prevention.
- **Process Capability Indices:** $C_{p}$ (potential), $C_{pk}$ (actual); both ideally $\geq 1.33$.
- **Six Sigma** targets **3.4 DPMO** at the 6Žā level.
- **QA Tools (7 QC Tools):** Pareto, Fishbone, Control Chart, Flowchart, Check Sheet, Histogram, Scatter Diagram.
- **Documentation Hierarchy:** Quality Manual ŌåÆ Procedures ŌåÆ Work Instructions ŌåÆ Records.
- **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (a core QA risk tool).
- **Three types of audits:** 1st Party (internal), 2nd Party (supplier), 3rd Party (certification body).
- **CAPA** = Corrective and Preventive Action ŌĆö the closing loop of QA.
- **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Deming's 14 Points** emphasize system thinking, continuous improvement, and elimination of fear for QA culture.
- **KTU Numerical Trick:** Always show the *initial CoQ*, the *revised CoQ*, and the **percentage savings** ŌĆö all three are required for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Quality ŌĆö The Foundational Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Aligned with ISO 9000:2015)** > **Quality** is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements. An **object** can be a product, service, process, person, resource, system, or any combination thereof. In simpler academic terms, quality is about **conformance to requirements** (Crosby's view) and **fitness for use** (Juran's view). It is not luxury or high cost ŌĆö it is about **consistently meeting customer expectations** in a reliable, predictable manner. > [!NOTE] > **Three Universal Perspectives of Quality (KTU High-Yield)** > 1. **User's View** ŌĆö Fitness for intended use (Juran) > 2. **Manufacturer's View** ŌĆö Conformance to design specifications (Crosby, Levitt) > 3. **Product-Based View** ŌĆö Quality is a precise and measurable variable (Taguchi's Quality Loss Function) ## 1.2 Quality Management ŌĆö Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Management (QM)** is a formalized system of documenting processes, procedures, responsibilities, and continuous improvement activities aimed at consistently meeting customer and regulatory requirements and enhancing stakeholder satisfaction. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Quality Management** is treated as the *parent discipline* that encompasses Quality Control (QC), Quality Assurance (QA), and the broader Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Five-Star Restaurant Chain** > Imagine a restaurant chain like "Burger King" operating in 50 cities. To call it a *quality-managed* brand, every customer ŌĆö irrespective of city ŌĆö must receive: > - The **same taste** (Standardization) > - The **same hygiene** (Compliance) > - The **same portion size** (Specification Conformance) > - **Courteous service** (Customer Satisfaction) > - A **receipt** (Documentation/Traceability) > - **Continuous menu improvement** (Kaizen / Continuous Improvement) > > If one outlet serves stale food, the entire brand reputation suffers. Therefore, **Quality Management is the operating manual of the entire chain**, not just a single outlet. This is the essence of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. ## 1.4 Why Quality Management Matters in Engineering In KTU's B.Tech context, every engineering discipline (Mechanical, Civil, Electrical, Electronics, Computer Science) must deliver products, software, or services that: - **Conform to IS/ISO/ASTM standards** - **Satisfy the customer** (internal or external) - **Minimize rework, defects, and warranty costs** - **Build long-term organizational reputation** ## 1.5 Core Terminology (KTU-Exam Friendly Glossary) | Term | Meaning (KTU 2024 Standard) | |---|---| | **Quality** | Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements | | **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational techniques to fulfil quality requirements (inspection-driven) | | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Planned systematic activities to provide confidence that requirements are met (process-driven) | | **Quality Management System (QMS)** | Formalized system documenting processes, responsibilities, and KPIs | | **TQM** | A management philosophy integrating all functions to focus on customer satisfaction | | **Kaizen** | Japanese term for continuous incremental improvement | | **Six Sigma** | Data-driven methodology aiming for $\leq 3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO) | | **BPM** | Business Process Management ŌĆö broader process optimization lens | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of Quality Management (Historical Timeline) Quality Management is **not new** ŌĆö it evolved across three industrial eras: > [!NOTE] > **Three Eras of Quality (KTU Board Hot Question)** > 1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1920s)** ŌĆö Focus on detecting defects after production. *Quality = Inspection*. > 2. **Quality Control Era (1920sŌĆō1950s)** ŌĆö Statistical methods introduced by Shewhart. *Quality = Statistical Control*. > 3. **Quality Management Era (1950s onwards)** ŌĆö Total involvement, customer focus, continuous improvement. *Quality = Strategic Weapon*. ## 2.2 The Quality Gurus ŌĆö Their Distinct Contributions (KTU Most Repeated Topic) This is the **single highest-weightage topic** in KTU Quality Management papers. Each guru has a unique philosophy. Memorize the **keywords** associated with each. | Guru | Year / Region | Key Philosophy | Famous Phrase / Concept | |---|---|---|---| | **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1924, USA | Father of Statistical Quality Control | **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** | | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1950, USA/Japan | Management responsibility for quality | **14 Points for Management**, System thinking | | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1954, USA/Japan | Quality is fitness for use | **Quality Trilogy** (Planning, Control, Improvement) | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956, USA | Total Quality Control | **TQC = Company-wide quality** | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1962, Japan | Quality circles, customer focus | **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram** | | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1980, Japan | Quality in design, loss to society | **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** | | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979, USA | Quality is free, prevention not inspection | **"Zero Defects"**, **"Do It Right First Time" (DIRFT)** | | **Joseph M. Duran** *(separate from Juran)* | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | | **M. Walton (Mary Walton)** | 1986 | Deming Prize implications | ŌĆö | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deming's 14 Points ŌĆö KTU Frequently Asked Sub-Question** > Point 1: *Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.* > Point 2: *Adopt the new philosophy.* > Point 3: *Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.* > Point 4: *End the practice of awarding business on price alone.* > Point 5: *Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service.* > Point 7: *Institute leadership.* > Point 11: *Eliminate numerical quotas.* > Point 13: *Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.* ## 2.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy (KTU 14-Mark Favourite) > [!NOTE] > Juran's Trilogy is a **three-part managerial framework**: > 1. **Quality Planning** ŌĆö Identify customers and their needs; develop product/process to meet those needs. > 2. **Quality Control** ŌĆö Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. > 3. **Quality Improvement** ŌĆö Establish infrastructure to continuously improve processes. ## 2.4 Deming's PDCA Cycle (Shewhart Cycle) This is the **operational backbone** of every QMS in the world (including ISO 9001). - **P ŌĆö Plan**: Identify the problem, analyse, develop a plan. - **D ŌĆö Do**: Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot). - **C ŌĆö Check**: Observe and measure results, compare with expected. - **A ŌĆö Act**: Standardize the improvement or restart the cycle. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet Since Quality Management is a management course, the "formulas" are primarily **quantitative quality metrics** that KTU frequently tests. | Metric | Formula | Standard / Benchmark | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | **Defect Rate** | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | Manufacturing QC | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units Passing Inspection First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | Target $\geq 95\%$ | Process capability check | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \ldots \times \text{FPY}_n$ | For multi-step processes | Software/assembly lines | | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Opportunities per Unit} \times \text{Units}} \times 10^{6}$ | Six Sigma target $\leq 3.4$ | Six Sigma | | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\geq 1.33$ capable, $\geq 1.67$ excellent | Process engineering | | **Taguchi's Quality Loss (QLF)** | $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}$ | Quadratic loss to society | Design optimization | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Minimize internal + external | QA economics | | **Pareto 80/20** | $80\%$ of defects come from $20\%$ of causes | Vital few vs. trivial many | Problem-solving | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Isolation Rule Applied:** Vertical bars $\vert$ used inside table cells (instead of pipes) to avoid breaking the markdown table. ## 2.6 Dimensions of Product & Service Quality > [!NOTE] > **David Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality (KTU Standard Reference)** > 1. Performance > 2. Features > 3. Reliability > 4. Conformance > 5. Durability > 6. Serviceability > 7. Aesthetics > 8. Perceived Quality For **services**, the SERVQUAL model adds: - **Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy** (5 dimensions). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF) Taguchi reframed quality as **loss imparted to society** from the moment a product deviates from its target. Unlike the traditional "inside-or-outside spec" view, Taguchi proposed a **continuous quadratic loss**. ### Step 1: Define the Target Value Let $T$ be the target value of a quality characteristic (e.g., diameter $= 10$ mm, voltage $= 5$ V). ### Step 2: Define the Actual Value Let $x$ be the actual measured value of the characteristic. ### Step 3: Define the Deviation The deviation from target is: $$ \Delta = x - T $$ ### Step 4: Define the Loss Proportionality Constant Let $k$ be the proportionality constant (engineering cost of being off-target). It is calculated as: $$ k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} $$ where $A_0$ is the cost of replacement/repair at the functional limit $\Delta_0$ (the maximum tolerable deviation before the product becomes non-functional). ### Step 5: Formulate the Loss Function $$ L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2} $$ $$ L(x) = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} \cdot (x - T)^{2} $$ ### Step 6: Interpret the Result - If $x = T$, then $L(x) = 0$ (no loss). - If $x \neq T$, even within specification limits, $L(x) > 0$ (loss exists). - Loss grows **quadratically** with deviation. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation (Why this matters)** > A $9.9$ mm bolt and a $10.1$ mm bolt both pass a $\pm 0.2$ mm spec, but the one farther from $10$ mm is costlier to society. This pushed engineers to design for **on-target**, not just "in-spec". This is foundational to **Robust Design**. ### Worked Example (KTU-Style) A resistor's target resistance is $R_T = 100\ \Omega$. Functional limit is $\pm 20\ \Omega$ where the cost of replacement is $A_0 = \text{Rs. }400$. Find loss when $R = 102\ \Omega$. $$ k = \dfrac{400}{(20)^{2}} = \dfrac{400}{400} = 1\ \text{Rs.}/\Omega^{2} $$ $$ L(102) = 1 \cdot (102 - 100)^{2} = 4\ \text{Rs.} $$ Even a $2\ \Omega$ deviation (within spec) costs the organization Rs. 4 per unit in societal loss. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Structure COPQ is the **monetary quantification of failure**. It is split into: | Category | Type | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Prevention Costs** | Proactive | Training, quality planning, supplier capability audits | | **Appraisal Costs** | Detective | Inspection, testing, calibration | | **Internal Failure Costs** | Reactive (in-house) | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | | **External Failure Costs** | Reactive (customer-side) | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strategic Insight (KTU Board Expectation)** > A *mature* quality organization spends more on **Prevention** and **Appraisal** (cheap early costs) and minimizes **Internal + External Failure** (expensive late costs). A *reactive* organization does the opposite and bleeds money. ## 3.3 Comparative Matrix: Quality Gurus' Philosophical Anchors This matrix is the **single most exam-ready artefact** for KTU Part B answers on Module 1. | Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa | Taguchi | Feigenbaum | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Origin / Era** | 1950, USA | 1954, USA | 1979, USA | 1962, Japan | 1980, Japan | 1956, USA | | **Core Idea** | System of profound knowledge | Quality trilogy | Conformance + prevention | Quality circles | Quality loss | Total quality control | | **View of Quality** | Predictable variation | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Customer-defined | Loss to society | Company-wide | | **Top Tool** | PDCA, 14 Points | Pareto, Quality Trilogy | Zero Defects, DIRFT | Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram | QLF, Robust Design | TQC framework | | **Customer Focus** | Indirect (via system) | Direct (needs identification) | Indirect (prevention) | Strong (next process = customer) | Indirect (loss) | Strong | | **Best Quote** | "Quality is made in the boardroom" | "Fitness for use" | "Quality is free" | "Quality begins and ends with education" | "Loss to society" | "Quality is everybody's job" | | **Japanese Award** | Deming Prize | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | Deming Prize (also) | ŌĆö | > [!NOTE] > **Deming Prize (Japan)** ŌĆö Established in 1951, awarded to companies excelling in quality. Both Deming and Taguchi are deeply linked to it. The prize **predates the Japan Quality Medal**. ## 3.4 Analytical Process: Pareto-Based Problem Solving (80/20) Used when problems are many, but a few dominate. 1. **Define** the problem category (e.g., "welding defects"). 2. **Collect** data on defect sub-types for a defined period. 3. **Tabulate** defect counts in descending order. 4. **Compute** the cumulative percentage: $$ \text{Cumulative \%} = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative count}}{\text{Total count}} \times 100 $$ 5. **Plot** bar chart + cumulative curve (Pareto Chart). 6. **Focus** corrective action on the "vital few" (typically up to $80\%$ cumulative). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Quality Management (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inspection Era
Pre-1920s] --> B[Quality Control Era
1920s to 1950s] B --> C[Quality Assurance Era
1950s to 1980s] C --> D[Total Quality Management
1980s to Present] D --> E[Six Sigma Lean Six Sigma
1990s to Present] A1[Detect defects after
they occur] -.-> A B1[Statistical process
control SPC] -.-> B C1[System based prevention
ISO 9000 BS 5750] -.-> C D1[Customer focus continuous
improvement all functions] -.-> D E1[Data driven DMAIC
3.4 DPMO] -.-> E ``` ## 4.2 Deming's PDCA Cycle ŌĆö Operational View ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Plan
Identify problem and
develop hypotheses] D[Do
Implement plan on
small scale pilot] C[Check
Measure results and
compare with targets] A[Act
Standardize improvement
or revise plan] P --> D D --> C C --> A A --> P S1[Continuous
Improvement Loop] -.-> P S2[If goal met
standardize] -.-> A S3[If not met
revise hypothesis] -.-> A ``` ## 4.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy ŌĆö Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STAGE1[Quality Planning] SP1[Identify customers] SP2[Determine customer needs] SP3[Develop product features] SP4[Develop process capabilities] SP1 --> SP2 --> SP3 --> SP4 end subgraph STAGE2[Quality Control] SC1[Choose control subjects] SC2[Choose measurement units] SC3[Establish measurement] SC4[Set standards and goals] SC5[Measure actual performance] SC6[Interpret differences] SC7[Act on differences] SC1 --> SC2 --> SC3 --> SC4 --> SC5 --> SC6 --> SC7 end subgraph STAGE3[Quality Improvement] SI1[Establish infrastructure] SI2[Identify improvement projects] SI3[Establish project teams] SI4[Provide resources and motivation] SI1 --> SI2 --> SI3 --> SI4 end STAGE1 --> STAGE2 STAGE2 --> STAGE3 STAGE3 -.->|Feedback| STAGE1 ``` ## 4.4 TQM Building Blocks ```mermaid flowchart TD CORE[Customer Focus] CORE --> P1[Total Employee Involvement] CORE --> P2[Continuous Improvement Kaizen] CORE --> P3[Process Centric Approach] CORE --> P4[Integrated System] CORE --> P5[Strategic and Systematic Approach] CORE --> P6[Fact Based Decision Making] CORE --> P7[Communications] P1 --> OUT[Enhanced Customer
Satisfaction and
Organizational Excellence] P2 --> OUT P3 --> OUT P4 --> OUT P5 --> OUT P6 --> OUT P7 --> OUT ``` ## 4.5 Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) ŌĆö P-A-I-E Model ```mermaid flowchart LR COPQ[Cost of Poor Quality] COPQ --> PREV[Prevention Costs
Training, Planning, Audits] COPQ --> APPR[Appraisal Costs
Inspection, Testing] COPQ --> INT[Internal Failure
Rework, Scrap] COPQ --> EXT[External Failure
Warranty, Returns, Lawsuits] PREV --> SUM[Total COPQ] APPR --> SUM INT --> SUM EXT --> SUM SUM --> STRAT[Strategic Goal
Shift spend from right side
to left side of COPQ] ``` ## 4.6 Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR HEAD[Problem Statement
e.g. Defective Welds] --> M M[Main Spine] --> M1[Manpower] M --> M2[Machine] M --> M3[Method] M --> M4[Material] M --> M5[Measurement] M --> M6[Environment] M1 --> C11[Skill gap] M1 --> C12[Fatigue] M2 --> C21[Calibration] M2 --> C22[Wear and tear] M3 --> C31[Procedure gap] M3 --> C32[Wrong settings] M4 --> C41[Raw material defect] M4 --> C42[Wrong grade] M5 --> C51[Gauge error] M5 --> C52[Sampling error] M6 --> C61[Humidity] M6 --> C62[Temperature] ``` ## 4.7 DMAIC Framework (Six Sigma) ŌĆö KTU Advanced View ```mermaid flowchart TD D[Define
Problem, customer, scope] M[Measure
Current performance, CTQ] A[Analyze
Root cause analysis] I[Improve
Pilot and implement] C[Control
Standardize and monitor] D --> M --> A --> I --> C C -.->|Loop back if drift| D C1[Goal 3.4 DPMO] -.-> C ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Model Note to Students:** KTU Part A expects **definition + 2ŌĆō3 key points + one-line industrial example**. No essays. Stick to crisp facts. ### Q1. Define Quality. Discuss the various dimensions of product quality. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements (ISO 9000:2015). - **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, and Perceived Quality. - **Example:** A smartphone is judged by all 8 dimensions ŌĆö speed, camera, battery life, defect-freedom, lifespan, service network, look, and brand trust. - *Concluding line:* A truly high-quality product must satisfy all 8 dimensions in balance. --- ### Q2. Explain the term "Quality Assurance" and differentiate it from "Quality Control". **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system to provide confidence that the product or service will satisfy quality requirements. QA is *process-oriented* and *preventive*. **Quality Control (QC):** Operational techniques and activities used to fulfil quality requirements, primarily through *inspection* of the product. QC is *product-oriented* and *detective*. | Aspect | QA | QC | |---|---|---| | Focus | Process | Product | | Nature | Preventive | Detective | | Tool | Audits, process control | Inspection, testing | --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two question sets. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Set 1: Quality Gurus** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management. Discuss its relevance in modern organizations. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Compare and contrast the contributions of Juran, Crosby, and Feigenbaum to Quality Management. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) ŌĆö Deming's 14 Points (7 marks) Deming's 14 Points are a foundational framework for management transformation. The key points (presented concisely as a model answer) are: 1. **Create constancy of purpose** for improvement of product and service, allocating resources for long-term planning. 2. **Adopt the new philosophy** ŌĆö accept that the current system must change. 3. **Cease dependence on inspection** ŌĆö build quality into the process from the start. 4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** ŌĆö minimize total cost by working with a single supplier. 5. **Improve constantly** every process for planning, production, and service. 6. **Institute training on the job.** 7. **Institute leadership** ŌĆö supervisors should help people do a better job. 8. **Drive out fear** so everyone can work effectively. 9. **Break down barriers between departments** ŌĆö team-based problem solving. 10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** that demand zero defects without method. 11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** ŌĆö replace with leadership. 12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.** 13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.** 14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.** **Modern Relevance (1 mark):** Deming's points are reflected in **ISO 9001**, **TQM**, and **Agile/Lean** management systems. His emphasis on *system thinking*, *prevention*, and *continuous improvement* is the bedrock of Industry 4.0 and digital transformation strategies. #### Model Solution for Q(b) ŌĆö Juran vs Crosby vs Feigenbaum (7 marks) A structured comparison should be written as a 3-row table (1.5 marks per guru + 1 mark for synthesis): | Aspect | Juran | Crosby | Feigenbaum | |---|---|---|---| | Core idea | Fitness for use; Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | Quality is free; Conformance to requirements; DIRFT (Do It Right First Time) | Total Quality Control (TQC) ŌĆö quality is everyone's job | | Origin | 1954 (USA/Japan) | 1979 (USA) | 1956 (USA) | | Major contribution | Quality Trilogy; Customer-needs identification; Pareto principle for quality | Zero Defects concept; Prevention-vs-Inspection cost argument | Company-wide TQC system involving all departments | | Signature phrase | "Fitness for use" | "Quality is free" | "Quality is everybody's job" | | Application | Suitable for strategic planning, project selection | Strong for manufacturing discipline, defect-prevention culture | Strong for large, multi-departmental organizations | | Limitation | Trilogy seen as abstract without operational tools | Overly audit/inspection-oriented; less customer-centric | Implementation across departments can be complex | **Synthesis (closing line):** While Juran gives the *strategic* lens, Crosby gives the *cultural* lens, and Feigenbaum gives the *organizational* lens. Together they form a holistic foundation of modern QMS. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Set 2: TQM & PDCA** > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the concept of Total Quality Management (TQM). Discuss its key principles and benefits. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain Deming's PDCA cycle. How is it applied in a manufacturing process? Give an example. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) ŌĆö TQM (7 marks) **Definition (1.5 marks):** Total Quality Management is a customer-focused management philosophy that involves *all members* of an organization in continuously improving all aspects of the business to deliver high-quality products and services that exceed customer expectations. **Key Principles (4 marks):** 1. **Customer Focus** ŌĆö The customer (internal or external) defines quality. 2. **Total Employee Involvement** ŌĆö Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, is responsible for quality. 3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** ŌĆö Small, incremental, perpetual improvements. 4. **Process-Centric Approach** ŌĆö Focus on processes, not just outputs. 5. **Integrated System** ŌĆö All departments are interconnected; quality is not a siloed function. 6. **Strategic and Systematic Approach** ŌĆö Quality is a strategic pillar, integrated with the organizational vision. 7. **Fact-Based Decision Making** ŌĆö Use SPC, Pareto, statistical tools. 8. **Communication** ŌĆö Effective and timely communication across all levels. **Benefits (1.5 marks):** - Enhanced customer satisfaction and loyalty - Reduced rework, scrap, warranty, and warranty costs - Higher employee morale and ownership - Improved market share and competitive advantage - Stronger supplier relationships #### Model Solution for Q(b) ŌĆö PDCA Cycle (7 marks) **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a circular flow of Plan ŌåÆ Do ŌåÆ Check ŌåÆ Act with arrows and brief descriptions (see Section 4.2 for the Mermaid reference). **Detailed Steps (3 marks):** - **Plan:** Define the problem (e.g., high rejection rate of a CNC shaft). Collect data, identify causes using Pareto/Fishbone. Develop a hypothesis and an action plan. - **Do:** Implement the plan on a small batch (pilot). Document observations. - **Check:** Compare actual results with expected. Use control charts and FPY. - **Act:** If the pilot succeeds, standardize across the line. If it fails, revise the plan and re-enter the cycle. **Example (1 mark):** A manufacturing firm faces a $12\%$ defect rate in capacitor soldering. The team hypothesizes that *soldering tip temperature* is the root cause. They set tip temperature to $370\degree C$ (Plan), run $50$ PCBs (Do), measure defect rate drops to $4\%$ (Check), update the SOP to $370\degree C$ and train all operators (Act). The cycle repeats for the next defect category. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU QP Code OEIET612** > 1. **Confusing Deming with Juran** ŌĆö Deming = System + 14 Points + PDCA; Juran = Trilogy + Fitness for Use. Do NOT interchange. > 2. **Writing QA = QC** ŌĆö They are NOT the same. QA is preventive (process), QC is detective (product). KTU examiners **specifically** test this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the diagram in PDCA / Fishbone answers** ŌĆö A 7-mark question **always** expects a labelled diagram. A text-only answer will lose 2ŌĆō3 marks. > 4. **Not remembering key years** ŌĆö Deming (1950), Juran (1954), Ishikawa (1962), Taguchi (1980), Crosby (1979), Feigenbaum (1956). These dates are common **fill-in-the-blanks** in KTU. > 5. **Confusing Six Sigma's DPMO target** ŌĆö It is **3.4 DPMO**, not zero. Many students write "0 defects" which is wrong. > 6. **Mixing up Garvin's 8 dimensions with SERVQUAL's 5** ŌĆö Garvin = product; SERVQUAL = service. They are not interchangeable. > 7. **Writing Taguchi's QLF as linear** ŌĆö It is **quadratic** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$. This is a frequently tested formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Quality Management (Module 1)** > - [ ] Quality = Degree of conformance to requirements (ISO 9000:2015). > - [ ] Three views: User, Manufacturer, Product-based. > - [ ] QA is *process-driven, preventive*; QC is *product-driven, detective*. > - [ ] **Deming** ŌåÆ PDCA, 14 Points, System thinking. USA/Japan, 1950. > - [ ] **Juran** ŌåÆ Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement), Fitness for use. USA, 1954. > - [ ] **Crosby** ŌåÆ Zero Defects, DIRFT, "Quality is free", Prevention. USA, 1979. > - [ ] **Feigenbaum** ŌåÆ TQC, Company-wide quality. USA, 1956. > - [ ] **Ishikawa** ŌåÆ Quality Circles, Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram. Japan, 1962. > - [ ] **Taguchi** ŌåÆ Quality Loss Function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$, Robust Design. Japan, 1980. > - [ ] **Shewhart** ŌåÆ Father of SQC; originated PDCA. USA, 1924. > - [ ] Three eras: Inspection ŌåÆ Quality Control ŌåÆ Quality Management. > - [ ] **Garvin's 8 dimensions** of product quality. > - [ ] **SERVQUAL's 5 dimensions** of service quality. > - [ ] **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. > - [ ] **Six Sigma target** = $3.4$ DPMO. > - [ ] **Pareto (80/20)** ŌĆö Vital few vs. trivial many. > - [ ] **TQM Principles** ŌĆö Customer focus, Total employee involvement, Continuous improvement, Process approach, Integrated system, Fact-based decisions, Communication, Strategic alignment. > - [ ] **Deming Prize** = Japanese award for quality excellence (1951). > - [ ] **Quality Gurus' Year-Side mapping is a recurrent 3-mark KTU question.** ---

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM) - Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM) - Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and business processes by involving all members of the organization ŌĆö from top management to front-line workers ŌĆö in a customer-centric, data-driven, and process-oriented approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEIET612):**
> TQM is a *systematic, integrated, and strategic* approach to managing an organization whose primary objective is to meet **customer expectations** by delivering products and services of the highest **quality**, while ensuring **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** and **total employee involvement (TEI)** across every functional level.

The word **"Total"** in TQM signifies that quality is *not* the responsibility of a single department (like Quality Assurance), but rather the responsibility of **every individual, every department, and every process** within the organization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Five-Star Restaurant Kitchen**
> 
> Imagine a premium restaurant where the Head Chef (Top Management) does not cook the food alone. Instead:
> - The **Chefs** (Production) cook with precision.
> - The **Servers** (Service) deliver with a smile.
> - The **Cleaners** (Housekeeping) maintain hygiene.
> - The **Sommelier** (Quality Inspector) tastes and approves.
> - The **Manager** (Operations) listens to customer feedback.
> 
> Everyone in the kitchen is **accountable for the taste, presentation, and hygiene** of the final dish. If one person fails, the entire customer experience collapses. TQM applies this exact same logic to an entire organization ŌĆö **quality is a shared, collective, and continuous responsibility.**

### 1.3 Core Components of TQM

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** | Understanding and meeting customer needs and expectations. |
| **Total Employee Involvement** | Every employee, from CEO to operator, participates. |
| **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Ongoing, incremental improvements in processes. |
| **Process-Centered Approach** | Focus on improving processes rather than blaming people. |
| **Integrated System** | All departments are interconnected and aligned. |
| **Fact-Based Decision Making** | Decisions driven by data, statistics, and measurable metrics. |
| **Communication** | Effective communication at all levels, both vertical and horizontal. |
| **Supplier Partnership** | Building long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with suppliers. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Remember:** In TQM, the customer is *not just* the external buyer ŌĆö it includes **internal customers** too. For example, the Accounts department is the "customer" of the Sales department for processing invoices.

### 1.4 Evolution of the TQM Concept

The TQM philosophy did not emerge overnight. It evolved through the contributions of multiple Quality Gurus over several decades:

- **1920s-1930s:** Walter Shewhart laid the foundation with Statistical Quality Control (SQC).
- **1950s:** W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran introduced quality concepts to post-war Japan.
- **1960s-1970s:** Kaoru Ishikawa popularized the concept of Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) in Japan.
- **1980s:** The term *Total Quality Management* was formally adopted in the West (USA, UK).
- **1987:** ISO 9000 standards formalized quality system requirements globally.
- **2015-Present:** ISO 9001:2015 emphasizes risk-based thinking and process approach.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of TQM

TQM rests on three foundational pillars that must work in harmony:

1. **Pillar 1 ŌĆö Customer Satisfaction (The "Why")**
   - TQM exists primarily to satisfy the customer. If the customer is not satisfied, the product/service has failed regardless of internal metrics.
   - Customer satisfaction is measured through surveys, Net Promoter Score (NPS), Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), and feedback loops.

2. **Pillar 2 ŌĆö Continuous Improvement (The "How")**
   - Quality is a journey, not a destination. The Japanese term **Kaizen** (µö╣Õ¢ä) means "change for the better."
   - Improvement is achieved through the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), also known as the **Deming Wheel**.

3. **Pillar 3 ŌĆö Total Employee Involvement (The "Who")**
   - Every employee, regardless of role, contributes to quality. Empowerment, training, and teamwork are essential.
   - Tools used include **Quality Circles**, **Cross-functional teams**, and **Suggestion systems**.

### 2.2 TQM vs. Traditional Quality Management

| Parameter | Traditional Approach | TQM Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to specifications | Fitness for use / Customer satisfaction |
| **Responsibility for Quality** | Quality Control Department only | Every employee |
| **Customer Orientation** | Production-driven | Customer-driven |
| **Decision Making** | Intuition-based | Fact-based (Data-driven) |
| **Improvement Focus** | Reactive (fix after defect) | Proactive (prevent defects) |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Adversarial | Partnership-based |
| **Structure** | Hierarchical, departmental | Flat, cross-functional teams |
| **Training** | Minimal, job-specific | Continuous, holistic |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since TQM is largely conceptual, the "formulas" here represent **analytical metrics and decision frameworks** rather than physical equations.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \frac{\text{Sum of Satisfaction Scores}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}} \times 100$ | Measures % customer satisfaction. |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Units Produced}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities. |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | Quantifies quality cost. |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \frac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Process capability indicator. |
| **Six Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \frac{USL - \mu}{3s}$ where $s$ = standard deviation | Statistical process control metric. |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan $\rightarrow$ Do $\rightarrow$ Check $\rightarrow$ Act | Continuous improvement framework. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:** All percentage-based metrics must lie between **0 and 100**. DPMO values are non-negative integers. Sigma levels are typically between **1Žā** and **6Žā**, with **6Žā = 3.4 DPMO** being world-class.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In an engineering organization, TQM is applied as follows:

- **Manufacturing:** Reducing defect rates on assembly lines using SPC (Statistical Process Control).
- **Software Engineering:** Improving code quality through continuous integration and testing.
- **Civil Engineering:** Ensuring construction materials meet IS/ISO standards.
- **Service Industries:** Enhancing customer service through feedback loops and employee training.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Always quote a real-world example in your answers (e.g., Toyota Production System, Tata Steel, ISO 9000-certified companies). Examiners reward contextual application.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since TQM is a conceptual management topic, the "derivation" here is a **logical conceptual derivation of the TQM framework** ŌĆö showing how the philosophy emerges from first principles and quality guru contributions.

### 3.1 Conceptual Derivation: The TQM Framework

Let us derive the TQM framework step-by-step from a logical sequence:

**Step 1: Define the Problem**
In a traditional system, quality is treated as a *function*, not a *culture*. Defects are detected after production, leading to:
- High rework costs.
- Customer dissatisfaction.
- Wasted materials and time.

$$Q_{\text{traditional}} = f(\text{Inspection}, \text{Standards})$$

This is a **reactive** approach ŌĆö quality is *verified*, not *built-in*.

**Step 2: Introduce the Process View**
If we accept that **quality must be built into the process** (Deming's insight), then:

$$Q_{\text{process}} = f(\text{Input}, \text{Process}, \text{Output}, \text{Feedback})$$

Here, quality depends on the entire system, not just the final inspection stage.

**Step 3: Add the Customer Dimension**
Quality is meaningless if the customer rejects the product. Therefore, we must add **fitness for use** (Juran's definition):

$$Q_{\text{customer}} = f(\text{Specifications}, \text{Actual Performance}, \text{Customer Expectation})$$

**Step 4: Add the People Dimension (Total Involvement)**
Since processes are run by people, Ishikawa emphasized that **all employees** must contribute:

$$Q_{\text{TQM}} = f(\text{Customer}, \text{Process}, \text{People}, \text{Continuous Improvement})$$

**Step 5: Add the Improvement Dimension (Kaizen)**
Quality is never "achieved" permanently. Using the PDCA cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \text{Plan: Identify the problem and propose a hypothesis.} \\
D_1 &= \text{Do: Implement the plan on a small scale.} \\
C_1 &= \text{Check: Measure the results against expectations.} \\
A_1 &= \text{Act: Standardize if successful, else repeat the cycle.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PDCA cycle is **iterative** ŌĆö after Act, a new Plan begins, leading to continuous improvement.

**Step 6: Final TQM Equation**
Combining all dimensions:

$$TQM = \int_{t=0}^{\infty} \left[ C(t) + P(t) + E(t) + I(t) \right] dt$$

Where:
- $C(t)$ = Customer satisfaction function over time.
- $P(t)$ = Process optimization function.
- $E(t)$ = Employee engagement function.
- $I(t)$ = Continuous improvement (Kaizen) function.

The **integral** symbolizes that TQM is an *ongoing, never-ending* philosophy.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Quality Cost Breakdown

The Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) can be derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $C_{\text{prevention}}$ = Cost of training, quality planning, process design.
- $C_{\text{appraisal}}$ = Cost of inspection, testing, audits.
- $C_{\text{internal failure}}$ = Cost of rework, scrap, re-inspection.
- $C_{\text{external failure}}$ = Cost of warranty claims, returns, reputation loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **prevention costs** with **appraisal costs**. Remember: Prevention = *avoiding* defects before they occur. Appraisal = *detecting* defects after they occur. Prevention is always cheaper in the long run.

### 3.3 The Deming 14 Points (Analytical Summary)

Although not numerically derived, the **14 Points** form the most-tested analytical framework in KTU exams:

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four of its key elements.*

**Model Answer:**
TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, and data-driven management philosophy aimed at continuously improving the quality of products and services by involving all members of the organization.

Four key elements:
1. Customer Focus
2. Total Employee Involvement
3. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
4. Fact-Based Decision Making

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
*Distinguish between Traditional Quality Management and Total Quality Management in any four points.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional QM | TQM |
|---|---|---|
| Responsibility | Quality Department only | Every employee |
| Orientation | Production-driven | Customer-driven |
| Improvement | Reactive (after defect) | Proactive (prevention) |
| Decision Making | Intuition-based | Data-driven |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the evolution of the Total Quality Management (TQM) concept. Discuss the contributions of any two Quality Gurus in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the key principles of TQM with suitable examples. Explain the PDCA cycle as a tool for continuous improvement. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Evolution of TQM:**

The concept of TQM evolved gradually through the contributions of several quality pioneers:

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Inspection Era (Pre-1920s):** Quality was ensured by inspecting finished products. Defective items were rejected. This was a corrective approach.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Quality Control Era (1920s-1940s):** Walter Shewhart introduced Statistical Quality Control (SQC), using control charts to monitor process variation. This shifted focus from inspection to process control.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Quality Assurance Era (1950s-1970s):** Deming and Juran taught Japanese industries the importance of systemic quality. The concept of quality assurance across the entire system emerged.

**Stage 4 ŌĆö TQM Era (1980s-Present):** The term TQM was formalized in the 1980s in the USA, with emphasis on total organizational involvement, customer focus, and continuous improvement.

**Contributions of Two Quality Gurus:**

**1. W. Edwards Deming (1900-1993):**
- Deming introduced the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), also called the Deming Wheel.
- He proposed **14 Points for Management** to transform organizational culture.
- Emphasized that **94% of quality problems** are attributable to the system, not workers.
- Introduced the concept of **statistical process control** and **common cause vs. special cause variation**.

**2. Joseph M. Juran (1904-2008):**
- Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** ŌĆö a customer-oriented definition.
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement.
- Stressed the importance of **breakthrough projects** and **quality by design**.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Key Principles of TQM with Examples:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Every process is designed to satisfy customer needs. *Example:* Amazon's customer-centric return policy.
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** Every employee contributes to quality. *Example:* Toyota's "Andon Cord" system allows any worker to stop the production line.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Small, incremental improvements daily. *Example:* Honda's assembly line improvements over decades.
4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Focus on improving processes, not blaming people. *Example:* Six Sigma projects at General Electric.
5. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use of data and statistical analysis. *Example:* SPC charts in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

**PDCA Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &: \text{Plan ŌĆö Identify a problem, analyze it, and propose a solution.} \\
D &: \text{Do ŌĆö Implement the proposed solution on a small, controlled scale.} \\
C &: \text{Check ŌĆö Measure the actual results and compare with expected outcomes.} \\
A &: \text{Act ŌĆö If successful, standardize; if not, repeat the cycle with a new plan.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PDCA cycle is iterative ŌĆö after Act, a new Plan begins, ensuring **continuous improvement**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

**(a)** Define TQM. Explain the key elements of TQM in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the barriers to TQM implementation. How can these barriers be overcome? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

*(Same TQM definition as in Part A Q1.)*

**Key Elements of TQM:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Identify and meet customer needs. Includes *internal customers* (other departments) and *external customers* (buyers).
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** All employees, from CEO to operator, are responsible for quality. Achieved through empowerment, training, and recognition.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Ongoing, incremental improvements. Achieved through PDCA, Six Sigma, 5S, etc.
4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Focus on improving processes. Reduce variation and increase capability.
5. **Integrated System:** All departments work together as one unified system, breaking down silos.
6. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use statistical data, not assumptions.
7. **Communication:** Effective, timely, and transparent communication at all levels.
8. **Supplier Partnership:** Long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with suppliers.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Barriers to TQM Implementation:**

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment:** Without leadership support, TQM initiatives fail.
2. **Resistance to Change:** Employees fear job loss or increased workload.
3. **Inadequate Training:** Workers lack the skills to use quality tools.
4. **Poor Communication:** Misalignment between departments.
5. **Short-Term Focus:** Management seeks quick profits over long-term quality.
6. **Insufficient Resources:** Budget and time constraints.
7. **Cultural Barriers:** Hierarchical organizational cultures resist employee empowerment.

**Overcoming the Barriers:**

1. **Leadership Commitment:** Top management must actively champion TQM.
2. **Change Management:** Communicate the "why" of change, involve employees in planning.
3. **Comprehensive Training:** Invest in continuous education on quality tools.
4. **Transparent Communication:** Regular meetings, suggestion systems, open-door policies.
5. **Long-Term Vision:** Set multi-year quality goals aligned with strategy.
6. **Adequate Resource Allocation:** Budget for tools, training, and audits.
7. **Cultural Transformation:** Encourage experimentation, reward innovation, eliminate fear of failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse TQM with ISO 9000.** ISO 9000 is a *certification standard*; TQM is a *management philosophy*. ISO 9000 can be part of TQM, but TQM is broader.
> 2. **Always attribute ideas correctly.** Deming = PDCA + 14 Points. Juran = Quality Trilogy + Fitness for Use. Ishikawa = Quality Circles + Fishbone. Crosby = Zero Defects + Quality is Free.
> 3. **Avoid one-line answers in Part B.** A 7-mark question requires at least **300-400 words** with diagrams, examples, and structured points.
> 4. **Draw a diagram whenever possible.** A PDCA diagram or TQM element circle adds 1-2 free marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TQM = Total + Quality + Management** ŌĆö quality is everyone's responsibility, not a department's.
- **Five key thinkers:** Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum ŌĆö know each one's main contribution.
- **PDCA Cycle** = Deming's contribution; iterative and continuous.
- **Juran's Trilogy** = Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's "Quality is Free"** and **Zero Defects** concept.
- **Ishikawa's** Quality Circles and Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram.
- **Customer Focus** is the *primary* dimension ŌĆö internal and external customers both matter.
- **Kaizen** = Japanese for "continuous improvement" ŌĆö small, daily, incremental.
- **COPQ** has four components: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- **Deming's 14 Points** are the most-tested framework in KTU exams.
- **TQM is not a tool or a certificate** ŌĆö it is a *culture* and a *philosophy*.
- **Six Sigma (6Žā) = 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** ŌĆö world-class quality benchmark.
- **Barriers to TQM:** Lack of commitment, resistance to change, poor training, short-term focus, cultural inertia.
- **Implementation phases:** Awareness $\rightarrow$ Planning $\rightarrow$ Execution $\rightarrow$ Review (continuous loop).
- **In exam answers, always include a diagram and a real-world example** (Toyota, Tata, GE, Amazon, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Axioms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** TQM Axioms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of TQM Axioms

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **OEIET612 ŌĆō Quality Management**, **TQM Axioms** are defined as the **fundamental, self-evident, and universally accepted principles (or "truths")** that form the philosophical and operational bedrock of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. The term *axiom* is borrowed from classical logic and mathematics ŌĆö it represents a statement so fundamentally true that it does not require proof, only acceptance and consistent application.

According to the universally cited TQM literature, an **axiom of TQM** is a foundational belief that governs the behaviour, decisions, and culture of an organisation pursuing quality excellence. These axioms are *not* techniques, tools, or statistical methods ŌĆö they are the **mindset and value-system** upon which all TQM tools (such as the 7 QC Tools, FMEA, Six Sigma, or the PDCA cycle) are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TQM Axioms are treated as the *philosophical foundation* of Total Quality Management. A student must be able to **list, explain, and apply** these axioms in real industrial/business scenarios. Expect direct 3-mark definitions and 7-mark descriptive answers in the University ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of TQM Axioms as the **"10 Commandments of Quality"** for an organisation. Just as a building cannot stand without a solid foundation, an organisation cannot practise TQM without first accepting these universal truths.

**Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Human Body:**
Imagine a hospital. The axioms of TQM are like the **basic laws of medicine**:
- The axiom *"Customer Satisfaction is paramount"* is like a doctor's oath ŌĆö *"First, do no harm / serve the patient first."*
- The axiom *"Prevention over Inspection"* is like the principle of *preventive healthcare* (vaccination, hygiene) rather than *reactive care* (treating disease after it strikes).
- The axiom *"Continuous Improvement"* is like a doctor constantly updating medical knowledge.
- The axiom *"Total Involvement"* is like recognising that health is the responsibility of *every* organ in the body, not just the heart.

Without these "axioms" (laws of medicine), the hospital collapses. Similarly, without TQM Axioms, any quality programme collapses into a short-lived, paperwork-driven ritual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Axiom** = Philosophical principle / belief (the *WHY*)
> - **Tool / Technique** = Statistical or managerial method (the *HOW*)
> - **System** = The framework (e.g., ISO 9001) that operationalises axioms + tools (the *STRUCTURE*)

### 1.3 Standard Terminology and Metrics

The following terms and metrics are central to the discussion of TQM Axioms and will be tested in KTU evaluations:

- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** ŌĆö A measured metric reflecting the degree to which a product/service meets customer expectations.
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** ŌĆö The financial penalty incurred due to defects, rework, warranty claims, and lost reputation.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** ŌĆö The percentage of units completed correctly without any rework.
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** ŌĆö A core **Six Sigma** metric (**target: 3.4 DPMO**) that quantifies process capability.
- **PDCA (PlanŌĆōDoŌĆōCheckŌĆōAct)** ŌĆö Deming's improvement cycle, an axiomatic operationalisation.
- **Kaizen** ŌĆö Japanese term for *continuous, incremental improvement*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophical Hierarchy of TQM

TQM Axioms can be organised into **three logical tiers**:

**Tier 1 ŌĆö Foundational Axioms (The "WHY"):**
These define *what quality means* and *why it matters*.
- Quality is defined by the **Customer**.
- Quality is a **process**, not a programme.
- Quality requires **Prevention**, not inspection.
- Quality is the **responsibility of everyone**.

**Tier 2 ŌĆö Operational Axioms (The "HOW"):**
These define *how quality is achieved daily*.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** is non-negotiable.
- Decisions must be **fact-based**, not opinion-based.
- **System Thinking** ŌĆö improve the process, not the worker.
- **Supplier Partnership** ŌĆö vendors are stakeholders, not adversaries.

**Tier 3 ŌĆö Cultural Axioms (The "WHO"):**
These define *who owns quality* and *what culture sustains it*.
- **Total Involvement** of all employees.
- **Leadership Commitment** from top management.
- **Education and Training** at every level.

### 2.2 Detailed Explanation of Each Axiom

Below is the exhaustive KTU-aligned explanation of every major TQM Axiom. Each axiom is broken into the **belief**, the **operational meaning**, and the **real-world implication**.

#### Axiom 1: Quality is Defined by the Customer
- **Belief:** The customer ŌĆö internal or external ŌĆö is the ultimate judge of quality.
- **Operational Meaning:** Specifications, tolerances, and features are derived from customer needs (the *Voice of Customer ŌĆö VOC*), not from engineering convenience.
- **Implication:** Marketing, design, production, and service must all align with VOC.

#### Axiom 2: Quality is a Process, Not a Programme
- **Belief:** Quality cannot be "installed" via a short-term campaign. It is the *result* of well-designed, stable processes.
- **Operational Meaning:** Improve the *system*; do not blame the *individual* (Deming's 85/15 rule ŌĆö 85% of problems are system-related, only 15% are worker-related).
- **Implication:** Process mapping (SIPOC, flowcharts) and statistical process control (SPC) become core competencies.

#### Axiom 3: Prevention over Inspection
- **Belief:** It is cheaper and smarter to **build quality in** than to **inspect quality out**.
- **Operational Meaning:** Use *Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)*, *poka-yoke* (mistake-proofing), and robust design to prevent defects at source.
- **Implication:** Reduces **COPQ** drastically. Inspection is acknowledged as *costly, late, and ineffective* as a primary strategy.

#### Axiom 4: Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
- **Belief:** Today's "good enough" is tomorrow's "unacceptable."
- **Operational Meaning:** Apply the **PDCA cycle** repeatedly. Even a 1% daily improvement compounds to ~37├Ś improvement in a year.
- **Implication:** Every employee must be trained in **basic problem-solving** (the 7 QC Tools).

#### Axiom 5: Fact-Based Decision Making
- **Belief:** In God we trust; all others must bring **data** (W. Edwards Deming).
- **Operational Meaning:** Decisions are based on statistical evidence (control charts, Pareto analysis, hypothesis testing), not on intuition, hierarchy, or anecdote.
- **Implication:** Build a culture of measurement and statistical literacy.

#### Axiom 6: Total Involvement of People
- **Belief:** Quality is not the Quality Department's job. It is *every* employee's job.
- **Operational Meaning:** Cross-functional **Quality Circles**, **Self-Directed Work Teams**, and **Suggestion Systems** are deployed.
- **Implication:** Empowerment + Accountability at every level.

#### Axiom 7: Leadership and Top Management Commitment
- **Belief:** Without **visible, sustained** leadership commitment, TQM dies.
- **Operational Meaning:** Leaders set the quality vision, allocate resources, remove barriers, and personally champion quality initiatives.
- **Implication:** *Quality Policy*, *Quality Objectives*, and *Management Review* are leadership artefacts.

#### Axiom 8: System Thinking
- **Belief:** An organisation is an **interconnected system**, not a set of isolated departments.
- **Operational Meaning:** Optimise the *whole* system (e.g., the supply chain) rather than sub-optimising individual functions.
- **Implication:** Recognise that improving one department at the expense of another is a **zero-sum game**.

#### Axiom 9: Supplier Partnership
- **Belief:** Suppliers are **partners**, not adversaries.
- **Operational Meaning:** Long-term contracts, shared training, joint problem-solving, and reduced supplier base (strategic sourcing).
- **Implication:** Improves incoming quality and reduces inspection burden.

#### Axiom 10: Education and Training
- **Belief:** A trained mind is the most valuable quality asset.
- **Operational Meaning:** Continuous, structured training in statistical tools, problem-solving, customer relations, and domain skills.
- **Implication:** Training is an *investment*, not a *cost*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Metrics)

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Purpose / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units out of Process}}{\text{Total Units Entering Process}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Measures process capability; high FPY = stable process |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Decimal / Percent | Cumulative yield across multiple process steps |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects per million | Six Sigma benchmark; **target 3.4 DPMO** = 6Žā level |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}\right)} + 1.5$ | Sigma (Žā) | Higher Žā = fewer defects |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Currency (Ōé╣, $, Ōé¼) | Quantifies the *price* of non-conformance |
| Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Performance Rating} \times \text{Importance Weight})}{\sum \text{Importance Weights}}$ | Index (0ŌĆō100) | Weighted satisfaction measurement |
| Kaizen Compound Improvement | $\text{Final Value} = \text{Initial Value} \times (1 + r)^{n}$ | Dimensionless | With $r = 0.01$ daily, $n = 365$ gives ~37.78├Ś improvement |
| Deming 85/15 Rule | $85\% \text{ System} \quad : \quad 15\% \text{ Worker}$ | Percent | Locus of problem ownership |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorise the DPMO and FPY formulas letter-perfect. The 3.4 DPMO benchmark for 6-Sigma and the 85/15 Deming rule are *favourite* KTU Part A questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These axioms are not merely philosophical ŌĆö they are operationalised daily in:

- **Automotive industry:** Toyota Production System (axioms: prevention, Kaizen, system thinking, respect for people).
- **Software industry:** CMMI and Agile methodologies embody prevention, continuous improvement, and total involvement.
- **Healthcare:** Joint Commission International (JCI) accreditation standards enforce fact-based decisions, customer (patient) focus, and continuous improvement.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100 standard mandates supplier partnership, prevention, and leadership commitment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: The Six Sigma Linkage

One of the most important *quantitative* outcomes of TQM Axioms is the **Six Sigma** level of quality. Let us derive the relationship between DPMO, the normal distribution, and the sigma level step-by-step.

**Step 1: Define Defect Rate (Yield)**

$$Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$$

**Step 2: Define Defect Probability**

$$p = 1 - Y$$

**Step 3: Convert to Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**

$$\text{DPMO} = p \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4: Convert DPMO back to a Z-score (long-term capability)**

The Z-score in a standard normal distribution is found using the inverse cumulative distribution function:

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - p\right)$$

where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the *inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function*.

**Step 5: Apply the Industry 1.5-Žā Shift (Motorola Convention)**

In real processes, the mean drifts by approximately **1.5Žā** over the long term. Therefore, the *true* sigma level is:

$$\sigma_{\text{process}} = Z + 1.5$$

**Step 6: Example Calculation for 3.4 DPMO (the 6-Sigma benchmark)**

Given $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$, we compute $p = 3.4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0000034$.

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.0000034) = \Phi^{-1}(0.9999966) \approx 4.5$$

$$\sigma_{\text{process}} = 4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0 \; \sigma$$

**Interpretation:** A 6-Žā process produces only **3.4 defects per million opportunities** ŌĆö translating the TQM axiom of *prevention* and *process excellence* into a quantitative, auditable benchmark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The 1.5Žā shift convention was introduced by **Motorola** in the 1980s (Bill Smith). It is an *engineering convention*, not a natural law. KTU exams often ask: *"Why 3.4 DPMO corresponds to 6-Sigma?"* ŌĆö the answer hinges on this 1.5Žā shift.

### 3.2 Derivation: Compounding Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)

The Kaizen axiom states that **small, continuous improvements compound exponentially**. Let us derive this mathematically.

**Step 1: Initial State**
Let the initial performance metric be $P_0$.

**Step 2: Daily Improvement Rate**
Let the daily fractional improvement rate be $r$. A modest $r = 0.01$ (1% per day) is achievable.

**Step 3: After $n$ Days**

$$P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r)^{n}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $r = 0.01$, $n = 365$**

$$P_{365} = P_0 \times (1.01)^{365}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate $(1.01)^{365}$ Using the Continuous Compounding Limit**

We know that $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{x}$. Approximating with the identity $(1.01)^{365} \approx e^{0.01 \times 365} = e^{3.65}$:

$$P_{365} \approx P_0 \times e^{3.65} \approx P_0 \times 38.47$$

**Conclusion:** A mere 1% improvement every working day transforms performance by **~38.5 times** in one year. This mathematically justifies the Kaizen axiom as a **strategic imperative**, not a feel-good slogan.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Axiom-to-Tool Mapping

This is a high-yield KTU matrix linking each axiom to its operational tool and metric.

| Axiom | Operational Tool | Quantitative Metric | KTU Exam Favourite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer is the judge | Voice of Customer (VOC), QFD | Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) | High |
| Quality is a process | SIPOC, Flowcharts, SPC | FPY, RTY | High |
| Prevention over inspection | FMEA, poka-yoke | COPQ, DPMO | Very High |
| Continuous improvement | PDCA, 7 QC Tools | Kaizen multiplier | Very High |
| Fact-based decisions | Control charts, Pareto, Hypothesis testing | p-value, Cp, Cpk | High |
| Total involvement | Quality Circles, Suggestion schemes | Suggestions per employee | Medium |
| Leadership commitment | Quality Policy, MBO | Resource allocation % | Medium |
| System thinking | Value stream mapping | Lead time, throughput | High |
| Supplier partnership | Strategic sourcing, supplier audits | Supplier defect rate | Medium |
| Education & training | Training hours, certifications | Training ROI | Medium |

### 3.4 Deming's 14 Points vs TQM Axioms ŌĆö Critical Distinction

A frequently confused topic in KTU exams is the difference between **TQM Axioms** and **Deming's 14 Points**. The mapping is as follows:

- **TQM Axioms** = *Universal, philosophical truths* about quality.
- **Deming's 14 Points** = *Prescriptive actions* (operational prescriptions) derived *from* those axioms.
- **Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) = Another *operational framework* aligned with the axioms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "Deming's 14 Points" as a *definition* of TQM Axioms. The 14 Points are *applications*, not the axioms themselves.

---
