## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] Define TQM Axioms. List any four major TQM Axioms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
TQM Axioms are the fundamental, self-evident, universally accepted philosophical principles that form the foundation of Total Quality Management. They represent the core beliefs upon which all quality tools, systems, and behaviours are built.

**List of Four Axioms (1.5 marks ŌĆö 0.375 each):**
1. **Customer Satisfaction** is the ultimate goal of any organisation.
2. **Quality is a process**, not a short-term programme.
3. **Prevention** is preferable to inspection.
4. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** must be embedded in the organisational culture.

*(Alternatively, students may list: Total Involvement, Fact-Based Decisions, Leadership Commitment, System Thinking, Supplier Partnership, Education & Training.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] Explain the TQM Axiom "Prevention over Inspection" with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):**
The axiom states that it is **far more effective and economical to design quality into a product or process at the design stage** than to detect defects through inspection after production. Inspection only *identifies* defects; prevention *eliminates* them at the source. This reduces rework, warranty costs, and customer dissatisfaction.

**Industrial Example (1 mark):**
In an automobile assembly line, instead of inspecting every car for loose wheel nuts at the end of the line, a **torque-limiting wrench (poka-yoke)** is installed at the wheel-station itself. This *prevents* the defect of an under-tightened wheel from ever occurring ŌĆö exemplifying prevention over inspection.

---

### PART B ŌĆö Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the ten TQM Axioms in detail. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Critically analyse how the TQM Axiom *"Customer is the Judge of Quality"* is implemented in a software services company. *(7 marks)*

##### **Solution to Q.A(a) ŌĆö The Ten TQM Axioms (7 marks):**

1. **Customer is the Judge (0.7 mark):** Quality is defined by the customer's needs, expectations, and perceptions ŌĆö not by internal standards. The *Voice of Customer* (VOC) drives design.

2. **Quality is a Process, not a Programme (0.7 mark):** Quality is the *output* of stable, well-designed processes. Improve the system, not the worker (Deming's 85/15 rule).

3. **Prevention over Inspection (0.7 mark):** Build quality in at the design stage using FMEA and poka-yoke. Inspection is a costly, late, and reactive approach.

4. **Continuous Improvement ŌĆö Kaizen (0.7 mark):** Apply the **PDCA cycle** repeatedly. A 1% daily improvement compounds to ~38├Ś in one year.

5. **Fact-Based Decision Making (0.7 mark):** Use data and statistical tools (control charts, Pareto, hypothesis testing). Intuition and hierarchy are *not* substitutes for evidence.

6. **Total Involvement of People (0.7 mark):** Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, is responsible for quality. Empowerment + accountability.

7. **Leadership & Top Management Commitment (0.7 mark):** Leaders set vision, allocate resources, and *personally* champion quality. Without this, TQM fails.

8. **System Thinking (0.7 mark):** Optimise the whole value chain, not isolated departments. Sub-optimisation creates silos.

9. **Supplier Partnership (0.7 mark):** Treat suppliers as long-term partners with shared goals, training, and joint problem-solving.

10. **Education and Training (0.7 mark):** Continuous, structured training in statistical tools, problem-solving, and soft skills. Training is an *investment*, not a cost.

##### **Solution to Q.A(b) ŌĆö Customer as Judge in a Software Services Company (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö VOC Capture (1.5 marks):**
The software company uses **interviews, surveys, focus groups, and user-story workshops** to capture the *Voice of Customer* before writing a single line of code. Personas, empathy maps, and journey maps are constructed.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Translating VOC into Specifications (1.5 marks):**
Customer needs are converted into **functional requirements, non-functional requirements (NFRs)** such as performance, security, and usability, and formal **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Agile & Iterative Validation (1.5 marks):**
The company adopts **Scrum / Agile** with **2-week sprints**. Each sprint ends with a *Sprint Review* where the customer (Product Owner) *accepts or rejects* user stories. This embodies the axiom ŌĆö the customer is the *real* judge.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Post-Delivery Measurement (1.5 marks):**
Tools such as **Net Promoter Score (NPS)**, **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)**, and **Customer Effort Score (CES)** are tracked continuously. Bug escape rates, churn, and renewal rates are correlated with quality perception.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
*Strengths:* Rapid feedback, customer-validated features, lower rework.
*Limitations:* Customer expectations evolve (Kano model's "delighters" become "must-haves" over time); thus, the axiom requires *dynamic* interpretation ŌĆö not a one-time VOC study but a *continuous dialogue*.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative:**

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
**(a)** Explain Deming's 85/15 Rule and the PDCA cycle as manifestations of TQM Axioms. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A manufacturing unit produces 50,000 units/day with 4 defect opportunities per unit. If the goal is to achieve 6-Sigma quality, calculate the maximum allowable DPMO and the corresponding defects/day. *(7 marks)*

##### **Solution to Q.B(a) ŌĆö Deming's 85/15 Rule & PDCA (7 marks):**

**Deming's 85/15 Rule (3.5 marks):**
According to W. Edwards Deming, approximately **85% of quality problems in an organisation are caused by the system (processes, procedures, management systems)** and only **15% are attributable to the individual worker**. The TQM axiom *"Quality is a Process, not a Programme"* is operationalised by this rule. When a defect occurs, managers must *first* investigate the system ŌĆö the training, the equipment, the procedure, the inputs ŌĆö before blaming the worker. This shifts the organisation from a *blame culture* to a *learning culture*.

**PDCA Cycle ŌĆö Deming's Improvement Wheel (3.5 marks):**

The **PlanŌĆōDoŌĆōCheckŌĆōAct** cycle operationalises the *Continuous Improvement* axiom:
- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse root cause (using 5-Why / Fishbone), and devise a countermeasure.
- **Do:** Implement the countermeasure on a small scale.
- **Check:** Measure outcomes using data; compare actual vs. expected results.
- **Act:** If successful, *standardise*; if not, revise the plan and re-iterate.

The PDCA cycle is repeated *indefinitely*, embodying *Kaizen* ŌĆö the axiom that improvement has noń╗łńé╣ (endpoint).

##### **Solution to Q.B(b) ŌĆö 6-Sigma DPMO Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Daily production = 50,000 units
- Defect opportunities per unit = 4
- Target: 6-Sigma quality

**Step 1: Recall 6-Sigma Benchmark (1 mark):**
At 6-Sigma level, the industry standard is **DPMO = 3.4** (with the 1.5Žā shift convention).

**Step 2: Calculate Total Opportunities per Day (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = \text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}$$

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = 50{,}000 \times 4 = 200{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply the DPMO Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$3.4 = \dfrac{D}{200{,}000} \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Maximum Allowable Defects $D$ (1.5 marks):**

$$D = \dfrac{3.4 \times 200{,}000}{10^{6}} = \dfrac{680{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.68$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Maximum allowable DPMO = 3.4 defects per million opportunities** *(0.5 mark)*
- **Maximum allowable defects per day Ōēł 0.68**, i.e., fewer than 1 defect per day in 200,000 opportunities. *(0.5 mark)*

**Engineering Insight (Bonus):** This is a *near-zero-defect* benchmark. Achieving it requires **prevention-based design, statistical process control, and disciplined PDCA cycles** ŌĆö directly invoking the TQM axioms of *Prevention*, *Process*, and *Continuous Improvement*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TQM Axioms with Deming's 14 Points.** The 14 Points are *prescriptions derived from* the axioms. Writing "create constancy of purpose" as an *axiom* will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5Žā shift** in DPMO-to-Sigma conversions. Many students compute $Z = 4.5$ but write the answer as "5-sigma," losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Vague answers on "Prevention vs Inspection."** Always quantify with **COPQ** (cost of poor quality) to score full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "why" and jumping to the "what."** KTU examiners award marks for *reasoning*, not just bullet points. Always state *why* an axiom matters.
> 5. **Mixing up TQM Axioms with ISO 9001 Clauses.** ISO 9001 is a *certifiable standard*; TQM axioms are *philosophical foundations*. They are related but not identical.
> 6. **Calculation errors in DPMO** ŌĆö forgetting to multiply opportunities by units, or misplacing the $10^6$ factor.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** TQM Axioms = fundamental, universal, self-evident beliefs forming the philosophical foundation of Total Quality Management.
- **Origin:** Rooted in the works of **W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, Philip Crosby, and Kaoru Ishikawa**.
- **Ten Core Axioms** (must memorise in order):
  1. Customer is the Judge
  2. Quality is a Process (not a programme)
  3. Prevention over Inspection
  4. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen / PDCA)
  5. Fact-Based Decision Making
  6. Total Involvement of People
  7. Leadership & Top Management Commitment
  8. System Thinking
  9. Supplier Partnership
  10. Education and Training
- **Deming's 85/15 Rule:** 85% system causes, 15% worker causes ŌĆö supports the *Process* axiom.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan ŌåÆ Do ŌåÆ Check ŌåÆ Act ŌĆö operationalises *Kaizen*.
- **Key Quantitative Metrics:**
  - $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}$ where $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit.
  - **6-Žā benchmark: DPMO = 3.4** (with 1.5Žā shift).
  - **Kaizen compound factor:** $(1.01)^{365} \approx 38.5$.
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI):** Weighted average of performance ├Ś importance.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality):** Internal failure + External failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs.
- **TQM Axioms ŌēĀ Deming's 14 Points ŌēĀ ISO 9001 Clauses** ŌĆö they are related but distinct concepts.
- **TQM Axioms ŌēĀ Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) ŌĆö the trilogy is an *operational model* derived from axioms.
- **Real-world exemplars:** Toyota Production System (axioms of Kaizen, prevention, respect for people); Motorola (origin of 6-Sigma).
- **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** Tier 1 (Why: Foundational) ŌåÆ Tier 2 (How: Operational) ŌåÆ Tier 3 (Who: Cultural).
- **Always answer in KTU exams:** Definition ŌåÆ List ŌåÆ Detailed Explanation ŌåÆ Industrial Example ŌåÆ Quantification (if applicable).
- **Common confusions to avoid:** Axioms vs Tools, Deming 14 Points vs TQM Axioms, Six Sigma vs TQM, ISO 9001 vs TQM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequences of Total Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Consequences of Total Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Consequences of Total Quality ŌĆō Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequences of Total Quality (TQM)** refer to the measurable outcomes, results, and transformative effects ŌĆö both tangible and intangible ŌĆö that an organization experiences as a direct result of the successful adoption, implementation, and cultural integration of Total Quality Management philosophy across all hierarchical levels and processes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, these consequences are classified into **four beneficiary domains** as per the David A. Garvin framework adapted for Quality Management curricula:

1. **Consequences to Customers**
2. **Consequences to Employees**
3. **Consequences to Suppliers / Partners**
4. **Consequences to the Society at Large**

Additionally, the syllabus emphasizes the **dual financial consequence**:
- **Costs of Poor Quality (COPQ)** ŌĆö losses incurred due to defects, rework, and warranty.
- **Costs of Quality Assurance** ŌĆö investments made to prevent defects and build quality systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Healthy Lifestyle" Metaphor**
>
> Think of TQM like a person adopting a strict, disciplined, healthy lifestyle ŌĆö eating nutritious food, exercising daily, sleeping well. The *consequences* of this lifestyle are not just one thing; they ripple outward:
>
> - To the **person** (employee): better energy, fewer sick days, pride in fitness.
> - To the **family** (customers): less stress, happier relationships, better service.
> - To the **gym trainer/dietician** (suppliers): long-term partnership, trust.
> - To the **community** (society): less burden on hospitals, more productive citizen.
> - And finally, the **financial outcome** ŌĆö you spend less on medicines (less COPQ) and more on gym memberships (prevention costs), but overall you are wealthier in the long run.
>
> Just like fitness, TQM is **not an event ŌĆö it is a culture with consequences**.

## 1.3 Key Constants / Standard Metrics

- **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality): Typically ranges from **15% to 40%** of total operating costs in unrefined organizations.
- **Target Industry Benchmark**: World-class organizations target **COPQ < 5%** of sales revenue.
- **Customer Retention Rate**: Each **5% improvement** in retention can increase profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Co. research, widely cited in KTU modules).
- **Pareto's 80/20 Rule**: ~**80%** of quality problems arise from ~**20%** of causes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Consequences ŌĆö Expanded Logic

### **Pillar 1: Consequences to Customers**

| Sub-Consequence | Operational Meaning |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Improved Product Reliability** | Fewer breakdowns, longer product life. |
| **Enhanced Customer Satisfaction** | Lower complaint rates, higher NPS (Net Promoter Score). |
| **Better Value for Money** | Optimal price-to-quality ratio. |
| **Reduced Total Cost of Ownership** | Lower maintenance, repair, and replacement costs over product lifecycle. |
| **Increased Trust & Loyalty** | Repeat purchase, brand advocacy. |

**The 'Why' Behind It:** When quality is built *in*, defects are prevented *upstream*, leading to superior products reaching the customer with **zero defects** culture alignment.

---

### **Pillar 2: Consequences to Employees**

- **Job Satisfaction** rises as workers are empowered through Quality Circles and Kaizen teams.
- **Pride of Workmanship** is cultivated ŌĆö every employee becomes a "quality inspector of their own output".
- **Reduced Workplace Stress** because rework, firefighting, and blame-culture decrease.
- **Improved Morale & Retention** as employees feel their contributions matter.
- **Skill Enhancement** through continuous training (a pillar of TQM).

---

### **Pillar 3: Consequences to Suppliers**

- **Long-Term Partnership Relationships** replace transactional, adversarial dealings.
- **Mutual Trust & Open Communication** ŌĆö suppliers are treated as extensions of the organization.
- **Stable Order Flow** ŌĆö fewer suppliers, higher volumes per supplier.
- **Collaborative Problem Solving** ŌĆö joint kaizen, shared technical knowledge.
- **Reduced Inspection Costs** ŌĆö incoming inspection reduces as supplier quality improves.

---

### **Pillar 4: Consequences to Society**

- **Environmental Protection** ŌĆö reduced waste, pollution, and resource consumption.
- **Resource Conservation** ŌĆö efficient use of raw materials and energy.
- **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** uplift ŌĆö ethical labor practices.
- **Economic Prosperity** ŌĆö higher employment, GDP contribution through quality exports.
- **Safer Products** in the market (e.g., automobile recalls reduced).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any numerical problem on this topic.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Currency (Ōé╣, $, etc.) |
| **COPQ as % of Sales** | $COPQ\,\% = \dfrac{COPQ}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Quality Cost Ratio (P-A Ratio)** | $P/A = \dfrac{\text{Prevention Cost}}{\text{Appraisal Cost}}$ | Dimensionless; **Target > 1** |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing without rework}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{\text{No. of Satisfied Customers}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Per Million Opportunities |
| **Return on Quality Investment** | $ROQI = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit from Quality}}{\text{Quality Investment Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Pareto Threshold** | $80\%$ of problems from $20\%$ of causes | Empirical; not a strict formula |

> **Note on notation:** All table cells use `\vert` or descriptive words instead of the vertical bar symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Indian manufacturing contexts (KSRTC buses, Tata Steel plants, Maruti Suzuki assembly lines), TQM consequences translate into:
- Reduced warranty claims.
- ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 certification readiness.
- Export competitiveness under Make-in-India initiatives.
- Lower employee attrition in shop-floor roles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COPQ Optimization Logic

The core philosophical derivation behind TQM consequences is that **prevention costs rise initially, but failure costs fall dramatically**, leading to a **net reduction in total quality cost**.

Let:

- $P$ = Prevention Cost
- $A$ = Appraisal Cost  
- $IFC$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $EFC$ = External Failure Cost

Total Cost of Quality (TCQ):

$$
TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC
$$

As quality investment ($P$) increases:

$$
P \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad IFC \downarrow \downarrow \quad \text{and} \quad EFC \downarrow \downarrow
$$

The optimal quality level $Q^*$ occurs where:

$$
\dfrac{d(TCQ)}{dP} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dP}{dP} + \dfrac{dA}{dP} + \dfrac{d(IFC)}{dP} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dP} = 0
$$

Since $\dfrac{dP}{dP} = 1$ (prevention cost rises 1:1 with investment) and both failure costs fall:

$$
1 + \dfrac{dA}{dP} + \dfrac{d(IFC)}{dP} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dP} = 0
$$

Because the failure costs drop faster than prevention rises, the net derivative becomes negative **beyond a threshold** ŌĆö meaning TCQ first rises, peaks, then falls. The minimum point of TCQ is the economically optimal quality investment.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit produces **50,000 units/month**. Data:

- Prevention cost = Ōé╣2,00,000
- Appraisal cost = Ōé╣1,50,000
- Internal failure cost = Ōé╣3,00,000
- External failure cost = Ōé╣1,50,000
- Sales revenue = Ōé╣50,00,000

**Compute:**

**(a)** Total COPQ
**(b)** COPQ as % of Sales
**(c)** P/A ratio
**(d)** Comment on TQM readiness.

---

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total Cost of Poor Quality:**

$$
COPQ = P + A + IFC + EFC
$$

$$
COPQ = 200000 + 150000 + 300000 + 150000
$$

$$
COPQ = 800000 \;\text{Ōé╣}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö COPQ as % of Sales:**

$$
COPQ\,\% = \dfrac{800000}{5000000} \times 100
$$

$$
COPQ\,\% = 16\%
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö P/A Ratio:**

$$
P/A = \dfrac{200000}{150000} = 1.33
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

- A **COPQ of 16%** is far above the world-class benchmark of **<5%**.
- A **P/A ratio of 1.33 (>1)** indicates the firm is **proactively investing in prevention**, which is a healthy sign.
- However, **IFC = Ōé╣3,00,000** is still the largest cost bucket, signaling **shop-floor process issues** that need root-cause analysis (Ishikawa / 5-Why).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always tie your numerical answer back to a **strategic recommendation** ŌĆö KTU evaluators award marks for interpretation, not just arithmetic.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Severity vs. Frequency of Consequences

| Consequence Domain | Likelihood of Realization | Severity if Realized | KTU Exam Weightage |
|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Customer satisfaction | **High** | **High** | Very High (Part B standard) |
| Employee retention | Medium | High | Medium (Part A) |
| Supplier partnership | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Society impact | Low (long-term) | Very High | Low (conceptual) |
| COPQ reduction | **High** | **High** | Very High (numerical) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö The Four Pillars of TQM Consequences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**"List any three consequences of TQM to employees."**

**Model Answer:**
The three major consequences of TQM to employees are:
1. **Improved Job Satisfaction** ŌĆö empowerment via quality circles and decision-making autonomy.
2. **Enhanced Pride of Workmanship** ŌĆö workers take ownership of output quality.
3. **Reduced Workplace Stress** ŌĆö fewer defects mean fewer firefighting situations, leading to a calmer work environment.

*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**"Define Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ). Mention its two main components."**

**Model Answer:**
COPQ is the total cost incurred by an organization due to defects, failures, and the activities required to detect and prevent them. Its two main components are:
1. **Internal Failure Cost** ŌĆö costs of rework, scrap, re-inspection within the organization.
2. **External Failure Cost** ŌĆö costs of warranty claims, returns, customer complaints, and recalls after delivery.

*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3)*

---

## **PART B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four categories of consequences of Total Quality Management**, with examples from a manufacturing organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company incurs the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention = Ōé╣4,00,000
- Appraisal = Ōé╣3,00,000
- Internal Failure = Ōé╣6,00,000
- External Failure = Ōé╣2,00,000
- Annual Sales = Ōé╣50,00,000

Calculate (i) Total COPQ, (ii) COPQ % of Sales, (iii) P/A ratio, and (iv) comment on the company's quality cost structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Categories of Consequences ŌĆö Detailed Explanation:**

| Category | Key Consequences | Manufacturing Example |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Customers** | Better product reliability, satisfaction, value, loyalty | Maruti Suzuki customers experience fewer breakdowns, leading to repeat purchases. |
| **Employees** | Job satisfaction, pride, reduced stress, skill uplift | Shop-floor workers at Tata Steel participate in Kaizen teams, gaining problem-solving skills. |
| **Suppliers** | Long-term partnership, trust, joint improvement | Bosch treats its Tier-1 suppliers as partners, sharing technical data for joint kaizen. |
| **Society** | Reduced pollution, safer products, CSR | A chemical plant adopting ISO 14001 reduces effluent discharge, benefiting the local community. |

*(Marks: 1 per category ├Ś 4 = 4 + 3 marks for examples and linkage = 7)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total COPQ:**

$$
COPQ = 400000 + 300000 + 600000 + 200000 = 1500000\;\text{Ōé╣}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö COPQ % of Sales:**

$$
COPQ\,\% = \dfrac{1500000}{5000000} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö P/A Ratio:**

$$
P/A = \dfrac{400000}{300000} = 1.33
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comments:**

- COPQ at **30% of sales** is **alarmingly high** ŌĆö far above the world-class benchmark of **<5%**.
- IFC at Ōé╣6,00,000 is the **dominant cost** ŌĆö indicating **process-level quality issues**.
- **P/A ratio > 1** shows management commitment to prevention, but prevention spending must be **redirected to root-cause analysis** (Ishikawa, 5-Why) to lower IFC.
- **Recommendation:** Implement **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **Poka-Yoke** devices on shop-floor to reduce internal failure costs by an estimated **40ŌĆō50%**.

*(Marks: 1+1+1+4 = 7 for arithmetic + interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **impact of TQM on suppliers and society** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Prevention Cost, Appraisal Cost, Internal Failure Cost, and External Failure Cost** with one example each. Compute the **Total Cost of Quality** for the following data:

| Cost Type | Amount (Ōé╣) |
|-----------|------------|
| Training programs | 1,00,000 |
| Incoming inspection | 80,000 |
| Rework | 1,20,000 |
| Warranty claims | 90,000 |
| Quality audits | 50,000 |
| Scrap | 70,000 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Impact on Suppliers & Society:**

- **Suppliers:** TQM creates a **partnering ecosystem** ŌĆö fewer vendors, long-term contracts, joint kaizen events. Example: Toyota's supplier development program.
- **Society:** Reduced environmental damage, safer products, ethical employment. Example: Wipro's green IT initiatives reducing e-waste.

*(Marks: 3 + 4 = 7)*

---

**(b) Categorization and Computation:**

| Item | Category | Amount (Ōé╣) |
|------|----------|------------|
| Training programs | Prevention | 1,00,000 |
| Incoming inspection | Appraisal | 80,000 |
| Quality audits | Appraisal | 50,000 |
| Rework | Internal Failure | 1,20,000 |
| Scrap | Internal Failure | 70,000 |
| Warranty claims | External Failure | 90,000 |

**Total Cost of Quality:**

$$
TCQ = 100000 + 80000 + 50000 + 120000 + 70000 + 90000
$$

$$
TCQ = 510000\;\text{Ōé╣}
$$

**COPQ %** (excluding prevention):

$$
COPQ = 80000 + 50000 + 120000 + 70000 + 90000 = 410000\;\text{Ōé╣}
$$

**P/A Ratio:**

$$
P/A = \dfrac{100000}{130000} = 0.77
$$

Since **P/A < 1**, the firm is **detection-oriented, not prevention-oriented** ŌĆö this is a **classical TQM gap** that must be addressed.

*(Marks: Categorization 3 + Computation 2 + Interpretation 2 = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Prevention with Appraisal** ŌĆö prevention *stops* defects; appraisal *detects* them. This distinction is **frequently tested** and commonly flubbed.
> 2. **Forgetting interpretation in numericals** ŌĆö KTU examiners award **2 to 3 marks** for commenting on COPQ % and P/A ratio *qualitatively*. Merely computing without commentary = **incomplete answer**.
> 3. **Missing "Society" in the four categories** ŌĆö students often list only Customers, Employees, Suppliers. Do not skip the **societal** pillar ŌĆö it is explicitly in the syllabus.
> 4. **Treating TQM as a one-time project** ŌĆö consequences emerge over **years**, not weeks. Mention this **temporal dimension** in long-answer questions.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Consequences of Total Quality**

- Ō£ģ The **four consequence domains** are: **Customers, Employees, Suppliers, Society**.
- Ō£ģ **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- Ō£ģ **World-class benchmark:** COPQ **< 5%** of sales; P/A ratio **> 1**.
- Ō£ģ Customer loyalty impact: **5% retention increase ŌåÆ 25%ŌĆō95% profit increase**.
- Ō£ģ TQM consequences are **long-term, cultural, and systemic** ŌĆö not quick fixes.
- Ō£ģ Pareto's **80/20** rule applies to cause distribution of defects.
- Ō£ģ Supplier partnership is a **strategic, not transactional**, relationship in TQM.
- Ō£ģ Societal consequences include **environmental, ethical, and economic** dimensions.
- Ō£ģ **Internal Failure Cost** is the largest in unrefined organizations ŌĆö fix this first.
- Ō£ģ Always pair numerical answers with **strategic recommendations** for full marks.
- Ō£ģ Reference framework: **David Garvin's** quality dimensions + **Deming's 14 Points** philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barriers to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Barriers to TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Barriers to TQM** are defined as the set of organizational, cultural, managerial, and systemic obstacles that actively prevent, delay, or dilute the successful planning, deployment, and sustenance of Total Quality Management (TQM) initiatives within an enterprise. These barriers act as **resistance forces** against the transformation required to adopt a quality-centric operating philosophy.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEIET612 ŌĆō Quality Management, Module 1)**, barriers to TQM are classified as the *inverse* of the TQM principles. Where there is commitment, training, and customer focus, TQM thrives. Where these are absent, barriers emerge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy ŌĆö "The Gym Membership Problem":**
> Imagine a person who buys a premium gym membership (this is the **TQM initiative**). The gym has world-class equipment, trainers, and diet plans (TQM tools like Kaizen, Six Sigma, QCC). However, the person **never actually goes to the gym**, skips trainer sessions, eats junk food, blames the gym for lack of results, and quits after two weeks.
> 
> In this analogy:
> - The **gym membership** = TQM program sanctioned by top management
> - **Skipping sessions** = Lack of employee involvement
> - **Ignoring the trainer** = Resistance to change
> - **Eating junk food** = Inadequate training/awareness
> - **Quitting** = Short-term focus / Loss of leadership commitment
> 
> The **barriers are not the absence of the gym** ŌĆö they are the *human and systemic behaviors* that prevent the program from delivering value.

## 1.3 Why Study Barriers?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, identifying barriers is the **first diagnostic step** before prescribing TQM solutions. Without removing barriers:
- Quality circles (QCC) become "talking shops"
- Statistical Process Control (SPC) becomes a paperwork ritual
- Customer focus remains a wall poster, not a process driver

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A 14-mark KTU question is frequently framed as *"Discuss the major barriers to TQM implementation with relevant examples"* or *"Explain how resistance to change acts as a barrier to TQM"*. Memorize the **categories** and at least **2ŌĆō3 specific barriers per category** with brief justifications.

## 1.4 The Six Major Categories of TQM Barriers (KTU Framework)

| # | Category | One-Line Description |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **People-Related Barriers** | Resistance from employees, fear, low morale |
| 2 | **Management-Related Barriers** | Lack of commitment, short-termism, poor leadership |
| 3 | **Resource-Related Barriers** | Inadequate budget, time, training, infrastructure |
| 4 | **System/Process-Related Barriers** | Poor measurement, rigid structure, no feedback |
| 5 | **Strategic/Cultural Barriers** | Misalignment with strategy, weak quality culture |
| 6 | **External/Environmental Barriers** | Supplier issues, market pressure, regulatory gaps |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Barriers (Layered Analysis)

### ­¤öĄ Layer 1 ŌĆö Barriers Related to **Top Management**

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment**
   - TQM is a long-term cultural transformation. If leaders pay only lip service, employees quickly sense the gap and disengage.
   - Symptom: *Quality policy signed but never enforced in board meetings.*

2. **Short-Term Financial Focus**
   - Shareholders and CFOs often demand quarterly ROI. TQM benefits typically accrue over 2ŌĆō5 years.
   - Symptom: *Budget cuts to quality training in the first lean year.*

3. **Inadequate Leadership & Vision**
   - Without a clear quality vision, departments pursue siloed KPIs that contradict TQM goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Pattern:** When a question lists "top management commitment" as the *single most critical* barrier, the expected answer justification is: *"Because TQM is a top-down philosophy, without senior leadership modeling quality behavior, all subordinate initiatives collapse."*

### ­¤¤ó Layer 2 ŌĆö Barriers Related to **Employees / Workforce**

4. **Resistance to Change**
   - Rooted in fear of job loss, new skill demands, or loss of comfort zones.
   - Rooted in **Kurt Lewin's Change Model**: *Unfreeze ŌåÆ Change ŌåÆ Refreeze* fails if "unfreeze" stage is skipped.

5. **Lack of Employee Involvement & Empowerment**
   - TQM requires bottom-up participation (QCC, suggestion schemes). Authoritarian cultures suppress this.

6. **Inadequate Training and Education**
   - Statistical tools (SPC, Pareto, Fishbone) cannot be applied by untrained staff.
   - Symptom: *Quality tools are deployed but data is misread.*

7. **Fear of Blame / Punitive Culture**
   - When mistakes are punished, employees hide defects instead of reporting them. This destroys the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.

### ­¤¤Ī Layer 3 ŌĆö Barriers Related to **Organization & Systems**

8. **Inadequate Quality Culture**
   - Culture eats strategy for breakfast. Without an embedded belief that "quality is everyone's job," TQM remains a department, not a movement.

9. **Poor Communication Systems**
   - Quality data must flow horizontally and vertically. Silos block this.

10. **Inadequate Measurement & Feedback**
    - Without KPIs, customer satisfaction surveys, and process capability indices (Cp, Cpk), improvement is guesswork.

11. **Inappropriate Organizational Structure**
    - Tall hierarchies slow decision-making. TQM thrives in flat, cross-functional structures.

### ­¤¤Ā Layer 4 ŌĆö Barriers Related to **Strategy & Resources**

12. **Lack of Strategic Planning Integration**
    - TQM must be embedded in the business strategy, not bolted on as a parallel program.

13. **Insufficient Resources (Time, Money, Tools)**
    - Quality is free only in the long run; it requires upfront investment in training, software (e.g., Minitab), and consultant fees.

14. **Poor Supplier Relationships**
    - If suppliers don't meet quality specs, internal TQM efforts are neutralized. *Your quality is only as good as your weakest supplier.*

### ­¤ö┤ Layer 5 ŌĆö External & Miscellaneous Barriers

15. **Lack of Customer Focus**
    - Organizations that assume they "know what customers want" without VOC (Voice of Customer) mechanisms.

16. **Regulatory & Market Pressures**
    - Rapidly changing compliance environments can force short-term fixes over long-term quality.

17. **Misuse of Quality Tools as Bureaucratic Rituals**
    - Filling Pareto charts and Fishbone diagrams without acting on insights.

## 2.2 The Resistance-to-Change Model (Lewin's Force Field)

TQM implementation is essentially a **change management** problem. The barriers can be visualized as *restraining forces* in **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Analysis**:

$$F_{net} = \sum F_{driving} - \sum F_{restraining}$$

Where:
- $F_{net}$ = Net force toward TQM adoption
- $F_{driving}$ = Forces pushing toward TQM (leadership vision, training, rewards)
- $F_{restraining}$ = **Barriers** (resistance, fear, resource scarcity, culture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** TQM succeeds when management **increases driving forces AND reduces restraining forces (barriers)** ŌĆö not just one of them. A common board-exam trap is the answer: *"Just train employees harder"* ŌĆö this ignores barrier removal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Exam Use |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|----------|
| Net TQM Force | $F_{net} = \sum F_{driving} - \sum F_{restraining}$ | Lewin's Force Field | Conceptual Q |
| Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ) | $CoPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}$ | Quality cost model | Justifies TQM |
| Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Measures process spread | Tool-based barrier |
| Six-Sigma Quality Level | $DPMO = 3.4$ per million | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Benchmark target |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (W_i \times S_i)}{Max\,Score} \times 100$ | Weighted satisfaction score | Removes "lack of VOC" barrier |
| Training Effectiveness | $TE = \dfrac{(Post\,Score - Pre\,Score)}{Max\,Score} \times 100$ | Measures training barrier removal | Justifies HR investment |
| PDCA Cycle | Plan $\rightarrow$ Do $\rightarrow$ Check $\rightarrow$ Act | Continuous improvement loop | Removes "no feedback" barrier |
| Pareto 80/20 Rule | $80\%$ of problems from $20\%$ of causes | Prioritization | Removes analysis paralysis |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Use the CoPQ model to justify TQM investment to CFOs by quantifying the cost of rejected parts and warranty claims.
- **Software Firms:** Apply Cpk metrics to remove the "inadequate measurement" barrier in agile sprints.
- **Civil Engineering Projects:** Use Pareto analysis on site defects to remove the "analysis paralysis" barrier.
- **Service Industries:** Use Voice of Customer (VOC) surveys to break the "assumed customer needs" barrier.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation ŌĆö Quantifying TQM Barriers Using the Force Field Model

In KTU examinations, students are often expected to *analytically discuss* how barriers interact. Below is a step-by-step logical derivation of how a single barrier ŌĆö **Resistance to Change** ŌĆö propagates through an organization, modeled mathematically.

### Step 1: Define the Resistance Function

Let the resistance to change in an organization of $N$ employees be:

$$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$$

where $R_i$ is the individual resistance of employee $i$.

### Step 2: Model Individual Resistance

Individual resistance is a function of three factors: **Fear ($F_i$)**, **Skill Gap ($G_i$)**, and **Past Negative Experience ($E_i$)**:

$$R_i = \alpha F_i + \beta G_i + \gamma E_i$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are weighting coefficients (with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$).

### Step 3: Expand Each Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
F_i &= \text{Fear of job loss + Fear of new tools} \\
G_i &= \text{Trainings attended}^{-1} \times \text{Tool complexity} \\
E_i &= \sum_{k=1}^{K} \delta_k \cdot \text{Prior failed initiative}_k
\end{aligned}$$

Each prior failed initiative is weighted by $\delta_k$ (a decay factor, $0 < \delta_k < 1$).

### Step 4: Net TQM Adoption Force

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= F_{driving} - R_{total} \\
&= F_{driving} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \alpha F_i + \beta G_i + \gamma E_i \right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: TQM Adoption Condition

For TQM to be successfully adopted, $F_{net}$ must exceed a critical threshold $F_{critical}$:

$$F_{net} \geq F_{critical} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad F_{driving} \geq F_{critical} + R_{total}$$

This means **management must increase driving forces beyond the sum of the critical threshold AND total resistance**.

### Step 6: Interpretation in Engineering Context

If $F_{driving} = 100$ units and $R_{total} = 85$ units, then $F_{net} = 15$ units. If $F_{critical} = 20$ units, TQM **fails** because $15 < 20$. Management has two levers:
1. **Increase $F_{driving}$** (more training, leadership, rewards) ŌĆö push to $F_{driving} = 110$, giving $F_{net} = 25 > 20$. Ō£ō
2. **Decrease $R_{total}$** (remove fear, fill skill gaps, communicate vision) ŌĆö push $R_{total}$ down to $70$, giving $F_{net} = 30 > 20$. Ō£ō

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This derivation, when presented in an exam, scores high marks if you **(a) clearly state assumptions, (b) explain each coefficient, and (c) conclude with a practical interpretation**. Pure math without engineering meaning loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Barrier Removal Strategies

| Barrier | Root Cause | Removal Strategy | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Lack of top management commitment | Leadership not accountable for quality | Make quality a board-level KPI; tie CEO bonus to CSI | 3ŌĆō4 marks (frequent) |
| Resistance to change | Fear, lack of trust | Lewin's 3-stage model + transparent communication | 4ŌĆō5 marks (very frequent) |
| Inadequate training | Resource scarcity, no TQM curriculum | Deploy TQM training matrix; partner with academic institutions | 2ŌĆō3 marks |
| Poor measurement | No KPIs, no SPC | Introduce Cp, Cpk, DPMO dashboards | 3 marks |
| Punitive culture | Blame-the-worker mindset | Adopt "mistake-proofing" (Poka-Yoke) and no-blame reporting | 3 marks |
| Inadequate resources | Budget cuts, ROI pressure | Build CoPQ business case; show 5-year payback | 2ŌĆō3 marks |
| Poor supplier quality | Weak vendor development | Implement Supplier Quality Assurance (SQA) programs | 2 marks |
| Lack of customer focus | No VOC mechanism | Deploy NPS, CSI surveys, Kano model | 3 marks |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (CoPQ Justification)

> A manufacturing firm reports:
> - Prevention cost = Ōé╣2,00,000
> - Appraisal cost = Ōé╣1,50,000
> - Internal failure cost = Ōé╣3,00,000
> - External failure cost = Ōé╣4,50,000

**Step 1:** Apply the CoPQ formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
CoPQ &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 2,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 3,00,000 + 4,50,000 \\
&= 11,00,000 \;\text{INR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Identify that **failure costs (internal + external = Ōé╣7,50,000) constitute ~68%** of total quality cost ŌĆö a classic sign of **reactive (appraisal-driven) quality culture**, which is itself a TQM barrier.

**Step 3:** Conclusion: Investing an additional Ōé╣1,00,000 in **prevention** (training, SPC) can realistically reduce failure costs by 40ŌĆō60%, yielding an ROI of 3ŌĆō4├Ś within one year. This argument **directly counters the "lack of resources" barrier**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Hierarchical Map of TQM Barriers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TQM IMPLEMENTATION"] --> B["BARRIERS TO TQM"]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Barrier Removal Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        I1["Diagnose Barriers via Surveys and Audits"]
        I2["Map to TQM Principles Gap"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESS STAGE"]
        P1["Prioritize Barriers by Pareto Analysis"]
        P2["Design Removal Strategy: Training, Leadership, Communication"]
        P3["Deploy SPC, VOC, QCC, PDCA Tools"]
    end

        O1["Monitor KPIs: CSI, Cpk, CoPQ, DPMO"]
        O2["Feedback to Leadership Dashboard"]
        O3["Continuous Improvement Loop"]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> O1 --> O2 --> O3
    O3 -.->|Feedback| I1
```

## 4.3 Force Field Analysis ŌĆö Driving vs Restraining Forces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRIVING["DRIVING FORCES (Promote TQM)"]
        DF1["Top Management Commitment"]
        DF2["Employee Training"]
        DF3["Customer Focus"]
        DF4["Quality Culture"]
    end

    subgraph RESTRAINING["RESTRAINING FORCES (Barriers)"]
        RF1["Resistance to Change"]
        RF2["Lack of Resources"]
        RF3["Poor Communication"]
        RF4["Short Term Focus"]
    end

    DRIVING --> GOAL["TQM SUCCESS"]
    RESTRAINING -.->|Oppose| GOAL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The green block represents forces pushing the organization toward TQM. The red block represents barriers (restraining forces). Equilibrium is reached when driving forces dominate. The TQM strategist's job is to **either strengthen driving forces or weaken restraining forces (barriers)**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**"List any six major barriers to the successful implementation of TQM in an organization."**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö each barrier: 0.5 mark):**

1. Lack of top management commitment and leadership.
2. Resistance to change from employees due to fear and uncertainty.
3. Inadequate training and education on quality tools and concepts.
4. Insufficient resources ŌĆö financial, time, and infrastructural.
5. Poor communication systems across departments and hierarchies.
6. Lack of a customer-focused culture and absence of Voice of Customer (VOC) mechanisms.
7. *(Optional 7th)* Weak measurement and feedback systems (no SPC, no KPIs).

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award full 3 marks when the student names **6 distinct, categorized barriers**. Repeating the same theme in different words (e.g., "no training" + "no education") is a common mistake that loses 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**"How does 'resistance to change' act as a barrier to TQM? Suggest one method to overcome it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):** Resistance to change is one of the most significant people-related barriers to TQM. Employees fear that new quality systems will increase their workload, expose their skill gaps, or even threaten job security. This fear manifests as passive non-cooperation, refusal to use new tools like SPC charts, and silent sabotage of quality circles. Since TQM relies on **bottom-up participation** and **cultural transformation**, any widespread resistance nullifies the initiative.

**Overcoming Method (1 mark):** Apply **Kurt Lewin's 3-Stage Change Model** ŌĆö *Unfreeze* (create awareness and urgency), *Change* (provide training and support), *Refreeze* (institutionalize new behaviors through rewards and recognition).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö With Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Discuss in detail the major **barriers to TQM implementation** under the categories of *people, management, and system* barriers. Provide examples for each. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Explain **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Analysis** as a framework for understanding and overcoming TQM barriers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**1. People-Related Barriers (2.5 marks):**

- **Resistance to Change:** Employees fear job loss, increased workload, or skill obsolescence. Example: Workers in a textile unit refusing to use new ERP-based quality logging.
- **Lack of Training:** Without training in SPC, Pareto, Fishbone, quality tools become paperwork. Example: Production staff filling Pareto charts without understanding them.
- **Fear of Blame:** Punitive cultures force workers to hide defects. Example: An auto-ancillary unit where assembly-line workers discard defective bolts secretly to avoid penalties.
- **Low Empowerment:** When employees cannot make decisions on the shop floor, Kaizen suggestions die. Example: A paint shop where painters cannot stop the line even when they spot a quality issue.

**2. Management-Related Barriers (2.5 marks):**

- **Lack of Top Management Commitment:** Quality policy remains on paper. Example: A CEO who signs the quality policy but never attends quality review meetings.
- **Short-Term Financial Focus:** Quality budgets cut in lean quarters. Example: An IT services firm that scrapped its Six Sigma Black Belt program to meet quarterly margin targets.
- **Inadequate Vision & Leadership:** Quality is treated as a department, not a philosophy. Example: A CEO delegating TQM entirely to the Quality Manager without strategic alignment.
- **Inconsistent Leadership Behavior:** Leaders demanding speed over quality. Example: Plant managers setting impossible delivery deadlines that override quality checks.

**3. System/Process-Related Barriers (2 marks):**

- **Inadequate Measurement:** No Cp, Cpk, DPMO tracking. Example: A foundry that tracks only output tonnage, not defect rates.
- **Poor Communication Systems:** Silos block cross-functional learning. Example: R&D and Production blaming each other for design defects.
- **Rigid Organizational Hierarchy:** Decisions take weeks. Example: A PSU where even minor process changes require 6-level approvals.
- **Lack of Feedback Loops:** No closed-loop PDCA. Example: Customer complaints registered but never analyzed for root cause.

---

#### **Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Kurt Lewin's Force Field Analysis (7 marks):**

Developed by social psychologist **Kurt Lewin** in the 1940s, the Force Field Analysis is a foundational change-management model that explains TQM implementation as a balance between **driving forces** and **restraining forces (barriers)**.

**Core Equation (2 marks):**

$$F_{net} = \sum F_{driving} - \sum F_{restraining}$$

- When $F_{net} > F_{critical}$, TQM is adopted successfully.
- When $F_{net} \leq F_{critical}$, TQM stalls or fails.

**Three-Stage Change Model (3 marks):**

1. **Unfreeze:** Communicate the urgent need for quality transformation. Diagnose existing barriers. Create awareness. (Example: A plant-wide "Quality Awareness Week".)
2. **Change:** Deploy new tools, training, and structures. (Example: SPC, QCC, 5S, Kaizen training programs.)
3. **Refreeze:** Lock in new behaviors through rewards, recognition, and updated policies. (Example: A "Quality Champion of the Month" award.)

**Application to TQM Barriers (2 marks):**

- **Driving Forces:** Leadership vision, training, customer focus, employee empowerment, reward systems.
- **Restraining Forces (Barriers):** Resistance to change, lack of resources, poor communication, short-termism, fear of blame.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Lewin's framework teaches that TQM is not a technical problem but a **human and systemic change problem**. Effective TQM leaders strengthen driving forces while simultaneously dismantling barriers, ensuring $F_{net}$ remains above the critical threshold.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the **role of organizational culture and leadership** as either enablers or barriers to TQM. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* "Without adequate measurement, TQM becomes a slogan." Critically analyze this statement in the context of **barriers to TQM**, citing the **Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ)** model and the **PDCA cycle**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Organizational Culture (3.5 marks):**

Culture is the **deepest barrier or enabler** of TQM. A culture that values:
- **Customer obsession:** Enables TQM.
- **Bureaucracy, blame, and siloed thinking:** Becomes a barrier.
- **Continuous learning:** Enables TQM.
- **Status quo and fear:** Becomes a barrier.

**Symptoms of a weak quality culture (1.5 marks):**
- Quality is "someone else's job" (the Quality Department).
- Mistakes are punished, not analyzed.
- Suggestions are ignored.

**Leadership's Role (2 marks):**
- Leaders model quality behavior (e.g., Gemba walks).
- Leaders allocate budget, time, and political capital to TQM.
- Leaders tie personal KPIs to quality outcomes.
- Without leadership commitment, TQM is reduced to slogans.

> *"Culture eats strategy for breakfast."* ŌĆö Peter Drucker (widely quoted concept).

---

#### **Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Statement Analysis (2 marks):**
The statement is **true**. TQM relies on data-driven decisions. Without measurement, organizations cannot identify defects, track improvement, or justify investments.

**CoPQ Model ŌĆö Justification of Measurement (2.5 marks):**

$$CoPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}$$

A typical reactive organization spends **60ŌĆō70%** of CoPQ on *failure costs*. By measuring CoPQ, management can justify investing in prevention and appraisal, thereby reducing total quality cost.

**PDCA Cycle ŌĆö The Measurement Backbone (2.5 marks):**

- **Plan:** Define metrics (Cp, Cpk, DPMO, CSI).
- **Do:** Implement the process and collect data.
- **Check:** Analyze data using SPC, Pareto.
- **Act:** Standardize improvements; loop back to Plan.

Without measurement, the **Check** stage collapses, breaking the cycle and converting TQM into a hollow slogan.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TQM Barrier Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Listing without classifying:** Writing 10 random barriers without grouping them (People / Management / System) loses 2 marks. Always use a **structured category approach**.
> 
> 2. **No examples:** KTU examiners reward **real-world or industry-specific examples** (e.g., "In an auto-ancillary unitŌĆ”"). A purely theoretical answer with zero examples caps at 60% marks.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the "Why":** Stating "lack of training is a barrier" earns only 0.5 mark. The full 1 mark requires explaining *why* ŌĆö "because untrained employees misread SPC charts, leading to wrong process adjustments."
> 
> 4. **Confusing TQM tools with TQM barriers:** Students often write "Pareto analysis is a barrier" ŌĆö this is wrong. The **misuse** of Pareto is a barrier, not the tool itself.
> 
> 5. **Skipping diagrams:** A 14-mark answer with **zero diagrams** loses 1ŌĆō2 marks in the "presentation" component. Always include at least one **Mermaid / flowchart / hierarchy** even if hand-drawn.
> 
> 6. **No conclusion:** A 14-mark answer **must end with a concluding paragraph** linking barriers to TQM success criteria.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> Ō£ģ **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Barriers to TQM**
> 
> - **TQM Barriers = Restraining Forces** in Lewin's Force Field. TQM success requires $F_{driving} > F_{restraining} + F_{critical}$.
> - **Six categories** to memorize: People, Management, System, Strategy, Resource, External.
> - **Top management commitment** is the *single most critical* barrier ŌĆö without it, TQM collapses.
> - **Resistance to change** is the most common *people-side* barrier; overcome it with **Lewin's 3-Stage Model** (Unfreeze ŌåÆ Change ŌåÆ Refreeze).
> - **Inadequate training** removes the ability to apply tools like SPC, Pareto, Fishbone, Cpk.
> - **Punitive culture** destroys the **PDCA cycle** by hiding defects.
> - **CoPQ** ($= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$) is the **measurement framework** that counters the "lack of resources" barrier.
> - **Customer focus** is removed when organizations assume (instead of measure) customer needs ŌĆö deploy **VOC, NPS, Kano model**.
> - **Supplier quality** is your quality ŌĆö weak suppliers nullify internal TQM.
> - **Culture eats strategy** ŌĆö without a quality culture, TQM remains a department, not a movement.
> - **Diagram + Examples + Classification + Conclusion** = the **14-mark answer formula** for KTU.
> - **Key terms to use in answers:** Force Field, Lewin's Model, PDCA, CoPQ, Cpk, VOC, Kaizen, QCC, SQA, CSI, DPMO.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question on barriers, allocate ~3 pages as follows:
> - **Page 1:** Introduction + 6 categorized barriers (1 example each)
> - **Page 2:** Deep dive into 2ŌĆō3 critical barriers (commitment, resistance, training) with examples
> - **Page 3:** Force Field Analysis + PDCA + CoPQ + Conclusion + 1 diagram

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deming Approach to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Deming Approach to TQM ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Deming Approach to Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, philosophy-driven management framework developed by **Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900ŌĆō1993)**, an American statistician, engineer, and management consultant. It represents a top-down, statistically grounded transformation model that shifts the organizational mindset from **inspection-based quality control** to **system-based quality improvement**, where the majority of quality problems are attributed to management and systemic processes rather than frontline workers. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the Deming Approach constitutes a *holistic transformation philosophy* consisting of four interrelated pillars: the **14 Points for Management**, the **Deming Chain Reaction**, the **PDCA (PlanŌĆōDoŌĆōCheckŌĆōAct) Cycle**, and the **System of Profound Knowledge**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Deming's approach is the most influential TQM philosophy globally. It formed the intellectual backbone of Japan's post-WWII industrial renaissance and gave birth to the prestigious **Deming Prize** (established 1951) ŌĆö the oldest and most respected award for quality excellence in the world. ### 1.2 Key Biographical & Historical Context | Parameter | Detail | | :--- | :--- | | Full Name | William Edwards Deming | | Lifespan | 1900 ŌĆō 1993 | | Nationality | American | | Core Discipline | Mathematical Physics & Statistics | | Famous For | Statistical Process Control, 14 Points, PDCA | | Japanese Legacy | Deming Prize, Japan's Quality Revolution | | Core Statistical Tool | Shewhart Control Charts (extended) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Intuitive View > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Orchestra Conductor** > Imagine an orchestra playing a symphony. If a violinist plays a wrong note, the conductor (management) does not fire the violinist (worker). Instead, the conductor asks: *Is the sheet music correct? Are the acoustics right? Is the violinist tired from over-rehearsals?* Deming's TQM philosophy is exactly this: **85% of quality problems lie in the system (the conductor, the score, the hall), and only 15% in the worker (the violinist).** The job of management is to fix the system, not blame the worker. This single insight ŌĆö the **85/15 Rule** ŌĆö is the philosophical bedrock of Deming's entire approach. > [!TIP] > **Why Deming's Approach Matters in KTU Exams** > The examiner frequently tests: (i) the listing and explanation of the 14 Points, (ii) the Deming Chain Reaction, (iii) PDCA stages, and (iv) the System of Profound Knowledge. Memorize with **understanding**, not just rote listing. ### 1.4 Core Statistical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Deming's 85/15 Rule:** **85%** of failures are attributable to the **system/process** (management responsibility), only **15%** to the **worker**. - **Deming Prize Year of Establishment:** **1951** (Japan). - **PDCA Iterations:** Continuous; theoretical minimum for systemic improvement Ōēł **3ŌĆō5 cycles**. - **Quality Cost Categories (PAF Model):** **4** categories ŌĆö Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Deming's Philosophy ŌĆö The Four Pillars Deming's TQM is built on **four interrelated theoretical pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific dimension of organizational quality. #### Pillar I: The 14 Points for Management Deming's 14 Obligations (Points) are the operational commandments that management must follow to instill quality. They are **not a checklist** but a **transformation manifesto**. | No. | Point (Short Title) | Core Idea | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Create Constancy of Purpose | Innovate, plan for the long term; focus on quality, not short-term profit | | 2 | Adopt the New Philosophy | Management must learn the new economic age and accept responsibility | | 3 | Cease Dependence on Inspection | Build quality *into* the product from the start | | 4 | End Lowest-Tender Purchasing | Minimize total cost; single supplier with long-term relationship | | 5 | Improve Constantly Every Process | Identify problems, fix root causes, reduce waste & cost | | 6 | Institute Training on the Job | Workers must be trained in statistical methods and modern techniques | | 7 | Institute Leadership | Supervise to help people do a better job; not just numbers | | 8 | Drive Out Fear | Fear prevents workers from asking questions or reporting problems | | 9 | Break Down Departmental Barriers | Cross-functional teamwork replaces silos | | 10 | Eliminate Slogans & Exhortations | Most problems are systemic; slogans don't fix systems | | 11 | Eliminate Numerical Quotas | Replace quotas with leadership & process improvement | | 12 | Remove Barriers to Pride of Workmanship | Eliminate the annual rating that destroys pride | | 13 | Institute Vigorous Education | Self-improvement for everyone | | 14 | Put Everybody to Work | Transformation is everyone's job, top to bottom | #### Pillar II: The Deming Chain Reaction This is the **economic argument** for quality. It demonstrates that quality improvements lead to lower costs, which lead to higher productivity and market share. The chain is unidirectional and self-reinforcing. $$ \text{Quality Improvement} \rightarrow \text{Lower Costs} \rightarrow \text{Higher Productivity} \rightarrow \text{Better Market Position} \rightarrow \text{Stay in Business} \rightarrow \text{Provide Jobs} $$ #### Pillar III: The PDCA Cycle (Deming / Shewhart Cycle) Originally conceptualized by Walter Shewhart and popularized by Deming. It is the **iterative engine** of continuous improvement. | Phase | Full Name | Activity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **P** | Plan | Identify the problem, analyze process, develop hypothesis, design intervention | | **D** | Do | Implement the change on a small/pilot scale; collect data | | **C** | Check (Study) | Evaluate results against expected outcomes; identify learnings | | **A** | Act | Standardize the change if successful, or revise and re-cycle if not | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction (Exam Favorite):** The Japanese call it **Deming Cycle**; the West often calls it **PDCA**. Shewhart conceived it, Deming popularized it. Both names are correct. #### Pillar IV: System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) Deming's magnum opus, introduced in his later years. It is a **map of the four lenses** through which management must view their organization. | Component | Discipline | Focus | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Appreciation for a System | Understanding the network of interdependent components | | 2 | Knowledge of Variation | Statistical thinking; common vs. special cause variation | | 3 | Theory of Knowledge | The epistemology of learning; prediction & comparison | | 4 | Psychology | Understanding human motivation, behavior, and interaction | > [!NOTE] > **The "SoPK" Equation** (conceptual): Effective leadership emerges only when all four lenses are applied simultaneously. Mastery of one without the others produces sub-optimization. ### 2.2 Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases of Management Deming identified **seven organizational ailments** that destroy quality culture: 1. **Lack of constancy of purpose** ŌĆö short-term thinking 2. **Emphasis on short-term profits and dividends** 3. **Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review** ŌĆö destroys teamwork 4. **Mobility of management** ŌĆö job-hopping executives never see long-term effects 5. **Running a company on visible figures alone** ŌĆö ignoring unknown/unknowns 6. **Excessive medical costs** ŌĆö burden of poor workplace health 7. **Excessive costs of warranty, liability, and litigation** ### 2.3 Obstacles (Barriers) to Transformation - **Hope for instant salvation** ŌĆö "Just give me the answer." - **Search for examples** ŌĆö copying Toyota/Honda without understanding the system - **Problem is in the workforce** ŌĆö blaming workers (the 85/15 misconception) - **Quality problems come from procurement** - **Quality problems come from poor design** (engineering) - **"Our quality problems are different"** ŌĆö denial syndrome - **Bad quality comes from poor testing** ŌĆö testing only finds defects, doesn't prevent them ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important Note on Tables:** In all KTU tables, vertical bars (e.g., for absolute value) are replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax integrity. | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Real-World Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cost of Quality (CoQ) ŌĆö PAF Model** | $C_{Q} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | $C_{P}$ = Prevention Cost (Ōé╣); $C_{A}$ = Appraisal Cost (Ōé╣); $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost (Ōé╣); $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost (Ōé╣) | Total economic burden of quality (or lack thereof) | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $C_{OPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Internal + External Failure Costs (Ōé╣) | Direct measure of waste; KPI for improvement | | **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** | $C_{C} = C_{P} + C_{A}$ | Prevention + Appraisal (Ōé╣) | Proactive investment in quality | | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** | $C_{NC} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Same as COPQ (Ōé╣) | Reactive loss; should be minimized | | **Optimal Quality Cost** | $\dfrac{dC_{Q}}{dC_{P}} = 0$ | Calculus optimization | Where marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost saved | | **Deming's 85/15 Rule** | $P_{system} : P_{worker} = 85 : 15$ | Probability ratio (%) | Allocates root-cause responsibility to management | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{good}$ = defect-free units; $N_{total}$ = total units | Process capability indicator | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_{i}$ | Product of n stage yields | Multi-stage process quality | | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = defects; $U$ = units; $O$ = opportunities/unit | Six-Sigma metric | | **Process Capability Index ($C_{p}$)** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL$ = Upper Spec Limit; $LSL$ = Lower Spec Limit; $\sigma$ = standard deviation | Statistical process control | | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\bar{X}$ = process mean | Centering indicator | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** Deming's SPC and PDCA reduce rework by **30ŌĆō60%** in assembly lines. - **Software Engineering:** PDCA underpins **Agile** and **DevOps** (Plan ŌåÆ Code ŌåÆ Test ŌåÆ Deploy ŌåÆ Iterate). - **Healthcare:** SoPK guides clinical process improvement, reducing hospital-acquired infections. - **Service Industry:** 14 Points structure service excellence audits (e.g., banking, aviation). - **Construction:** PAF model tracks quality costs across project phases. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: The Deming Chain Reaction ŌĆö Mathematical Justification The Deming Chain Reaction is not merely qualitative; it has a mathematical interpretation in terms of quality costs and market dynamics. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define Initial Cost Structure** Let the per-unit production cost under poor quality be: $$ C_{poor} = C_{M} + C_{IF} + C_{EF} $$ where $C_{M}$ is the material cost, $C_{IF}$ is the internal failure cost, and $C_{EF}$ is the external failure cost. **Step 2 ŌĆö Effect of Quality Improvement** When processes are improved (reduced variation, fewer defects), failure costs decrease: $$ C_{improved} = C_{M} + C_{P} + C_{A} + \alpha \cdot (C_{IF} + C_{EF}) $$ where $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the defect reduction factor and $C_{P}$, $C_{A}$ are prevention and appraisal costs. **Step 3 ŌĆö Net Cost Reduction** $$ \Delta C = C_{poor} - C_{improved} = (1 - \alpha)(C_{IF} + C_{EF}) - (C_{P} + C_{A}) $$ For the chain reaction to trigger (cost reduction), we require: $$ \Delta C > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 - \alpha)(C_{IF} + C_{EF}) > C_{P} + C_{A} $$ **Interpretation:** The savings from reduced failures must exceed the investment in prevention and appraisal. **Step 4 ŌĆö Productivity Gain** Lower defects mean less rework, faster throughput, and better cycle time. If the original cycle time per good unit is $T_{0}$ and the rework factor is $\beta$ (fraction requiring rework): $$ T_{good} = T_{0} (1 + \beta) \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{good}^{'} = T_{0} (1 + \alpha \beta) $$ Productivity ratio: $$ P_{gain} = \frac{T_{good}}{T_{good}^{'}} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \alpha \beta} > 1 $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Market Capture & Long-Term Survival** With better quality and lower price, market share grows. If market share shifts from $M_{0}$ to $M_{1} = M_{0}(1 + \gamma)$ where $\gamma$ is the growth factor: $$ M_{1} > M_{0} \quad \text{and} \quad \pi_{long-term} > \pi_{short-term} $$ **Conclusion:** The chain reaction holds mathematically: quality ŌåÆ cost down ŌåÆ productivity up ŌåÆ market up ŌåÆ survival. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Quality Cost (Economic Model) The total cost of quality $C_{Q}$ is a function of prevention investment $C_{P}$: $$ C_{Q}(C_{P}) = C_{P} + C_{A}(C_{P}) + C_{IF}(C_{P}) + C_{EF}(C_{P}) $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Behavior of Sub-Costs** As prevention investment increases: - $C_{P}$ rises **linearly** (or convexly): $C_{P} = k_{1} \cdot x$ - $C_{A}$ rises slightly: $C_{A} = k_{2} \cdot x^{0.5}$ - $C_{IF}$ decreases: $C_{IF} = k_{3} \cdot e^{-x}$ - $C_{EF}$ decreases sharply: $C_{EF} = k_{4} \cdot e^{-2x}$ where $x = C_{P}$ and $k_{i}$ are empirical constants. **Step 2 ŌĆö Total Cost Function** $$ C_{Q}(x) = k_{1}x + k_{2}\sqrt{x} + k_{3}e^{-x} + k_{4}e^{-2x} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö First-Order Optimality Condition** For minimum total cost, take the derivative and set it to zero: $$ \frac{dC_{Q}}{dx} = k_{1} + \frac{k_{2}}{2\sqrt{x}} - k_{3}e^{-x} - 2k_{4}e^{-2x} = 0 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve Iteratively (Newton's Method)** Initial guess $x_{0}$ refined by: $$ x_{n+1} = x_{n} - \frac{C_{Q}^{'}(x_{n})}{C_{Q}^{''}(x_{n})} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Verification (Second-Order Condition)** $$ \frac{d^{2}C_{Q}}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{k_{2}}{4x^{3/2}} + k_{3}e^{-x} + 4k_{4}e^{-2x} $$ If $C_{Q}^{''}(x^{*}) > 0$ at the critical point, then $x^{*}$ is a **minimum** (optimal prevention investment). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Insight:** In most industrial case studies, the **optimal $C_{P}$ is 15ŌĆō25% of total quality cost**, after which diminishing returns set in. This is why the PAF (PreventionŌĆōAppraisalŌĆōFailure) model recommends investing in prevention early, not just at the end. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Deming's 14 Points vs. Juran & Crosby (Comparative) | Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Origin** | Statistician (USA) | Engineer (USA) | Line worker turned executive (USA) | | **Core Idea** | System transformation | Quality planning, control, improvement trilogy | Zero defects, conformance to requirements | | **Cost Focus** | Quality = Lower Cost | Cost of poor quality | Price of non-conformance | | **Approach** | Top-down philosophy | Cross-functional (planning + control) | Behavioral/attitudinal change | | **Methodology** | PDCA, SPC, SoPK | Quality Trilogy (QP, QC, QI) | 14 Steps to Quality Improvement | | **Best For** | Large-scale transformation | Structured programs | Cultural attitude change | | **Criticism** | Statistical methods intimidate workers | Heavy planning overhead | Zero defects seen as unattainable | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: PAF Cost Calculation **Problem:** A firm reports the following annual quality costs (in Ōé╣ Lakhs): - Prevention: **Ōé╣ 5** - Appraisal: **Ōé╣ 8** - Internal Failure: **Ōé╣ 12** - External Failure: **Ōé╣ 20** **Compute (a) Total Cost of Quality, (b) Cost of Poor Quality, (c) Cost of Conformance, (d) Ratio of Prevention to Failure Cost.** **Step 1 ŌĆö Total Cost of Quality** $$ C_{Q} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 5 + 8 + 12 + 20 = \text{Ōé╣ 45 Lakhs} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** $$ C_{OPQ} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 12 + 20 = \text{Ōé╣ 32 Lakhs} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Cost of Conformance (CoC)** $$ C_{C} = C_{P} + C_{A} = 5 + 8 = \text{Ōé╣ 13 Lakhs} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Ratio of Prevention to Failure** $$ R = \frac{C_{P}}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} = \frac{5}{32} \approx 0.156 \;\;(\text{or } 15.6\%) $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Benchmark:** A healthy TQM organization should have $R \geq 0.30$ (i.e., prevention investment is at least 30% of failure costs). A value of 0.156 indicates the firm is **under-investing in prevention** and bearing excessive reactive costs ŌĆö a classic case for invoking Deming's philosophy. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 The Deming Chain Reaction ŌĆö Causal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[QUALITY
IMPROVEMENT] --> B[COSTS
DECREASE] B --> C[PRODUCTIVITY
INCREASES] C --> D[PRICE STAYS
COMPETITIVE] D --> E[CAPTURES
MARKET SHARE] E --> F[STAYS IN
BUSINESS] F --> G[PROVIDES
JOBS] G -.-> A style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 The PDCA Cycle ŌĆö Iterative Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PLAN
Identify problem
Analyze process
Set objectives] D[DO
Implement on

Evaluate results

KNOWLEDGE - SoPK] SOC --> S1[APPRECIATION
FOR A SYSTEM] SOC --> S2[KNOWLEDGE
OF VARIATION] SOC --> S3[THEORY OF

not sub-parts] S1 --> S1B[Understand
interdependencies] S2 --> S2A[Common cause vs
special cause variation] S2 --> S2B[Statistical thinking
SPC and control charts] S3 --> S3A[Epistemology of
learning] S3 --> S3B[Prediction and
comparison] S4 --> S4A[Human motivation
and behavior] S4 --> S4B[Intrinsic vs
extrinsic drivers] style SOC fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style S3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style S4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style S1A fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style S1B fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style S2A fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 style S2B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 style S3A fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style S3B fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style S4A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style S4B fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Deming's 14 Points ŌĆö Structural Map ```mermaid flowchart TB D14P[DEMING 14 POINTS
FOR MANAGEMENT] D14P --> C1[CONSTANCY
OF PURPOSE

LEADERSHIP
