## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model 2024]**
**Define "Quality" and "Zero Defects" as per Philip B. Crosby.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
According to Philip B. Crosby:

- **Quality** is defined as *"conformance to requirements"*. A product or service is of high quality when it exactly meets the specifications laid down for it ŌĆö not when it is luxurious or elegant.
- **Zero Defects (ZD)** is a *performance standard* that demands every employee commit to doing the job right the first time. It is **not a motivational slogan** but a serious, measurable engineering and managerial standard implying that defects are not acceptable under any circumstance.

**(1 + 1 = 2 marks for definitions, 1 mark for distinction / explanation = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model 2024]**
**What is the "Price of Nonconformance" (PONA)? List its four components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Price of Nonconformance (PONA)** is the monetary measure of all costs incurred because things were *not done right the first time*. Crosby insisted that quality must be expressed in the language of money so that management understands its importance.

The **four components** of PONA are:

1. **Prevention Cost** ŌĆö expenditure on training, design reviews, and process planning to prevent defects.
2. **Appraisal Cost** ŌĆö expenditure on inspection, testing, and audits to detect defects.
3. **Internal Failure Cost** ŌĆö expenditure on scrap, rework, and re-inspection *before* the product reaches the customer.
4. **External Failure Cost** ŌĆö expenditure on warranty claims, returns, and lost goodwill *after* delivery to the customer.

**(1 mark definition + 2 marks for four components = 3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A ŌĆö (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model 2024]**
**(a)** Explain Crosby's *Four Absolutes of Quality Management* in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **seven** steps of Crosby's Fourteen Steps for Quality Improvement, with emphasis on their managerial intent. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Q.A (a) ŌĆö Crosby's Four Absolutes (7 Marks)**

Crosby argued that quality management is built upon **four absolutes** ŌĆö inviolable principles that every manager must accept.

**Absolute 1: Quality means conformance to requirements, not elegance.**

Quality is defined strictly as the degree to which a product or service conforms to its stated requirements (specifications, drawings, customer agreement). It is **measurable** and **binary**: a part either meets the spec or it does not. Crosby rejected vague definitions like "goodness" or "excellence."

*Engineering example:* A bolt manufactured to M12 ├Ś 1.75 specification with 10.9 grade is of high quality if every dimension matches the drawing within tolerance ŌĆö not because it looks shiny.

**Absolute 2: Prevention, not inspection.**

It is far cheaper and more effective to design processes that *prevent* defects than to inspect them out afterwards. Inspection only identifies defects already made; it does not eliminate them. Crosby's **1-10-100 rule** quantifies this: prevention costs 1 unit, appraisal 10 units, and failure 100 units.

**Absolute 3: Zero Defects is the only performance standard.**

The only acceptable quality target is **zero defects**. Crosby rejected the philosophy of *"acceptable quality level (AQL)"* ŌĆö the idea that a certain percentage of defects is tolerable. Each defect represents wasted resources and unhappy customers.

**Absolute 4: Measure quality by the Price of Nonconformance (PONA).**

Quality's economic value must be measured in money. When managers see that defects cost the company crores in rework, warranty, and lost customers, they invest in prevention. PONA is the financial mirror of quality performance.

> **Mark Distribution (for Examiner):**
> - Each Absolute: ~1.5 marks (definition + explanation + example).
> - Total: 4 ├Ś 1.5 = 6 marks + 1 mark for introduction/conclusion.

---

##### **Solution to Q.A (b) ŌĆö Seven Steps of Crosby's 14 Steps (7 Marks)**

*(Examiner note: KTU allows any 7; the following are the most commonly tested.)*

**Step 1 ŌĆö Management Commitment:**
Senior management must publicly declare its commitment to quality improvement. Without this top-level commitment, all subsequent steps collapse. The CEO must visibly lead the program.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quality Measurement:**
Quality must be measured objectively using defect metrics ŌĆö defects per million opportunities (DPMO), scrap rate, rework hours, customer complaints. *"You cannot manage what you cannot measure."*

**Step 4 ŌĆö Cost of Quality (PONA) Evaluation:**
Translate quality into financial terms by computing the Price of Nonconformance. This converts quality from an abstract ideal into a budget-level concern that managers cannot ignore.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Corrective Action System:**
Establish a formal closed-loop system ŌĆö typically a **Corrective Action Request (CAR)** ŌĆö where any worker can raise a defect, and management must respond within a defined cycle, identify root cause, and verify closure.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Supervisor Training:**
All supervisors undergo formal training in ZD philosophy and quality tools. Supervisors are the bridge between management intent and worker execution; they must be *converted first* before workers.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Zero Defects Day (ZD Day):**
A single, designated day when the entire organization ceremonially launches the Zero Defects program. Each employee takes a personal pledge: *"I will do my job right the first time."* This is symbolic but psychologically powerful.

**Step 14 ŌĆö Do It Over Again:**
Quality improvement is *cyclical*. After completing all 14 steps, the organization restarts at Step 3 (re-measurement) to set higher goals. Crosby rejected the idea of a "finished" quality program.

> **Mark Distribution (for Examiner):**
> - 7 steps ├Ś 1 mark each for clear explanation = 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION B ŌĆö (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **Price of Nonconformance (PONA)** concept with its four cost components and show how Crosby uses it as the financial lever for quality improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Crosby's quality philosophy with that of **Deming** and **Juran**, highlighting key differences in approach. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Q.B (a) ŌĆö PONA (7 Marks)**

The **Price of Nonconformance (PONA)** ŌĆö also called the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** ŌĆö is the total monetary cost a company bears *because things were not done right the first time*. Crosby insisted that managers think about quality in the language of money, because only then does quality earn a seat at the boardroom table.

The mathematical expression is:

$$
\text{PONA} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

**1. Prevention Cost:**
Money spent *before* defects occur ŌĆö training, design reviews, process capability studies, supplier evaluation. These are *voluntary* investments.

**2. Appraisal Cost:**
Money spent on inspection, testing, calibration of measuring instruments, quality audits. These are *detection* activities.

**3. Internal Failure Cost:**
Costs of defects caught *within* the organization ŌĆö scrap, rework, re-inspection, machine downtime, production delays.

**4. External Failure Cost:**
Costs of defects that reach the *customer* ŌĆö warranty claims, returns, repairs, lawsuits, and ŌĆö most damaging ŌĆö **loss of goodwill and brand reputation**.

Crosby's pivotal insight: **External Failure Costs typically dominate total PONA**, and they are *the most preventable* via investment in Prevention Cost. The relationship can be summarized:

$$
\uparrow \text{Prevention Cost} \;\Rightarrow\; \downarrow\downarrow \text{External Failure Cost} \;\Rightarrow\; \downarrow \text{Total PONA}
$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Equation (1) + four components (4) + Crosby's lever argument (2) = 7 marks.

---

##### **Solution to Q.B (b) ŌĆö Crosby vs. Deming vs. Juran (7 Marks)**

| Dimension | Crosby | Deming | Juran |
|-----------|--------|--------|-------|
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to requirements | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability | Fitness for use |
| **Primary Focus** | Prevention, Zero Defects | Statistical process control, systems thinking | Quality planning, control, improvement (Juran Trilogy) |
| **Approach to People** | Motivation, ZD pledges, recognition | Worker pride, removing fear | Project teams, quality planning |
| **View of Inspection** | Wasteful ŌĆö prevent instead | Necessary but secondary | Necessary as part of control |
| **Quality Standard** | Zero Defects (binary) | Reduce variation continuously | No defects at the source, but accept AQL in practice |
| **Cost Emphasis** | PONA ŌĆö strong financial focus | Cost of poor quality + system cost | Cost of poor quality |
| **Implementation Tool** | 14 Steps roadmap | 14 Points + PDSA cycle | Juran Trilogy + Quality by Design |
| **Cultural Posture** | Top-down, managerial | Top-down + statistical tools | Cross-functional, planning-heavy |

**Key Differences:**

- **Crosby** is **binary and motivational** ŌĆö quality is either there or not; ZD is non-negotiable.
- **Deming** is **statistical and systemic** ŌĆö focus is on reducing *variation* in processes; he blames the system, not the worker.
- **Juran** is **planning-oriented** ŌĆö quality must be *designed in*, planned, controlled, and improved through structured projects.

**Similarities:** All three reject inspection-only approaches; all three emphasize top-management responsibility; all three view quality as a *system-wide* cultural change rather than a department's job.

> **Mark Distribution:** Comparison table (4) + 2 key differences (2) + 1 mark synthesis = 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Defining Quality as "meeting customer expectations"** ŌĆö that is **Juran's** definition. Crosby defines it as **conformance to requirements**. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Writing "Zero Defects is a slogan"** ŌĆö Crosby repeatedly insisted ZD is a *performance standard*, not a slogan. Lose 1 mark for stating otherwise.
> 3. **Skipping the timeline (12ŌĆō18 months)** ŌĆö KTU examiners award a bonus mark for mentioning the implementation duration.
> 4. **Confusing QIT (Step 2) with Ad Hoc ZD Committee (Step 7)** ŌĆö QIT is *permanent and ongoing*; Ad Hoc Committee is *temporary and event-specific*.
> 5. **Calling Step 14 a "termination"** ŌĆö Step 14 is a *loop back*, not an end. Always mention *"Do It Over Again"* explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (Crosby's #1 absolute ŌĆö never forget this).
- **Zero Defects is a performance standard, NOT a motivational slogan.**
- **Prevention is cheaper than inspection** (1-10-100 rule).
- **PONA has 4 components:** Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- **The 14 Steps form a 12ŌĆō18 month roadmap**, organized into 5 phases.
- **Step 1 = Management Commitment** (foundation) ŌåÆ **Step 14 = Do It Over Again** (continuity).
- **QIT (Step 2) ŌēĀ Ad Hoc Committee (Step 7)** ŌĆö QIT is permanent; Ad Hoc is temporary.
- **Step 9 (ZD Day)** is a *single ceremonial event*, not a recurring one.
- **Step 11 (Error Cause Removal)** ŌĆö Crosby agreed with Deming: **95% of defects are system-caused, 5% worker-caused.**
- **Step 14 is a LOOP BACK to Step 3**, not an endpoint ŌĆö quality is a journey.
- **Crosby vs. Juran's definition trap:** Conformance vs. Fitness for Use.
- **Crosby vs. Deming's blame philosophy:** Crosby focused on individual commitment; Deming focused on system variation.
- **The Cost of Quality (PONA) equation** is a favorite 3-mark question ŌĆö memorize the 4 components.
- **Crosby's framework is action-oriented and prescriptive**, whereas Deming's 14 Points are *philosophical and systemic*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategic Quality Management: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strategic Quality Management: Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is a strategic financial measurement framework that quantifies the total expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure product/service conformance to specified quality standards, plus the financial losses arising from non-conformance. It is the monetary aggregate of all resources devoted to quality-related activities across the value chain, captured through a structured cost classification system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize This):**
> "Cost of Quality is the sum of all costs incurred in preventing poor quality, appraising product quality to ensure conformance, and the costs of failure (both internal and external) resulting from products/services not meeting quality requirements." ŌĆö Aligned with the **Joseph Juran P-A-F (Prevention-Appraisal-Failure) Model**, the official KTU-recommended framework.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **renovating your kitchen**. Before starting, you hire an architect to draw accurate blueprints (Prevention). During construction, an inspector checks the plumbing and wiring at every stage (Appraisal). If a plumber makes a bad pipe joint, you discover it during inspection and fix it before the tiles are laid (Internal Failure). But if the bad joint is discovered only after the kitchen is fully tiled and water damage ruins the flooring (External Failure), the cost of fixing it skyrockets.

**The lesson:** Every rupee spent *upfront* on prevention saves *several rupees* in failure costs later. This is the foundational economic logic of COQ.

### 1.3 The Four Standard Cost Categories (KTU Mandatory Classification)

| Category | Symbol | Timing of Cost Occurrence | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs** | $C_P$ | Before defect occurs | Quality planning, training, supplier capability audits |
| **Appraisal Costs** | $C_A$ | During/after production | Inspection, testing, calibration |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | $C_{IF}$ | Before reaching customer | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| **External Failure Costs** | $C_{EF}$ | After reaching customer | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lost goodwill |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Prevention and Appraisal costs are **"Costs of Conformance"** (good quality investment). Internal and External Failure costs are **"Costs of Non-Conformance"** (bad quality penalty).

### 1.4 P-A-F Cost Relationship

$$\text{Total Cost of Quality (COQ)} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

$$\text{Conformance Cost} = C_P + C_A$$

$$\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COQ}_{total} = C_{conformance} + C_{non\text{-}conformance}}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 P-A-F Model ŌĆö Detailed Component Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs ($C_P$) ŌĆö "Stop the Defect Before It Starts"

- **Quality planning & administration**: Cost of preparing quality manuals, procedures, and quality system documentation (e.g., ISO 9001 documentation).
- **Training & education**: Cost of quality-related training programs for employees at all levels.
- **Product/process design**: Design reviews, FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis), prototyping, Design of Experiments (DoE).
- **Supplier capability assessment**: Auditing and rating suppliers to ensure incoming material quality.
- **Quality improvement projects**: Cost of Kaizen, Six Sigma, Lean initiatives.

> [!TIP]
> **Strategic Insight:** Prevention is the only category where increased spending *reduces* total COQ. Every $1 spent on prevention saves typically $3ŌĆō$10 in failure costs.

#### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs ($C_A$) ŌĆö "Detect the Defect Before the Customer Does"

- **Incoming inspection**: Testing raw materials and components on receipt.
- **In-process inspection**: Monitoring production line at various stages.
- **Final product testing**: Functional tests, performance verification.
- **Calibration of test equipment**: Maintaining accuracy of measurement instruments.
- **Quality audits**: Internal audits of the QMS (Quality Management System).

#### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$) ŌĆö "Caught Inside the Factory"

- **Scrap**: Defective units that cannot be economically repaired.
- **Rework**: Cost of correcting defects to make the product salable.
- **Re-inspection**: Cost of re-testing reworked products.
- **Downtime**: Production stoppages caused by quality problems.
- **Failure analysis**: Investigation costs to identify root causes of defects.

#### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$) ŌĆö "Caught by the Customer"

- **Warranty claims**: Free repairs/replacements under warranty.
- **Returned products**: Handling and processing customer returns.
- **Recalls**: Massive costs to retrieve defective products from the market.
- **Field service**: On-site repair costs.
- **Lost goodwill**: Damaged brand reputation, lost future sales (often a *hidden* cost).
- **Legal liability**: Lawsuits, settlements, regulatory fines.

### 2.2 The Quality Cost Curve (Juran's Optimization Model)

According to **Joseph Juran**, as prevention costs rise, failure costs fall more steeply. The optimal COQ occurs at the intersection of the rising conformance curve and the falling non-conformance curve.

### 2.3 The Hidden Costs of Quality

Beyond the visible P-A-F costs, organizations incur **hidden quality costs** that do not appear in accounting books but are real economic losses:

- Customer dissatisfaction leading to lost sales.
- Excess inventory built up to buffer against quality problems.
- Lost productivity due to rework interruptions.
- Excess supplier management time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **1-10-100 Rule** (also called the Rule of Ten) states: If a defect costs **$1** to fix at the design stage, it costs **$10** to fix at the manufacturing stage and **$100** (or more) to fix after delivery to the customer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Application in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality | Direct calculation in numerical problems |
| $\text{COQ}\% = \dfrac{\text{COQ}}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100$ | COQ as % of sales | Benchmarking across industries (typical: 5ŌĆō25%) |
| $\text{Cost per Unit} = \dfrac{\text{COQ}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | Per-unit quality burden | Comparing two product lines |
| $\text{Savings Ratio} = \dfrac{C_{EF,\text{before}} - C_{EF,\text{after}}}{C_{P,\text{increase}}}$ | ROI of prevention | Justifying quality investments |
| $\text{Defect Rate} (\text{DPMO}) = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma application |
| $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units Out}}{\text{Total Units In}} \times 100\%$ | First-pass yield | Manufacturing effectiveness |
| $\text{ROL} = \text{ROL}_{\text{compliance}} - \text{ROL}_{\text{noncompliance}}$ | Return on Quality investment | Strategic decision-making |

**Where:** $D$ = Number of defects, $U$ = Number of units, ROL = Return on Quality.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering industries (automotive, aerospace, electronics), COQ analysis drives:
- **Supplier Quality Engineering**: Justifying supplier audits vs. incoming inspection costs.
- **Design Reviews**: Quantifying savings from FMEA-driven design changes.
- **Six Sigma Projects**: Cost-benefit validation of $DMAIC$ improvement projects.
- **ISO 9001 Implementation**: Building the financial case for the Quality Management System.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal COQ Point (Juran's Economic Balance Model)

Let us derive the mathematical condition for minimizing total COQ.

**Step 1: Define the cost functions.**
Let $x$ represent the level of investment in prevention/appraisal activities (a continuous variable, e.g., percentage of revenue spent on $C_P + C_A$).

**Step 2: Model the failure cost as a decreasing function of prevention investment.**

$$C_F(x) = \dfrac{K}{x^n}$$

where $K$ is a positive constant representing the baseline failure cost when $x \to 0$, and $n > 0$ is the elasticity of failure-cost reduction with respect to prevention investment.

**Step 3: Model the prevention/appraisal cost as a linearly increasing function.**

$$C_{PA}(x) = m \cdot x$$

where $m$ is the marginal cost per unit of prevention effort.

**Step 4: Total COQ as a function of $x$.**

$$\text{COQ}(x) = C_{PA}(x) + C_F(x) = m \cdot x + \dfrac{K}{x^n}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate with respect to $x$ to find the minimum.**

$$\dfrac{d(\text{COQ})}{dx} = m - \dfrac{n \cdot K}{x^{n+1}}$$

**Step 6: Set the derivative equal to zero for the optimum.**

$$m - \dfrac{n \cdot K}{x^{n+1}} = 0$$

**Step 7: Solve for the optimal $x^*$.**

$$m = \dfrac{n \cdot K}{x^{n+1}}$$

$$x^{n+1} = \dfrac{n \cdot K}{m}$$

$$\boxed{x^* = \left(\dfrac{n \cdot K}{m}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n+1}}}$$

**Step 8: Verify it is a minimum using the second derivative test.**

$$\dfrac{d^2(\text{COQ})}{dx^2} = \dfrac{n(n+1) \cdot K}{x^{n+2}} > 0 \quad \text{(since all parameters are positive)}$$

Hence $x^*$ is confirmed as a **minimum**.

**Step 9: Substitute back to obtain minimum COQ.**

$$\text{COQ}_{\min} = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{nK}{m}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n+1}} + K \cdot \left(\dfrac{m}{nK}\right)^{\tfrac{n}{n+1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** The optimal prevention investment $x^*$ depends on (a) the baseline failure cost $K$, (b) the elasticity $n$ (how effectively prevention reduces failures), and (c) the marginal prevention cost $m$. Higher $n$ (more effective prevention systems) shifts the optimum to the *right* ŌĆö meaning greater prevention investment is justified.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A KTU-evaluated manufacturing firm records the following annual quality costs (in Ōé╣ Lakhs):

- Prevention: Ōé╣ 8
- Appraisal: Ōé╣ 6
- Internal Failure: Ōé╣ 15
- External Failure: Ōé╣ 21
- Total Revenue: Ōé╣ 500

**Calculate:** (a) Total COQ, (b) COQ as % of sales, (c) Cost of Conformance, (d) Cost of Non-Conformance.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total COQ:**

$$\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 8 + 6 + 15 + 21$$

$$\boxed{\text{COQ} = \text{Ōé╣ 50 Lakhs}}$$

**(b) COQ as % of sales:**

$$\text{COQ}\% = \dfrac{50}{500} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{COQ}\% = 10\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> Industry benchmarks: World-class manufacturers operate at **< 5%**. If COQ > 15%, the organization has serious quality issues requiring strategic intervention.

**(c) Cost of Conformance:**

$$C_{conformance} = C_P + C_A = 8 + 6$$

$$\boxed{C_{conformance} = \text{Ōé╣ 14 Lakhs}}$$

**(d) Cost of Non-Conformance:**

$$C_{non\text{-}conformance} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 15 + 21$$

$$\boxed{C_{non\text{-}conformance} = \text{Ōé╣ 36 Lakhs}}$$

**Strategic Recommendation:** Failure costs (Ōé╣ 36 L) dominate prevention costs (Ōé╣ 8 L). The firm should **increase prevention investment** by 50ŌĆō100% to drive down the failure cost curve.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: COQ Decision Framework

| Observation | Diagnosis | Strategic Action |
|---|---|---|
| $C_P$ very low, $C_{EF}$ very high | Neglect of prevention | Heavy investment in training, supplier audits, design reviews |
| $C_A$ very high, $C_{IF}$ low | Over-inspection culture | Shift from detection to prevention |
| $C_{IF}$ rising while $C_A$ stable | Process capability deteriorating | Statistical Process Control (SPC) deployment |
| $C_{EF}$ > $C_{IF}$ | Poor outgoing quality control | Strengthen final inspection and customer feedback loops |
| All P-A-F categories rising | Quality cost reporting broken | Re-baseline the COQ measurement system |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the P-A-F Cost System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Organizational Operations] --> inputB[Quality Cost Identification System]
    inputB --> catA[Category CP - Prevention Costs]
    inputB --> catB[Category CA - Appraisal Costs]
    inputB --> catC[Category CIF - Internal Failure Costs]
    inputB --> catD[Category CEF - External Failure Costs]

    catA --> sub1[Quality Planning]
    catA --> sub2[Training and Education]
    catA --> sub3[Design Reviews]
    catA --> sub4[Supplier Audits]

    catB --> sub5[Incoming Inspection]
    catB --> sub6[In-process Inspection]
    catB --> sub7[Final Testing]
    catB --> sub8[Equipment Calibration]

    catC --> sub9[Scrap]
    catC --> sub10[Rework]
    catC --> sub11[Re-inspection]
    catC --> sub12[Production Downtime]

    catD --> sub13[Warranty Claims]
    catD --> sub14[Product Returns]
    catD --> sub15[Field Service Costs]
    catD --> sub16[Lost Goodwill]

    sub1 --> aggregator[COQ Aggregation Engine]
    sub2 --> aggregator
    sub3 --> aggregator
    sub4 --> aggregator
    sub5 --> aggregator
    sub6 --> aggregator
    sub7 --> aggregator
    sub8 --> aggregator
    sub9 --> aggregator
    sub10 --> aggregator
    sub11 --> aggregator
    sub12 --> aggregator
    sub13 --> aggregator
    sub14 --> aggregator
    sub15 --> aggregator
    sub16 --> aggregator

    aggregator --> output1[Total COQ Value]
    aggregator --> output2[COQ Percentage of Revenue]
    aggregator --> output3[Cost per Unit Metric]
    aggregator --> output4[Strategic Dashboard for Management]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Strategic Decision Pathway for COQ Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Quarterly COQ Data Collection]) --> measure[Measure All Four Categories]
    measure --> compare[Compare with Industry Benchmark]
    compare --> decision{High Failure Cost}
    decision -->|Yes| action1[Increase Prevention Investment]
    decision -->|No| action2[Maintain Current Strategy]
    action1 --> monitor[Re-measure COQ Next Quarter]
    action2 --> monitor
    monitor --> analyze[Trend Analysis and Reporting]
    analyze --> review[Management Review Meeting]
    review --> start
```

### 4.3 Schematic: The 1-10-100 Rule (Cost Multiplication Across Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Stage 1: Design Stage] -->|Cost Multiplier = 1| stage2[Stage 2: Manufacturing Stage]
    stage2 -->|Cost Multiplier = 10| stage3[Stage 3: Customer / Field Stage]
    stage3 -->|Cost Multiplier = 100| stage4[Stage 4: Legal and Brand Damage]

    stage1 --> label1[Fix a defect for Rs 1]
    stage2 --> label2[Fix the same defect for Rs 10]
    stage3 --> label3[Fix the same defect for Rs 100]
    stage4 --> label4[Brand and legal damage may exceed Rs 1000]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]** Define the term **Cost of Quality (COQ)**. List its four main categories with one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial measure of all resources expended to achieve and assure conformance to quality, plus the costs arising from non-conformance.

The four categories are:
1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Example ŌĆö Employee quality training programs.
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Example ŌĆö Incoming raw material inspection.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Example ŌĆö Rework of defective products.
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Example ŌĆö Warranty claims from customers.

$$\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]** Explain the **"1-10-100 Rule"** in Cost of Quality with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

The 1-10-100 Rule (also called the Rule of Ten) states that the cost of fixing a defect multiplies by approximately **10 times** as it progresses through each stage of the product life cycle:

- **Design Stage:** Cost = **1 unit** (e.g., Ōé╣ 100) ŌĆö change in design drawing.
- **Manufacturing Stage:** Cost = **10 units** (e.g., Ōé╣ 1,000) ŌĆö rework, scrap, machine reset.
- **Customer / Field Stage:** Cost = **100 units** (e.g., Ōé╣ 10,000) ŌĆö warranty repair, return shipping.

**Example:** A tolerance error in a brake disc design costs Ōé╣ 200 to correct in CAD during design, Ōé╣ 2,000 to rework on the shop floor, and Ōé╣ 20,000 to recall and replace in customer vehicles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Do NOT confuse the 1-10-100 Rule with the P-A-F model. The Rule is about **cost magnification across stages**, while P-A-F is about **cost classification categories**. Many students mix these up and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Prevention-Appraisal-Failure (P-A-F) Model** of Cost of Quality. List at least **four components** under each category with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has the following annual quality cost data:
- Prevention Cost = Ōé╣ 4 Lakhs
- Appraisal Cost = Ōé╣ 6 Lakhs
- Internal Failure Cost = Ōé╣ 12 Lakhs
- External Failure Cost = Ōé╣ 18 Lakhs
- Total Sales = Ōé╣ 400 Lakhs

Calculate: (i) Total COQ, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, (iv) COQ as a percentage of sales. What strategic recommendation would you give to management? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) P-A-F Model Explanation (7 Marks):**

The P-A-F Model, developed by **Joseph Juran**, classifies all quality-related expenditures into four mutually exclusive categories. This classification helps managers identify where money is being spent and where waste is concentrated.

**Category 1 ŌĆö Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place. These are the most strategic investments.
- Quality planning (Ōé╣): preparing procedures, quality manuals, control plans.
- Training (Ōé╣): employee skill development in quality tools.
- Product design reviews: FMEA, Design for Six Sigma, prototyping.
- Supplier capability assessment: auditing, rating, certification of suppliers.

**Category 2 ŌĆö Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Costs incurred to *detect* defects during production before the product reaches the customer.
- Incoming material inspection.
- In-process and final inspection.
- Test equipment calibration.
- Quality audits.

**Category 3 ŌĆö Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs arising from defects detected *before* the product is shipped.
- Scrap (defective units discarded).
- Rework (correction of defects).
- Re-inspection (testing of reworked items).
- Production downtime due to quality issues.

**Category 4 ŌĆö External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs arising from defects discovered *after* the product reaches the customer.
- Warranty claims and settlements.
- Returned goods handling.
- Field service and recall costs.
- Lost customer goodwill and brand damage.

**Strategic Significance:** Prevention and Appraisal costs are *"Costs of Conformance"* (investments in good quality). Internal and External Failure costs are *"Costs of Non-Conformance"* (penalties for bad quality). World-class organizations maximize the conformance side to minimize the failure side.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Total COQ:**

$$\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 4 + 6 + 12 + 18 = \boxed{\text{Ōé╣ 40 Lakhs}}$$

**(ii) Cost of Conformance:**

$$C_{conf} = C_P + C_A = 4 + 6 = \boxed{\text{Ōé╣ 10 Lakhs}}$$

**(iii) Cost of Non-Conformance:**

$$C_{non\text{-}conf} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 12 + 18 = \boxed{\text{Ōé╣ 30 Lakhs}}$$

**(iv) COQ as Percentage of Sales:**

$$\text{COQ}\% = \dfrac{40}{400} \times 100 = \boxed{10\%}$$

**Strategic Recommendation:**
The Cost of Non-Conformance (Ōé╣ 30 Lakhs) is **three times** the Cost of Conformance (Ōé╣ 10 Lakhs). The External Failure Cost (Ōé╣ 18 L) is the largest single category, indicating significant post-delivery quality problems. Recommendation:
1. Increase Prevention investment from Ōé╣ 4 L to Ōé╣ 8 L (training, supplier audits, FMEA).
2. Improve Final Inspection processes to catch defects before shipment.
3. Establish a Customer Feedback Loop to identify field failures and feed them back into design.
4. Target a 25ŌĆō30% reduction in COQ within 12 months.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Hidden Costs of Quality**. Why do these costs often escape traditional accounting systems, and how can they be estimated? Provide examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Six Sigma project at a KTU-evaluated firm reveals the following:
- 1,000 units produced per day.
- 250 defect opportunities per unit.
- 8,750 total defects observed per day.

Calculate the **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** and the corresponding **Sigma Level**. Comment on the process capability. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Hidden Costs of Quality (7 Marks):**

Hidden Costs of Quality are quality-related costs that do **not appear explicitly** in the organization's financial statements but represent real economic losses.

**Examples of Hidden Costs:**
- **Lost customer goodwill** leading to reduced future sales.
- **Excess inventory** maintained as a buffer against quality issues.
- **Lost productivity** when operators stop production lines to deal with defects.
- **Excess supplier management time** spent on resolving quality disputes.
- **Engineering redesign time** not captured in standard cost accounting.

**Why They Escape Traditional Accounting:**
- They are spread across multiple departments and not tagged to a single cost center.
- They are absorbed into overhead and not separated out.
- They occur as opportunity costs (lost sales) rather than actual cash outflows.

**How to Estimate Them:**
- Use activity-based costing (ABC) to trace hidden costs.
- Conduct employee surveys and time-and-motion studies.
- Estimate lost sales through customer churn analysis.
- Use industry benchmarks to extrapolate hidden cost magnitude (typically 3ŌĆō4├Ś visible COQ).

---

**(b) DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Units produced per day ($U$) = 1,000
- Defect opportunities per unit ($O$) = 250
- Total defects observed per day ($D$) = 8,750

**Step 1: Calculate Total Opportunities.**

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = U \times O = 1{,}000 \times 250 = 250{,}000$$

**Step 2: Calculate DPMO.**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{\text{Total Opportunities}}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{8{,}750 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 35{,}000}$$

**Step 3: Determine the Sigma Level.**

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **DPMO = 35,000 corresponds to approximately 4.0 Sigma.**

**Step 4: Comment on Process Capability.**

- A process at **4.0 Sigma** has a defect rate of ~6.2%, which is **NOT world-class** (world-class is 6 Sigma = 3.4 DPMO).
- The process requires significant improvement through DMAIC methodology.
- Recommendation: Apply Root Cause Analysis (Ishikawa, Pareto), Statistical Process Control (SPC), and Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) to elevate the process to at least 5.0 Sigma.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Critical Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Many students write $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U} \times 10^6$ instead of dividing by *opportunities*. This is a **2-mark loss**.
> 2. **Sigma Lookup Without Reference:** Always state the conversion table source or memorize: 6Žā = 3.4 DPMO, 5Žā = 233 DPMO, 4Žā = 6,210 DPMO (classic) or use Motorola's 1.5Žā shift table.
> 3. **No Strategic Conclusion:** Numerical answers without managerial interpretation lose 1ŌĆō2 marks in part (b).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **COQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$** ŌĆö Always state the formula before plugging in values.
- **Conformance Costs** ($C_P + C_A$) are investments; **Non-Conformance Costs** ($C_{IF} + C_{EF}$) are penalties.
- **World-class benchmark:** COQ < 5% of revenue; > 15% signals a quality crisis.
- **P-A-F Model** is attributed to **Joseph Juran** (not Deming, not Crosby ŌĆö common confusion in exams).
- **Prevention costs are the only category** where increased spending *reduces* total COQ.
- **The 1-10-100 Rule:** Costs multiply 10├Ś at each successive stage from design ŌåÆ manufacturing ŌåÆ customer.
- **Hidden costs** typically equal 3ŌĆō4├Ś the visible COQ and are missed by traditional accounting.
- **DPMO Formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ where $O$ is opportunities per unit.
- **Sigma Levels:** 6Žā Ōēł 3.4 DPMO; 5Žā Ōēł 233 DPMO; 4Žā Ōēł 6,210 DPMO (classic) or 35,000 (with 1.5Žā shift).
- **Strategic Priority:** Always recommend *increasing prevention investment* if failure costs dominate.
- **Juran's Optimization:** Optimal COQ occurs at the intersection of the rising (PA) curve and falling (F) curve.
- **Crosby vs. Juran:** Crosby emphasizes "quality is free" (prevention-based); Juran emphasizes optimization (the fitness-for-use approach).
- **ISO 9001 Linkage:** COQ is the financial backbone of any QMS implementation justification.
- **Six Sigma Tools:** DPMO, DMAIC, and Pareto analysis are the most frequently tested tools in KTU exams.
- **Always** end a numerical problem with a **strategic recommendation** ŌĆö this earns the final 1ŌĆō2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Customer Satisfaction ŌĆō Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Satisfaction (CS)** is defined as a *post-purchase evaluative judgment* concerning a specific product or service, reflecting the degree to which the perceived performance (or outcome) of that product or service matches the customer's pre-purchase expectations.

Mathematically expressed by the **ExpectationŌĆōDisconfirmation Paradigm (EDP)**:

$$\text{Satisfaction} = f(\text{Performance}, \text{Expectations})$$

When **Performance > Expectations** ŌåÆ *Positive Disconfirmation* ŌåÆ Customer is **Delighted/Satisfied**.
When **Performance = Expectations** ŌåÆ *Simple Confirmation* ŌåÆ Customer is **Neutral/Satisfied**.
When **Performance < Expectations** ŌåÆ *Negative Disconfirmation* ŌåÆ Customer is **Dissatisfied**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Customer Satisfaction is one of the three primary pillars of the **European Foundation for Quality Management (EFQM) Model** (alongside People and Society). It directly drives Customer Loyalty, Retention, and ultimately long-term profitability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The most widely cited industry metric is the **American Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI)**, scored on a normalized **0ŌĆō100 scale** (anchored at 1ŌĆō10 Likert inputs), published quarterly by the University of Michigan in partnership with the American Society for Quality (ASQ).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō The Restaurant Experience

Imagine walking into a small restaurant. Before entering, you *expect* good food, clean tables, and polite service (your **Expectations**). 

- If the food is **exceptional**, beyond what you imagined ŌåÆ You are *delighted* and will tell 5 friends (Positive Disconfirmation).
- If the food is **average**, just as you expected ŌåÆ You are *satisfied* but indifferent (Confirmation).
- If the food is **cold and bland**, worse than expected ŌåÆ You will never return and may post a 1-star review (Negative Disconfirmation).

This mental transaction is *literally* what a customer undergoes for every purchase ŌĆö from buying a mobile phone to using a bank loan. **Customer Satisfaction is the difference between what you promised and what you delivered.**

## 1.3 Strategic Importance in Engineering & Service Industries

In manufacturing (Toyota, Boeing) and service industries (airlines, IT, healthcare), satisfaction is treated as a **leading indicator of financial performance**. A **5% increase in customer retention** statistically correlates with a **25%ŌĆō95% increase in net present value (NPV)** of a customer over their lifetime ŌĆö a figure popularized by **Frederick Reichheld (Bain & Company)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Gurus on Satisfaction:**
> - *Philip B. Crosby*: "Quality is free, but no one knows the price of non-conformance ŌĆö that price is the lost customer."
> - *W. Edwards Deming*: "A customer is the most important visitor on our premises; he is not dependent on us ŌĆö we are dependent on him."

## 1.4 Distinction: Customer Satisfaction vs. Customer Loyalty

| Construct | Definition | Measurement | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Satisfaction** | Emotional response after a single transaction | Transactional surveys (CSAT) | Short-term, episodic |
| **Loyalty** | Repeated purchase intent over time | Behavioral tracking, NPS | Long-term, cumulative |

**Key Insight for KTU:** Loyalty is a *consequence* of sustained satisfaction. However, high satisfaction does *not automatically* guarantee loyalty (a phenomenon called the **"Satisfaction Trap"**, where customers are satisfied but switch due to price or convenience).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ExpectationŌĆōDisconfirmation Paradigm (EDP) ŌĆō Operational Breakdown

The dominant theoretical model for understanding Customer Satisfaction, originally proposed by **Richard L. Oliver (1980)**. The operational sequence is:

1. **Pre-purchase Expectation Formation** ŌĆō The customer develops anticipations based on:
   - Past experience
   - Word-of-mouth (WOM)
   - Marketing communications
   - Brand reputation
2. **Consumption / Usage** ŌĆō The product or service is experienced.
3. **Performance Perception** ŌĆō A subjective judgment of how well the experience delivered.
4. **Disconfirmation Computation (Mental)** ŌĆō The brain subconsciously compares *Perceived Performance* vs *Expectation*.
5. **Satisfaction Judgment** ŌĆō A positive, neutral, or negative affective state is produced.
6. **Post-purchase Behavior** ŌĆō Repurchase intent, WOM, complaints, or switching.

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Models of Customer Satisfaction

### 2.2.1 The SERVQUAL Model (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988)

Measures service quality across **five dimensions** (the "RATER" framework). The gap between expectations and perceptions defines quality:

$$\text{Service Quality (SERVQUAL Score)} = \sum_{i=1}^{5}(P_i - E_i)$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Perception score for dimension $i$
- $E_i$ = Expectation score for dimension $i$
- The 5 dimensions are: **R**eliability, **A**ssurance, **T**angibles, **E**mpathy, **R**esponsiveness.

### 2.2.2 The Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984)

Categorizes product/service attributes into three classes that drive satisfaction differently:

| Attribute Type | Customer Reaction if Present | Customer Reaction if Absent | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | No increase in satisfaction | Severe dissatisfaction | Non-negotiable baseline |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Satisfaction rises linearly with quality | Dissatisfaction | Should be improved competitively |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Strong satisfaction boost | No dissatisfaction | Innovation differentiator |

### 2.2.3 The American Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI) Model

A causal model with three antecedents feeding into Satisfaction:

$$\text{ACSI} = f(\text{Perceived Quality}, \text{Perceived Value}, \text{Customer Expectations})$$

The standardized ACSI equation (Forne et al.):

$$\text{ACSI}_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \bar{x}_{ij} - \text{Min}}{\text{Range}} \times 100$$

Where $\bar{x}_{ij}$ is the mean score for question $i$ in company $j$, $w_i$ is its weight, and Min/Range standardize to 0ŌĆō100.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations** ŌĆö they appear in KTU ESE problems (Part A and Part B) and numerical case studies.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disconfirmation | $D = P - E$ | $P$ = Perceived Performance, $E$ = Expectation | Likert points |
| 2 | SERVQUAL Gap | $SQ_k = \sum_{i=1}^{5}(P_i - E_i)$ | $P_i$, $E_i$ = perception, expectation for dimension $i$ | Gap score (can be negative) |
| 5 | Gap 5 (Service Quality) | $\text{Gap 5} = \text{Gap 1} + \text{Gap 2} + \text{Gap 3} + \text{Gap 4}$ | Five provider-customer gaps | Composite index |
| 6 | ACSI Score | $\text{ACSI} = \frac{\bar{x}_{\text{sat}} - 1}{9} \times 100$ | $\bar{x}_{\text{sat}}$ = mean satisfaction on 1ŌĆō10 scale | 0 ŌĆō 100 |
| 7 | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | From 0ŌĆō10 likelihood-to-recommend question | $-100$ to $+100$ |
| 8 | Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) | $\text{CSAT} = \frac{N_{\text{satisfied}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Number of positive responses | 0 ŌĆō 100 (%) |
| 9 | Customer Effort Score (CES) | $\text{CES} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Effort}_i}{n}$ | Mean effort rating (1=very low, 7=very high) | 1 ŌĆō 7 (lower is better) |
| 10 | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r}{(1+d)^t}$ | $R_t$ = revenue, $C_t$ = cost, $r$ = retention, $d$ = discount | Currency (Ōé╣ / $) |
| 11 | Customer Retention Rate | $\text{CRR} = \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100$ | $C_E$ = customers at end, $C_N$ = new, $C_S$ = start | 0 ŌĆō 100 (%) |
| 12 | Defection (Churn) Rate | $\text{Churn} = 1 - \text{CRR}$ | Complement of retention | 0 ŌĆō 100 (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** In numerical questions, always show the substitution step *before* the final numerical result. The examiner awards 1 mark for the *correct formula*, 1 mark for *substitution*, and 1 mark for the *final answer* in a 3-mark Part A.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Six Sigma (DMAIC)**: The *Define* phase requires a Voice of Customer (VOC) capture, and the *Measure* phase tracks CSAT, NPS, and CES as primary Y-variables.
- **Reliability Engineering**: Customer complaints (negative satisfaction) directly feed Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) as inputs for severity scoring.
- **Lean Manufacturing**: Voice of Customer informs the *value* definition in value-stream mapping ŌĆö a non-valued feature is, by definition, dissatisfaction-creating.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Computing SERVQUAL Gap Scores

**Problem Statement:** A B.Tech final-year project team surveyed 100 students of a college canteen on 5 SERVQUAL dimensions. The aggregated scores (out of 7) are:

| Dimension | Expectation ($E$) | Perception ($P$) |
|---|---|---|
| Tangibles | 6.5 | 4.0 |
| Reliability | 6.0 | 3.5 |
| Responsiveness | 6.2 | 4.5 |
| Assurance | 5.8 | 4.8 |
| Empathy | 5.5 | 3.0 |

**Required:** Compute the overall SERVQUAL score and identify the worst-performing dimension.

### Step 1: Define the SERVQUAL gap formula

$$SQ_i = P_i - E_i$$

### Step 2: Substitute each dimension

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_{\text{Tangibles}} &= 4.0 - 6.5 = -2.5 \\
SQ_{\text{Reliability}} &= 3.5 - 6.0 = -2.5 \\
SQ_{\text{Responsiveness}} &= 4.5 - 6.2 = -1.7 \\
SQ_{\text{Assurance}} &= 4.8 - 5.8 = -1.0 \\
SQ_{\text{Empathy}} &= 3.0 - 5.5 = -2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Sum to get overall SERVQUAL score

$$\text{Overall SERVQUAL} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} SQ_i$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overall SERVQUAL} &= (-2.5) + (-2.5) + (-1.7) + (-1.0) + (-2.5) \\
&= -10.2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

A **negative** SERVQUAL score indicates that perceived service is *worse* than expected. The canteen is failing in **Tangibles, Reliability, and Empathy** (tied at $-2.5$). The *lowest priority for quick fix* is Empathy (since empathy gaps are hardest to bridge), while **Tangibles** (cleanliness, d├®cor) is the easiest quick win.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference for KTU Answer:** When presenting the interpretation, always identify (a) magnitude, (b) sign, and (c) which dimension requires the most strategic attention.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Net Promoter Score (NPS) Computation

**Problem Statement:** An e-commerce company collected 1,000 post-purchase responses to the question *"On a scale of 0ŌĆō10, how likely are you to recommend us to a friend?"* The distribution is:

- **Promoters (9ŌĆō10):** 480
- **Passives (7ŌĆō8):** 320
- **Detractors (0ŌĆō6):** 200

**Required:** Compute the NPS and benchmark it.

### Step 1: Convert raw counts to percentages

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Promoters} &= \frac{480}{1000} \times 100 = 48\% \\
\%\text{Passives} &= \frac{320}{1000} \times 100 = 32\% \\
\%\text{Detractors} &= \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the NPS formula

$$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= 48\% - 20\% \\
&= 28
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Benchmark against industry standards

- **NPS < 0** ŌåÆ Critical; most customers are detractors.
- **0 Ōēż NPS Ōēż 30** ŌåÆ Good (the company is in this range).
- **30 < NPS Ōēż 70** ŌåÆ Excellent (world-class firms like Apple, Tesla operate here).
- **NPS > 70** ŌåÆ Exceptional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NPS for this firm (28) is *good* but not *world-class*. The firm should investigate why 20% of customers are detractors ŌĆö typically root-caused by delivery delays, defective products, or poor grievance redressal.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Customer Retention and CLV

**Problem Statement:** A telecom company has 10,000 customers at the start of Q1. During the quarter, 1,200 new customers joined, and 800 left. The average monthly revenue per customer is Ōé╣500, the cost-to-serve is Ōé╣200, the discount rate is 10% annually, and the average customer lifetime is 3 years.

**Required:** Compute the (a) Customer Retention Rate (CRR), (b) Defection (Churn) Rate, and (c) Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) using the simplified formula.

### Step 1: Customer Retention Rate

$$\text{CRR} = \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100$$

Where:
- $C_E$ = Customers at the end of period
- $C_N$ = New customers
- $C_S$ = Customers at the start

**Determine $C_E$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= C_S + C_N - \text{Churned} \\
C_E &= 10{,}000 + 1{,}200 - 800 = 10{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute into CRR formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= \frac{10{,}400 - 1{,}200}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{9{,}200}{10{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Defection (Churn) Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Churn Rate} &= 1 - \text{CRR} \\
&= 1 - 0.92 \\
&= 0.08 = 8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Customer Lifetime Value (Simplified Formula)

$$\text{CLV} = (\text{Revenue per month} - \text{Cost per month}) \times \text{Retention Ratio} \times \frac{1}{d}$$

Where $d$ is the monthly discount rate ($d = 10\% / 12 = 0.833\%$ per month).

**Compute monthly margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= 500 - 200 = 300 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute retention ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute into CLV formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} &= 300 \times 0.92 \times \frac{1}{0.00833} \\
&= 300 \times 0.92 \times 120 \\
&= 33{,}120 \text{ per customer}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1% improvement in retention (from 92% to 93%) increases CLV by approximately Ōé╣3,300 per customer. Multiply by 10,400 customers ŌåÆ **~Ōé╣3.43 Cr additional lifetime value per percentage point of retention** ŌĆö this is the *financial justification* for every customer satisfaction initiative.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Each Model

| Situation / Industry | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Service sector (banking, hospitality) | SERVQUAL | Captures expectationŌĆōperception gap systematically |
| Product design and innovation | Kano Model | Differentiates basic vs. excitement features |
| Cross-industry benchmarking | ACSI | Standardized, comparable across firms |
| Quick loyalty snapshot | NPS | Single-question, real-time tracking |
| Operational complaint analysis | FMEA + Voice of Customer | Root-cause mapping of dissatisfaction |
| Long-term financial planning | CLV + Retention | Connects satisfaction to profit |

```mermaid

    D -->|Performance Equals Expectation| F[Confirmation]
    D -->|Performance Less Than Expectation| G[Negative Disconfirmation]
    E --> H[Customer Satisfaction High]
    F --> I[Customer Satisfaction Moderate]
    G --> J[Customer Dissatisfaction]
    H --> K[Repurchase and Loyalty]
    I --> L[Indifference or Switching]
    J --> M[Complaints, Returns, Negative Word of Mouth]
    K --> N[Long Term Profitability and Brand Equity]
    L --> N
    M --> O[Service Recovery Required]
    O --> P[Feedback to Quality System]
    P --> A
    N --> A
```
