> **Interpretation**: The above diagrams render as visual flowcharts in any Mermaid-compatible Markdown viewer (GitHub, Obsidian, VS Code). They are **substitutes for hand-drawn fishbones** in digital KTU assignment submissions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]`
**Define Cause and Effect Analysis. Who developed it and when?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Cause and Effect Analysis, also known as the Fishbone Diagram or Ishikawa Diagram, is a structured quality management tool used to systematically identify and visualize the potential causes of a specific problem (effect).
>
> It was developed by **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo in **1968**, originally proposed at a quality control symposium in Tokyo. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for name, 1 mark for inventor and year)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**List any six main categories of causes used in the 6M model of Ishikawa Diagram.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The 6M model categorizes causes as:
>
> 1. **Man** ŌĆö Workforce, training, skill
> 2. **Machine** ŌĆö Equipment, tools, maintenance
> 3. **Material** ŌĆö Raw materials, inputs
> 4. **Method** ŌĆö Process, procedures, workflow
> 5. **Measurement** ŌĆö Inspection, gauges, calibration
> 6. **Mother Nature** ŌĆö Environment, temperature, humidity
>
> (┬Į mark each ŌĆö full marks for all six correctly stated)

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Essay Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024]`

> **Q. (a)** Draw a Cause and Effect (Fishbone) Diagram for the following quality problem in a CNC machining workshop:
> *"Out of 500 aluminium brackets produced daily, 60 are rejected for dimensional out-of-tolerance in the bore diameter (spec: 20 ┬▒ 0.05 mm)."*
> Use the 6M framework and identify at least **three sub-causes under each main category**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the **5 Whys technique** with reference to one root cause identified in part (a). How does the 5 Whys technique complement the Fishbone Diagram? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the Effect Clearly (1 mark)**

$$
E = \text{"60 of 500 aluminium brackets (12\%) rejected for bore diameter out-of-tolerance beyond 20 ┬▒ 0.05 mm"}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the Fishbone Diagram (5 marks)**

The diagram should have a horizontal spine ending at the effect box, with six branches for the 6M categories. The expected sub-causes are:

| Main Category | Sub-Cause 1 | Sub-Cause 2 | Sub-Cause 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Man** | Operator not certified on CNC 850 | Inadequate training on tool offset | Fatigue due to 12-hr shift |
| **Machine** | Spindle bearing worn (radial play) | Coolant flow inconsistent | CNC controller software outdated |
| **Material** | Aluminium bar hardness varies (6061 vs 6063) | Clamping distortion on thin bosses | Bar stock stored near coolant (corrosion) |
| **Method** | Cutting feed rate 0.15 mm/rev vs spec 0.10 | Depth of cut exceeds 1.5 mm recommendation | No tool wear compensation in G-code |
| **Measurement** | Bore gauge not calibrated for last 6 months | Operator reads gauge at different temperature | CMM probe damage undetected |
| **Mother Nature** | Workshop temperature 38 ┬░C (thermal expansion) | Humidity affects aluminium surface | Lighting inadequate for visual inspection |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Highlight Likely Causes (1 mark)**

The examiner expects you to **box, circle, or asterisk** the 2ŌĆō3 most probable causes, e.g. worn spindle bearing, incorrect feed rate, and uncalibrated bore gauge.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**5 Whys on "Worn Spindle Bearing":** (4 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Why is bore out-of-tolerance?} &\to \text{Spindle runout exceeds 0.03 mm} \\
\text{Why is runout high?} &\to \text{Spindle bearing worn out} \\
\text{Why is bearing worn?} &\to \text{No preventive replacement at 5000-hour mark} \\
\text{Why no preventive replacement?} &\to \text{PM schedule lists spindle as 8000-hour} \\
\text{Why is interval wrong?} &\to \text{OEM manual not updated for current duty cycle} \\
\textbf{ROOT CAUSE} &\to \text{Engineering documentation error ŌĆö needs revision}
\end{aligned}
$$

**How 5 Whys Complements Fishbone (3 marks):**

- The Fishbone gives **breadth** ŌĆö it lists all possible causes from a wide angle.
- The 5 Whys gives **depth** ŌĆö it drills down into one specific cause to find the true root.
- Together, they form the **DefineŌĆōAnalyze backbone of DMAIC**.
- Fishbone identifies *what* the causes might be; 5 Whys identifies *why* those causes exist.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]`

> **Q. (a)** Explain the concept of Cause and Effect Analysis. Discuss the **6M framework** with industry-relevant examples for each category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Compare and contrast the Cause and Effect Diagram with **Pareto Analysis**. Describe a real-time situation where both tools are applied **sequentially** to solve a quality problem. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** As defined in Section 1.1 ŌĆö Cause and Effect Analysis is a structured visualization technique developed by Kaoru Ishikawa in 1968 to identify the potential causes of a problem (effect).

**6M Framework Explanation (5 marks ŌĆö 1 mark per category with example):**

1. **Man**: Operator fatigue, lack of training, low morale ŌĆö *e.g., untrained welder producing porous welds*.
2. **Machine**: Worn bearings, poor calibration, outdated software ŌĆö *e.g., CNC machine with backlash causing dimensional errors*.
3. **Material**: Variable raw material quality, contamination ŌĆö *e.g., cement lumps in concrete mix*.
4. **Method**: Outdated SOPs, missing process steps ŌĆö *e.g., missing pre-heating in metal forming*.
5. **Measurement**: Uncalibrated gauges, wrong inspection technique ŌĆö *e.g., using steel scale instead of vernier for 0.01 mm tolerance*.
6. **Mother Nature**: Humidity, temperature, dust ŌĆö *e.g., high humidity causing rust on stored steel sheets*.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q. (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Cause & Effect Diagram | Pareto Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| **Output** | List of potential causes | Ranked bar chart |
| **Purpose** | Identify causes | Prioritize causes |
| **Data Needed** | None (brainstorming) | Frequency/cost data |
| **Visual Form** | Fishbone | Bar chart + cumulative % line |
| **When Used** | Early in investigation | After data collection |

**Real-Time Sequential Application (3 marks):**

> In a packaging industry facing 8% leakage in bottle caps:
>
> - **Step 1 (Fishbone):** Team identifies 6 main categories and 22 sub-causes, including torque setting, cap material, capping machine speed, operator training, cap storage temperature, and torque tester calibration.
> - **Step 2 (5 Whys):** Drills into "torque setting inconsistent" ŌåÆ finds torque controller faulty.
> - **Step 3 (Pareto):** Frequency data collected on 22 causes ŌĆö finds **3 causes account for 78% of leaks**: faulty torque controller, worn cap feeder, and uncalibrated torque tester.
> - **Step 4 (CAPA):** Replace torque controller, schedule weekly calibration, retrain operator.
> - **Result:** Leakage drops from 8% to 0.9% within one month.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cause and Effect Questions:**
>
> 1. **Vague Effect Statement (ŌĆō2 marks):** Writing *"high rejection"* instead of *"15% rejection in part X for reason Y."* Always quantify.
> 2. **Wrong Framework (ŌĆō3 marks):** Using **4P (marketing)** in a **manufacturing defect** question. Always default to **6M** unless the question is explicitly about a service.
> 3. **Missing Arrows/Direction (ŌĆō1 mark):** The spine must always point **left-to-right**, with branches angling **toward** the spine (like fish ribs).
> 4. **No Sub-Causes (ŌĆō2 marks):** Simply listing the 6M categories without sub-causes gets only 2 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Skipping 5 Whys (ŌĆō3 marks):** Forgetting to drill down makes the answer incomplete.
> 6. **No Prioritization (ŌĆō1 mark):** Examiner expects you to mark *most likely* causes ŌĆö use circles or asterisks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Full name:** Ishikawa Diagram / Fishbone Diagram ŌĆö invented by **Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968**.
> - **Part of:** The **7 QC Tools** (alongside Pareto, Histogram, Control Chart, Scatter Diagram, Flowchart, Checklist).
> - **Purpose:** Identify **potential** causes of a problem ŌĆö it is **not** a verification tool.
> - **6M Model (manufacturing default):** **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.**
> - **8P Model (service industry):** **Product, Price, Place, Promotion, People, Process, Physical Evidence, Performance.**
> - **Pair with:** 5 Whys (depth) + Pareto (priority) + FMEA (risk) + CAPA (action).
> - **Spine direction:** Always **left-to-right**; effect on the right.
> - **Construction steps:** Define effect ŌåÆ form team ŌåÆ choose categories ŌåÆ draw branches ŌåÆ brainstorm sub-causes ŌåÆ apply 5 Whys ŌåÆ highlight top causes.
> - **Best for:** Multi-causal problems where root cause is unknown.
> - **Limitation:** Does **not** show inter-cause relationships ŌĆö use **Ishikawa + Inter-relationship Diagram (Affinity/KJ)** for that.
> - **KTU Bloom's Level:** Mostly **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)**.
> - **KTU CO Mapping:** **CO4 ŌĆö Apply quality tools.**
> - **Expected marks distribution:** Definition 3 marks | Diagram 7 marks | Comparison/Application 4 marks (in a 14-mark question).
> - **Mnemonic for 6M:** **"My Mother Makes Mango Milkshake"** ŌåÆ Method, Mother Nature, Man, Machine, Material, Measurement.
> - **Mnemonic for 8P:** **"Please Pay Proper Price, Process People Politely"** ŌåÆ Product, Price, Place, Promotion, People, Process, Physical Evidence, Performance.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes ŌĆö Cause and Effect Analysis**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, OEIET612 ŌĆö Quality Management, CO4: Apply quality improvement tools and techniques.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Flow Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Process Flow Chart** (also called a **Process Flow Diagram** or **Flow Process Chart**) is a **pictorial/graphical representation** that systematically documents every sequential step, decision point, delay, transport, inspection, and storage operation involved in carrying out a process from its initial input to its final output. It is classified as one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7-QC Tools)** under the Japanese quality framework popularized by **Kaoru Ishikawa**.

According to the KTU 2024 OEIET612 syllabus, the Process Flow Chart is categorized as a **Cause-and-Effect / Process Analysis Tool** used during the *Plan* and *Do* phases of the PDCA (PlanŌĆōDoŌĆōCheckŌĆōAct) cycle to:
- Document the "as-is" state of any workflow.
- Identify Non-Value-Added (NVA) activities.
- Serve as the foundation for subsequent tools such as **Pareto Analysis**, **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagrams**, and **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A Process Flow Chart is a visual roadmap of a process that uses standardized symbols to depict the sequence of operations, the flow of materials or information, decision branches, and feedback loops, thereby enabling the analyst to comprehend, standardize, and continuously improve the process."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are explaining to a **first-time visitor** how a **South Indian filter coffee** is prepared in a traditional *chettanŌĆÖs* shop. You would naturally say:

1. Boil milk ŌåÆ 2. Add coffee decoction ŌåÆ 3. Add sugar ŌåÆ 4. Pour between tumblers (frothing) ŌåÆ 5. Serve in a davara.

If a *foreign tourist* stood beside you, the most effective way to communicate would be to **draw boxes connected by arrows** ŌĆö showing each step in sequence, with a diamond (decision) asking *"Is sugar required?"* Yes/No branches. That visual map is precisely a **Process Flow Chart**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A Process Flow Chart is essentially the **"Google Maps route" of any business process** ŌĆö it tells you where you start, which turns to take (decisions), where you might hit traffic (delays), and where you finally arrive (output). It replaces lengthy written Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) with a single, universally understandable diagram.

## 1.3 Historical Context & KTU Significance

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Originator** | **Frank Gilbreth** (1921) ŌĆö presented to the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) |
| **Standardization Body** | **ANSI/ISO** standards (ANSI Y15.5, ISO 5807) |
| **Classification** | One of the **7 QC Tools** of Quality Management |
| **KTU Module Placement** | Module 4 ŌĆö Tools of Quality (Process Analysis Tools) |
| **Primary Use** | Process documentation, standardization, bottleneck identification |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 scheme places this topic in **Module 4: Quality Control Tools & Techniques**. Expect a **direct 3-mark definition question** and likely a **7-mark construction/drawing question** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

A Process Flow Chart operates on the principle of **process decomposition** ŌĆö breaking down a complex macro-process into a sequence of micro-activities, each represented by a standardized geometric symbol. The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: Standardized Symbol Vocabulary (ANSI Y15.5 / ISO 5807)
Every activity in any process can be classified into **one of five universal symbols**. This standardization is what makes flow charts **language-independent** and **discipline-independent**.

### Pillar 2: Sequential Logic Flow
The chart enforces a **strict left-to-right / top-to-bottom flow** governed by arrows (flowlines). This linear (or branched) structure maps the **temporal/causal order** of operations.

### Pillar 3: Process Granularity (Top-Down Decomposition)
A process can be charted at multiple levels:
- **Level 1 ŌĆö Macro Flowchart**: Entire process on a single page (5ŌĆō10 steps).
- **Level 2 ŌĆö Detailed Flowchart**: Sub-steps expanded.
- **Level 3 ŌĆö Micro/Procedure Flowchart**: Lowest-level work instructions.

## 2.2 The Five Universal Symbols (KTU Must-Know)

| # | Symbol Shape | Activity Type | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oval / Terminator** (rounded rectangle) | **Start / End** | Marks the beginning or termination of the process | "Receive Order" / "Dispatch Goods" |
| 2 | **Rectangle** | **Operation / Process** | A task that transforms input into output | "Assemble engine block" |
| 3 | **Diamond** | **Decision** | A branching point with Yes/No or True/False outcome | "Is payment approved?" |
| 4 | **Parallelogram** | **Input / Output (Data)** | Data or material entering/leaving the process | "Customer fills form" |
| 5 | **Arrow** (flowline) | **Flow Direction** | Indicates the sequence of steps | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Drawing an **incorrect symbol** (e.g., using a rectangle for a decision) is one of the most common reasons students lose 1ŌĆō2 marks in drawing questions. Always draw the diamond with the *question* inside and label the *Yes/No* branches on the outgoing arrows.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Sheet ŌĆö Types of Process Flow Charts

| # | Type | Purpose | When Used (KTU Context) | Typical Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Flow Chart** | Shows how a product/service is made | Manufacturing, services | 10ŌĆō50 steps |
| 2 | **Work Flow Chart** | Documents the movement of people/tasks | Administrative processes | 5ŌĆō20 steps |
| 3 | **Swimlane (Deployment) Flowchart** | Clarifies **who** does **what** | Cross-functional processes (HR, Finance, Production) | 10ŌĆō30 steps |
| 4 | **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** | Tracks information movement | IT/software engineering | Variable |
| 5 | **Value Stream Map (VSM)** | Identifies value-added vs non-value-added | Lean Manufacturing | Full value stream |

## 2.4 Process Flow Chart vs. Other QC Tools ŌĆö KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Process Flow Chart | Pareto Chart | Fishbone Diagram | Check Sheet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Show process sequence | Prioritize problems | Identify root causes | Collect frequency data |
| **Output Type** | Pictorial flow | Bar graph | Cause-effect tree | Tally table |
| **Data Required** | None (qualitative) | Quantitative | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| **Phase in PDCA** | Plan | Check | Analyze | Do/Check |
| **Time to Construct** | 30ŌĆō60 min | 1ŌĆō2 hrs | 45ŌĆō90 min | 15ŌĆō30 min |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering disciplines, Process Flow Charts are deployed as:

- **Manufacturing Engineering**: Documenting CNC machining sequences, assembly line balancing.
- **Civil Engineering**: Concrete pouring sequences, soil testing workflows.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Engine assembly, heat-treatment processes.
- **Software Engineering**: Algorithm flowcharts (precursor to pseudocode).
- **Healthcare/Service**: Patient admission protocols, complaint resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** Process Flow Charts are a **prerequisite for ISO 9001:2015** Quality Management System (QMS) certification. Every documented procedure in an ISO-compliant firm **must** be supported by a maintained flow chart.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Construction Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 8-Step KTU-Standard Construction Procedure

Below is the **exhaustive, examiner-approved step-by-step methodology** to construct a Process Flow Chart. Each step must be demonstrated in a 7-mark construction answer.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Process Boundaries
Clearly establish the **starting point (Start symbol)** and the **ending point (End symbol)**. Without defined boundaries, the chart will either be too broad or too narrow.
- **Why?** Prevents scope creep and ensures all team members are aligned on what is being mapped.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Identify All Stakeholders / Departments Involved
List the actors (people, machines, departments) who interact with the process. This becomes critical for the **Swimlane variant**.
- **How?** Conduct a brief Gemba walk or interview process owners.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Brainstorm All Activities Sequentially
Enumerate every operation, decision, input/output, and delay from start to end. Use sticky notes on a whiteboard (Kaizen approach).
- **Output:** A raw, unordered list of 15ŌĆō50 micro-activities.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Sequence the Activities Chronologically
Arrange the activities in the exact order they occur in real time. Insert decision diamonds at branching points.
- **Logic:** If activity B *cannot* begin before activity A completes, then A precedes B.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Assign Correct Symbols
Map each activity to one of the five standardized symbols. Mis-symbology is the #1 cause of valuation deduction.
- **Rule of Thumb:** *Action ŌåÆ Rectangle, Question ŌåÆ Diamond, Start/Stop ŌåÆ Oval, Data ŌåÆ Parallelogram.*

### Step 6 ŌĆö Draw Flowlines (Arrows)
Connect symbols with arrows indicating direction of flow. Default direction is **top-to-bottom** and **left-to-right**.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Validate with the Process Owner
Walk through the chart with the actual operator/owner. Any missing step or incorrect sequence must be corrected.
- **Validation Rule:** Every path from Start must terminate at End (no infinite loops, no dead ends).

### Step 8 ŌĆö Finalize, Version, and Publish
Add a title block, legend (symbol key), revision date, and author. Store centrally for ISO compliance.

## 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö KTU-Style Problem Solving

### **Problem (KTU ESE Pattern):**
*"Construct a Process Flow Chart for the admission process of a B.Tech student in KTU, starting from the publication of the allotment list to final joining at the college."*

### **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Boundaries:**
- **Start:** Allotment list published by CEE Kerala.
- **End:** Student joins college and submits original certificates.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Stakeholders:**
- CEE Kerala, KTU University, College Admission Office, Student, Bank (for fee).

**Step 3ŌĆō5 ŌĆö Sequenced Activities with Symbols:**

| Step # | Activity | Symbol Type | Symbol Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Publish Allotment List | Start | Oval |
| 2 | Student logs into portal | Operation | Rectangle |
| 3 | Pay fee online | Input/Output | Parallelogram |
| 4 | Is payment successful? | Decision | Diamond |
| 5a | (Yes) Generate admission memo | Operation | Rectangle |
| 5b | (No) Return to Step 3 | Loop arrow | Arrow |
| 6 | Report to college in person | Operation | Rectangle |
| 7 | Submit documents & certificates | Input/Output | Parallelogram |
| 8 | Are documents complete? | Decision | Diamond |
| 9a | (Yes) Confirm admission | Operation | Rectangle |
| 9b | (No) Return to Step 7 | Loop arrow | Arrow |
| 10 | Issue ID & orientation schedule | Operation | Rectangle |
| 11 | Joining Complete | End | Oval |

**Step 6 ŌĆö Flowlines:** Each arrow connects the previous symbol to the next, with "Yes" / "No" labels on decision branches.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Validation:** Confirmed with the Admission Officer at a sample KTU-affiliated college.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Finalization:** Add legend, date, version.

## 3.3 Mathematical / Logical Validation (KTU Formula Approach)

While Process Flow Charts are predominantly qualitative, KTU examiners sometimes ask for **process efficiency metrics** derived from the chart. The two key metrics are:

$$
\text{Value-Added Ratio (VAR)} = \frac{\sum \text{Value-Added (VA) Time}}{\sum \text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Efficiency} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{NVA Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Bottleneck Utilization} = \frac{\text{Cycle Time of Bottleneck Step}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic:**
> Total Cycle Time = VA Time + Business-Value-Added (BVA) Time + Non-Value-Added (NVA) Time.
> The chart's purpose is to **isolate NVA time** (delays, redundant inspections, rework loops) so that process re-engineering can target their elimination.

### Numerical Example:
A 10-step manufacturing process has the following breakdown (in minutes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VA Time} &= 40 \text{ min} \\
\text{BVA Time} &= 15 \text{ min} \\
\text{NVA Time} &= 25 \text{ min} \\
\text{Total Cycle Time} &= 40 + 15 + 25 = 80 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \frac{40}{80} \times 100\% = 50\% \\
\text{Process Efficiency} &= \left(1 - \frac{25}{80}\right) \times 100\% = 68.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **50%** of total cycle time adds real value; the remaining **30.25 minutes** are candidates for elimination through Lean/Six Sigma intervention.

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Process Flow Chart. List any four standardized symbols used in its construction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Process Flow Chart** is a graphical representation that depicts the sequential steps, decision points, and flow of inputs/outputs within a process, using standardized symbols to enable clear documentation, analysis, and improvement of the process.

The four standardized symbols are:

1. **Oval (Rounded Rectangle)** ŌĆö represents the Start or End of the process.
2. **Rectangle** ŌĆö represents an Operation or Process step that transforms input into output.
3. **Diamond** ŌĆö represents a Decision point with Yes/No or True/False branches.
4. **Parallelogram** ŌĆö represents Input or Output of data/material.

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four correct symbols.)*

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Process Flow Chart and a Pareto Chart.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Process Flow Chart | Pareto Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Shows the sequence of steps in a process | Prioritizes problems by frequency/impact |
| **Visual Form** | Boxes connected by arrows | Bars arranged in descending order |
| **Data Requirement** | Qualitative (no numerical data needed) | Quantitative (numerical frequency data) |
| **PDCA Phase** | Plan / Do | Check / Analyze |

*(Award 3 marks: 1 mark per correct differentiation point ├Ś 3 differences.)*

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** question from the choice provided. Each Part B carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### **Question B.1 (Choice A) ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**Explain the concept of a Process Flow Chart. Discuss its advantages and limitations as a quality management tool.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (3 Marks):**
A Process Flow Chart is a pictorial representation of all the steps, operations, decisions, and flow of materials or information in a process. It employs standardized symbols (ovals, rectangles, diamonds, parallelograms, and arrows) as defined by ANSI Y15.5 and ISO 5807 standards. It is one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality** and is primarily used to **document the current ("as-is") state** of a process, enabling analysis, standardization, and continuous improvement.

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
1. **Visual Clarity:** Makes complex processes immediately understandable to all stakeholders.
2. **Identifies NVA Activities:** Highlights delays, redundancies, and bottlenecks for elimination.
3. **Foundation for Improvement:** Serves as the baseline for Kaizen, Lean, and Six Sigma projects.
4. **Training Aid:** Excellent onboarding tool for new employees.
5. **ISO Compliance:** Required documentation for ISO 9001:2015 QMS certification.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
1. **Static Snapshot:** Becomes outdated quickly if the process changes.
2. **Complexity:** Large processes may become unwieldy and require swimlane or multi-level breakdown.
3. **No Quantitative Insight:** Does not directly show statistical variation or root causes (requires complementary tools).
4. **Time Intensive:** Constructing detailed charts for complex processes can take hours.
