### **Question B.2 (Choice B) ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**Describe the various types of Process Flow Charts. When is each type most appropriately used?**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| # | Type | Description | Appropriate Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Flow Chart** | Linear sequence of steps | Documenting a single, well-defined manufacturing or service process |
| 2 | **Work Flow Chart** | Tracks movement of people/documents | Administrative processes like invoice approval, leave application |
| 3 | **Swimlane (Deployment) Flowchart** | Groups steps by department/role | Cross-functional processes spanning HR, Finance, Production |
| 4 | **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** | Tracks information between systems | IT/software system design and analysis |
| 5 | **Value Stream Map (VSM)** | Maps material & information flow with timeline | Lean Manufacturing projects to identify waste |
| 6 | **Top-Down Flowchart** | Hierarchical with progressive detail | Complex processes requiring multi-level decomposition |
| 7 | **BPMN (Business Process Model & Notation)** | Advanced standardized notation | Enterprise-level process automation and workflow engines |

*(Award 1 mark for each correctly described type, up to 7 marks.)*

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**
**A 12-step manufacturing process has the following time breakdown (in minutes): VA = 60, BVA = 25, NVA = 35. Calculate the Value-Added Ratio and Process Efficiency. Interpret the results and recommend improvement actions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Value-Added (VA) Time = **60 min**
- Business-Value-Added (BVA) Time = **25 min**
- Non-Value-Added (NVA) Time = **35 min**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate Total Cycle Time:**

$$
\text{Total Cycle Time} = \text{VA} + \text{BVA} + \text{NVA} = 60 + 25 + 35 = 120 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate Value-Added Ratio (VAR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \frac{\text{VA Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{60}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate Process Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Efficiency} &= \left(1 - \frac{\text{NVA Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{35}{120}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= (1 - 0.2917) \times 100\% \\
&= 70.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation (2 Marks):**
- Only **50%** of the total cycle time (60 out of 120 minutes) creates actual value for the customer.
- **35 minutes (29.17%)** is Non-Value-Added activity ŌĆö pure waste.
- The Process Efficiency of **70.83%** is acceptable but has significant room for improvement; world-class Lean organizations target **>85% efficiency**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recommended Actions (2 Marks):**
1. Conduct a **Kaizen workshop** to identify and eliminate NVA activities (delays, rework, redundant inspections).
2. Apply the **5S methodology** to organize the workplace and reduce motion waste.
3. Implement **Just-In-Time (JIT)** to minimize inventory-related NVA.
4. Re-engineer the process using **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** if 30%+ of activities are NVA.

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Process Flow Chart questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Symbol Usage (-2 marks):** Using a *rectangle* instead of a *diamond* for decisions. This is the single most common error.
> 2. **Missing Labels on Decision Branches (-1 mark):** Every outgoing arrow from a decision diamond **must** be labeled "Yes" or "No" / "True" or "False". Unlabeled branches are treated as incomplete.
> 3. **No Start/End Symbols (-1 mark):** Every flow chart must begin with an **Oval (Terminator)** and end with one as well.
> 4. **Floating Symbols (-1 mark):** Every symbol must be connected by an arrow. An unconnected box is an incomplete process step.
> 5. **Confusing Flow Chart with Block Diagram (-1 mark):** A block diagram shows *system architecture*; a flow chart shows *temporal sequence*. Do not interchange them.
> 6. **Skipping the Legend (-0.5 to -1 mark):** Always include a small symbol legend at the bottom-right corner of your chart.
> 7. **No Title Block (-0.5 marks):** Mention "Process Flow Chart for [Process Name]" at the top.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Definition:** A Process Flow Chart is a **pictorial representation of process steps** using standardized symbols.
- Ō£ģ **Originator:** **Frank Gilbreth, 1921** (American Society of Mechanical Engineers).
- Ō£ģ **Standard:** **ANSI Y15.5** and **ISO 5807** govern the symbols.
- Ō£ģ **5 Universal Symbols:** **Oval** (Start/End), **Rectangle** (Operation), **Diamond** (Decision), **Parallelogram** (Input/Output), **Arrow** (Flowline).
- Ō£ģ **Categorization:** One of the **7 Basic Tools of Quality** (alongside Pareto, Fishbone, Check Sheet, Histogram, Scatter Diagram, Control Chart).
- Ō£ģ **PDCA Position:** Primarily used in the **Plan and Do** phases.
- Ō£ģ **Construction Steps:** Define boundaries ŌåÆ Identify stakeholders ŌåÆ Brainstorm activities ŌåÆ Sequence ŌåÆ Assign symbols ŌåÆ Draw arrows ŌåÆ Validate ŌåÆ Finalize.
- Ō£ģ **Types:** Process, Workflow, Swimlane, DFD, VSM, Top-Down, BPMN.
- Ō£ģ **Key Metrics:** VAR (Value-Added Ratio) and Process Efficiency; aim for **VAR > 50%** and **Efficiency > 85%** (world-class).
- Ō£ģ **NVA Identification:** The primary purpose of a flow chart is to **spot and eliminate Non-Value-Added activities**.
- Ō£ģ **ISO Connection:** Mandatory documentation under **ISO 9001:2015 QMS** Clause 4.4 (Process Approach).
- Ō£ģ **Drawing Rule:** Always draw **decision diamonds with the question inside** and **label Yes/No branches on the outgoing arrows**.
- Ō£ģ **Validation Rule:** Every path from Start **must** terminate at End (no infinite loops, no dead ends).
- Ō£ģ **Examiner Tip:** A well-labeled, neat chart with a **legend and title block** typically scores **1ŌĆō2 marks higher** than a sloppy but technically correct one.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 7-mark drawing questions, spend **2 minutes planning** the sequence on rough paper, then **5 minutes drawing** neatly with a ruler. For 3-mark definition questions, memorize the **5 symbols** verbatim ŌĆö they are guaranteed to be asked in some form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Check Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Check Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check Sheet (Tally Sheet / Checklist):** A structured, pre-formatted data collection document used to record specific types of data in a consistent, real-time, and location-based manner. It is the **first of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7 QC Tools)** and is primarily used during the *Data Collection* phase of the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, DMAIC methodology, and Kaizen workshops.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a check sheet is defined as:
> *"A simple, paper-based or electronic form designed to facilitate the systematic recording of observations, defects, events, or measurements as they occur, enabling easy summarization and pattern recognition for subsequent statistical analysis."*

It is classified under the **Data Collection Tools** category of the 7 QC Tools, alongside Stratification (the other sometimes considered as data collection tool before analysis tools like Pareto, Histogram, Scatter, Control Chart, and Fishbone).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist":**

Imagine a pilot preparing to take off a commercial aircraft. The pilot does NOT rely on memory alone. Instead, the pilot uses a **printed checklist** that has:
- Each item to verify (fuel, flaps, altimeter, doors)
- A tick box to mark once verified
- Spaces to record specific values (e.g., fuel quantity in liters, altimeter reading in inches of mercury)

If a problem occurs (e.g., a hydraulic failure), the maintenance crew fills out an **incident tally sheet** recording:
- Date, time, flight number
- Type of failure
- Severity
- Part that failed

**That is exactly what a Quality Check Sheet is.** It transforms raw observations into structured, analyzable data ŌĆö just as a pilot's checklist transforms pre-flight memories into verified, recorded evidence.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of a Check Sheet

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Simplicity** | Designed for easy use by frontline workers without statistical training |
| **Real-time Recording** | Data is captured at the moment of occurrence, not retroactively |
| **Location-specific** | Used at the actual workplace (gemba) where the process occurs |
| **Standardization** | All observers record data in identical format |
| **Self-documenting** | The format itself guides what data to collect |
| **Quantitative/Qualitative** | Can record both numeric values and categorical outcomes |

## 1.4 Position in the Quality Toolkit

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Position of Check Sheet in 7 QC Tools:**
> 1. **Data Collection Tools:** Check Sheet, Stratification
> 2. **Data Analysis Tools:** Pareto Chart, Histogram, Scatter Diagram
> 3. **Problem Identification Tools:** Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram, Control Chart

The check sheet is the **upstream feeder** ŌĆö no analysis tool (Pareto, Histogram, Scatter) can function without raw data first collected via a properly designed check sheet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Check Sheets

### Why Use a Check Sheet?
1. **Eliminates Selective Observation Bias** ŌĆö All occurrences are recorded systematically, not just the "memorable" ones.
2. **Enables Pattern Detection** ŌĆö Time-stamped records reveal recurring defects at specific shifts, machines, or operators.
3. **Foundation for Pareto Analysis** ŌĆö Check sheets are the *data source* for Pareto charts (the 80/20 rule visualization).
4. **Cost-Effective** ŌĆö Requires no software; can be deployed on the shop floor instantly.
5. **Engages Workers** ŌĆö Operators become active participants in quality data collection.

### How Does a Check Sheet Work? ŌĆö Operational Logic
The operational logic follows a 5-stage flow:

- **Stage 1 ŌĆö Define the Objective:** Clarify *what* data is being collected and *why*. (e.g., "Identify the most frequent defect type on Line 3.")
- **Stage 2 ŌĆö Identify Data Categories:** Decide the classification dimensions (e.g., defect type, location, time, machine, operator).
- **Stage 3 ŌĆö Design the Form Layout:** Create columns/rows for each data category, with tally sections.
- **Stage 4 ŌĆö Collect Data at Source:** Workers fill in the sheet as events occur at the gemba.
- **Stage 5 ŌĆö Compile & Transfer:** Summarize tallies into a database for Pareto, histogram, or trend analysis.

## 2.2 Components of a Standard Check Sheet

A well-designed check sheet must contain these **eight mandatory components**:

1. **Title** ŌĆö Clear identification of the data collection purpose
2. **Date/Time Period** ŌĆö When the data is being collected
3. **Location/Workstation** ŌĆö Where the data is being collected
4. **Data Source Identifier** ŌĆö Operator name, machine ID, line number
5. **Measurement Items/Categories** ŌĆö The variables or defect types being recorded
6. **Recording Method** ŌĆö Tally marks (||||), tick marks (Ō£ō), or numeric values
7. **Totals Row/Column** ŌĆö For aggregation
8. **Remarks/Notes Section** ŌĆö For unusual observations

## 2.3 Types of Check Sheets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification: Five Major Types of Check Sheets**

| S.No. | Type | Purpose | Use Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Classification Check Sheet** | Records how often each defect/cause occurs | Defect type tally at injection molding |
| 2 | **Location Check Sheet** | Marks *where* on a product defects appear | Body panel scratch mapping in automotive |
| 3 | **Defect Cause Check Sheet** | Records the suspected cause of each defect | 5-Why preliminary data |
| 4 | **Frequency Distribution / Histogram Sheet** | Records measured values to build a histogram | Bolt diameter measurements on shop floor |
| 5 | **Conformance / Audit Checklist** | Yes/No compliance verification | ISO 9001 internal audit, pre-flight checks |

### 2.3.1 Detailed Description of Each Type

**(a) Classification Check Sheet (Most Common)**
- Tally marks under each defect category
- Subtotals per category
- Grand total at the bottom
- *Example:* "Daily Defect Tally ŌĆö Assembly Line A ŌĆö Date: __"

**(b) Location Check Sheet (Defective Spot Map)**
- A simplified diagram of the product
- Marks placed directly on the diagram at defect locations
- Reveals spatial patterns of defects

**(c) Defect Cause Check Sheet**
- Captures *what* defect, *when* it occurred, *who* was the operator, *which* machine
- Often uses 3-4 columns: Time | Defect | Operator | Machine

**(d) Frequency Distribution Check Sheet**
- Pre-defined measurement intervals in rows
- Tally marks accumulate per interval
- Directly converts into a histogram

**(e) Conformance / Audit Checklist**
- Binary Yes/No or Ō£ō/Ō£Ś columns
- Pass/Fail counts
- Common in regulatory compliance, safety audits, and pre-delivery inspections

## 2.4 Design Principles (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite ŌĆö The 7 Design Principles of a Check Sheet:**
> 1. **KISS Principle** ŌĆö Keep It Simple, Stupid. No complex statistics on the form.
> 2. **Collect Only Required Data** ŌĆö Avoid "just in case" columns that burden workers.
> 3. **Test the Sheet First** ŌĆö Pilot run with 2-3 users before full deployment.
> 4. **Include Clear Instructions** ŌĆö Workers must know exactly how to fill it.
> 5. **Build in Timestamping** ŌĆö Hour/shift columns enable time-series analysis.
> 6. **Easy to Summarize** ŌĆö Layout should allow rapid hand-tabulation.
> 7. **Place at Point of Use** ŌĆö The form lives where the work happens.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Quantitative Metrics:** Check sheets are predominantly qualitative/descriptive tools. However, the data they collect feeds into quantitative formulas for downstream tools.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Rate** | $\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | Quantifies quality level from check sheet data | Percentage (%) |
| **Pareto Cumulative %** | $\text{Cum \%} = \frac{\sum \text{Frequency up to category }i}{\text{Total Frequency}} \times 100$ | Determines vital few from useful many | Percentage (%) |
| **Relative Frequency** | $f_i = \frac{n_i}{N}$ | Proportion of occurrence per category | Dimensionless |
| **Tally to Count Conversion** | Every 4th stroke crosses the previous 4 (tally of 5 = #####) | Visual aid for fast counting | Count |
| **Defects Per Unit (DPU)** | $\text{DPU} = \frac{\text{Total Defects from sheet}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | Standard Six Sigma metric | Defects/Unit |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}} \times 10^6$ | Process capability benchmark | PPM |
| **Sample Size for Attribute Check** | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Statistical sample size (95% CI, $Z = 1.96$) | Count |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Check Sheet Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | End-of-line defect tally, paint defect location mapping |
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | Solder joint defects per shift on SMT line |
| **Civil Engineering** | Concrete cube compressive strength log, rebar inspection |
| **Software Testing** | Bug classification by module/severity |
| **Healthcare** | Patient triage checklist, surgical instrument count |
| **Aviation** | Pre-flight checklist, maintenance defect log |
| **Food Industry** | Daily hygiene compliance audit checklist |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure to Design a Check Sheet

The complete procedure follows a **10-step sequential algorithm**:

### Step 1: Define the Problem Statement
Clearly articulate the *what* and *why* of data collection.

$$\text{Example: ``Reduce scratch defects in painted door panels on Line 2 by 50\% in Q1.''}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Data Categories
Determine the classification dimensions using a **stratification matrix**.

$$\text{Categories} = \{\text{Defect Type},\ \text{Shift},\ \text{Operator},\ \text{Machine ID}\}$$

### Step 3: Decide on the Recording Method
Choose between:
- **Tally marks** (for count data, fastest to fill)
- **Numeric values** (for measurement data, e.g., diameter in mm)
- **Binary tick (Ō£ō/Ō£Ś)** (for pass/fail audit sheets)

### Step 4: Sketch the Form Layout
- **Header zone:** Title, date, location, operator
- **Body zone:** Rows = sub-categories, Columns = shift/time
- **Footer zone:** Totals, remarks, signature

### Step 5: Pilot Test the Form
Run for **1-2 days** with 2-3 frontline users to identify ambiguities.

### Step 6: Refine the Form
Incorporate feedback. Remove redundant columns; clarify abbreviations.

### Step 7: Train the Data Collectors
Brief operators on the standardized filling method to ensure **inter-rater reliability**.

### Step 8: Deploy at the Gemba
Affix the sheets at the workstation, printed on weather-resistant paper if needed.

### Step 9: Collect Data for a Statistically Valid Period
Generally **2-4 weeks** or until **minimum 100 events** are recorded (for binomial proportions, the rule of three applies: $n \geq 30$ gives reliable proportions).

### Step 10: Compile and Feed Forward
Transfer tally totals into a spreadsheet and construct the **Pareto chart** for prioritization.

## 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö Designing a Defect Classification Check Sheet

**Scenario:** A manufacturing unit produces 500 capacitors/day. The Quality Manager wants to identify the most frequent defect type.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Problem Statement:**
*"Identify the dominant defect type among 4 categories: (a) Capacitance out-of-spec, (b) Leakage current high, (c) Physical damage, (d) Lead-wire broken."*

**Step 2 ŌĆö Design the Check Sheet Layout:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{DAILY DEFECT CLASSIFICATION CHECK SHEET} \\
&\text{Line: B-3 \quad Date: 12-Mar-2025 \quad Shift: A \quad Inspector: R. Kumar} \\
&\text{---------------- Tally Columns ----------------} \\
&\text{Defect Type} \quad \vert \quad \text{Tally} \quad \vert \quad \text{Total} \\
&\text{Capacitance OOS} \quad \vert \quad \text{|||| |||| |||| ||} \quad \vert \quad 18 \\
&\text{Leakage High} \quad \vert \quad \text{|||| |||| ||} \quad \vert \quad 13 \\
&\text{Physical Damage} \quad \vert \quad \text{|||| |||| |||| |||| ||||} \quad \vert \quad 36 \\
&\text{Lead-Wire Broken} \quad \vert \quad \text{|||| ||||} \quad \vert \quad 8 \\
&\text{---------------- Grand Total ----------------} \quad \vert \quad 75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Defect Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Defect Rate} &= \frac{75}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 15\% \\
\text{Capacitance OOS Rate} &= \frac{18}{500} \times 100 = 3.6\% \\
\text{Leakage High Rate} &= \frac{13}{500} \times 100 = 2.6\% \\
\text{Physical Damage Rate} &= \frac{36}{500} \times 100 = 7.2\% \\
\text{Lead-Wire Broken Rate} &= \frac{8}{500} \times 100 = 1.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Feed into Pareto Analysis:**
Physical Damage (48% of all defects) emerges as the **vital few** ŌĆö top priority for root cause investigation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Check Sheet vs. Other QC Tools

| Comparison Parameter | Check Sheet | Pareto Chart | Histogram | Fishbone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Raw data collection | Prioritization of categories | Distribution shape | Root cause analysis |
| **Stage in DMAIC** | Define + Measure | Analyze | Analyze | Analyze |
| **Output Type** | Counts/Tallies | Ranked bar chart | Frequency plot | Causal diagram |
| **Quantitative?** | Both QL & QT | Quantitative | Quantitative | Qualitative |
| **Input Data** | Observations | Check sheet output | Measurement data | Brainstorming |
| **User Skill Level** | Low (operator) | Medium (engineer) | Medium (engineer) | High (team) |
| **Time to Create** | Minutes | Hours (post-data) | Hours (post-data) | Hours (workshop) |
| **Position in Flow** | 1st | 2nd | 2nd/3rd | 3rd/4th |

## 3.4 Common Errors in Check Sheet Design (Board Exam Trap)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Errors Students Make in Check Sheet Design Questions:**
> 1. **Combining measurement and classification data** in the same row ŌĆö causes tally confusion.
> 2. **Omitting the timestamp** ŌĆö destroys time-series pattern analysis.
> 3. **Using too many categories** (>10) ŌĆö creates "analysis paralysis."
> 4. **No clear unit specification** ŌĆö e.g., "Length" without "in mm."
> 5. **Collecting data retrospectively** from memory ŌĆö defeats the real-time purpose.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Check Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Header Block Title Date Location Operator] --> B1[Body Block Categories and Tally Cells]
    B1 --> C1[Subtotal Block Per Category Totals]
    C1 --> D1[Grand Total Block Aggregate Count]
    D1 --> E1[Remarks Block Observations and Anomalies]
    E1 --> F1[Output Stream Forward to Pareto or Histogram Tool]
    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Check Sheet to Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Problem] --> S2[Step 2 Define Categories]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Design Layout]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Pilot Test]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Deploy at Gemba]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Collect Real Time Data]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Tally and Aggregate]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Feed to Pareto Chart]
    S7 --> S9[Step 8b Feed to Histogram]
    S8 --> S10[Step 9 Identify Vital Few]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Step 10 Initiate Root Cause Analysis Fishbone]
    style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S7 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S10 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S11 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree of Check Sheet Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CS[Check Sheet Master Type] --> T1[Classification Tally Sheet]
    CS --> T2[Location Defect Spot Map]
    CS --> T3[Frequency Distribution Histogram Sheet]
    CS --> T4[Defect Cause Investigation Sheet]
    CS --> T5[Conformance Audit Checklist]
    T1 --> T1a[Used for Pareto Analysis]
    T3 --> T3a[Used for Histogram Construction]
    T5 --> T5a[Used for ISO Audits and Compliance]
    T2 --> T2a[Used for Spatial Pattern Detection]
    style CS fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Integration with PDCA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Plan Phase Objective Setting] --> D[Do Phase Data Collection via Check Sheet]
    D --> C[Check Phase Pareto and Histogram Analysis]
    C --> A[Act Phase Root Cause and Countermeasure]
    A --> P
    D -.feeds.-> D1[Raw Tally Data]
    C -.outputs.-> C1[Vital Few Identification]
    A -.outputs.-> A1[Standardized Improvement]
    style P fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Check Sheet. List any four types of check sheets. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** A Check Sheet is a structured, pre-formatted data collection document used to record specific types of data systematically in real-time at the location where the data is generated. It is the first of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality and serves as the data foundation for downstream analytical tools such as Pareto charts and histograms.

**Four Types (1.5 Marks ŌĆö 0.375 each):**
1. **Classification Check Sheet** ŌĆö Tallies frequencies of different defect categories
2. **Location Check Sheet** ŌĆö Maps defects on a product diagram to identify spatial patterns
3. **Defect Cause Check Sheet** ŌĆö Records suspected cause for each defect occurrence
4. **Conformance/Audit Checklist** ŌĆö Binary pass/fail recording for compliance verification

---

### Question 2: State any six design principles of a good check sheet. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks ŌĆö 0.5 each):**

1. **Simplicity (KISS)** ŌĆö Easy for frontline workers to use without statistical training
2. **Only required data** ŌĆö Avoid "just in case" columns
3. **Clear instructions** ŌĆö Workers must know exactly how to fill it
4. **Timestamping built-in** ŌĆö Enables time-series analysis
5. **Pilot tested** ŌĆö Validate with users before full deployment
6. **Easy summarization layout** ŌĆö Allows rapid hand-tabulation
7. **Located at point of use** ŌĆö Form lives where the work happens
8. **Standardized categories** ŌĆö All users interpret categories identically

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö 14 Marks

**Q. (a) Explain the concept of a Check Sheet as a Quality Management tool. Discuss its significance in the data collection phase of the PDCA cycle with a suitable example. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 Marks):** A check sheet is a structured data collection form designed to record observations in a standardized, real-time, location-based format. It is the first of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality and acts as the *upstream data feeder* for analytical tools.

**Characteristics (2 Marks):**
- Pre-formatted with categories
- Real-time recording at the workplace (gemba)
- Simple and operator-friendly
- Enables pattern detection
- Cost-effective

**Significance in PDCA ŌĆö Data Collection Phase (2 Marks):**
- In the **Plan** phase, check sheets help define measurable objectives.
- In the **Do** phase, they capture raw process data as it happens.
- In the **Check** phase, the data feeds into Pareto, Histogram, and Control Charts.
- In the **Act** phase, the patterns identified guide standardisation.

**Example (1 Mark):**
In an automotive assembly line, a daily defect classification check sheet is used to tally 4 defect types: paint scratch, dent, misaligned part, and missing fastener. After 2 weeks, 250 defects are recorded: 120 paint scratches, 60 dents, 45 misalignments, 25 missing fasteners. This feeds a Pareto chart showing paint scratches account for 48% ŌĆö triggering a 5-Why investigation.

**Key Benefits (1 Mark):** Eliminates selective observation bias, engages workers, provides audit trail.

---

**(b) Design a Check Sheet for recording customer complaints in a restaurant, classified by complaint type (Food Quality, Service Delay, Billing, Ambience, Hygiene) and time of day (Lunch, Dinner). State the data categories, layout, and a 5-step procedure for its deployment. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Data Categories (2 Marks):**
- **By Complaint Type:** Food Quality, Service Delay, Billing, Ambience, Hygiene
- **By Time:** Lunch (12 PM ŌĆō 4 PM), Dinner (7 PM ŌĆō 11 PM)
- **Metadata:** Date, Day, Manager Name, Total Covers

**Designed Check Sheet Layout (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{RESTAURANT CUSTOMER COMPLAINT CHECK SHEET} \\
&\text{Date: ____/____/____ \quad Day: Mon/Tue/Wed/Thu/Fri/Sat/Sun} \\
&\text{Manager: ____________________ \quad Total Covers: _______} \\
&\text{------------------ Time Slot ------------------} \\
&\text{Complaint Type} \quad \vert \quad \text{Lunch Tally} \quad \vert \quad \text{Dinner Tally} \quad \vert \quad \text{Total} \\
&\text{Food Quality} \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{Service Delay} \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{Billing Error} \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{Ambience} \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{Hygiene} \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{------------------ GRAND TOTAL ------------------} \quad \vert \quad \quad \\
&\text{Remarks: ____________________________________________}
\end{aligned}
$$

**5-Step Deployment Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. **Train staff** on how to fill the sheet for every complaint received.
2. **Place forms** at the cashier and hostess desk.
3. **Collect daily** and transfer tallies to a weekly spreadsheet.
4. **Run weekly Pareto** to identify top complaint type per time slot.
5. **Review with management** every Monday and initiate corrective action.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö 14 Marks

**Q. (a) Compare and contrast the different types of Check Sheets with suitable engineering examples. Explain when each type is most appropriately used. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Tabular Comparison (4 Marks):**

| Type | Engineering Example | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| Classification | Defect tally on CNC machining line | Need to count frequencies of defect categories |
| Location (Spot Map) | Crack mapping on a casting | Need to identify spatial clustering of defects |
| Frequency Distribution | Bolt diameter measurement tally | Have continuous measurement data to form histogram |
| Defect Cause | 5M (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement) tagging | Need to correlate defect with suspected cause |
| Conformance / Audit | ISO 9001 internal audit on shop floor | Need binary pass/fail compliance recording |

**Detailed Discussion (3 Marks):**
- **Classification sheets** dominate in high-volume production (e.g., 10,000 units/day in FMCG) where defects are countable and categorical.
- **Location sheets** are critical in sheet-metal and casting industries where defect position is informative about tool wear, mould design, or handling.
- **Frequency distribution sheets** serve as the on-line data accumulator for statistical process control (SPC) and histogram construction.
- **Defect cause sheets** form the first step in the *Define-Measure-Analyse* phase of DMAIC.
- **Conformance checklists** are mandated in regulated industries ŌĆö pharmaceutical (FDA), aerospace (AS9100), and automotive (IATF 16949).

**Engineering Use-Case Selection Logic (1 Mark):** Choose the type based on whether the data is **count-based, position-based, measurement-based, cause-based, or compliance-based**.

---

**(b) A manufacturing unit produces 1,000 resistors per day. Over 5 days, the following defects were recorded via a check sheet: [7 Marks]**

| Day | Open Circuit | Resistance OOS | Physical Crack | Lead Broken |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12 | 18 | 6 | 4 |
| 2 | 14 | 16 | 8 | 5 |
| 3 | 11 | 19 | 7 | 3 |
| 4 | 13 | 17 | 9 | 6 |
| 5 | 10 | 20 | 5 | 2 |

**Calculate:**
**(i) Total defects per category**
**(ii) Defect rate for each category**
**(iii) The cumulative percentage and identify the "vital few" using Pareto analysis**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total Defects per Category (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Open Circuit Total} &= 12 + 14 + 11 + 13 + 10 = 60 \\
\text{Resistance OOS Total} &= 18 + 16 + 19 + 17 + 20 = 90 \\
\text{Physical Crack Total} &= 6 + 8 + 7 + 9 + 5 = 35 \\
\text{Lead Broken Total} &= 4 + 5 + 3 + 6 + 2 = 20 \\
\text{Grand Total} &= 60 + 90 + 35 + 20 = 205
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Defect Rate per Category (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Units} &= 1000 \times 5 = 5000 \\
\text{Open Circuit Rate} &= \frac{60}{5000} \times 100 = 1.20\% \\
\text{Resistance OOS Rate} &= \frac{90}{5000} \times 100 = 1.80\% \\
\text{Physical Crack Rate} &= \frac{35}{5000} \times 100 = 0.70\% \\
\text{Lead Broken Rate} &= \frac{20}{5000} \times 100 = 0.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Pareto Cumulative % (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Ranked: Resistance OOS (90) > Open Circuit (60) > Crack (35) > Lead (20)} \\
&\text{Cum\% for Resistance OOS} = \frac{90}{205} \times 100 = 43.90\% \\
&\text{Cum\% for Open Circuit} = \frac{90+60}{205} \times 100 = \frac{150}{205} \times 100 = 73.17\% \\
&\text{Cum\% for Physical Crack} = \frac{150+35}{205} \times 100 = \frac{185}{205} \times 100 = 90.24\% \\
&\text{Cum\% for Lead Broken} = \frac{205}{205} \times 100 = 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pareto Table:**

| Rank | Defect | Count | % of Total | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resistance OOS | 90 | 43.90% | 43.90% |
| 2 | Open Circuit | 60 | 29.27% | 73.17% |
| 3 | Physical Crack | 35 | 17.07% | 90.24% |
| 4 | Lead Broken | 20 | 9.76% | 100.00% |

**Vital Few Identification (1 Mark):** The top 2 categories ŌĆö **Resistance OOS (43.90%)** and **Open Circuit (29.27%)** ŌĆö together account for **73.17%** of all defects, constituting the *vital few* that warrant immediate root cause investigation (likely targeting the resistance calibration process and lead-wire soldering station).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Check Sheet Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "real-time" principle** (ŌĆō1 mark) ŌĆö Check sheets must capture data at occurrence, not retrospectively.
> 2. **Missing the eight mandatory components** (ŌĆō2 marks) ŌĆö Examiners explicitly check for: Title, Date, Location, Source ID, Categories, Recording Method, Totals, Remarks.
> 3. **Confusing Check Sheet with Checklist** (ŌĆō1 mark) ŌĆö A *checklist* is one type; a *check sheet* is the broader family.
> 4. **No link to downstream tools** (ŌĆō1 mark) ŌĆö Always mention that the check sheet feeds the Pareto chart.
> 5. **In Pareto calculations, wrong cumulative formula** (ŌĆō2 marks) ŌĆö Cumulative % is based on ranked (descending) categories, not on the original order.
> 6. **In numerical problems, missing units** (ŌĆō1 mark) ŌĆö Always write %, count, or mm explicitly.
> 7. **Not stating the position in 7 QC Tools** (ŌĆō0.5 mark) ŌĆö Always mention: "1st of the 7 QC Tools, used in Data Collection."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Check Sheets**

### Core Facts
- Check Sheet = **1st of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7 QC Tools)**
- Primary purpose: **structured real-time data collection at the source (gemba)**
- It is the **upstream feeder** for Pareto Charts, Histograms, and Control Charts
- Position in PDCA: **Do phase (data collection)** and feeds the **Check phase (analysis)**

### Five Types to Memorize
1. **Classification Tally Sheet** (most common ŌĆö defect counts)
2. **Location / Defect Spot Map** (spatial pattern on product diagram)
3. **Frequency Distribution Sheet** (measurement intervals ŌåÆ feeds histogram)
4. **Defect Cause Sheet** (5M categorization: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement)
5. **Conformance / Audit Checklist** (binary Yes/No for ISO audits)

### Eight Mandatory Components
Title, Date/Time, Location, Data Source ID, Measurement Categories, Recording Method, Totals, Remarks

### Seven Design Principles
KISS, Required data only, Clear instructions, Timestamping, Pilot testing, Easy summarization, Point of use

### Key Quantitative Formulas
- Defect Rate: $\text{Defects} / \text{Units Inspected} \times 100$
- Pareto Cumulative %: $\sum f_i / N \times 100$ (on ranked categories)
- Tally rule: 4 vertical strokes + 1 diagonal = 5 (####)
- DPU: Total Defects / Units Produced
- DPMO: Defects / (Units ├Ś Opportunities) ├Ś $10^6$

### KTU Board Exam Tips
- Always draw the **actual layout** of the check sheet in design questions
- Always **link to downstream tool** (Pareto, Histogram) in theory questions
- Always mention **8 components + 7 design principles** for full marks in definition questions
- In Pareto numericals: **rank in descending order FIRST**, then compute cumulative %

### Key Conceptual Distinctions
- **Check Sheet vs. Checklist** ŌåÆ Check Sheet collects data; Checklist verifies completion
- **Check Sheet vs. Stratification** ŌåÆ Check Sheet records raw data; Stratification pre-categorizes it
- **Check Sheet vs. Control Chart** ŌåÆ Check Sheet is static/snapshot; Control Chart is dynamic/time-series

### Real-World Anchor Phrases for Theory Answers
- *"Check Sheets are the foundation upon which all statistical quality tools are built."*
- *"Without data, there is no analysis; without a check sheet, there is no data."*
- *"The gemba is where the check sheet lives ŌĆö not in the manager's office."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scatter Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scatter Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Scatter Diagram** (also known as a **Scatter Plot**, **Scatter Chart**, or **Correlation Diagram**) is a fundamental graphical statistical tool of quality management that displays the relationship between two quantitative variables by plotting paired data points on a two-dimensional Cartesian plane.

In the context of KTU Quality Management (OEIET612), a scatter diagram is officially classified as one of the **"Seven Tools of Quality Control"** (7 QC Tools), originally codified by **Kaoru Ishikawa** of Japan. It is specifically used in the *Analyze* phase of the DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology under Six Sigma, as well as throughout the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Scatter Diagram is a graphical technique used to study the possible relationship between two variables. The independent variable (cause) is plotted on the X-axis and the dependent variable (effect) is plotted on the Y-axis. By observing the pattern of plotted points, the analyst can determine whether the two variables are correlated, and if so, the nature and strength of that correlation.*

**Key Terminology:**
- **Independent Variable (X-axis):** The presumed *cause* or *input* variable that the analyst controls or observes (e.g., temperature, pressure, training hours).
- **Dependent Variable (Y-axis):** The *effect* or *output* variable that responds to changes in the independent variable (e.g., product yield, defect rate, sales).
- **Data Pair (x, y):** A single observation containing one measurement of X and its corresponding measurement of Y.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a baker trying to understand why some cakes turn out fluffy and others flat. You suspect that the **amount of baking powder** (a cause) influences the **height of the cake** (an effect).

You bake 20 cakes, recording how much baking powder you used in each, and measuring the final height. If you simply listed these numbers in a table, it would be hard to see a pattern. But if you plotted each cake as a single dot on a graph ŌĆö with baking powder on the horizontal axis and cake height on the vertical axis ŌĆö you would immediately *see* the story: maybe the dots slope upward (more powder = taller cakes), or maybe they scatter randomly (powder doesn't matter at all).

That visual story is what a **Scatter Diagram** provides. It is the **"lens"** through which two variables are observed together, allowing the human brain (which is exceptional at pattern recognition) to instantly detect relationships that raw numbers would hide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
> Consider a heat treatment process. An engineer suspects that the **soaking temperature** (X) affects the **ultimate tensile strength** of a steel alloy (Y). By plotting each heat-treatment batch as a point on a scatter diagram, the engineer can visually confirm whether higher temperatures yield stronger steel, and how tight (predictable) that relationship is. This is the everyday reality of process optimization in manufacturing.

## 1.3 Standard Statistical Metrics Used

The following **bold** values are standard reference benchmarks used universally in interpreting scatter diagrams:

- **Correlation Coefficient Range:** $\mathbf{-1 \leq r \leq +1}$
- **Perfect Positive Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = +1}$
- **Perfect Negative Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = -1}$
- **No Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = 0}$
- **Strong Correlation Threshold:** $\mathbf{\vert r \vert \geq 0.7}$
- **Weak Correlation Threshold:** $\mathbf{\vert r \vert \leq 0.3}$
- **Minimum Recommended Sample Size (KTU standard):** $\mathbf{n \geq 30}$ paired observations for reliable inference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of a Scatter Diagram

The scatter diagram serves **four core analytical purposes** within quality management:

1. **Relationship Detection:** To discover and confirm whether a statistically meaningful relationship exists between two variables.
2. **Pattern Classification:** To classify the nature of the relationship (positive, negative, linear, non-linear, or none).
3. **Strength Quantification:** To visually estimate the strength (tightness) of the association.
4. **Causality Exploration (Preliminary):** To provide *initial evidence* supporting or refuting a cause-and-effect hypothesis, although correlation does not strictly prove causation.

## 2.2 The Logic Step-by-Step (How a Scatter Diagram Works)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Hypothesis Formation
Before plotting, the analyst must form a clear hypothesis: *"Does variable X influence variable Y?"* This guides data collection.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Paired Data Collection
Collect **n pairs** of (xßĄó, yßĄó) observations where each pair represents simultaneous measurements under identical conditions. The KTU convention is **n Ōēź 30**.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Axis Assignment
- Place the **independent (cause)** variable on the **horizontal X-axis**.
- Place the **dependent (effect)** variable on the **vertical Y-axis**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Plotting
Mark each pair (xßĄó, yßĄó) as a single dot on the graph. **Do not connect the dots with lines** ŌĆö the points are individual observations.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Pattern Recognition
Examine the cloud of points to determine the dominant trend:
- Upward slope ŌåÆ positive correlation
- Downward slope ŌåÆ negative correlation
- Random cloud ŌåÆ no correlation
- Curved shape ŌåÆ non-linear correlation

### Step 6 ŌĆö Quantification
Compute the **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (r)** to numerically confirm the visual impression.

## 2.3 Seven Recognizable Scatter Patterns

| # | Pattern Name | Visual Description | Correlation Coefficient (r) | KTU Interpretation |
|---|--------------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Strong Positive | Tight cluster sloping upward (lower-left to upper-right) | $r \approx +1$ | Direct proportional cause-effect |
| 2 | Weak Positive | Loose cloud sloping gently upward | $0 < r < +0.5$ | Likely relationship, noisy |
| 3 | Strong Negative | Tight cluster sloping downward (upper-left to lower-right) | $r \approx -1$ | Inverse cause-effect |
| 4 | Weak Negative | Loose cloud sloping gently downward | $-0.5 < r < 0$ | Possible inverse influence |
| 5 | No Correlation | Random circular cloud, no slope | $r \approx 0$ | Variables are independent |
| 6 | Perfect Positive | All points lie exactly on a straight upward line | $r = +1$ | Mathematical certainty |
| 7 | Non-Linear (Curvilinear) | Points form a U-shape, parabola, or exponential curve | $r \approx 0$ but non-linear fit exists | Non-linear relationship |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for the Scatter Diagram topic. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Formula Symbol | Formula Expression | Meaning / Use |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Pearson Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}$ | Computes $r$ directly from raw data |
| Pearson Coefficient (Mean Form) | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Computes $r$ using deviations from means |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = r^2$ | Proportion of variance in Y explained by X |
| Mean of X | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Average of independent variable |
| Mean of Y | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i}{n}$ | Average of dependent variable |
| Regression Slope | $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Slope of best-fit line on scatter |
| Regression Intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | Y-intercept of best-fit line |
| Regression Line Equation | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | Best-fit line used for prediction |
| Strength Threshold (Strong) | $\vert r \vert \geq 0.7$ | Indicative of strong relationship |
| Strength Threshold (Moderate) | $0.3 < \vert r \vert < 0.7$ | Indicative of moderate relationship |
| Strength Threshold (Weak) | $\vert r \vert \leq 0.3$ | Indicative of weak or no relationship |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | X Variable (Cause) | Y Variable (Effect) | Practical Use |
|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Manufacturing | Cutting speed (rpm) | Surface roughness (┬Ąm) | Optimize machining parameters |
| Civil Engineering | Cement content (kg/m┬│) | Compressive strength (MPa) | Mix design optimization |
| Software Quality | Code review duration (hrs) | Defect density (per KLOC) | Process improvement |
| Chemical Engineering | Reaction temperature (┬░C) | Conversion yield (%) | Reactor optimization |
| Healthcare Quality | Handwashing frequency | Hospital infection rate | Hygiene protocol design |
| Operations Management | Employee training hours | Productivity index | HR policy validation |

## 2.6 Limitations and Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Frequently Tests These Limitations:**
> 1. **Correlation ŌēĀ Causation:** A scatter diagram only shows *association*; it cannot prove that X *causes* Y. A hidden third variable (lurking variable) may be responsible.
> 2. **Outlier Sensitivity:** A single extreme point can dramatically distort the perceived pattern.
> 3. **Sample Size Dependency:** With small $n$ (< 30), patterns can appear by random chance.
> 4. **Linearity Assumption:** Pearson's $r$ only measures *linear* relationships; it will report $r \approx 0$ even for perfect parabolic data.
> 5. **Range Restriction:** If X is sampled over a narrow range, the true relationship may be hidden.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Pearson's Correlation Coefficient (r)

The Pearson Correlation Coefficient is the **single most important quantitative metric** associated with a scatter diagram. We derive it step-by-step below.

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

Pearson's $r$ is a **dimensionless, unit-free number** that quantifies two things simultaneously:
- The **direction** (sign) of the linear relationship: positive or negative.
- The **strength** (magnitude) of the linear relationship: how tightly the points cluster around a straight line.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Starting Intuition:** We want a measure that becomes large when high X corresponds to high Y, and large negative when high X corresponds to low Y.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define Covariance:**
The covariance measures the joint variability of X and Y around their means.

$$
\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})
$$

*Logic:* If when $x_i > \bar{x}$ we also have $y_i > \bar{y}$, the product $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ is positive, contributing to a positive covariance.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Normalize by Standard Deviations:**
Covariance has units (the product of X-units and Y-units), so it cannot be universally compared. Divide by the product of standard deviations to remove units.

$$
r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute Definitions of Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_X = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}, \quad \sigma_Y = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}}} \\[1em]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\dfrac{1}{n}\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} \\[1em]
&= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The $\frac{1}{n}$ cancels from numerator and denominator, giving the cleaner form. This is the **mean-deviation form** of $r$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand to Raw-Score (Computational) Form:**

Expanding $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = x_i y_i - \bar{x} y_i - \bar{y} x_i + \bar{x}\bar{y}$ and summing over all $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) &= \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y} - n\bar{y}\bar{x} + n\bar{x}\bar{y} \\
&= \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n}$:

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = \sum x_i y_i - \frac{(\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{n}
$$

Similarly for the variance terms:

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}
$$

$$
\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Raw-Score Formula:**

$$
\boxed{r = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}}
$$

*Logic:* This form is preferred for hand calculation because all quantities can be computed directly from raw data without first calculating means.

## 3.2 Proof that $-1 \leq r \leq +1$

We prove the boundedness of $r$ using the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**, which states that for any two real vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$:

$$
\left(\sum u_i v_i\right)^2 \leq \left(\sum u_i^2\right)\left(\sum v_i^2\right)
$$

Let $u_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ and $v_i = y_i - \bar{y}$. Applying Cauchy-Schwarz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})\right]^2 &\leq \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \\[0.5em]
\frac{\left[\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})\right]^2}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2} &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
\left[\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}\right]^2 &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
r^2 &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
|r| &\leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $-1 \leq r \leq +1$. **QED.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equality cases:**
> - $r = +1$ occurs when $(y_i - \bar{y}) = k(x_i - \bar{x})$ for some positive constant $k$ (perfect positive linear).
> - $r = -1$ occurs when $(y_i - \bar{y}) = k(x_i - \bar{x})$ for some negative constant $k$ (perfect negative linear).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A quality engineer studies the relationship between **Annealing Temperature (┬░C)** (X) and **Hardness (HRC)** (Y) for a steel alloy. Data from $n = 5$ batches:

| Batch | $x_i$ (┬░C) | $y_i$ (HRC) |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | 700 | 42 |
| 2 | 750 | 48 |
| 3 | 800 | 55 |
| 4 | 850 | 60 |
| 5 | 900 | 65 |

**Required:** Compute $r$ and interpret.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Required Sums

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $700+750+800+850+900 = 4000$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $42+48+55+60+65 = 270$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $490000+562500+640000+722500+810000 = 3\,225\,000$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $1764+2304+3025+3600+4225 = 14\,918$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $(700)(42)+(750)(48)+(800)(55)+(850)(60)+(900)(65) = 29400+36000+44000+51000+58500 = 218\,900$ |

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Raw-Score Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum x_i y_i &= (5)(218\,900) = 1\,094\,500 \\
(\sum x_i)(\sum y_i) &= (4000)(270) = 1\,080\,000 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 1\,094\,500 - 1\,080\,000 = 14\,500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum x_i^2 &= (5)(3\,225\,000) = 16\,125\,000 \\
(\sum x_i)^2 &= (4000)^2 = 16\,000\,000 \\
\text{Term A} &= 16\,125\,000 - 16\,000\,000 = 125\,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum y_i^2 &= (5)(14\,918) = 74\,590 \\
(\sum y_i)^2 &= (270)^2 = 72\,900 \\
\text{Term B} &= 74\,590 - 72\,900 = 1690
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{14\,500}{\sqrt{(125\,000)(1690)}} = \frac{14\,500}{\sqrt{211\,250\,000}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{14\,500}{14\,533.6} \approx 0.9977
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation

Since $r \approx 0.998$, which is greater than **0.7**, we have a **strong positive linear correlation**. Hardness increases almost perfectly with annealing temperature in this range.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Regression Line (Optional but High-Yield)

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{2900}{25\,000} = 0.116 \\[0.5em]
\bar{x} &= 800, \quad \bar{y} = 54 \\[0.5em]
a &= 54 - (0.116)(800) = 54 - 92.8 = -38.8 \\[0.5em]
\hat{y} &= -38.8 + 0.116x
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The regression line can be used to predict the hardness for any annealing temperature within the observed range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö How a Scatter Diagram is Constructed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STEP 1\nIDENTIFY PROBLEM"]:::startNode --> node2["STEP 2\nFORM HYPOTHESIS\nDoes X affect Y?"]
    node2 --> node3["STEP 3\nCOLLECT PAIRED DATA\nMinimum n = 30 pairs"]
    node3 --> node4["STEP 4\nASSIGN AXES\nX = Cause on Horizontal\nY = Effect on Vertical"]
    node4 --> node5["STEP 5\nPLOT POINTS\nEach pair is one dot"]
    node5 --> node6["STEP 6\nOBSERVE PATTERN\nShape of cloud"]
    node6 --> decision1{"Pattern Visible?"}
    decision1 -- YES --> node7["STEP 7A\nCOMPUTE r VALUE\nQuantify strength"]
    decision1 -- NO --> node8["STEP 7B\nNO CORRELATION\nr near 0"]
    node7 --> node9["STEP 8\nDRAW BEST FIT LINE\nRegression line"]
    node9 --> node10["STEP 9\nINTERPRET AND DECIDE\nUse for process control"]
    node8 --> node10

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef default fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Pattern Recognition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["OBSERVE SCATTER CLOUD"] --> nodeB{"Direction of Cloud?"}
    nodeB -- "Slopes Upward" --> nodeC{"Tight or Loose?"}
    nodeB -- "Slopes Downward" --> nodeD{"Tight or Loose?"}
    nodeB -- "Random No Slope" --> nodeE["NO CORRELATION\nr approx 0"]
    nodeB -- "Curved Shape" --> nodeF["NON LINEAR\nUse polynomial fit"]

    nodeC -- "Tight Cluster" --> nodeG["STRONG POSITIVE\nr greater than 0.7"]
    nodeC -- "Loose Cloud" --> nodeH["WEAK POSITIVE\nr between 0 and 0.5"]

    nodeD -- "Tight Cluster" --> nodeI["STRONG NEGATIVE\nr less than minus 0.7"]
    nodeD -- "Loose Cloud" --> nodeJ["WEAK NEGATIVE\nr between minus 0.5 and 0"]

    nodeG --> nodeK["INFERENCE\nCause effect confirmed\nPredict and control"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeL["INFERENCE\nNo linear relation\nInvestigate other causes"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["INFERENCE\nNon linear relation\nApply transformation"]

    classDef strongPos fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strongNeg fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonlinear fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Position of Scatter Diagram in the 7 QC Tools Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sevenQC["7 QC TOOLS KAORU ISHIKAWA"]
        direction TB

    end

    phase1["IDENTIFY Problem"] --> tool1
    tool1 --> tool2
    tool2 --> tool3
    tool3 --> tool4
    tool4 --> tool5
    tool5 --> tool6
    tool6 --> tool7
    tool7 --> output["QUALITY DECISION"]

    classDef highlight fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF,font-weight:bold

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Interpretation of r

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["COMPUTED r VALUE"] --> range1{"Range Check\nminus 1 to plus 1"}
    range1 -- "INVALID" --> errorA["ERROR\nRecalculate data"]
    range1 -- "VALID" --> rval{"Magnitude"}

    rval -- "abs r approx 1" --> outA["PERFECT CORRELATION\nDeterministic link\nPredict with certainty"]
    rval -- "0.7 to 1.0" --> outB["STRONG CORRELATION\nReliable prediction\nAct on relationship"]
    rval -- "0.3 to 0.7" --> outC["MODERATE CORRELATION\nLikely relation but noisy\nInvestigate further"]
    rval -- "0.0 to 0.3" --> outD["WEAK CORRELATION\nPoor predictive power\nReject hypothesis"]
    rval -- "r approx 0" --> outE["NO LINEAR CORRELATION\nCheck non linear patterns"]
    rval -- "negative values" --> outF["INVERSE RELATIONSHIP\nIncrease X decreases Y"]

    classDef perfect fill:#00C853,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strong fill:#76FF03,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef weak fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef none fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class outA,outF perfect
    class outB strong
    class outC moderate
    class outD weak
    class outE none
```
