# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**Q: Define a Scatter Diagram. State any three types of correlation patterns observable in a scatter diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Scatter Diagram** is a graphical quality tool that displays the relationship between two quantitative variables by plotting paired data points, with the independent variable (cause) on the X-axis and the dependent variable (effect) on the Y-axis.

**Three correlation patterns:**
1. **Positive Correlation:** As X increases, Y also increases. The cloud of points slopes upward from lower-left to upper-right.
2. **Negative Correlation:** As X increases, Y decreases. The cloud slopes downward from upper-left to lower-right.
3. **No Correlation:** The points form a random cloud with no discernible trend, indicating the variables are statistically independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student mentions *both* variables (X and Y), identifies the axis assignment, and lists at least 3 distinct patterns.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between correlation and causation. Why is this distinction important when interpreting a scatter diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Correlation | Causation |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Definition | Statistical association between two variables | One variable directly produces a change in another |
| Proof Required | Pairwise data observation | Controlled experimentation with isolation of variables |
| Direction | Bidirectional observation | Unidirectional (cause ŌåÆ effect) |
| Proof Tool | Scatter Diagram, Correlation Coefficient | Randomized Controlled Trial, DOE |

**Importance in Scatter Diagram Interpretation:**
A scatter diagram may show a *strong* correlation ($r = 0.9$) between two variables, but this does **not** prove that X causes Y. A **lurking variable** (a hidden third factor) may be driving both. For example, ice-cream sales and drowning deaths are positively correlated, but eating ice-cream does not *cause* drowning ŌĆö *summer heat* is the lurking variable causing both.

Hence, scatter diagrams should be used as a **preliminary indicator** of relationships, prompting further investigation via designed experiments before any causal claim is made.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for constructing and interpreting a scatter diagram. Discuss any four recognizable correlation patterns with suitable sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data shows the relationship between the **number of hours of quality training (X)** given to employees and the **number of defects per batch (Y)** observed in their work. Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient and interpret the result.

| Employee | Training Hours (X) | Defects (Y) |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | 10 | 50 |
| 2 | 15 | 45 |
| 3 | 20 | 40 |
| 4 | 25 | 30 |
| 5 | 30 | 25 |
| 6 | 35 | 20 |
| 7 | 40 | 15 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Procedure for Constructing a Scatter Diagram:**

1. **Define the Problem:** Clearly identify the two variables suspected of being related. Establish which is the cause (X) and which is the effect (Y).
2. **Collect Paired Data:** Gather at least 30 pairs (xßĄó, yßĄó) of observations under identical conditions to ensure statistical reliability.
3. **Draw the Axes:** Place the independent variable X on the horizontal axis and dependent variable Y on the vertical axis. Label both axes with units and provide equal-scale gridlines.
4. **Plot the Points:** Each pair (xßĄó, yßĄó) is marked as a single dot. Dots are not connected.
5. **Observe the Pattern:** Visually analyze the cloud to determine trend.
6. **Quantify:** Calculate Pearson's $r$ for confirmation.
7. **Draw the Best-Fit Line:** A regression line $\hat{y} = a + bx$ summarizes the trend.
8. **Interpret and Act:** Use findings for process control or further experimentation.

**Four Recognizable Correlation Patterns:**

| Pattern | Description | Correlation Value |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| Strong Positive | Tight upward-sloping cluster; as X rises, Y rises consistently | $r \approx +1$ |
| Strong Negative | Tight downward-sloping cluster; as X rises, Y falls | $r \approx -1$ |
| No Correlation | Random circular cloud; no slope or pattern | $r \approx 0$ |
| Non-Linear (Curvilinear) | Points trace a curve (parabola, exponential); linear fit is inappropriate | $r \approx 0$ but relationship exists |

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Required Sums (n = 7):**

| Quantity | Calculation | Value |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $10+15+20+25+30+35+40$ | $175$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $50+45+40+30+25+20+15$ | $225$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $100+225+400+625+900+1225+1600$ | $5075$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $2500+2025+1600+900+625+400+225$ | $8275$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $500+675+800+750+750+700+600$ | $4775$ |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Pearson Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i) \\
&= (7)(4775) - (175)(225) \\
&= 33\,425 - 39\,375 = -5950
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term A} &= n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 = (7)(5075) - (175)^2 = 35\,525 - 30\,625 = 4900 \\
\text{Term B} &= n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2 = (7)(8275) - (225)^2 = 57\,925 - 50\,625 = 7300
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{-5950}{\sqrt{(4900)(7300)}} = \frac{-5950}{\sqrt{35\,770\,000}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{-5950}{5980.80} \approx -0.9949
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation:**

Since $r \approx -0.995$, this is a **very strong negative correlation**. As training hours (X) increase, defects per batch (Y) decrease in a near-perfectly linear fashion. The quality management implication is that **investing in employee training dramatically reduces defects**, and the relationship is highly predictable.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** What is a Scatter Diagram? Explain its role as one of the seven tools of quality control. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process engineer studies the effect of **Catalyst Concentration (%)** (X) on **Reaction Yield (%)** (Y). The data obtained is:

| Trial | X (%) | Y (%) |
|-------|--------|--------|
| 1 | 1.0 | 65 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 72 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 78 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 85 |
| 5 | 3.0 | 90 |
| 6 | 3.5 | 93 |
| 7 | 4.0 | 95 |

Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient, draw the regression line equation, and predict the yield at $X = 4.5\%$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Definition:** A scatter diagram is a two-dimensional graphical plot of paired (X, Y) data points used to visually and statistically identify the relationship between a cause variable (X) and an effect variable (Y).

**Role in 7 QC Tools:**
It belongs to the family of "**Seven Tools of Quality Control**" codified by Kaoru Ishikawa, alongside Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, Check Sheets, Control Charts, Histograms, Pareto Charts, and Flowcharts. Specifically, the scatter diagram is the **only tool among the seven that examines the relationship *between* two variables**. It is used in the *Analyze* phase of DMAIC and helps verify cause-effect hypotheses.

**Advantages:**
1. **Visual Clarity:** Instantly reveals relationship patterns through visual inspection.
2. **Quantification:** Allows computation of Pearson's $r$ for objective strength measurement.
3. **Simplicity:** Requires no advanced mathematical training to construct and interpret.
4. **Versatility:** Applicable to any two continuous variables across all engineering disciplines.
5. **Predictive Power:** Combined with regression, enables forecasting.

**Limitations:**
1. **Only Two Variables:** Cannot handle multi-variable relationships.
2. **Correlation ŌēĀ Causation:** Strong correlation does not imply causal link.
3. **Outlier Sensitivity:** A few extreme points can distort the visual and statistical impression.
4. **Linearity Bias:** Pearson's $r$ misses non-linear (curvilinear) patterns.
5. **No Time Component:** Cannot show how relationships evolve over time.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Sums (n = 7):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $1.0+1.5+2.0+2.5+3.0+3.5+4.0 = 17.5$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $65+72+78+85+90+93+95 = 578$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $1.0+2.25+4.0+6.25+9.0+12.25+16.0 = 50.75$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $4225+5184+6084+7225+8100+8649+9025 = 48\,492$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $65+108+156+212.5+270+325.5+380 = 1517$ |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Pearson's r:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (7)(1517) - (17.5)(578) \\
&= 10\,619 - 10\,115 = 504
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term A} &= (7)(50.75) - (17.5)^2 = 355.25 - 306.25 = 49.0 \\
\text{Term B} &= (7)(48\,492) - (578)^2 = 339\,444 - 334\,084 = 5360
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{504}{\sqrt{(49.0)(5360)}} = \frac{504}{\sqrt{262\,640}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{504}{512.49} \approx 0.9834
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Regression Line:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= 17.5/7 = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = 578/7 \approx 82.571 \\
b &= \frac{504}{49} = 10.286 \\
a &= 82.571 - (10.286)(2.5) = 82.571 - 25.714 = 56.857 \\
\hat{y} &= 56.857 + 10.286x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Prediction at X = 4.5%:**

$$
\hat{y} = 56.857 + 10.286(4.5) = 56.857 + 46.286 = 103.14\%
$$

*Note:* A predicted yield exceeding 100% indicates the model is being **extrapolated** beyond the observed data range ($X_{max} = 4.0\%$). The prediction should be treated with caution, and further experimental data at higher concentrations is recommended to validate the model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Axis Inversion:** Students often place the dependent variable on the X-axis. Always: Cause ŌåÆ X-axis, Effect ŌåÆ Y-axis.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** When interpreting, mention the units of both variables (e.g., "training hours" and "defects per batch").
> 3. **Confusing $r$ and $r^2$:** $r^2$ is the *coefficient of determination* (proportion of variance explained), not the correlation strength.
> 4. **Conflating Tools:** A scatter diagram does *not* control a process (that's a control chart) or rank causes (that's a Pareto chart).
> 5. **Missing the n = 30 caveat:** State that conclusions from small samples are unreliable.
> 6. **Ignoring Lurking Variables:** Always caution against inferring causation from correlation alone.
> 7. **Skipping the Equation on the Graph:** When asked to draw, students often forget to write the regression equation $\hat{y} = a + bx$ on the chart ŌĆö this loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Scatter Diagram**

**Core Definition:**
- Graphical tool to study relationship between two variables.
- Part of the **7 QC Tools** (Kaoru Ishikawa).
- X-axis = independent (cause); Y-axis = dependent (effect).

**Key Patterns (must know all 7):**
- Strong positive, weak positive, strong negative, weak negative, no correlation, perfect positive/negative, non-linear (curvilinear).

**Mandatory Formulas:**
- $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}$
- $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$
- $R^2 = r^2$
- Regression: $\hat{y} = a + bx$ where $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$, $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$.

**Critical Numerical Boundaries:**
- $-1 \leq r \leq +1$
- $\vert r \vert \geq 0.7$ ŌåÆ strong; $0.3 < \vert r \vert < 0.7$ ŌåÆ moderate; $\vert r \vert \leq 0.3$ ŌåÆ weak.

**Universal Limitations (always state in answers):**
- Correlation ŌēĀ Causation.
- Outlier sensitivity.
- Only handles two variables.
- Pearson's $r$ only measures linear association.
- Sample size $n \geq 30$ preferred.

**Steps to Draw (memorize the 8-step sequence):**
1. Identify problem ŌåÆ 2. Form hypothesis ŌåÆ 3. Collect pairs (n Ōēź 30) ŌåÆ 4. Draw axes ŌåÆ 5. Plot points ŌåÆ 6. Observe pattern ŌåÆ 7. Compute r ŌåÆ 8. Draw regression line & interpret.

**Common Engineering Applications:**
- Cutting speed vs surface roughness (manufacturing).
- Cement content vs compressive strength (civil).
- Temperature vs yield (chemical).
- Training hours vs defects (HR quality).
- Code reviews vs defect density (software quality).

**Last-Minute Exam Pearls:**
- Always state units in axis labels.
- Always mention "lurking variable" caveat when interpreting.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, you must present the *formula table*, the *computed sums table*, the *substituted numerical equation*, and the *interpretation sentence*. Missing any one of these costs marks.
- For non-linear patterns, mention that Pearson's $r$ would falsely indicate "no correlation"; polynomial regression or transformation is required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pareto Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pareto Chart # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Pareto Chart (Definition ŌĆō KTU Standard)** > A **Pareto Chart** is a dual-axis graphical quality tool that combines a **descending bar graph** (representing the frequency of occurrence of defects, causes, or problems) with a **cumulative percentage line (Pareto curve)** plotted on the same diagram. It is constructed on the principle that a majority of problems (typically **80%**) are caused by a small number of causes (typically **20%**). It is one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality** recognized by KTU and is widely used in the **PlanŌĆōDoŌĆōCheckŌĆōAct (PDCA)** cycle under the *Check* phase to prioritize improvement actions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > Pareto Chart is officially listed in **Module 4 ŌĆō Quality Control Tools (Statistical Process Control Tools)** of OEIET612. Examiners frequently frame questions around the **"interpretation of the 80/20 rule"** and the **"steps to construct a Pareto Chart."** Marks are heavily awarded for correctly identifying the cumulative percentage logic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are the **warden of a hostel** with **100 discipline complaints** in a month. After sorting them, you find: - 40 complaints are about **noise after 10 PM** - 25 complaints are about **food quality** - 15 complaints are about **wifi issues** - 12 complaints are about **water supply** - 8 complaints are about **miscellaneous reasons** A Pareto Chart would immediately tell you: *"Fix the noise problem and you have already solved 40% of all complaints!"* You don't have to spend equal time on every issue ŌĆö the chart visually proves that tackling the tallest bar (the "vital few") yields the biggest payoff. This is the essence of the **Pareto Principle**, originally formulated by Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto** (1897) after observing that **80% of Italy's wealth was owned by 20% of the population**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** | Roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes | | **Vital Few** | The small number of categories (left side of chart) responsible for most problems | | **Useful Many** | The larger number of categories (right side) with minor individual impact | | **Cumulative Curve (Pareto Line)** | A superimposed line showing running total percentage | | **YŌéü (Left Axis)** | Frequency or cost of each category | | **YŌéé (Right Axis)** | Cumulative percentage (0% to 100%) | --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation The Pareto Chart is built on three foundational pillars: 1. **Categorization** ŌĆō Data is classified into measurable groups (defect types, complaint categories, failure modes). 2. **Ranking** ŌĆō Categories are arranged in **descending order of frequency** (tallest bar on the left). 3. **Cumulative Aggregation** ŌĆō A running percentage line is overlaid to identify the "vital few." ### 2.1.1 The 80/20 Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > The Pareto Principle does **not** rigidly mean 80% and 20%. KTU examiners expect students to interpret it as a **"rule of thumb"** indicating that **a small proportion of causes typically accounts for a large proportion of effects.** The exact ratio varies by industry and dataset. ## 2.2 Steps to Construct a Pareto Chart (KTU Board Favorite) 1. **Identify the Problem** ŌĆō Decide what to analyze (e.g., types of defects, reasons for customer complaints). 2. **Select Categories** ŌĆō Choose mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive classifications. 3. **Collect Data** ŌĆō Gather frequency or cost data over a defined period. 4. **Tabulate Frequencies** ŌĆō Count occurrences per category. 5. **Rank in Descending Order** ŌĆō Largest frequency first; keep an "Other" category last if needed. 6. **Calculate Cumulative Frequency and Percentage** ŌĆō Using the formulas below. 7. **Plot the Bars** ŌĆō Bars on the left Y-axis (frequency). 8. **Overlay the Curve** ŌĆō Cumulative percentage points on the right Y-axis (0ŌĆō100%), connected by a line. 9. **Label Axes and Title** ŌĆō Clearly mark X-axis (categories), YŌéü (frequency), YŌéé (cumulative %). 10. **Analyze** ŌĆō Identify the "vital few" (left of the 80% mark on the curve). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Relative Frequency** | $f_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Proportion of total for category $i$ | Dimensionless | | **Cumulative Frequency** | $C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | Running total up to category $k$ | Count | | **Cumulative Percentage** | $P_k = \dfrac{C_k}{\sum n_i} \times 100$ | Running percentage total | Percent (%) | | **Pareto Ratio** | $P \approx 80:20$ | Empirical rule of thumb | Ratio | | **Vital Few Cut-off** | $P_k \geq 80\%$ | Categories to the left of this are vital few | Percent | > **Note on LaTeX:** All vertical bars and absolute values in the table have been written using `\vert` or `\mid` syntax to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** Identifying which defect type causes the most rejections on a production line. - **Software Engineering:** Ranking bug categories to prioritize testing effort. - **Service Industry:** Analyzing customer complaints (TQM applications). - **Supply Chain:** Determining which supplier causes most delivery delays. - **Healthcare (used in KTU-linked case studies):** Identifying primary causes of patient readmission. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style ŌĆō 7 Mark Standard) > **Problem Statement:** > A manufacturing firm recorded the following **types of defects** in a batch of **500 produced units** during a quality audit. Construct a Pareto Chart (table form) and identify the "vital few." | Defect Type | Frequency (nßĄó) | |---|---| | Surface Scratch | 220 | | Dimensional Error | 95 | | Paint Defect | 80 | | Weld Crack | 50 | | Missing Component | 30 | | Other | 25 | | **Total** | **500** | ### 3.1.1 Step 1 ŌĆō Rank in Descending Order Already sorted: Surface Scratch (220) ŌåÆ Dimensional Error (95) ŌåÆ Paint Defect (80) ŌåÆ Weld Crack (50) ŌåÆ Missing Component (30) ŌåÆ Other (25). ### 3.1.2 Step 2 ŌĆō Compute Cumulative Frequency For each category $k$, we calculate: $$C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$$ Executing step by step: $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= n_1 = 220 \\[4pt] C_2 &= n_1 + n_2 = 220 + 95 = 315 \\[4pt] C_3 &= C_2 + n_3 = 315 + 80 = 395 \\[4pt] C_4 &= C_3 + n_4 = 395 + 50 = 445 \\[4pt] C_5 &= C_4 + n_5 = 445 + 30 = 475 \\[4pt] C_6 &= C_5 + n_6 = 475 + 25 = 500 \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity Check:** $C_6 = 500$ matches $\sum n_i = 500$. Ō£ō ### 3.1.3 Step 3 ŌĆō Compute Cumulative Percentage Using the formula: $$P_k = \dfrac{C_k}{\sum n_i} \times 100$$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= \frac{220}{500} \times 100 = 44.00\% \\[4pt] P_2 &= \frac{315}{500} \times 100 = 63.00\% \\[4pt] P_3 &= \frac{395}{500} \times 100 = 79.00\% \\[4pt] P_4 &= \frac{445}{500} \times 100 = 89.00\% \\[4pt] P_5 &= \frac{475}{500} \times 100 = 95.00\% \\[4pt] P_6 &= \frac{500}{500} \times 100 = 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 ŌĆō Final Analytical Matrix | Rank | Defect Type | Frequency (nßĄó) | Cumulative Frequency (CŌé¢) | Cumulative % (PŌé¢) | |:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Surface Scratch | 220 | 220 | 44.00% | | 2 | Dimensional Error | 95 | 315 | 63.00% | | 3 | Paint Defect | 80 | 395 | **79.00%** | | 4 | Weld Crack | 50 | 445 | 89.00% | | 5 | Missing Component | 30 | 475 | 95.00% | | 6 | Other | 25 | 500 | 100.00% | ### 3.1.5 Step 5 ŌĆō Identification of "Vital Few" > [!IMPORTANT] > Reading the Cumulative % column: by the end of **Rank 3 (Paint Defect)**, the cumulative percentage is **79%**, which is approximately the **80% mark** on the Pareto Curve. > > **Conclusion:** The **"Vital Few"** are: > 1. Surface Scratch > 2. Dimensional Error > 3. Paint Defect > > The firm should focus its quality improvement resources on these **3 categories (50% of categories)** to eliminate approximately **79% of all defects.** The remaining 3 categories form the **"Useful Many."** ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison: Before vs. After Pareto | Aspect | Without Pareto Chart | With Pareto Chart | |---|---|---| | Resource Allocation | Spread equally across all defects | Focused on "vital few" | | Decision Time | Long, unfocused | Quick, data-driven | | Improvement Yield | Marginal | High (Pareto-optimal) | | Management Reporting | Confusing | Visually intuitive | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆō Pareto Chart Construction Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Identify Problem] --> B[Select Defect Categories] B --> C[Collect Frequency Data] C --> D[Rank in Descending Order] D --> E[Compute Cumulative Frequency Ck] E --> F[Compute Cumulative Percentage Pk] F --> G[Plot Bar Chart on Left Y-Axis] G --> H[Plot Cumulative Curve on Right Y-Axis] H --> I[Draw 80% Reference Line] I --> J[Identify Vital Few vs Useful Many] J --> K[Recommend Focused Improvement Actions] K --> L[End: Decision Output] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆō Pareto Chart Structural Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LEFT[Left Y-Axis Domain] Y1[Frequency / Cost Scale] end subgraph MID[Main Plotting Area] B1[Bar 1 - Tallest] B2[Bar 2] B3[Bar 3] B4[Bar 4] B5[Bar 5] B6[Bar 6 - Shortest] L1((Point 1)) L2((Point 2)) L3((Point 3)) L4((Point 4)) L5((Point 5)) L6((Point 6)) C80[80% Reference Line] end subgraph RIGHT[Right Y-Axis Domain] Y2[Cumulative Percentage 0 to 100] end subgraph BOT[Bottom X-Axis] X1[Category Labels in Descending Order] end Y1 --> MID MID --> Y2 BOT --> MID L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 C80 -.cuts.-> MID style LEFT fill:#FFFACD style RIGHT fill:#E0FFFF style MID fill:#F5F5DC style BOT fill:#FFE4E1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Cause-Effect Mapping for Pareto Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Pareto Chart Output] --> VF{Vital Few Region
Left of 80% Mark} P --> UM{Useful Many Region
Right of 80% Mark} VF --> A1[Concentrate Resources Here] VF --> A2[Root Cause Analysis - 5 Whys] VF --> A3[Immediate Corrective Action] VF --> A4[Monitor Closely in PDCA Check] UM --> B1[Lower Priority] UM --> B2[Group into Other Category] UM --> B3[Periodic Review Only] style VF fill:#FF6347,color:#fff style UM fill:#4682B4,color:#fff style A1 fill:#FFA07A style A2 fill:#FFA07A style A3 fill:#FFA07A style A4 fill:#FFA07A style B1 fill:#B0C4DE style B2 fill:#B0C4DE style B3 fill:#B0C4DE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Note:** The "vital few" region is where the curve crosses the 80% mark. In the worked example, this occurs at the **3rd bar (Paint Defect)**, indicating that the first three categories deserve top priority. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆō Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Question]`** **Q:** State the **Pareto Principle**. Briefly explain the concept of the **"Vital Few"** and **"Useful Many"** with an example. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Pareto Principle**, also known as the **80/20 Rule**, was proposed by Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto**. It states that approximately **80% of the effects** (problems, defects, costs) are caused by only **20% of the causes** (categories). - **Vital Few:** The small number of categories that contribute to the majority of the problem. *Example: In a manufacturing unit, 3 out of 10 defect types (30%) may account for 80% of total rejections.* - **Useful Many:** The remaining categories that have a relatively minor individual impact, but together form a noticeable portion. **Key Takeaway:** Quality managers focus on the **vital few** for maximum improvement impact. **(3 Marks)** --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question]`** **Q:** List any **six steps** involved in the construction of a **Pareto Chart**. **Model Answer (3 Marks) ŌĆō Any 6 of the following:** 1. Identify the problem and the categories to be analyzed. 2. Collect data on frequency (or cost) for each category. 3. Tabulate the frequencies. 4. Rank categories in **descending order** of frequency. 5. Compute **cumulative frequency** and **cumulative percentage** for each rank. 6. Plot bars (frequency on the left Y-axis, categories on the X-axis). 7. Overlay the **cumulative percentage curve** on the right Y-axis (0ŌĆō100%). 8. Mark the **80% reference line** and identify the vital few. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆō Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice) > **Note:** KTU ESE follows a **choice-based pattern**. The following two questions are mutually exclusive alternatives for a single 14-mark slot. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024 Model ŌĆō Choice 1]`** **(a)** Define a **Pareto Chart**. Explain its **purpose, structure, and construction** in detail with a labeled diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A call center recorded the following reasons for **customer complaints in a week**: Busy Line: 180, Rude Staff: 120, Delayed Response: 90, Wrong Information: 60, Others: 50. Construct the **Pareto table** and identify the **"Vital Few."** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) ŌĆō Definition, Purpose, Structure, Construction (7 Marks) - **Definition:** A Pareto Chart is a graphical tool combining a **descending bar chart** and a **cumulative percentage line graph**, used to identify the most significant causes of a problem. - **Purpose:** - To prioritize problems based on frequency/cost. - To focus improvement efforts on the "vital few." - To support decision-making in TQM and Six Sigma projects. - **Structure:** - **X-axis:** Categories in descending order of frequency. - **Left Y-axis (YŌéü):** Frequency or cost (numerical scale). - **Right Y-axis (YŌéé):** Cumulative percentage (0% to 100%). - **Bars:** Represent individual category frequency. - **Curve:** Represents cumulative percentage. - **Construction Steps:** (Write any 5ŌĆō6 from the 8-step list given in Section 2.2.) - **Diagram:** Students should draw a labeled schematic with bars in descending order and an overlaid cumulative line touching 100% at the last bar. **(7 Marks ŌĆō 2 for definition/purpose, 2 for structure/diagram, 3 for construction steps)** #### Part (b) ŌĆō Worked Pareto Table (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆō Total Frequency:** $$N = 180 + 120 + 90 + 60 + 50 = 500$$ **Step 2 ŌĆō Compute Cumulative Frequency and Percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= 180,\quad P_1 = \frac{180}{500} \times 100 = 36.00\% \\[4pt] C_2 &= 180+120 = 300,\quad P_2 = \frac{300}{500} \times 100 = 60.00\% \\[4pt] C_3 &= 300+90 = 390,\quad P_3 = \frac{390}{500} \times 100 = 78.00\% \\[4pt] C_4 &= 390+60 = 450,\quad P_4 = \frac{450}{500} \times 100 = 90.00\% \\[4pt] C_5 &= 450+50 = 500,\quad P_5 = \frac{500}{500} \times 100 = 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Pareto Table:** | Rank | Complaint Type | Frequency | Cum. Frequency | Cum. % | |:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Busy Line | 180 | 180 | 36.00% | | 2 | Rude Staff | 120 | 300 | 60.00% | | 3 | Delayed Response | 90 | 390 | **78.00%** | | 4 | Wrong Information | 60 | 450 | 90.00% | | 5 | Others | 50 | 500 | 100.00% | **Identification of Vital Few:** The cumulative percentage crosses the **80% mark between Rank 3 and Rank 4.** Therefore, the **Vital Few** are: **(1) Busy Line, (2) Rude Staff, (3) Delayed Response.** These three categories account for **78% of all complaints** from just 60% of the categories. The call center should focus retraining and staffing on these three issues. **(7 Marks ŌĆō 2 for totals, 3 for cumulative calculations, 2 for vital few identification and conclusion)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024 Model ŌĆō Choice 2]`** **(a)** Differentiate between the **Pareto Principle** and the **PDCA Cycle**. How does the Pareto Chart integrate with PDCA? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An automobile service center received **warranty claims** due to the following defects: Brake Failure: 70, Engine Overheating: 55, Electrical Fault: 45, Transmission Slip: 20, Suspension Noise: 10. Construct the **Pareto Chart analysis** and recommend **two improvement strategies.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) ŌĆō Pareto Principle vs. PDCA Cycle (7 Marks) | Aspect | Pareto Principle | PDCA Cycle | |---|---|---| | Origin | Vilfredo Pareto (1897) | Walter Shewhart / W. Edwards Deming | | Type | Statistical observation principle | Iterative 4-step management method | | Focus | Identifying the most significant causes | Continuously improving processes | | Output | Prioritization of issues | Standardization of improvements | | Phases | Single concept | Plan ŌåÆ Do ŌåÆ Check ŌåÆ Act | **Integration with PDCA:** - **Plan Phase:** Construct the Pareto Chart to identify the **vital few** problems to tackle. - **Do Phase:** Implement corrective actions targeting the vital few. - **Check Phase:** Reconstruct the Pareto Chart after the action to verify whether the dominant categories have changed or reduced. - **Act Phase:** If successful, standardize the solution; if not, return to Plan and re-analyze. Thus, the Pareto Chart acts as a **diagnostic and verification tool** within the **PDCA framework.** **(7 Marks ŌĆō 3 for comparison table, 4 for PDCA integration explanation)** #### Part (b) ŌĆō Automobile Warranty Claims Pareto Analysis (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆō Total:** $$N = 70 + 55 + 45 + 20 + 10 = 200$$ **Step 2 ŌĆō Cumulative Calculations:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= 70,\quad P_1 = \frac{70}{200} \times 100 = 35.00\% \\[4pt] C_2 &= 125,\quad P_2 = \frac{125}{200} \times 100 = 62.50\% \\[4pt] C_3 &= 170,\quad P_3 = \frac{170}{200} \times 100 = 85.00\% \\[4pt] C_4 &= 190,\quad P_4 = \frac{190}{200} \times 100 = 95.00\% \\[4pt] C_5 &= 200,\quad P_5 = 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ **Pareto Table:** | Rank | Defect | Frequency | Cum. Freq. | Cum. % | |:---:|---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Brake Failure | 70 | 70 | 35.00% | | 2 | Engine Overheating | 55 | 125 | 62.50% | | 3 | Electrical Fault | 45 | 170 | **85.00%** | | 4 | Transmission Slip | 20 | 190 | 95.00% | | 5 | Suspension Noise | 10 | 200 | 100.00% | **Vital Few:** The first **3 categories** (Brake Failure, Engine Overheating, Electrical Fault) cross the 80% mark at **85%** cumulatively. **Two Improvement Strategies:** 1. **Brake System Overhaul:** Conduct a root-cause analysis (5 Whys / Fishbone) on brake failures; upgrade brake pad material supplier; introduce 100% brake-testing on every vehicle pre-delivery. 2. **Engine Cooling & Electrical Audit:** Revise coolant quality checks and recalibrate ECU firmware; perform mandatory pre-delivery electrical diagnostics on every unit. **(7 Marks ŌĆō 2 for cumulative calculations, 2 for table and vital few, 3 for strategies with justification)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions (KTU Examiner Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting to rank in descending order** ŌĆō Bars must be sorted from tallest to shortest; random order costs **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing the two Y-axes** ŌĆō The **left axis is frequency**, the **right axis is cumulative % (0ŌĆō100).** Reversing them is a frequently penalized error. > 3. **Skipping cumulative % calculation** ŌĆō Just drawing bars is incomplete; the **cumulative curve is mandatory** for a valid Pareto Chart. > 4. **Not identifying the vital few** ŌĆō A Pareto Chart without interpretation scores **zero** on the analysis part. > 5. **Treating 80/20 as rigid** ŌĆō The principle is a **rule of thumb**, not a mathematical law. Stating "it must be exactly 80%" will lose marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆō Pareto Chart** - Ō£ģ **Inventor:** Vilfredo Pareto (1897) ŌĆō Italian economist. - Ō£ģ **Core Idea:** **80% of problems are caused by 20% of causes** (rule of thumb). - Ō£ģ **Tool Category:** One of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality**. - Ō£ģ **Chart Type:** **Dual-axis** ŌĆō bars (left axis) + cumulative line (right axis). - Ō£ģ **X-axis:** Categories in **descending order of frequency**. - Ō£ģ **Left Y-axis:** **Frequency or cost** of each category. - Ō£ģ **Right Y-axis:** **Cumulative percentage** ranging from **0% to 100%.** - Ō£ģ **Vital Few:** Categories contributing to **~80% of the problem** (left of the 80% line). - Ō£ģ **Useful Many:** Categories contributing to the **remaining ~20%** (right of the 80% line). - Ō£ģ **Key Formulae:** - Cumulative Frequency: $C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ - Cumulative Percentage: $P_k = \dfrac{C_k}{\sum n_i} \times 100$ - Ō£ģ **Usage in PDCA:** Used in **Plan** (problem prioritization) and **Check** (verifying improvement) phases. - Ō£ģ **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** Construction steps, cumulative % table, vital few identification, and integration with PDCA. - Ō£ģ **Common Errors:** Unsorted bars, wrong axis assignment, missing cumulative line, no interpretation, treating 80/20 as exact. ---

topics.title: Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Histogram** is a type of bar chart that graphically represents the **frequency distribution of a continuous dataset** by organizing data into successive, non-overlapping numerical intervals called **bins** (or **classes**). Each bar's height is proportional to the number of observations (frequency) that fall within that specific interval. Histograms are one of the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality** identified by **Kaoru Ishikawa**, and they are universally applied in **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** to visualize the inherent variability of a manufacturing or service process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> A Histogram is a vertical bar-chart representation of a frequency distribution in which the **class intervals are plotted along the X-axis** and the **frequencies (counts) are plotted along the Y-axis**, with adjacent bars touching each other (unlike a bar chart) because the variable under study is *continuous*, not categorical.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Bucket Analogy"

Imagine a long pipeline carrying thousands of **identical metal balls** of slightly different sizes flowing past a quality-inspection station. Instead of inspecting each ball, the inspector lines up **transparent buckets** (these are your *bins*). Each bucket covers a size range ŌĆö for example, *Bucket 1* holds balls between 19.95 mm and 20.00 mm, *Bucket 2* holds 20.00 mm to 20.05 mm, and so on. After the run, the inspector simply **stacks the buckets and looks at how tall each pile is**. Tall piles mean many balls of that size; short piles mean few.

That **visual "skyline of piles"** IS the histogram. The wider the base of any pile, the broader that size range; the taller the pile, the more frequent that size. The **shape of the skyline** tells the quality engineer whether the process is centered, drifting, or showing strange multi-modal patterns ŌĆö all without doing any heavy math.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Touching Bars" Matter:**
> In a *bar chart*, bars are separated (gaps) because the categories are discrete (e.g., Male / Female). In a *histogram*, bars must touch because the variable is **continuous** ŌĆö there is no gap between 20.01 mm and 20.02 mm; it is the same physical dimension. This single visual cue differentiates a histogram from a bar chart in KTU examination questions.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations

| Term | Standard Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Class Interval (Bin)** | $[L_i, L_{i+1})$ | A continuous numeric range that bounds a group of data. |
| **Class Width** | $h$ | The constant size of every bin across the histogram. |
| **Class Mark (Midpoint)** | $m_i$ | The center value of a bin: $m_i = (L_i + L_{i+1})/2$. |
| **Frequency** | $f_i$ | The raw count of observations falling inside bin $i$. |
| **Relative Frequency** | $r_i$ | The proportion of data in bin $i$: $r_i = f_i / N$. |
| **Cumulative Frequency** | $F_i$ | The running total of frequencies up to and including bin $i$. |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Mnemonic ŌĆö "C-L-M-F-R-C"**: *Class ŌåÆ Limits ŌåÆ Midpoint ŌåÆ Frequency ŌåÆ Relative ŌåÆ Cumulative.* These are the six mandatory columns of any KTU-asked histogram construction table.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic ŌĆö Six Operational Steps

A histogram is **not** drawn by simply plotting data; it is constructed by transforming raw observations into an organized frequency table first. Below is the rigorous, ordered logic KTU examiners expect.

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Collect a Representative Sample:** Record a minimum of **N Ōēź 50** observations (industrial best practice is **N Ōēź 100**) of the quality characteristic. The sample must be **random and stable**, taken from a process operating under steady conditions.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Determine the Range (R):** Compute the spread of the dataset.
$$R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$$
where $X_{\max}$ and $X_{\min}$ are the largest and smallest observations in the sample.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Choose the Number of Classes (k):** Apply the empirical **Sturges' Rule** (the KTU-preferred formula):
$$k = 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$
Round $k$ to the nearest convenient integer (typically between **5 and 15** for SPC applications). For very large samples ($N > 200$), use the more refined **Rice Rule**: $k = 2 \cdot N^{1/3}$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Class Width (h):** Divide the range by the number of classes and round **up** to a measurement-friendly value.
$$h = \lceil R / k \rceil$$
The ceiling function ensures **no data is lost** at the boundaries.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Tally Frequencies into Bins:** Sort each observation into its corresponding $[L_i, L_{i+1})$ bin. Tabulate $f_i$, $r_i$, and $F_i$.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Plot the Histogram:** Draw the X-axis from $L_1$ to $L_{k+1}$, the Y-axis from $0$ to $\max(f_i)$, and erect adjacent bars of constant width $h$ with height $f_i$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-Friendly Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable.** Memorize the units, conditions, and the *rounding* behavior of $h$.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Range | Same as data unit (e.g., mm) |
| 2 | $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10} N$ | Number of classes (Sturges) | Dimensionless; round to int |
| 3 | $h = \lceil R / k \rceil$ | Class width | Round **up** to convenient value |
| 4 | $m_i = (L_i + L_{i+1})/2$ | Class midpoint | Same as data unit |
| 5 | $r_i = f_i / N$ | Relative frequency | $0 \le r_i \le 1$ |
| 6 | $F_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$ | Cumulative frequency | Monotonically increasing |
| 7 | $\bar{X}_{\text{approx}} = \dfrac{\sum f_i \cdot m_i}{N}$ | Approximate mean from histogram | Same as data unit |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{approx}} = h \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum f_i u_i^2}{N} - \left(\dfrac{\sum f_i u_i}{N}\right)^2}$ | Approximate std. dev. | Where $u_i = (m_i - A)/h$ (coded) |

## 2.3 Why Histograms Are Indispensable in Quality Engineering

- **Process Capability Insight:** Overlaying specification limits (LSL, USL) on a histogram instantly reveals the process spread versus the tolerance window.
- **Communication:** Histograms provide a **picture of variability** that is comprehensible to floor operators, managers, and customers alike ŌĆö no statistical background required.
- **Pre-Control Diagnostic:** Before deploying SPC charts (X-bar, R-chart), engineers use histograms to confirm the process is **stable and approximately normal** ŌĆö a KTU-favoured sub-question in Module 4.
- **Shape Diagnosis:** Specific shapes of histograms (skewed, bimodal, edge-peaked) directly point to specific assignable causes ŌĆö making the histogram a *diagnostic*, not just a descriptive, tool.

## 2.4 Histogram vs. Bar Chart ŌĆö The Definitive Distinction

| Feature | Histogram | Bar Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Variable type | **Continuous** | Categorical / Discrete |
| Bars touch? | **Yes** (mandatory) | No (gaps allowed) |
| X-axis | Numerical intervals | Discrete labels |
| Re-ordering bars? | **No** (order fixed by value) | Yes (any order works) |
| Gap between bars | Only when class is empty | Always present |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark Loss:** Students frequently label the X-axis as "Class" and Y-axis as "Count" without specifying the *quality characteristic* and its unit. Always write, for example, **"Diameter (mm)"** on X and **"Frequency (count)"** on Y.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important sub-question in KTU Module 4** is *"Construct a histogram for the given dataset."* The fully-worked solution below uses an authentic, KTU-style quality engineering dataset. **No steps are skipped** ŌĆö examiners can award method marks at any stage.

## 3.1 The Problem Statement

A CNC turning cell produces steel shafts whose **outer diameter** is the critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristic. The **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)** is **19.90 mm** and the **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** is **20.10 mm**. The Quality Engineer recorded **N = 50** consecutive diameter measurements (in mm):

```
20.02, 19.98, 20.05, 20.01, 19.96, 20.04, 20.00, 19.99, 20.03, 20.02,
19.97, 20.01, 20.05, 19.98, 20.00, 20.02, 19.99, 20.03, 20.01, 19.96,
20.04, 20.02, 19.98, 20.00, 20.03, 20.01, 19.99, 20.05, 20.02, 19.97,
20.00, 20.04, 20.01, 19.98, 20.03, 20.02, 19.99, 20.00, 20.05, 20.01,
19.97, 20.03, 20.02, 19.98, 20.04, 20.00, 20.01, 20.03, 19.99, 20.02
```

**Tasks (typical 14-mark KTU question):**
- (a) Construct a frequency distribution table using **6 classes**.
- (b) Plot the histogram, and comment on the **process capability** with respect to LSL/USL.

## 3.2 Solution to Part (a) ŌĆö Exhaustive Frequency Table Construction

### Step A1 ŌĆö Identify Min, Max, and Range

Scanning the dataset, we identify:
$$X_{\min} = 19.96 \text{ mm}, \quad X_{\max} = 20.05 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore the **Range** is:
$$R = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 20.05 - 19.96 = 0.09 \text{ mm}$$

### Step A2 ŌĆö Verify the Class Count (k = 6, given)

We check this against **Sturges' Rule** for $N = 50$:
$$k_{\text{Sturges}} = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(50) = 1 + 3.322 \times 1.6990 = 1 + 5.6439 = 6.644 \approx 7$$

The problem specifies **k = 6**, which is acceptable and gives a slightly wider bin ŌĆö perfectly within engineering tolerance.

### Step A3 ŌĆö Compute the Class Width (h)

$$h = \lceil R / k \rceil = \lceil 0.09 / 6 \rceil = \lceil 0.015 \rceil = 0.015 \text{ mm}$$

### Step A4 ŌĆö Establish the Class Limits (Begin Slightly Below the Minimum)

We anchor the **first lower limit** at $L_1 = 19.95$ mm (one $h$-unit below $X_{\min}$ to give a clean starting point) and add $h$ repeatedly.

### Step A5 ŌĆö Tally Frequencies (Full 6-Class Frequency Table)

| Class # | Class Interval (mm) | Midpoint $m_i$ | Tally (showing last 5 of 50) | Frequency $f_i$ | Relative $r_i$ | Cumulative $F_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[19.95, 19.965)$ | $19.9575$ | \||\||  | 2 | 0.04 | 2 |
| 2 | $[19.965, 19.98)$ | $19.9725$ | \||\|| \|| | 5 | 0.10 | 7 |
| 3 | $[19.98, 19.995)$ | $19.9875$ | \||\|| \||\||  | 8 | 0.16 | 15 |
| 4 | $[19.995, 20.01)$ | $20.0025$ | \||\|| \||\||\||  | 12 | 0.24 | 27 |
| 5 | $[20.01, 20.025)$ | $20.0175$ | \||\|| \||\||\||  | 13 | 0.26 | 40 |
| 6 | $[20.025, 20.04)$ | $20.0325$ | \||\|| \||\||  | 7 | 0.14 | 47 |
| ŌĆö | $[20.04, 20.055]$ | $20.0475$ | \||\|| | 3 | 0.06 | 50 |
| **Total** | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | **$\sum f_i = 50$** | **1.00** | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check (Mandatory in KTU solutions):** $\sum f_i = 2 + 5 + 8 + 12 + 13 + 7 + 3 = 50 = N$ Ō£ō
> Also $\sum r_i = 0.04 + 0.10 + 0.16 + 0.24 + 0.26 + 0.14 + 0.06 = 1.00$ Ō£ō
> **Any mismatch loses 1 mark.**

### Step A6 ŌĆö Approximate Process Mean (Bonus Examiner Credit)

Using the grouped-data formula:
$$\bar{X}_{\text{approx}} = \frac{\sum f_i \cdot m_i}{N}$$

Computing the numerator:
$$\sum f_i \cdot m_i = (2)(19.9575) + (5)(19.9725) + (8)(19.9875) + (12)(20.0025)$$
$$+ (13)(20.0175) + (7)(20.0325) + (3)(20.0475)$$
$$= 39.9150 + 99.8625 + 159.9000 + 240.0300 + 260.2275 + 140.2275 + 60.1425$$
$$= 1000.3050$$

Therefore:
$$\bar{X}_{\text{approx}} = \frac{1000.3050}{50} = 20.0061 \text{ mm}$$

The process mean lies very close to the **target** of $\dfrac{19.90 + 20.10}{2} = 20.00$ mm ŌĆö excellent centering.

## 3.3 Solution to Part (b) ŌĆö Histogram Plot and Process Capability Interpretation

### Step B1 ŌĆö Drawing the Histogram

The X-axis spans **19.95 mm to 20.055 mm** in equal $h = 0.015$ mm steps; the Y-axis spans **0 to 13** (the maximum frequency). Each bar is a **closed rectangle** of width $h$ and height $f_i$, with bars touching along the X-axis (left half-open interval notation $[L, L+h)$).

| Interval (mm) | Bar Height (count) |
|---|---|
| 19.95 ŌĆō 19.965 | Ō¢īŌ¢ī (2) |
| 19.965 ŌĆō 19.98 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (5) |
| 19.98 ŌĆō 19.995 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (8) |
| 19.995 ŌĆō 20.01 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (12) |
| 20.01 ŌĆō 20.025 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (13) |
| 20.025 ŌĆō 20.04 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (7) |
| 20.04 ŌĆō 20.055 | Ō¢īŌ¢īŌ¢ī (3) |

### Step B2 ŌĆö Specifying Process Capability (Verbal Analysis)

Observing the histogram against the **LSL = 19.90** and **USL = 20.10** specification lines:

- **No observation falls below LSL or above USL.** All 50 measurements lie *comfortably inside* the specification window ŌĆö visual confirmation of **100 % conformance in this sample**.
- The distribution appears **bell-shaped and approximately symmetric**, with a slight peak just to the right of the centre. This is consistent with a process operating under **common-cause variation only** (statistically stable).
- The **process is well-centred** (mean $\approx 20.006$ mm vs. midpoint 20.000 mm) and **has ample margin** to the specification limits (a buffer of $\sim 0.094$ mm on each side versus a $6\sigma$ of approximately $\sim 0.03$ mm).

### Step B3 ŌĆö Verdict

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner-Ready Conclusion (Write this verbatim in your KTU answer book):**
> "The histogram exhibits a roughly **normal, single-peaked** shape fully contained within the specification limits. The process is **capable, centred, and stable**. The machine is operating at its natural design limits but with sufficient tolerance cushion; no assignable cause is suspected from the histogram alone. Continued monitoring via an **X-bar / R chart** is recommended."

### Step B4 ŌĆö Decision Tree for Histogram Shape Diagnosis

| Observed Shape | Likely Cause | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal (Bell)** | Process in statistical control | Continue SPC; compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ |
| **Skewed (Right)** | Tool wear, one-sided tolerance | Implement preventive tool replacement |
| **Bimodal (Two Peaks)** | Two different machines / materials mixed | Investigate lot segregation |
| **Edge-Peaked** | Operator adjustment to limits | Audit work-instruction compliance |
| **Plateau / Comb** | Incorrect bin width, rounding of data | Reconstruct with $h$ adjusted |
| **Truncated** | Inspection gauge rejecting out-of-spec parts | Check 100 %-vs-sampling inspection policy |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid does not natively render bar-chart histograms with proportional widths. The diagram below presents the **Functional Construction Workflow** (the decision-flow that a quality engineer follows) and a **Block Architecture** mapping every component of a histogram and its diagnostic outputs. Both are standard KTU Module-4 answer-sketches.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö Histogram Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Raw Dataset of N observations]) --> stepB[Step 1: Identify Xmax and Xmin]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Compute Range R = Xmax minus Xmin]
    stepC --> stepD{Step 3: Number of classes k given or apply Sturges Rule}
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Compute class width h equals ceiling of R divided by k]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Set class limits Li starting at L1 less than Xmin]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Tally each observation into its bin]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Build frequency table with fi, ri, Fi]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Plot bars on X axis of width h and Y axis of height fi]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Overlay LSL and USL specification lines]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Diagnose shape and conclude on process capability]
    stepK --> stepL([End: Histogram and Interpretation Report])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Block Architecture of a Histogram (Inputs ŌåÆ Engine ŌåÆ Outputs)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        A1[Raw measurements N ge 50]
        A2[Specification limits LSL and USL]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[CONSTRUCTION ENGINE]
        B1[Range R]
        B2[Class Count k]
        B3[Class Width h]
        B4[Frequency Tally fi]
        B5[Midpoint mi]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        C1[Histogram Plot]
        C2[Frequency Table]
        C3[Approximate mean Xbar]
        C4[Approximate sigma]
    end

    subgraph DIAG[DIAGNOSTIC LAYER]
        D1[Shape Classification]
        D2[Process Capability Verdict]
        D3[Action Plan]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    A1 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    A2 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> B5
    B4 --> C3
    B5 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Block Schematic ŌĆö The "Five Anatomy" of a Histogram (Text-Only Skeleton for Exam Sheet)

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|  TITLE: Histogram of <CTQ characteristic> (n = N)             |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              ^|
|   Y-   |                                                     |Y|
|   A-   |              [Bar 5]   [Bar 6]                      |-A
|   X-   |         [Bar 4]                                     |-X
|   I-   |   [Bar 3]                                            |-I
|   S-   |[Bar 2]                                              |-S
|       |[B 1]                                                  |
|       +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+    |
|         L1   L2   L3   L4   L5   L6   L7 (class limits)     |
|       <---- X-AXIS: Class Interval (continuous variable) ---> |
|                                                              |
|       |----LSL-------|               |----USL-------|         |
|       (vertical dashed specification lines, optional)        |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
        (Bars MUST touch; no gaps; constant width h)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question patterns below mirror the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** guidelines for OEIET612 ŌĆö Quality Management. Marks are calibrated to the official scheme: **Part A (3 marks)**, **Part B (14 marks with sub-choices)**. All questions are freshly constructed; they are not memory-quoted from any past paper, but the cognitive levels (L1ŌĆōL4) and difficulty distribution match the December 2023 / 2024 KTU pattern.

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Define a Histogram. State any two situations in which a histogram is preferred over other Seven Tools of Quality.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Histogram is a graphical representation of a frequency distribution of a **continuous** dataset, in which the data are grouped into adjacent, non-overlapping intervals (bins) plotted along the X-axis, with the frequency of observations in each bin plotted as adjacent vertical bars along the Y-axis. (2 marks)
> *Two situations:* (i) To study the **inherent variability** of a manufacturing process before deploying control charts; (ii) To **communicate process capability** to non-statistical audiences such as floor operators or customers, by overlaying specification limits. (1 mark for any two valid points)

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Histogram and a Pareto Chart. State one engineering application of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Histogram** displays the *frequency* of a continuous variable across numeric intervals, with bars touching and ordered strictly by value. A **Pareto Chart** displays the *frequency of categorical defects* (or causes) in **descending order of magnitude**, with a superimposed cumulative-percentage curve (Pareto principle ŌĆö 80/20 rule). (2 marks)
> *Application of Histogram:* Studying the diameter distribution of turned shafts against LSL/USL. *Application of Pareto Chart:* Ranking customer complaints by defect type to prioritise corrective action. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal-Choice Mandate:** KTU 2024 ESE awards the student the right to pick **one** of two parallel questions. Both questions below carry **identical marks (14)**, cover **the same module-level CO**, and require the **same cognitive depth** ŌĆö they are interchangeable in difficulty.

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]`

**(a) "The histogram is the most under-utilized yet most informative of the Seven Basic Tools of Quality." Discuss this statement with reference to the construction steps, interpretable shapes, and engineering utility of a histogram. (7 marks)**

**(b) The following data represent the tensile strength (in MPa) of 60 aluminium alloy specimens tested in a QC lab. Construct a frequency distribution table using 7 classes, draw the histogram, and comment on the process distribution shape. (7 marks)**

| Specimen Strength Data (MPa) |
|---|
| 248, 252, 255, 250, 253, 257, 251, 254, 256, 252, 249, 253, 255, 258, 251, 254, 252, 256, 250, 253, 255, 257, 252, 254, 251, 256, 253, 255, 254, 252, 250, 257, 253, 255, 251, 256, 254, 252, 255, 253, 257, 251, 254, 256, 252, 255, 253, 250, 258, 254, 252, 255, 251, 256, 253, 254, 255, 252, 257 |

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(a) [7 marks]

1. **(2 marks) Construction steps:** Identify min/max, compute Range $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$, decide $k$ (Sturges' or given), compute $h = \lceil R/k \rceil$, define class limits $[L_i, L_{i+1})$ starting at or below $X_{\min}$, tally frequencies $f_i$, draw adjacent bars.
2. **(3 marks) Interpretable shapes with assignable-cause mapping:** Normal (common-cause, capable); Skewed-right (tool wear); Skewed-left (gauge-bias or incoming-material hardness); Bimodal (two populations, e.g., mixed lots); Edge-peaked (operator over-control to limits); Plateau (incorrect bin width); Comb (data-rounding effect); Truncated (inspection-rejection bias).
3. **(2 marks) Engineering utility:** Pre-control diagnostic before X-bar / R-chart, communication of process spread to non-statistical stakeholders, estimation of approximate $\bar{X}$ and $\sigma$ for capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, root-cause input for fishbone / 5-Why analysis.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** $X_{\min} = 248$ MPa, $X_{\max} = 258$ MPa, $R = 10$ MPa.
**Step 2:** $h = \lceil 10/7 \rceil = 2$ MPa.
**Step 3:** Classes starting at 248 (in 2 MPa steps): [248,250), [250,252), [252,254), [254,256), [256,258), [258,260). (1 mark)
**Step 4:** Tallied frequency table (verified by sum = 60):

| Class (MPa) | $f_i$ |
|---|---|
| 248 ŌĆō 250 | 3 |
| 250 ŌĆō 252 | 9 |
| 252 ŌĆō 254 | 16 |
| 254 ŌĆō 256 | 19 |
| 256 ŌĆō 258 | 11 |
| 258 ŌĆō 260 | 2 |
| **Total** | **60** Ō£ō |

**Step 5:** Histogram drawn with bars of constant width 2 MPa, height equal to $f_i$. (2 marks)
**Step 6:** Shape comment ŌĆö single-peaked, **approximately symmetric, bell-shaped** (mild central peak around 254ŌĆō256 MPa with tails on both sides). The distribution is **consistent with a normal process under common-cause variation**, indicating a **stable and capable** alloy-strength property. (1 mark for shape, 1 mark for engineering comment)

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]`

**(a) Explain the role of a histogram in Statistical Process Control (SPC). How does the interpretation of a histogram complement the use of X-bar and R-charts? (7 marks)**

**(b) A bottle-filling line fills 500 ml beverage bottles. A random sample of 80 bottles is drawn, and the actual fill volumes (in ml) are summarized below in 8 pre-computed classes. Verify the frequency totals, compute the relative and cumulative frequencies, draw the histogram, and assess the process with respect to a target of 500 ml and an allowable tolerance of ┬▒5 ml. (7 marks)**

| Class (ml) | Frequency $f_i$ |
|---|---|
| 492 ŌĆō 494 | 3 |
| 494 ŌĆō 496 | 7 |
| 496 ŌĆō 498 | 14 |
| 498 ŌĆō 500 | 22 |
| 500 ŌĆō 502 | 20 |
| 502 ŌĆō 504 | 9 |
| 504 ŌĆō 506 | 4 |
| 506 ŌĆō 508 | 1 |

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(a) [7 marks]

1. **(3 marks) Role of Histogram in SPC:** The histogram provides a **snapshot of process performance over a period of time**. It is the first diagnostic tool a quality engineer deploys to determine whether the process is in statistical control and approximately normally distributed. If the histogram shows normality, the X-bar chart (for process mean) and R-chart (for process variability) can be confidently constructed with $3\sigma$ control limits. If the histogram reveals non-normality (e.g., bimodal), it signals that the data may originate from multiple populations, demanding root-cause investigation *before* any control chart is computed.
2. **(2 marks) Complementation:** The X-bar / R chart monitors the process *in real time* and flags when a point falls outside control limits, signalling an *assignable cause*. The histogram, in contrast, looks at the **overall pattern of variation** to determine if the spread is consistent with the natural capability of the process, or if it has drifted, broadened, or changed shape. Together, they form a **two-layer** quality system: the histogram sets the *baseline capability*, and the X-bar / R chart *guards* that baseline.
3. **(2 marks) Engineering Significance:** A histogram that is centred and within specification means the process is *capable* ($C_p \ge 1.0$). A X-bar / R chart that stays in control means the process is *consistent*. Only when **both** conditions hold is the process deemed "in statistical control and capable of meeting specifications" ŌĆö the formal definition of a Six-Sigma-quality process.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify Total:** $\sum f_i = 3 + 7 + 14 + 22 + 20 + 9 + 4 + 1 = 80 = N$ Ō£ō (1 mark)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct full table** (1 mark for layout):

| Class (ml) | $f_i$ | $r_i = f_i / 80$ | $F_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 492 ŌĆō 494 | 3 | 0.0375 | 3 |
| 494 ŌĆō 496 | 7 | 0.0875 | 10 |
| 496 ŌĆō 498 | 14 | 0.1750 | 24 |
| 498 ŌĆō 500 | 22 | 0.2750 | 46 |
| 500 ŌĆō 502 | 20 | 0.2500 | 66 |
| 502 ŌĆō 504 | 9 | 0.1125 | 75 |
| 504 ŌĆō 506 | 4 | 0.0500 | 79 |
| 506 ŌĆō 508 | 1 | 0.0125 | 80 |
| **Total** | **80** | **1.0000** | ŌĆö |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Histogram plot:** Bars of constant width 2 ml touching each other; X-axis from 492 to 508 ml; Y-axis from 0 to 22 (max frequency). (2 marks)
**Step 4 ŌĆö Process assessment:** The tolerance window is 495 to 505 ml (500 ┬▒ 5 ml). (1 mark for computation, 1 mark for interpretation)
- Observations **below 495 ml**: $f_1 = 3$ (3.75 % of sample) ŌĆö *underfilled*, potential regulatory issue.
- Observations **above 505 ml**: $f_7 + f_8 = 5$ (6.25 % of sample) ŌĆö *overfilled*, profit loss.
- The distribution is **mildly left-skewed** (longer tail toward 492ŌĆō494 ml), suggesting possible **filling-nozzle wear** or **under-pressure** in the early part of the run.
- **Conclusion:** The process mean is close to target, but the **spread exceeds the ┬▒5 ml tolerance**, yielding an estimated **process capability defect rate of ~10 %**. Maintenance on the filling nozzles is recommended, and an X-bar / R chart should be deployed to monitor stability. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\sum f_i = N$** at the bottom of the table ŌĆö 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Plotting the histogram as a "bar chart" with gaps** between bars ŌĆö examiners treat this as a conceptual error and **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Failing to label the X-axis unit** (e.g., "Diameter (mm)" not just "Diameter") ŌĆö 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Drawing a conclusion without referencing the shape** (e.g., writing only "the process is good" instead of "the histogram is bell-shaped and centred at 20.00 mm, fully within LSL and USL") ŌĆö 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Mixing up the slope of skewness direction** (writing "right-skewed" when the long tail is on the *left*) ŌĆö 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Not overlaying the specification limits (LSL, USL)** when interpretation is required ŌĆö 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition** ŌĆö A Histogram is a bar chart of a **continuous** frequency distribution; bars **must touch**.
- **Seven Tools of Quality origin** ŌĆö Kaoru Ishikawa, Japan (post-WWII statistical quality-control era).
- **Construction pipeline** ŌĆö Collect $N$ ŌåÆ Range $R$ ŌåÆ Class count $k$ (Sturges' $1 + 3.322\log_{10} N$) ŌåÆ Class width $h = \lceil R/k \rceil$ ŌåÆ Class limits $[L_i, L_{i+1})$ ŌåÆ Tally $f_i$ ŌåÆ Plot.
- **Mandatory table columns** ŌĆö Class interval, midpoint $m_i$, frequency $f_i$, relative $r_i$, cumulative $F_i$.
- **Two key sanity checks** ŌĆö $\sum f_i = N$ and $\sum r_i = 1.00$.
- **Shape diagnostics** ŌĆö Normal, Skewed (R/L), Bimodal, Edge-Peaked, Plateau, Comb, Truncated.
- **Approximate $\bar{X}$** ŌĆö $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum f_i m_i}{N}$.
- **Approximate $\sigma$** ŌĆö Use coded midpoints $u_i = (m_i - A)/h$ and apply $\sigma = h \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum f_i u_i^2}{N} - \left(\dfrac{\sum f_i u_i}{N}\right)^2}$.
- **Key distinctions** ŌĆö Histogram (continuous, bars touch) vs. Bar Chart (categorical, bars gapped); Histogram (any order of bar height) vs. Pareto Chart (sorted descending + cumulative curve).
- **Engineering uses** ŌĆö Pre-SPC diagnosis, process capability communication, specification-limit overlay, baseline for $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$.
- **Always include in your KTU answer** ŌĆö Title, axis labels *with units*, LSL/USL overlay, shape comment, process verdict, and an action recommendation.
- **Examiner hot-spot** ŌĆö Whenever the problem says "construct a histogram," you *must* show the full frequency table first; jumping straight to the plot **costs 2ŌĆō3 marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Chart:** A Control Chart is a statistical quality-control tool (developed by **Walter A. Shewhart** in **1924**) that monitors, controls, and improves a process over time by distinguishing between *common cause* (inherent process) variation and *special cause* (assignable) variation. It is a time-sequenced plot of a critical process/quality characteristic with a **Central Line (CL)** representing the process mean, and **Upper and Lower Control Limits (UCL & LCL)** typically set at **┬▒3 standard deviations** ($\pm 3\sigma$) from the mean.

A control chart is essentially a **graphical hypothesis test** of the null hypothesis $H_0$: *"The process is in statistical control."* Each plotted point is a sample statistic ($\bar{X}, R, p, c$, etc.) drawn from a rational subgroup of size $n$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Traffic Speed Dashboard"

Imagine a stretch of highway where the legal speed limit is **80 km/h**. The traffic police set checkpoints:
- **Average legal speed** = Central Line (CL)
- **Speed guns trigger alert if speed crosses 80 ┬▒ 3Žā buffer** = UCL & LCL
- **A car going 95 km/h occasionally** ŌåÆ common cause (everyone speeds a little)
- **A car going 140 km/h or driving on the wrong side** ŌåÆ special cause (drunk driver, accident, police chase) ŌĆö alarm!

Similarly, a control chart acts as the *process police officer*: it tolerates normal randomness but **flags** abnormal disturbances. If every point lies between UCL and LCL in a **random pattern**, the process is **"In-Control" (Stable)**. If points breach limits or form non-random patterns, the process is **"Out-of-Control"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction ŌĆö KTU Board Exam Hotspot:**
> - **Specification Limits (SL)** are defined by the **customer / design / engineering** (what the product *should* be). They represent **voice of the customer**.
> - **Control Limits (CLimits)** are calculated from **process data statistics** ($\pm 3\sigma$). They represent **voice of the process**.

### 1.3 Classification of Control Charts

| Family | Type | Statistic Plotted | Sample Size | Subgroups |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variables Charts** | X╠ä & R chart | Mean and Range | $n \geq 2$ (typically 2ŌĆō10) | Continuous data (length, weight, voltage, diameter) |
| | X╠ä & Žā chart | Mean and Std. Deviation | $n \geq 10$ | Continuous data, larger subgroups |
| | X & R chart (Individuals) | Single observation & Moving Range | $n = 1$ | Slow/expensive measurements |
| **Attributes Charts** | p-chart | Fraction defective | Variable $n$ | Pass/Fail (binary) data |
| | np-chart | Number of defectives | Constant $n$ | Pass/Fail data |
| | c-chart | Number of defects | Constant inspection unit | Count of defects (e.g., scratches) |
| | u-chart | Defects per unit | Variable inspection unit | Non-conformities per unit area/volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Decision Rule ŌĆö Which Chart to Use?**
> 1. If the quality characteristic is **measurable on a continuous scale** ŌåÆ **Variables chart**.
> 2. If the characteristic is a **count** (defects/defectives) ŌåÆ **Attributes chart**.
> 3. If subgroup size $n$ is **constant** ŌåÆ use $\bar{X}\&R$ (or $np, c$).
> 4. If $n$ is **variable** ŌåÆ use $X\&\sigma$, $p$, or $u$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rational Subgroup Concept

A **rational subgroup** is a set of observations (sample of size $n$) selected such that:
- All observations within a subgroup are produced under **homogeneous conditions**.
- Each subgroup represents a **"snapshot"** of the process at a specific point in time.
- Subgroups are chosen to **maximize the chance of detecting variation *between* time periods** while minimizing chance of variation *within* a subgroup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** If subgroups are formed incorrectly (e.g., mixing morning and night shifts in one subgroup), within-subgroup variation inflates $\sigma$, masks real process shifts, and the chart becomes insensitive.

### 2.2 Control Chart Structure ŌĆö Anatomy

Every control chart has three fundamental horizontal reference lines:

1. **Center Line (CL)** = expected value of the statistic under statistical control.
2. **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** = CL + 3Žā of the statistic.
3. **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** = CL ŌłÆ 3Žā of the statistic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL} &= \mu_{\text{stat}} + 3\sigma_{\text{stat}} \\
\text{CL} &= \mu_{\text{stat}} \\
\text{LCL} &= \mu_{\text{stat}} - 3\sigma_{\text{stat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ┬▒3Žā Limit ŌĆö Why 3?**
> Assuming a Normal distribution, the area under the curve outside $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is only **0.27%** (roughly **3.4 defects per million opportunities ŌĆö 3.4 DPMO**). This is the foundation of **Six Sigma**. Choosing 3Žā balances **Type I error** (false alarm) vs. **Type II error** (missed detection).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use the table below as the last-page reference for every numerical problem on Control Charts.**

| # | Chart Type | Center Line (CL) | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **X╠ä chart** (known $\mu, \sigma$) | $\mu$ or $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\mu + A\sigma$ | $\mu - A\sigma$ |
| 2 | **X╠ä chart** (unknown Žā, using R╠ä) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| 3 | **X╠ä chart** (unknown Žā, using S╠ä) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ |
| 4 | **R chart** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |
| 5 | **Žā chart** (using S╠ä) | $\bar{S}$ (or $c_4 \bar{S}$ for $\sigma$) | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ |
| 6 | **p-chart** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ |
| 7 | **np-chart** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |
| 8 | **c-chart** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| 9 | **u-chart** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ |
| 10 | **X-chart (Individuals)** | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + 3\dfrac{\bar{MR}}{d_2}$ | $\bar{X} - 3\dfrac{\bar{MR}}{d_2}$ |
| 11 | **MR-chart (Moving Range)** | $\bar{MR}$ | $D_4 \bar{MR}$ | $D_3 \bar{MR}$ |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Control Limits ŌĆö The Shewhart Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation below is **guaranteed high-yield** for KTU 14-mark problems. Practice writing the full chain of logic.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the sampling distribution.**
If individual measurements $X$ are normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, then the sample mean $\bar{X}$ from a subgroup of size $n$ is normally distributed with:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\bar{X}] &= \mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu \\
\sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Central Limit Theorem reasoning.**
By the CLT, even if $X$ is not perfectly normal, $\bar{X}$ approaches normality for moderate $n$. So we can use the normal probability distribution as the reference.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the ┬▒3Žā rule.**
A point $\bar{X}$ is considered "out-of-control" if it falls outside:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu \pm 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \mu \pm 3 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert Žā into R╠ä (when Žā is unknown, the most common KTU scenario).**
We use the relationship $\sigma \approx \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$, where $d_2$ is a constant dependent on $n$ (mean of W/Žā distribution).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\bar{X}} &= \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the constant $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive the R-chart limits.**
The range $R$ has mean $E[R] = d_2 \sigma$ and standard deviation $\sigma_R = d_3 \sigma$. Define $D_3 = 1 - \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$ and $D_4 = 1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_R &= D_4 \bar{R} \\
\text{CL}_R &= \bar{R} \\
\text{LCL}_R &= D_3 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö X╠ä and R Chart (Full Solution)

> **Problem:** A precision bearing manufacturer takes 5 samples (each of size $n=4$) every hour. The collected $\bar{X}$ and $R$ values (in microns deviation from nominal) are:
>
> | Sample | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | $\bar{X}$ | 33 | 41 | 37 | 35 | 39 |
> | $R$ | 8 | 11 | 9 | 6 | 10 |
>
> Construct the **X╠ä-chart** and **R-chart**.

**Solution:**

**Step A ŌĆö Compute grand averages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \dfrac{33 + 41 + 37 + 35 + 39}{5} = \dfrac{185}{5} = 37 \\
\bar{R} &= \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k} = \dfrac{8 + 11 + 9 + 6 + 10}{5} = \dfrac{44}{5} = 8.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B ŌĆö Look up constants for $n = 4$:**
$A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

**Step C ŌĆö Compute X╠ä chart limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 37 + (0.729)(8.8) = 37 + 6.4152 = 43.42 \\
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= 37 \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 37 - 6.4152 = 30.58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D ŌĆö Compute R-chart limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(8.8) = 20.08 \\
\text{CL}_R &= 8.8 \\
\text{LCL}_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(8.8) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E ŌĆö Plot interpretation.**
- All $\bar{X}$ points (33, 41, 37, 35, 39) lie within [30.58, 43.42] ŌåÆ **X╠ä chart in control**.
- All $R$ points lie within [0, 20.08] ŌåÆ **R chart in control**.
- **Conclusion:** Process is in **statistical control** at this stage.

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö p-Chart (Variable Sample Size)

> **Problem:** A textile mill inspects fabric rolls for weaving defects. Data for 6 days:
>
> | Day | Rolls Inspected ($n_i$) | Defective Rolls ($d_i$) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 100 | 8 |
> | 2 | 150 | 12 |
> | 3 | 120 | 9 |
> | 4 | 180 | 16 |
> | 5 | 130 | 7 |
> | 6 | 160 | 13 |
>
> Construct the **p-chart** and identify any out-of-control points.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute total and average fraction defective.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{p} &= \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i} = \dfrac{8 + 12 + 9 + 16 + 7 + 13}{100 + 150 + 120 + 180 + 130 + 160} = \dfrac{65}{840} = 0.0774
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate limits for each sample size (limits vary because $n_i$ varies).**

For each day $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_i &= \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}} \\
\text{LCL}_i &= \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}} \;\; (\text{if negative, set to } 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Day | $n_i$ | $p_i = d_i/n_i$ | $3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n_i}$ | UCL$_i$ | LCL$_i$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 0.0800 | 0.0801 | 0.1575 | 0.0000 (Ōēł0) | In-control |
| 2 | 150 | 0.0800 | 0.0654 | 0.1428 | 0.0120 | In-control |
| 3 | 120 | 0.0750 | 0.0731 | 0.1505 | 0.0043 | In-control |
| 4 | 180 | 0.0889 | 0.0587 | 0.1361 | 0.0187 | In-control |
| 5 | 130 | 0.0538 | 0.0703 | 0.1477 | 0.0071 | In-control |
| 6 | 160 | 0.0813 | 0.0634 | 0.1408 | 0.0140 | In-control |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation.**
All points lie within their respective control limits ŌåÆ process is in **statistical control**. To improve quality, the team must now reduce common-cause variation (e.g., refit looms, retrain operators).

### 3.4 Pattern Analysis ŌĆö Western Electric / Nelson Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beyond the ┬▒3Žā rule**, KTU examiners often test **out-of-control patterns** even when points lie *within* the limits. Apply these supplementary tests.

| Rule # | Pattern Description | Indication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | One point outside ┬▒3Žā limits | Out-of-control signal |
| 2 | 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond ┬▒2Žā (same side) | Shift in process mean |
| 3 | 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond ┬▒1Žā (same side) | Small drift |
| 4 | 8 consecutive points on same side of center line | Mean shift |
| 5 | 6 consecutive points steadily increasing or decreasing | Trend |
| 6 | 15 consecutive points within ┬▒1Žā | Reduced variation (stratification) |
| 7 | 14 alternating up-down points | Systematic variation |
| 8 | 8 consecutive points beyond ┬▒1Žā (both sides) | Bimodal / mixture |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Chart Construction Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Identify Quality Characteristic] --> nodeB{Data Type?}
    nodeB -->|Continuous measurable| nodeC[Variables Chart]
    nodeB -->|Count defective or defect| nodeD[Attributes Chart]
    nodeC --> nodeE{Subgroup Size n?}
    nodeE -->|n = 2 to 10| nodeF[Xbar and R Chart]
    nodeE -->|n greater than 10| nodeG[Xbar and S Chart]
    nodeE -->|n = 1, slow process| nodeH[X and MR Chart]
    nodeD --> nodeI{What is being counted?}
    nodeI -->|Defective units, variable n| nodeJ[p Chart]
    nodeI -->|Defective units, constant n| nodeK[np Chart]
    nodeI -->|Number of defects, constant unit| nodeL[c Chart]
    nodeI -->|Defects per unit, variable n| nodeM[u Chart]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Calculate Control Limits Using A2 D3 D4]
    nodeG --> nodeO[Calculate Control Limits Using A3 B3 B4]
    nodeH --> nodeP[Use d2 D4 for MR Chart]
    nodeJ --> nodeQ[Compute p bar and variable limits per sample]
    nodeK --> nodeR[Use n constant in limits]
    nodeL --> nodeS[Use c bar plus minus 3 sqrt c bar]
    nodeM --> nodeT[Compute u bar and limits per n]
    nodeN --> nodeU[Plot all subgroup statistics over time]
    nodeO --> nodeU
    nodeP --> nodeU
    nodeQ --> nodeU
    nodeR --> nodeU
    nodeS --> nodeU
    nodeT --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeV{All points within limits and random}
    nodeV -->|YES| nodeW[Process IN CONTROL: Monitor and reduce common cause]
    nodeV -->|NO| nodeX[Process OUT OF CONTROL: Identify special cause and remove it]
    nodeX --> nodeY[Recompute limits using only in control data]
    nodeY --> nodeU
```

flowchart LR
