### 4.3 Block Architecture ŌĆö Statistical Process Control (SPC) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Raw Material Input] --> node2[Production Process]
    node2 --> node3[Sample Subgroup of size n]
    node3 --> node4[Measure Quality Characteristic]
    node4 --> node5[Compute Sample Statistic: X bar, R, p, c, u]
    node5 --> node6[Plot Point on Control Chart]
    node6 --> node7{Inside control limits and random pattern}
    node7 -->|YES| node8[Process Stable: Continue Production]
    node7 -->|NO| node9[Trigger Alarm: Investigate Assignable Cause]
    node9 --> node10[Root Cause Analysis: Ishikawa, 5 Why]
    node10 --> node11[Implement Corrective Action]
    node11 --> node12[Verify with New Subgroups]
    node12 --> node6
    node8 --> node13[Long Term: Reduce Common Cause via DMAIC]
    node13 --> node2
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Control Limits** and **Specification Limits**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Control Limits | Specification Limits |
|---|---|---|
| **Set by** | Process statistics (calculated) | Customer / Design engineer (given) |
| **Reflects** | Voice of the process | Voice of the customer |
| **Typical width** | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (Ōēł 99.73% of variation) | Engineering tolerance band |
| **Purpose** | Detect process shifts | Define acceptable product |
| **Mutual position** | Should lie **inside** specs for capable process | Should lie **outside** control limits |

**(1 mark each for any 3 distinct differences.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** Define **Rational Subgroup**. Why is it important in Control Chart construction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **rational subgroup** is a set of measurements produced under **homogeneous conditions** such that each subgroup represents a "snapshot" of the process at one instant of time, while differences **between** subgroups capture the *opportunity for variation* over time.

**Importance (any 3 points, 1 mark each):**
1. Maximizes the chance of detecting assignable causes **between** time periods.
2. Ensures within-subgroup variation reflects only **common cause** noise.
3. Provides valid estimates of $\sigma$ via $\bar{R}/d_2$ or $\bar{S}/c_4$.
4. Avoids the **Stratification Error** (mixing sources of variation within a subgroup).

---

### Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö X╠ä and R Chart with Stability Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

> The diameter of a shaft (in mm) is monitored. 6 samples of size $n = 4$ each are drawn:
>
> | Sample | $\bar{X}$ (mm) | Range $R$ (mm) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 10.4 | 0.4 |
> | 2 | 10.2 | 0.5 |
> | 3 | 10.6 | 0.3 |
> | 4 | 10.1 | 0.6 |
> | 5 | 10.5 | 0.4 |
> | 6 | 10.3 | 0.5 |
>
> **Specification limits** are $10 \pm 0.8$ mm. Construct the **X╠ä and R charts**. State if the process is in statistical control. Also compute **Process Capability Index $C_p$**.

**(a) X╠ä and R Chart Construction ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Grand averages.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \dfrac{10.4 + 10.2 + 10.6 + 10.1 + 10.5 + 10.3}{6} = \dfrac{62.1}{6} = 10.35 \\
\bar{R} &= \dfrac{0.4 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.6 + 0.4 + 0.5}{6} = \dfrac{2.7}{6} = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Constants for $n = 4$:** $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö X╠ä chart limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 10.35 + (0.729)(0.45) = 10.35 + 0.328 = 10.678 \\
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= 10.35 \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 10.35 - 0.328 = 10.022
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö R-chart limits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_R &= (2.282)(0.45) = 1.027 \\
\text{CL}_R &= 0.45 \\
\text{LCL}_R &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stability verdict.**
- All $\bar{X}$ values lie between 10.022 and 10.678 ŌåÆ **X╠ä chart in control**.
- All $R$ values lie between 0 and 1.027 ŌåÆ **R chart in control**.
- **Process is in statistical control.** Ō£ō

**(b) Process Capability Index $C_p$ ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Estimate process standard deviation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.45}{2.059} = 0.2185 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute specification spread.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{USL} &= 10.8, \quad \text{LSL} = 9.2 \\
\text{Tolerance} &= \text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 1.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute process spread ($6\sigma$ band).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
6\hat{\sigma} &= 6 \times 0.2185 = 1.311 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{1.6}{1.311} = 1.221
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.221 > 1.0$ means the process spread fits inside specification limits. Since $C_p < 1.33$, the process is **marginally capable** (does not meet typical Six-Sigma standard of $C_p \geq 1.33$, but is acceptable for most industries).

---

#### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö p-Chart with Attribute Data

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

> An automotive parts supplier inspects batches of fuel injectors. The number of defective injectors in 12 successive lots (each of size 200) is:
>
> **25, 32, 18, 28, 35, 22, 30, 27, 24, 29, 33, 21**
>
> Construct a **p-chart**. Is the process in control? If not, identify out-of-control lots and suggest possible causes.

**(a) p-chart Construction ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\bar{p}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum d_i &= 25+32+18+28+35+22+30+27+24+29+33+21 = 324 \\
\bar{p} &= \dfrac{324}{12 \times 200} = \dfrac{324}{2400} = 0.135
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute standard error (constant $n = 200$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.135 \times 0.865}{200}} = \sqrt{0.000584} = 0.02416
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Control limits (constant since $n$ constant).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL} &= 0.135 + 3(0.02416) = 0.135 + 0.0725 = 0.2075 \\
\text{CL} &= 0.135 \\
\text{LCL} &= 0.135 - 0.0725 = 0.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $p_i = d_i/n$ for each lot.**

| Lot | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | In Control? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25 | 0.125 | Ō£ō |
| 2 | 32 | 0.160 | Ō£ō |
| 3 | 18 | 0.090 | Ō£ō |
| 4 | 28 | 0.140 | Ō£ō |
| 5 | 35 | **0.175** | Ō£ō |
| 6 | 22 | 0.110 | Ō£ō |
| 7 | 30 | 0.150 | Ō£ō |
| 8 | 27 | 0.135 | Ō£ō |
| 9 | 24 | 0.120 | Ō£ō |
| 10 | 29 | 0.145 | Ō£ō |
| 11 | 33 | **0.165** | Ō£ō |
| 12 | 21 | 0.105 | Ō£ō |

**(b) Stability Analysis and Corrective Action ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Stability verdict.**
All $p_i$ values lie within [0.0625, 0.2075]. **Process is in statistical control.** Ō£ō

**Step 2 ŌĆö However**, the process has **13.5% defectives**, which is unacceptably high. The system is stable but **not capable**. This is the classic case of *common-cause dominance* ŌĆö only management can reduce it (via Kaizen, poka-yoke, design changes).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Suggested improvement actions (any 5):**
1. **Pareto Analysis** of defect types ŌĆö focus on the vital few.
2. **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** ŌĆö install sensors/gauges to prevent defective output.
3. **Operator training** on critical assembly steps.
4. **Machine maintenance schedule** (TPM) to reduce intermittent failures.
5. **Supplier Quality Improvement** for raw material uniformity.
6. **DOE (Design of Experiments)** to identify the dominant process variables.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong constant lookup:** Using $A_2$ for $n=3$ when the question states $n=4$. **Always verify $n$** before picking constants.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$** ŌĆö using $\mu$ when only sample data is given. **Rule:** $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$, NOT $\sum X_i / (k \cdot n)$.
> 3. **Setting negative LCL to actual negative:** If $\text{LCL} < 0$, set it to **0** for $R, p, c, u$ charts. NEVER plot a negative lower limit on an attribute chart.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ and $\sigma$:** For $n \le 6$, **R-chart is preferred** because $\bar{R}$ is a more efficient estimator of $\sigma$ for small samples.
> 5. **Mixing Specification Limits and Control Limits:** A process in **statistical control** may still produce **out-of-spec** products. Always check capability ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) separately.
> 6. **Forgetting units in $\sigma$ estimate:** $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ must be reported with the same unit as the measurement.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Control Chart** = time-ordered plot with CL, UCL, LCL at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ of the statistic.
- Ō£ģ Inventor: **Walter A. Shewhart, Bell Labs, 1924**.
- Ō£ģ Two broad families: **Variables** ($\bar{X}\&R$, $\bar{X}\&\sigma$, $X\&MR$) and **Attributes** ($p, np, c, u$).
- Ō£ģ Choose chart by **(a)** data type, **(b)** subgroup size constancy, **(c)** what is being counted.
- Ō£ģ **Master formulas:**
  - $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$
  - $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$
  - $\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$
  - $\text{UCL}_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
  - $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$
- Ō£ģ **$\pm 3\sigma$ ŌćÆ 0.27% outside = 3.4 DPMO** = Six Sigma benchmark.
- Ō£ģ **Rational subgroup** minimizes within-group variation and maximizes between-group detection.
- Ō£ģ **Out-of-control signals:** point beyond ┬▒3Žā, 8 points one side, 6 increasing, 2 of 3 beyond 2Žā, etc.
- Ō£ģ **Process Capability:** $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL})/(6\sigma)$; $C_p \ge 1.33$ is industry standard, $C_p \ge 1.5$ is preferred.
- Ō£ģ **In-control ŌēĀ Capable:** a stable process can still produce defects ŌĆö common-cause variation needs systemic reduction.
- Ō£ģ Always **recompute limits** after removing assignable causes (revised $\bar{\bar{X}}$, $\bar{R}$, $\bar{p}$).
- Ō£ģ For $n \le 6$, use **R-chart**; for $n \ge 10$, use **Žā-chart**.
- Ō£ģ **Negative LCL ŌåÆ set to 0** for attribute charts (R, p, c, u).
- Ō£ģ Mention **OC curve and ARL** in long answers to score **extra credit** (Average Run Length ARL$_0$ = 370.4 for ┬▒3Žā without supplementary rules).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a stable (in-control) process to produce output that conforms to the customer's specification limits. It quantifies the relationship between the **natural process spread** (defined by the process standard deviation $\sigma$) and the **engineering specification spread** (defined by the Upper Specification Limit $USL$ and Lower Specification Limit $LSL$).

Mathematically, the most fundamental representation is the **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)**:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

Where the term $6\sigma$ represents the natural process spread (assuming a normal distribution where $99.73\%$ of output falls within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$). The index is dimensionless and unit-free, allowing direct comparison across different processes, products, or industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Process Capability belongs to Module 4 (Statistical Quality Control) of OEIET612. Mastery of $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, and $P_{pk}$ is mandatory for Part A and Part B examination questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** *Capability* refers to the inherent design of the process ŌĆö it addresses the question *"Can this process ever meet the specifications?"*. *Performance*, in contrast, refers to what the process actually delivered over a specific time window.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **bread baker** in a bakery. The customer's specification is that every bread loaf must weigh between **490 g and 510 g** (a 20 g tolerance window). Your baking process, however, naturally produces loaves whose weights scatter around a mean of 500 g with a standard deviation of 3 g.

- The **specification spread** ($USL - LSL$) = 20 g (what the customer demands).
- The **natural process spread** ($6\sigma$) = $6 \times 3 = 18$ g (what your oven naturally produces).
- Since 18 g < 20 g, your process can *potentially* fit inside the customer's tolerance ŌĆö your process is **capable**.

Now, if the customer tightens the window to **496 g ŌĆō 504 g** (only 8 g wide), your $6\sigma = 18$ g no longer fits. Your $C_p$ drops below 1.0, and the process is **not capable**, regardless of how perfectly it is centered. The oven is just too inconsistent for the new, tighter requirements.

This is the essence of process capability: it is the **geometric marriage** between what the process *naturally does* and what the design *needs it to do*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Spread vs Tolerance"* ŌĆö Capability is essentially the ratio *Tolerance ├Ę Process Spread*. Higher value = more capable process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capability Logic Chain

To compute any capability index, the KTU examiner expects you to follow this exact logical sequence:

1. **Establish Statistical Control (Stability)** ŌĆö Use a control chart (X╠ä-R or I-MR) to confirm the process is in-control. If a process is not stable, capability indices are mathematically meaningless.
2. **Estimate the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** ŌĆö Either from a control chart ($\bar{R}/d_2$) or directly from sample data ($\sigma = s$).
3. **Compute the Process Mean ($\mu$)** ŌĆö Average of all individual measurements.
4. **Compare Process Spread to Specification Spread** ŌĆö Using $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, etc.

## 2.2 The Two Pillars: $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

### $C_p$ ŌĆö Process Capability Index (Spread Only)
The $C_p$ index measures the **potential capability** ŌĆö what the process *could* achieve if it were perfectly centered. It ignores centering entirely.

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation Rules:**
- $C_p \geq 1.00$ ŌåÆ Process spread fits inside specification (marginally capable).
- $C_p = 1.33$ ŌåÆ Industry minimum acceptable capability.
- $C_p = 1.50$ ŌåÆ Good capability for new processes.
- $C_p = 1.67$ ŌåÆ Excellent capability (historically required for automotive QS-9000).
- $C_p \geq 2.00$ ŌåÆ **Six Sigma** world-class capability.

### $C_{pk}$ ŌĆö Process Capability Index (Spread + Centering)
The $C_{pk}$ index measures the **actual capability** ŌĆö accounting for both spread *and* centering of the process relative to the target.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

The two components are often written as:
- **$C_{PU}$ (Upper Capability)** = $(USL - \mu) / 3\sigma$
- **$C_{PL}$ (Lower Capability)** = $(\mu - LSL) / 3\sigma$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ Gap:** If $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, the process is capable in *spread* but not *centered*. A re-centering of the mean can dramatically improve $C_{pk}$ without changing $C_p$.

### $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ ŌĆö Process Performance Indices
These indices use the **overall standard deviation $s$** (computed from all individual data points over a long period) rather than the within-subgroup $\sigma$.

$$P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s}$$

$$P_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3s},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right)$$

They capture **long-term process performance**, including shifts, drifts, and assignable causes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | All in same units | Measures potential capability (spread only) |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL})$ | Min of two values | Measures actual capability (spread + centering) |
| 3 | $C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided metric | Distance from mean to upper limit, in $\sigma$ units |
| 4 | $C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided metric | Distance from mean to lower limit, in $\sigma$ units |
| 5 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | $s$ = overall std. dev. | Long-term performance index |
| 6 | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3s},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Long-term indices | Actual long-term capability |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$ depends on $n$ | Estimate $\sigma$ from control chart range |
| 8 | $Z_{USL} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal variable | Distance to USL in $\sigma$ units |
| 9 | $Z_{LSL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal variable | Distance to LSL in $\sigma$ units |
| 10 | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | Motorola Six Sigma metric |
| 11 | $\text{Yield \%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage | % of conforming units |
| 12 | $\text{Sigma Level} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{2\sigma} + 1.5\sigma$ | Six Sigma with 1.5$\sigma$ shift | Motorola Six Sigma methodology |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Automotive, Aerospace):** Supplier quality audits universally require $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. Critical characteristics (safety items) often require $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Six Sigma demands $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$, equivalent to $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Healthcare/Pharmaceutical:** Process capability indices are part of FDA Process Validation guidelines.
- **Service Industries:** Adapted for call center wait times, transaction processing accuracy, and delivery timeliness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $C_p$ from First Principles

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Process Distribution
Assume the process output $X$ follows a normal distribution:

$$X \sim N(\mu,\ \sigma^2)$$

By the empirical rule ($68.2-95.4-99.73$ rule), nearly all observations lie within:

$$\mu \pm 3\sigma$$

This $6\sigma$ band is called the **natural process spread** or **Voice of the Process (VOP)**.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Define the Specification Window
The customer defines:

$$\text{USL} = \text{Upper Specification Limit}, \quad \text{LSL} = \text{Lower Specification Limit}$$

This band, $\text{USL} - \text{LSL}$, is the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** ŌĆö the tolerance window the design requires.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Form the Ratio
The capability index is the ratio of allowable spread to actual spread:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{VOC}}{\text{VOP}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

This derivation is intentionally simple ŌĆö the elegance of $C_p$ lies in its dimensionless nature (it cancels any unit system).

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_{pk}$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize Centering Failure
$C_p$ assumes $\mu$ is exactly at the midpoint of the specification:

$$\mu_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$$

When the mean drifts, $C_p$ still reports the same value ŌĆö *the spread has not changed* ŌĆö even though defect rates explode. To fix this, we introduce one-sided indices.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Upper One-Sided Capability
The distance from the process mean to the upper specification, expressed in units of $3\sigma$:

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Lower One-Sided Capability
The distance from the lower specification to the process mean, in $3\sigma$ units:

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Take the Worst Case
The true capability is limited by the *nearer* specification limit:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) \\[4pt]
&= \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Relationship to $C_p$
A useful identity:

$$C_{pk} = C_p \cdot (1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **centering index**:

$$k = \frac{\vert \text{Target} - \mu \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$$

Note: All vertical bars are written using `\vert` syntax to maintain LaTeX/markdown safety.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Exhaustive Solution)

### Problem Statement
A CNC turning process produces shafts with the following data:
- $USL = 24.50$ mm, $LSL = 23.50$ mm
- Process mean $\mu = 24.10$ mm
- Within-subgroup standard deviation $\sigma = 0.10$ mm
- Subgroup size $n = 5$, with sample range $\bar{R} = 0.20$ mm

**Required:** Compute $C_p$, $C_{PU}$, $C_{PL}$, $C_{pk}$, and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify $\sigma$ via control chart constant**
For $n = 5$, the standard $d_2 = 2.326$:

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.20}{2.326} = 0.0860\ \text{mm}$$

The problem provides $\sigma = 0.10$ mm ŌĆö we use this value as the official estimate.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Specification Spread**

$$USL - LSL = 24.50 - 23.50 = 1.00\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $C_p$**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.00}{6 \times 0.10} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.00}{0.60} \\[4pt]
&= 1.667
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute One-Sided Indices**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.50 - 24.10}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.40}{0.30} = 1.333$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.10 - 23.50}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.60}{0.30} = 2.000$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) = \min(1.333,\ 2.000) = 1.333$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation**
- $C_p = 1.667$ ŌåÆ Process spread is excellent (well above $1.33$ minimum).
- $C_{pk} = 1.333$ ŌåÆ Process meets industry minimum, but is **off-center toward USL**.
- The limiting factor is the upper specification ŌĆö the mean is closer to $USL$ than to $LSL$.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process downward to $\mu \approx 24.00$ mm; $C_{pk}$ would then rise toward $C_p = 1.667$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.667$ | $1.667$ | Capable and centered | Maintain control |
| 2 | $1.667$ | $1.333$ | Capable, off-center toward USL | Re-center downward |
| 3 | $1.667$ | $0.833$ | Capable, severely off-center | Re-center urgently |
| 4 | $1.000$ | $1.000$ | Marginal capability, centered | Reduce variation |
| 5 | $0.800$ | $0.500$ | Not capable, off-center | Redesign process |
| 6 | $0.800$ | $0.800$ | Not capable, centered | Major variation reduction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Capability Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Capability Study] --> B[Step 1 Collect Data]
    B --> C[Step 2 Verify Stability Control Chart]
    C --> D{Process In Control?}
    D -- No --> E[Remove Assignable Causes SPC]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3 Estimate sigma within subgroup]
    F --> G[Step 4 Compute Mean mu]
    G --> H[Step 5 Calculate Cp]
    H --> I[Step 6 Calculate Cpk]
    I --> J{Cpk is 1.33 or greater}
    J -- Yes --> K[Process Capable Document]
    J -- No --> L[Step 7 Diagnose Centering or Spread]
    L --> M{Re centering Possible}
    M -- Yes --> N[Adjust Mean]
    M -- No --> O[Step 8 Reduce Variation DMAIC]
    O --> F
    N --> F
    K --> P[Step 9 Calculate DPMO and Yield]
    P --> Q[End Report Capability]
```

## 4.2 Capability Scenarios Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Scenario A Centered and Capable]
        A1[mu centered Cp high Cpk equals Cp]
    end
    subgraph S2[Scenario B Off Center Capable]
        B1[mu shifted toward USL Cp high Cpk less than Cp]
    end
    subgraph S3[Scenario C Not Capable Centered]
        C1[sigma too large Cp less than 1 Cpk equals Cp]
    end
    subgraph S4[Scenario D Not Capable Off Center]
        D1[Both problems Cp less than 1 Cpk less than Cp]
    end
    S1 --> X[Outcome Acceptable]
    S2 --> Y[Re center]
    S3 --> Z[Reduce Variation]
    S4 --> W[Redesign Process]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Mapping (Input ŌåÆ Process ŌåÆ Output ŌåÆ Capability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Raw Material] --> Proc[Machining Process]
    In2[Machine Settings] --> Proc
    In3[Operator Skill] --> Proc
    In4[Environmental Conditions] --> Proc
    Proc --> Out1[Output Distribution]
    Out1 --> Stat[Compute Mean and sigma]
    Stat --> Comp[Compare with USL and LSL]
    Comp --> Cp[Cp Index]
    Comp --> Cpk[Cpk Index]
    Comp --> DPMO[Defects per Million]
    Cp --> Dec[Capability Decision]
    Cpk --> Dec
    DPMO --> Dec
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each scenario in the block diagram above maps directly to a row in the analytical decision matrix from Section 3.4. Students should be able to draw and label these scenarios in the examination for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Process Capability. Explain the significance of $C_{pk}$ over $C_p$.**

**Model Answer:**
Process capability is a statistical measure of the ability of a stable process to produce output within the customer's specification limits. It is quantified using capability indices.

$C_p$ measures only the **potential capability** ŌĆö the ratio of specification spread to natural process spread. It assumes the process is perfectly centered and ignores any mean shift.

$C_{pk}$ measures the **actual capability** ŌĆö accounting for both the process spread *and* the location of the process mean. It is calculated as the minimum of the one-sided upper and lower capabilities. Therefore, $C_{pk}$ provides a more realistic and conservative picture of process performance, making it the preferred index in industry and KTU examinations.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between $C_p / C_{pk}$ and $P_p / P_{pk}$?**

**Model Answer:**
The indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ use the **within-subgroup standard deviation** ($\sigma$, often estimated via $\bar{R}/d_2$ from control charts), reflecting **short-term inherent capability** of a stable process.

In contrast, $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use the **overall standard deviation** ($s$, computed from all individual data points over time), capturing **long-term performance** that includes process shifts, drifts, and assignable causes.

In practice, $P_{pk}$ is always $\leq C_{pk}$. If $C_{pk} \gg P_{pk}$, the process is statistically stable but drifting over the long term ŌĆö a signal that SPC control charts need reinforcement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** A packaging machine fills bags with nominal 500 g of rice. The specification limits are $USL = 515$ g and $LSL = 485$ g. From a stable in-control process, 25 samples of size 5 each were collected. The grand average is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 502$ g and the average range is $\bar{R} = 4.0$ g. For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{PU}$, $C_{PL}$, and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the capability. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the concept of Six Sigma with reference to DPMO. A process operates at a $4.5\sigma$ quality level. Calculate the DPMO assuming a 1.5$\sigma$ shift, and the corresponding rolled throughput yield for 4 sequential processes. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{4.0}{2.326} = 1.720\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{515 - 485}{6 \times 1.720} = \frac{30}{10.32} = 2.907$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $C_{PU}$:**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{515 - 502}{3 \times 1.720} = \frac{13}{5.16} = 2.519$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $C_{PL}$:**

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{502 - 485}{3 \times 1.720} = \frac{17}{5.16} = 3.295$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) = \min(2.519,\ 3.295) = 2.519$$

**Comment:** The process exhibits excellent capability ($C_p = 2.907$). However, it is slightly off-center toward the USL ($C_{PU} = 2.519$ is the limiting value). The process is capable but a small downward re-centering (target $\mu = 500$ g) would maximize $C_{pk}$.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Six Sigma Definition:**
Six Sigma is a quality management methodology that seeks to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It is equivalent to a $C_{pk}$ of $2.00$ and corresponds to a process operating at $6\sigma$ with a $1.5\sigma$ allowance for long-term mean shift.

**Step 2 ŌĆö DPMO at $4.5\sigma$:**
At $4.5\sigma$ with a $1.5\sigma$ shift, the effective distance to the nearest specification is $4.5 - 1.5 = 3.0\sigma$.

From standard normal tables, the area beyond $3.0\sigma$ (one tail) $\approx 0.00135$, or $1350$ ppm. For two-sided:

$$DPMO \approx 2 \times 1350 = 2700\ \text{defects per million opportunities}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for 4 Processes:**
Each process has yield $Y_i = 1 - 2700/10^6 = 0.99730$.

$$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} Y_i = (0.99730)^4 = 0.98923 = 98.92\%$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between $C_p = 1.00$, $C_p = 1.33$, and $C_p = 1.67$. (7 marks)

**b)** A precision grinding process has $USL = 30.020$ mm, $LSL = 29.970$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size 4 gave $\bar{\bar{X}} = 29.995$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm. For $n = 4$, $d_2 = 2.059$. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. If the process mean shifts to $30.005$ mm due to tool wear, calculate the new $C_{pk}$ and DPMO. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

Three normal-distribution curves overlaid on the same specification window:

1. **$C_p = 1.00$:** The $6\sigma$ spread exactly equals $USL - LSL$. Curves just touch both limits. **Marginal capability** ŌĆö any shift produces defects.

2. **$C_p = 1.33$:** The $6\sigma$ spread occupies $75\%$ of the specification window ($6\sigma / 8\sigma = 0.75$). There is a $1\sigma$ buffer on each side. **Industry minimum acceptable** capability.

3. **$C_p = 1.67$:** The $6\sigma$ spread occupies only $60\%$ of the specification window ($6\sigma / 10\sigma = 0.60$). There is a $2\sigma$ buffer on each side. **Preferred** capability for new processes; required for safety-critical characteristics.

**Verbal explanation:** $C_p$ increases as the natural process variation decreases relative to the specification width. Higher $C_p$ implies greater robustness against process shifts and is the hallmark of a well-designed, mature process.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.012}{2.059} = 0.005828\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $C_p$ (mean = 29.995 mm):**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{30.020 - 29.970}{6 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.050}{0.03497} = 1.430$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute one-sided indices:**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{30.020 - 29.995}{3 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.025}{0.01748} = 1.430$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{29.995 - 29.970}{3 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.025}{0.01748} = 1.430$$

(Process is well-centered; $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.430$.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö New $C_{pk}$ with mean shift to $30.005$ mm:**

$$C_{PU}^{\text{new}} = \frac{30.020 - 30.005}{0.01748} = \frac{0.015}{0.01748} = 0.858$$

$$C_{PL}^{\text{new}} = \frac{30.005 - 29.970}{0.01748} = \frac{0.035}{0.01748} = 2.002$$

$$C_{pk}^{\text{new}} = \min(0.858,\ 2.002) = 0.858$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö DPMO Calculation:**

The limiting side is now the upper specification. Compute the $Z$-score:

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{30.020 - 30.005}{0.005828} = 2.574$$

From standard normal tables, area beyond $Z = 2.574$ (one tail) $\approx 0.00503$.

$$\text{DPMO} = 2 \times 0.00503 \times 10^6 \approx 10{,}060\ \text{defects per million}$$

(Note: Assuming two-sided risk; if the question expects only the upper tail, DPMO $\approx 5{,}030$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $d_2$:** When $\sigma$ is not given directly, examiners expect $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$. Marks are deducted for using $\bar{R}$ as if it were $\sigma$.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing mm with cm or grams with kg in $C_p$ calculation is a guaranteed zero. Always verify all quantities are in identical units.
> 3. **Misreporting $C_{pk}$:** Students often compute only $C_{PU}$ or only $C_{PL}$ and call it $C_{pk}$. The full procedure ŌĆö *both* one-sided values *plus* the minimum ŌĆö must be shown.
> 4. **Ignoring Stability Precondition:** Capability indices assume statistical control. If the question describes an unstable process, indices must be rejected ŌĆö this is a 2-mark deduction item in KTU valuations.
> 5. **$\sigma$ vs $s$ Mix-up:** For $C_p / C_{pk}$, use within-subgroup $\sigma$; for $P_p / P_{pk}$, use overall $s$. Confusing the two loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Capability** quantifies the inherent ability of a process to meet specifications.
- The two foundational indices are $C_p$ (spread only) and $C_{pk}$ (spread + centering).
- $C_p$ is calculated using $USL - LSL$ divided by $6\sigma$, where $\sigma$ is the within-subgroup standard deviation.
- $C_{pk}$ is the minimum of $C_{PU}$ and $C_{PL}$, capturing real-world off-centering.
- $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use overall standard deviation $s$ for long-term performance evaluation.
- A $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the universally accepted minimum capability benchmark; $C_p \geq 1.67$ is preferred.
- $C_{pk} = C_p$ implies a perfectly centered process; $C_{pk} \ll C_p$ implies an off-center process needing recentering.
- $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ is the KTU-expected estimator from X╠ä-R control charts.
- Six Sigma demands $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$, equivalent to $3.4$ DPMO with a $1.5\sigma$ long-term shift allowance.
- Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) is the product of yields across multiple sequential process stages.
- Capability analysis must always follow a stability check (control chart) ŌĆö capability is undefined for unstable processes.
- For $C_p$ calculations, ensure all numerical values share identical units before substitution.
- The Six Sigma 1.5$\sigma$ shift is a Motorola convention, not a statistical theorem ŌĆö examiners may ask its rationale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taguchi's Robust Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Taguchi's Robust Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Robust Design (TRD)** is an engineering methodology developed by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi** (Japanese engineer/statistician) that seeks to **minimize the variation (or sensitivity) of a product's or process's functional performance against uncontrollable environmental conditions and manufacturing imperfections**, by **optimally choosing the levels of controllable design parameters (control factors)** so that the output remains stable and on-target.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, robust design is positioned under **Offline Quality Control (Quality Engineering)** and is an **offline** counterpart to statistical process control (SPC). The "noise" in the system is captured mathematically using the **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** and quantified using the **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio**.

| Term Used in KTU Syllabus | Standard Taguchi Equivalent | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Control Factors (Signal Factors) | Design Parameters | Controllable, adjustable by engineer |
| Noise Factors | Disturbance Parameters | Uncontrollable in field |
| Inner Array | Orthogonal Array for control factors | Experiment on design |
| Outer Array | Orthogonal Array for noise factors | Outer environment loop |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The All-Terrain Car Analogy**
> Imagine you have designed a car. You want it to deliver the *same* smooth ride-quality (functional performance) whether the road is smooth, bumpy, in rain, in heat, or when the fuel quality is poor. You cannot control the road or weather ŌĆö these are **Noise Factors**. However, you *can* choose the suspension stiffness, tyre pressure, engine tuning, gear ratio ŌĆö these are **Control Factors**.
>
> Taguchi's philosophy: instead of building a car that *tolerates* bad roads (just meeting specs), build a car whose performance is **insensitive** to bad roads. That insensitivity = **Robustness**.

The two-stage decomposition Taguchi proposed:
1. **System Design** ŌĆō pick the right technological concept.
2. **Parameter Design** ŌĆō choose the *levels* of parameters so the system is robust.
3. **Tolerance Design** ŌĆō tighten tolerances only if economically justified after parameter design.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always distinguish **Parameter Design** (the heart of Taguchi's method) from **Tolerance Design** (a follow-up activity). Most KTU questions target Parameter Design.

### 1.3 Why Conventional Quality Thinking Fails

The classical view: *"A product within tolerance = Good product; outside tolerance = Defective."* Taguchi's view: *"Any deviation from target causes loss to society, even within tolerances."* This is formalized through the **Quality Loss Function**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Quality Loss Function (QLF)

Taguchi rejected the binary "go / no-go" tolerance view and replaced it with a **quadratic loss function** that assigns a monetary value to *every* deviation from the target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Cornerstones of Taguchi's Philosophy**
> 1. **Quality Loss to Society** ŌĆō Loss occurs the moment performance deviates from target, *even within tolerance*.
> 2. **Continuous Improvement** ŌĆō Loss is a continuous function; reduce variability to reduce loss.
> 3. **Robustness** ŌĆō Performance should be insensitive to noise.

The QLF is mathematically stated as:

$$
L(y) \;=\; k\,(y - m)^{2}
$$

where:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $L(y)$ | Quality loss when characteristic takes value $y$ | Currency (Ōé╣, \$) |
| $y$ | Actual measured value of quality characteristic | Depends on characteristic |
| $m$ | Target value (nominal) of the characteristic | Same unit as $y$ |
| $k$ | Proportionality constant (loss coefficient) | Currency / (unit)┬▓ |
| $A_{0}$ | Average cost of replacement/repair at the tolerance limit | Currency |
| $\Delta_{0}$ | Tolerance limit ($= T - m$ where $T$ is upper spec) | Same unit as $y$ |

The constant $k$ is derived by setting $L = A_{0}$ at $y = m \pm \Delta_{0}$:

$$
k \;=\; \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}}
$$

### 2.2 Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **S/N ratio** is Taguchi's single-number measure of robustness. It is the **logarithmic ratio of the "signal" (desired effect, e.g., the mean) to the "noise" (unwanted variation, e.g., the variance).** A **higher S/N ratio implies greater robustness** (lower variation relative to the desired output).

Taguchi defined **three categories** of quality characteristics, each with a dedicated S/N formula:

**Case 1 ŌĆö Larger-the-Better (LTB):**
Used when the goal is to **maximize** the response (e.g., tensile strength, fuel efficiency, yield, reliability).

$$
\eta \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{\,2}}\right] \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Case 2 ŌĆö Smaller-the-Better (STB):**
Used when the goal is to **minimize** the response (e.g., defects, surface roughness, emissions, leakage).

$$
\eta \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}^{\,2}\right] \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Case 3 ŌĆö Nominal-the-Best (NTB):**
Used when there is a **specific target value** $m$, and deviation in *either* direction is undesirable (e.g., diameter of a shaft, voltage of a battery, pH of a solution).

$$
\eta \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right] \;\; \text{dB}
$$

where $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}$ and $S^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö At-a-Glance Reference

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table for revision. These are the five formulas you must memorise for any Taguchi problem in the KTU ESE.**

| Sl. | Concept | Formula | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality Loss | $L(y) = k(y-m)^{2}$ | Currency | Any deviation from target |
| 2 | Loss Coefficient | $k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{\,2}$ | Currency/(unit)┬▓ | Given tolerance cost |
| 3 | S/N ŌĆö LTB | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_{i}^{2}\right]$ | dB | Maximise the response |
| 4 | S/N ŌĆö STB | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}\right]$ | dB | Minimise the response |
| 5 | S/N ŌĆö NTB | $\eta = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\bar{y}^{\,2}/S^{2}\right]$ | dB | Target value specified |
| 6 | Mean | $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}$ | unit | Always |
| 7 | Variance | $S^{2} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2}$ | (unit)┬▓ | Always |
| 8 | MSD (Larger-the-better) | $\text{MSD}_{L} = \frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_{i}^{2}$ | (unit)$^{-2}$ | Substitute into LTB |
| 9 | MSD (Smaller-the-better) | $\text{MSD}_{S} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}$ | (unit)┬▓ | Substitute into STB |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Mechanical:** Designing an automotive brake pad whose friction coefficient is insensitive to temperature & humidity.
* **Electronics:** Designing a circuit whose output voltage stays at **5.0 V ┬▒ 0.05 V** even when supply voltage and load resistance vary (noise).
* **Manufacturing:** Choosing cutting speed, feed, depth-of-cut such that surface roughness is robust against material hardness variation.
* **Software:** Choosing server configuration such that response time is robust against traffic bursts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quality Loss Function

Taguchi started from the premise: *"Loss to society is proportional to the square of the deviation from target."*

**Step 1 ŌĆö Hypothesis:** Loss is some continuous function of deviation $d = y - m$.

$$
L(y) = f(d), \quad d = y - m
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Symmetry Argument:** Loss must be symmetric about the target (deviation +0.1 mm hurts as much as ŌłÆ0.1 mm):

$$
L(m + \delta) = L(m - \delta)
$$

This forces $f$ to be an even function of $d$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quadratic Form (small-deviation Taylor expansion):** Near the target, expand $L$ as a Taylor series about $m$:

$$
L(y) = L(m) + L^{\prime}(m)\,(y-m) + \tfrac{1}{2}L^{\prime\prime}(m)\,(y-m)^{2} + \dots
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Boundary Conditions Applied:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(m) &= 0 \quad &\text{(no loss when on-target)} \\
L^{\prime}(m) &= 0 \quad &\text{(stationary point at target ŌĆö minimum loss)} \\
\text{Let } k &= \tfrac{1}{2}L^{\prime\prime}(m) \quad &\text{(second-derivative constant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Taylor expansion collapses to:

$$
L(y) \;=\; k\,(y-m)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Determining $k$ from Specification:** At the tolerance limit $y = m + \Delta_{0}$, the loss equals the average replacement cost $A_{0}$:

$$
A_{0} \;=\; k\,\Delta_{0}^{\,2}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad k \;=\; \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}}
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\;L(y) \;=\; \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}}\,(y-m)^{2}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the S/N Ratio for Nominal-the-Best

The Mean Square Deviation (MSD) from target $m$ is:

$$
\text{MSD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-m)^{2}
$$

Expanding $(y_i - m)^2 = (y_i - \bar{y} + \bar{y} - m)^2$ and simplifying using $\sum(y_i - \bar{y}) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSD} &= \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left[(y_{i}-\bar{y}) + (\bar{y}-m)\right]^{2} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2} + \dfrac{2(\bar{y}-m)}{n}\sum(y_{i}-\bar{y}) + (\bar{y}-m)^{2} \\
&= \sigma^{2} + (\bar{y}-m)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **robust** design, we want the variance term small *and* the bias term small. Taguchi collapses this into a single index ŌĆö the **S/N ratio** ŌĆö by treating $\bar{y}^{2}$ as signal and $S^{2}$ as noise:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right] \;\; \text{dB}\;}
$$

Maximising $\eta$ simultaneously pushes $\bar{y}$ toward the target (good signal) and minimises $S^{2}$ (small noise).

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Quality Loss Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A critical shaft diameter has target $m = 50$ mm. The engineering tolerance is $\Delta_{0} = \pm 2$ mm. The average cost to replace a shaft found at the tolerance limit is $A_{0} = \text{Rs. } 400$. Compute:
* (i) the loss coefficient $k$,
* (ii) the quality loss when the actual diameter is $y = 51.5$ mm,
* (iii) the quality loss when the actual diameter is $y = 49$ mm.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**(i) Loss coefficient:**

$$
k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{400}{(2)^{2}} = \dfrac{400}{4} = \text{Rs. } 100 \text{ per mm}^{2}
$$

**(ii) Loss at $y = 51.5$ mm:**

$$
L(51.5) = 100 \times (51.5 - 50)^{2} = 100 \times (1.5)^{2} = 100 \times 2.25 = \text{Rs. } 225
$$

**(iii) Loss at $y = 49$ mm:**

$$
L(49) = 100 \times (49 - 50)^{2} = 100 \times (-1)^{2} = 100 \times 1 = \text{Rs. } 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A shaft *inside* the tolerance band (49 mm is well within $50 \pm 2$) still imposes a **Rs. 100 loss to society** ŌĆö Taguchi's central insight. The conventional "go/no-go" view treats this as a perfect product. Taguchi's view says the customer is being short-changed by 1 mm.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö S/N Ratio (Larger-the-Better)

**Problem Statement:** Five samples of a high-strength alloy are tested for ultimate tensile strength (MPa): **460, 470, 465, 480, 475.** Compute the S/N ratio assuming *larger-the-better* characteristic.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{2}}$ for each observation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{460^{2}} &= \dfrac{1}{211{,}600} = 4.7259 \times 10^{-6} \\
\dfrac{1}{470^{2}} &= \dfrac{1}{220{,}900} = 4.5274 \times 10^{-6} \\
\dfrac{1}{465^{2}} &= \dfrac{1}{216{,}225} = 4.6253 \times 10^{-6} \\
\dfrac{1}{480^{2}} &= \dfrac{1}{230{,}400} = 4.3403 \times 10^{-6} \\
\dfrac{1}{475^{2}} &= \dfrac{1}{225{,}625} = 4.4318 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum and compute MSD:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5}\dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{2}} = (4.7259 + 4.5274 + 4.6253 + 4.3403 + 4.4318) \times 10^{-6} = 22.6507 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\text{MSD} = \dfrac{1}{5} \times 22.6507 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5301 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute S/N ratio:**

$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}(4.5301 \times 10^{-6}) = -10 \times (-5.3442) = \mathbf{53.44 \; dB}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö S/N Ratio (Nominal-the-Best)

**Problem Statement:** Five resistors are rated at **100 ╬®**. The measured resistances are: **98, 102, 99, 101, 100 ╬®.** Compute the S/N ratio (NTB).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Mean:**

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{98 + 102 + 99 + 101 + 100}{5} = \dfrac{500}{5} = 100 \; \Omega
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2} &= (98-100)^{2} + (102-100)^{2} + (99-100)^{2} + (101-100)^{2} + (100-100)^{2} \\
&= 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sample variance:**

$$
S^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2} = \dfrac{10}{5-1} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö S/N ratio:**

$$
\eta = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right] = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{100^{2}}{2.5}\right] = 10\,\log_{10}(4000) = 10 \times 3.602 = \mathbf{36.02 \; dB}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Which S/N to Use

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decision Tree for S/N Selection (KTU-favourite exam question):**

| If the Quality Characteristic is... | Goal | S/N Formula | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strength, yield, efficiency, reliability | **Maximise** | LTB | Tensile strength, mileage, MTBF |
| Defects, errors, roughness, leakage | **Minimise** | STB | Surface roughness, emissions, defects per million |
| Has a fixed *target* value with two-sided tolerance | **Hit target, minimise scatter** | NTB | Diameter, voltage, resistance, pH, weight |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taguchi's Two-Stage Optimization Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture** ŌĆö illustrates how Inner Array (control factors) and Outer Array (noise factors) interact to compute the S/N ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IA["Inner Array L9 L18 Orthogonal Array of Control Factors"] --> SD["Stage 1 System Design and Parameter Selection"]
    OA["Outer Array Noise Factors Outer Environment Conditions"] --> SD
    SD --> EXP["Conduct N Experiments under each Noise Condition"]
    EXP --> OBS["Observed Quality Characteristic y subscript ij"]
    OBS --> SNR["Compute S to N Ratio eta in dB"]
    SNR --> ANOVA["ANOVA on eta Identify Significant Control Factors"]
    ANOVA --> OPT["Optimal Factor Levels Selection"]
    OPT --> CONF["Confirmation Run Validate Predicted Optimum"]
    CONF --> IMP["Implement Robust Process Design"]
```

### 4.2 End-to-End Taguchi Robust Design Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1 Define the Problem and Quality Characteristic"] --> stepB["Step 2 Identify the Target Value and Tolerance"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3 List All Control Factors and Levels"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4 List All Noise Factors and Levels"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5 Select Appropriate Orthogonal Array L4 L9 L18 L27"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6 Conduct Experiments per Orthogonal Array"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7 Compute S to N Ratio for Each Run"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 8 Perform ANOVA on S to N Ratios"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 9 Identify Optimal Control Factor Levels"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 10 Run Confirmation Experiment"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Step 11 Implement and Monitor the Robust Design"]
```

### 4.3 Taguchi's Loss-to-Society Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QLF["Quality Loss Function L of y equals k times y minus m squared"] --> CONV["Conventional View Loss equals zero within tolerance band"]
    QLF --> TAG["Taguchi View Loss is a continuous quadratic function peaking at spec limit A0"]
    TAG --> IMP1["Engineers must drive variation toward target to minimise loss"]
    TAG --> IMP2["Variation equals loss even within spec limits"]
    TAG --> IMP3["Quality improvement means reducing variance not just hitting spec"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Robust Design as a System-Engineering Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Customer Requirements and Target m"] --> CTRL["Controllable Inputs Control Factors"]
    NOISE["Uncontrollable Disturbances Noise Factors Temperature Humidity Wear"] --> SYS["Process or Product System"]
    CTRL --> SYS
    SYS --> OUT["Output Quality Characteristic y"]
    OUT --> COMP["Comparator Against Target m"]
    COMP --> LOSS["Quality Loss L of y to Society"]
    LOSS --> FEED["Feedback for Robust Redesign"]
    FEED --> CTRL
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Viva:** The arrows in **4.4** demonstrate why Taguchi's approach is *systemic*: instead of inspecting the output (SPC), it re-engineers the *input control-factor levels* so that the output is naturally robust to noise, **before** the product is even shipped.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF). Why is it considered superior to the traditional "tolerance-based" acceptance criterion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Taguchi's Quality Loss Function is a **quadratic relationship between the deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value and the loss incurred by society**, expressed as:

$$
L(y) = k\,(y - m)^{2} \quad \text{where} \quad k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{\,2}
$$

It is superior because:

| Aspect | Traditional View | Taguchi's QLF |
|---|---|---|
| Loss inside tolerance | Treated as **zero** | Treated as **positive, continuous** |
| Loss outside tolerance | Sudden jump | Smooth, quadratic rise |
| Customer perspective | Ignored | Loss to *society* captured |
| Improvement driver | Pass/Fail | Reduce variance always |

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark definition + 2 marks comparison table.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**Differentiate between Control Factors and Noise Factors. Why does Taguchi prefer "robustness over tolerance tightening"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Control Factors** are design parameters that the engineer can **set and adjust at will** (e.g., cutting speed, pressure, resistor value). They form the **inner array** of experiments.
* **Noise Factors** are *uncontrollable* disturbances in the field or factory (e.g., ambient temperature, supply voltage variation, raw-material hardness). They form the **outer array**.

Taguchi prefers robustness because tightening tolerances (tolerance design) **increases manufacturing cost** (Ōé╣Ōåæ with $\Delta_{0}\downarrow$), whereas parameter design reduces sensitivity *without* raising manufacturing cost ŌĆö it is the **most economical** form of quality improvement. *(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for rationale.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-level internal choice ŌĆö answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **philosophy** of Taguchi's Robust Design. Discuss, with a suitable **industrial example**, the concept of *inner array* and *outer array* in parameter design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Taguchi's philosophy rests on three pillars:
1. **Quality = minimum loss to society** ŌĆö not merely conformance to specs.
2. **Variation is the enemy** ŌĆö even within-tolerance variation creates loss.
3. **Design for robustness first, tighten tolerances only if necessary.**

**Industrial Example ŌĆö Filament Current in a CRT Display Tube:**
The goal is to set the *filament voltage* (control factor) such that the *screen luminance* stays at target 250 cd/m┬▓ regardless of ambient temperature (noise factor: 20┬░C to 40┬░C) or ageing of the heater.

* **Inner Array (Control Factors)** ŌĆö orthogonal array L9 of 3 control factors:
  * A: Filament voltage (5.5 V, 6.0 V, 6.5 V)
  * B: Cathode coating thickness (low, medium, high)
  * C: Heater wire gauge (24, 26, 28 AWG)

* **Outer Array (Noise Factors)** ŌĆö L4 array:
  * N1: Temperature (20┬░C, 40┬░C)
  * N2: Humidity (40%, 80%)

Each of the 9 inner-array runs is tested under all 4 outer-array conditions ŌåÆ 9 ├Ś 4 = 36 experiments. For each inner-array run, the S/N ratio is computed (Nominal-the-Best, since luminance target = 250 cd/m┬▓), and the combination of A, B, C with the highest average S/N ratio across noise conditions is selected as the optimum.

**Why inner/outer arrays?** They allow simultaneous evaluation of many factors with a fraction of the experiments required by full factorial (which would be $3^3 \times 2^2 = 108$ runs ŌĆö here we use only 36). *(4 marks for philosophy + 3 marks for inner/outer array example.)*

---

**(b)** A resistor manufacturer produces a component with target resistance **$m = 100 \; \Omega$** and tolerance $\Delta_{0} = \pm 5 \; \Omega$. The average replacement cost at the tolerance limit is $A_{0} = \text{Rs. } 500$. The resistance of a batch of 5 resistors is measured: **97, 102, 99, 104, 98 ╬®.**

Compute:
   (i) the **loss coefficient** $k$,
   (ii) the **quality loss** for the resistor reading 102 ╬®,
   (iii) the **S/N ratio** (Nominal-the-Best) for the batch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Loss coefficient:**

$$
k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{500}{(5)^{2}} = \dfrac{500}{25} = \mathbf{Rs.\; 20\; \text{per}\;\Omega^{2}}
$$

**(ii) Quality loss at $y = 102 \; \Omega$:**

$$
L(102) = 20 \times (102 - 100)^{2} = 20 \times 4 = \mathbf{Rs.\; 80}
$$

**(iii) S/N ratio (Nominal-the-Best):**

*Mean:*

$$
\bar{y} = \dfrac{97 + 102 + 99 + 104 + 98}{5} = \dfrac{500}{5} = 100 \; \Omega
$$

*Sum of squared deviations:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum(y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2} &= (-3)^{2} + (2)^{2} + (-1)^{2} + (4)^{2} + (-2)^{2} \\
&= 9 + 4 + 1 + 16 + 4 = 34
\end{aligned}
$$

*Sample variance:*

$$
S^{2} = \dfrac{34}{5-1} = \dfrac{34}{4} = 8.5
$$

*S/N ratio:*

$$
\eta = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right] = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10000}{8.5}\right] = 10\,\log_{10}(1176.47) = 10 \times 3.0706 = \mathbf{30.71\; dB}
$$

*(Examiner note: 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii). Full marks require showing all arithmetic steps.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, KTU Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **three categories of quality characteristics** in Taguchi's method with suitable examples. Derive the **S/N ratio formula for the Nominal-the-Best case**, starting from the Mean Square Deviation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Three Categories:**

| Category | Goal | S/N Formula | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | Maximise response | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_{i}^{2}\right]$ | Tensile strength, fuel efficiency |
| **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | Minimise response | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}\right]$ | Surface roughness, defect rate |
| **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | Hit target exactly | $\eta = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\bar{y}^{2}/S^{2}\right]$ | Resistance, voltage, diameter |

**Derivation of NTB S/N Ratio:**

Mean Square Deviation from target $m$:

$$
\text{MSD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-m)^{2}
$$

Decompose $y_i - m = (y_i - \bar{y}) + (\bar{y} - m)$ and expand the square:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSD} &= \dfrac{1}{n}\sum(y_{i}-\bar{y})^{2} + \dfrac{2(\bar{y}-m)}{n}\sum(y_{i}-\bar{y}) + (\bar{y}-m)^{2} \\
&= \sigma^{2} + (\bar{y}-m)^{2} \quad \text{(since } \sum(y_i-\bar{y}) = 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taguchi's insight: for NTB, we treat $\bar{y}^{2}$ as the **signal strength** and $S^{2}$ as the **noise variance**. A higher ratio means the signal (target achievement) dominates over the noise (variation).

Hence the **S/N ratio**:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\bar{y}^{\,2}}{S^{2}}\right]\;}
$$

This single index simultaneously rewards being close to target (large $\bar{y}^{2}$) and penalising scatter (large $S^{2}$). *(3 marks for categories + 4 marks for derivation with proper algebraic steps.)*

---

**(b)** A process engineer measures the **surface roughness (Ra in $\mu$m)** of 4 bearings under each of two noise conditions. The engineer wishes to **minimise** surface roughness (smaller-the-better characteristic).

Data:

| Run | Condition 1 (Ra) | Condition 2 (Ra) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.82 | 0.91 |
| 2 | 0.65 | 0.72 |
| 3 | 0.74 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 0.58 | 0.66 |

Compute the **S/N ratio (STB) for each run** and identify the run with the **lowest variation** (highest robustness). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

For STB:

$$
\eta = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}\right], \quad n = 2
$$

**Run 1:**

$$
\sum y_{i}^{2} = (0.82)^{2} + (0.91)^{2} = 0.6724 + 0.8281 = 1.5005
$$

$$
\eta_{1} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1.5005}{2}\right] = -10\,\log_{10}(0.7503) = -10 \times (-0.1248) = \mathbf{1.25 \; dB}
$$

**Run 2:**

$$
\sum y_{i}^{2} = (0.65)^{2} + (0.72)^{2} = 0.4225 + 0.5184 = 0.9409
$$

$$
\eta_{2} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.9409}{2}\right] = -10\,\log_{10}(0.4705) = -10 \times (-0.3275) = \mathbf{3.28 \; dB}
$$

**Run 3:**

$$
\sum y_{i}^{2} = (0.74)^{2} + (0.80)^{2} = 0.5476 + 0.6400 = 1.1876
$$

$$
\eta_{3} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1.1876}{2}\right] = -10\,\log_{10}(0.5938) = -10 \times (-0.2262) = \mathbf{2.26 \; dB}
$$

**Run 4:**

$$
\sum y_{i}^{2} = (0.58)^{2} + (0.66)^{2} = 0.3364 + 0.4356 = 0.7720
$$

$$
\eta_{4} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.7720}{2}\right] = -10\,\log_{10}(0.3860) = -10 \times (-0.4133) = \mathbf{4.13 \; dB}
$$

| Run | S/N Ratio (dB) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.25 | Lowest (worst) |
| 2 | 3.28 | 3rd |
| 3 | 2.26 | 2nd |
| **4** | **4.13** | **Highest (most robust)** |

**Conclusion:** **Run 4** exhibits the lowest variation in surface roughness across the two noise conditions and is therefore the most **robust setting**. The engineer should adopt the control-factor combination used in Run 4 as the optimal parameter design.

*(Examiner note: 2 marks per run ├Ś 3 runs = 6 marks + 1 mark for final conclusion. Award full marks only if each logarithmic step is shown explicitly.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S^2$ with $\sigma^2$:** Taguchi's textbook formula uses sample variance $S^2 = \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2/(n-1)$. Some students divide by $n$, leading to a wrong answer. Always check whether the denominator is $(n-1)$ ŌĆö examiners explicitly test this.
>
> 2. **Wrong sign convention in S/N ŌĆö LTB / STB:** Both have a **negative** sign in front. Students forget it and report positive log values, getting inverted conclusions.
>
> 3. **Misidentifying S/N category:** A frequent trap: *"tensile strength" is LTB (larger better) ŌĆö but if asked to compute *deviation from minimum specification*, examiners may switch the category. Read the question stem carefully.*
>
> 4. **Confusing Loss Function $k$ with $\Delta_{0}$:** $k$ has units of **currency / (unit)┬▓**. Substituting a wrong dimension costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in MSD / $S^{2}$ calculation:** Examiners explicitly ask for *each* squared-deviation term to be shown. Do not write "$\sum y_i^2 = 22.65 \times 10^{-6}$" without showing the individual squares.
>
> 6. **Mixing up inner array and outer array:** Inner array = control factors (engineer sets them); outer array = noise factors (engineer cannot). Reversing this loses at least 2 marks in a 7-mark question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Taguchi's Robust Design**

- **Definition:** Robust Design = engineering the product/process so that functional performance is **insensitive** to noise factors.
- **Three Pillars:** Loss to society ┬Ę Continuous variation ┬Ę Robustness over tolerance.
- **QLF:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$ with $k = A_{0}/\Delta_{0}^{2}$; loss exists *inside* tolerance.
- **S/N Categories & Formulas:**
  - LTB ŌåÆ $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_i^2\right]$
  - STB ŌåÆ $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right]$
  - NTB ŌåÆ $\eta = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\bar{y}^{2}/S^{2}\right]$
- **Higher S/N = More Robust** (the index to maximise).
- **Inner Array** = orthogonal array of **control** factors; **Outer Array** = orthogonal array of **noise** factors.
- **Three Phases of Taguchi Design:** System Design ŌåÆ Parameter Design ŌåÆ Tolerance Design.
- **Parameter Design is the heart** of Taguchi's method; it comes *before* tolerance design and is the most cost-effective quality lever.
- **Common application areas:** Manufacturing process optimisation, electronics, automotive, materials, software reliability.
- **Memorise:** Mean formula $\bar{y}$, Variance formula $S^{2}$ with $(n-1)$ divisor, log conversion to dB.
- **Units:** $k$ ŌåÆ currency/(unit)┬▓; $\eta$ ŌåÆ decibels (dB); $\Delta_{0}$ ŌåÆ same unit as $y$.
- **Exam one-liner to remember:** *"Taguchi says: Make it robust, then ship it; tighten specs only as the last resort."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a **holistic, company-wide, proactive and preventive maintenance philosophy** that aims to maximize **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by eliminating breakdowns, defects, accidents, and waste through the **participation of all employees** ŌĆö from top management to shop-floor operators.

TPM was first formalized in **1971** by **Seiichi Nakajima** at **Nippondenso** (a Toyota Group company). It evolved from **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** into a system that integrates maintenance with manufacturing, quality, safety, and administration to create a **"Zero Loss"** culture.

The **objective** of TPM can be summarized in five zeros (the **5-Zero Goal**):

| Zero Loss Target | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Zero Defects** | No defective products leave the process |
| **Zero Breakdown** | No unplanned equipment stoppages |
| **Zero Accidents** | Safe working environment for all |
| **Zero Pollution** | No environmental contamination |
| **Zero Waste** | Optimal use of materials, time, and energy |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Human Body

Imagine your body is a piece of industrial equipment.

- You brush your teeth daily, eat healthy food, and exercise ŌĆö these are **preventive self-maintenance** (similar to **Autonomous Maintenance / Jishu Hozen** in TPM).
- You visit a doctor for annual check-ups ŌĆö this is **Planned Maintenance**.
- If your back pains daily because your chair is broken, you fix the chair instead of taking painkillers ŌĆö this is **Root-Cause Improvement** (the philosophy of *Poka-Yoke* and *Why-Why Analysis* in TPM).

> [!NOTE]
> **In TPM, the operator owns the machine.** Just as you know your own body better than anyone else, the operator who runs the machine daily knows its "voice," vibration, and behaviour best. TPM empowers that operator to clean, inspect, lubricate, and tighten (the **CILT** activities) before a technician is even called.

## 1.3 Why TPM Matters in KTU Context

Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic directly supports:

- **CO4:** *Apply modern maintenance and quality tools for industrial performance improvement.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Big Losses (µĀĖÕ┐āŃü«ÕģŁÕż¦µÉŹÕż▒)

TPM classifies every production loss into **Six Big Losses**, which are grouped under three macro-categories that form the **OEE multiplier chain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Six Big Losses** are the backbone of any OEE-related KTU numerical question. Memorize the mapping between loss type and OEE component.

| OEE Component | Six Big Loss Category | Loss Type | Typical Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Availability** | 1. **Breakdown Loss** | Equipment Failure | Component wear, sudden breakdown |
| **Availability** | 2. **Setup / Adjustment Loss** | Changeover | Die change, model changeover |
| **Performance** | 3. **Small Stops / Idling Loss** | Minor Stoppages | Sensor jam, temporary blockage |
| **Performance** | 4. **Reduced Speed Loss** | Speed Reduction | Running below designed cycle time |
| **Quality** | 5. **Defect / Rework Loss** | Process Defects | Scrap and rework during steady-state |
| **Quality** | 6. **Startup / Yield Loss** | Reduced Yield | Defects during warm-up / start-up |

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of OEE Computation

$$OEE = Availability \times Performance \times Quality$$

Where each factor is bounded by a **world-class benchmark** and a typical industrial value.

$$OEE_{World\text{-}Class} \geq 85\%$$

## 2.3 Reliability & Maintainability Metrics

These two metrics are asked repeatedly in KTU university exams under Module 4.

**Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** ŌĆö measures *reliability*:

$$MTBF = \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$$

**Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** ŌĆö measures *maintainability*:

$$MTTR = \frac{Total\ Downtime\ for\ Repairs}{Number\ of\ Failures}$$

**Inherent Availability (AI)** ŌĆö the bridge between MTBF and MTTR:

$$A_I = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> For every KTU Part B question on TPM, you must derive at least **one** of the following. Note the use of `\vert` instead of literal pipes so the markdown table does not break.

| \textbf{Symbol / Term} | \textbf{Formula} | \textbf{Units / Range} | \textbf{Engineering Meaning} |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Availability (A) | $\dfrac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100$ | \% (0ŌĆō100) | Time the equipment is ready vs. scheduled |
| Performance (P) | $\dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces}{Operating\ Time} \times 100$ | \% (0ŌĆō100) | Speed efficiency vs. design speed |
| Quality (Q) | $\dfrac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100$ | \% (0ŌĆō100) | First-pass yield |
| OEE | $A \times P \times Q \div 10000$ | \% (0ŌĆō100) | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| MTBF | $\dfrac{Total\ Uptime}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability indicator |
| MTTR | $\dfrac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Maintainability indicator |
| Inherent Availability | $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | ratio (0ŌĆō1) | Intrinsic uptime potential |
| TEEP | $OEE \times \dfrac{Loading\ Time}{Calendar\ Time}$ | \% | Total Effective Equipment Performance |

## 2.5 The 8 Pillars of TPM (Õģ½Õż¦µö»µ¤▒)

TPM is implemented through **8 Pillars**, each addressing a specific loss domain:

1. **Pillar 1 ŌĆō Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance):** Operators do daily CILT ŌĆö *Cleaning, Inspection, Lubrication, Tightening*.
2. **Pillar 2 ŌĆō Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement):** Cross-functional teams eliminate specific big losses using **DMAIC / PDCA**.
3. **Pillar 3 ŌĆō Planned Maintenance:** Time-based, condition-based, and predictive maintenance schedules.
4. **Pillar 4 ŌĆō Quality Maintenance:** *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing), *5-Why*, root-cause analysis to achieve **Zero Defects**.
5. **Pillar 5 ŌĆō Early Equipment Management:** Design new equipment for *easy maintenance, safety, and reliability* from Day 1.
6. **Pillar 6 ŌĆō Training & Education:** Build multi-skilled operators and engineers.
7. **Pillar 7 ŌĆō Safety, Health & Environment:** Eliminate unsafe conditions; link directly to **5-Zero Goal**.
8. **Pillar 8 ŌĆō TPM in Administration:** Extend TPM philosophy to office, stores, and supply chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical ŌĆö OEE Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most Module 4 numericals are *OEE + MTBF/MTTR* combined questions worth 7ŌĆō14 marks. Always show **units** and **final % conversion**.

### **Problem Statement**

A CNC machine in a Kerala-based precision components factory has the following data for one shift (8 hours = 480 minutes):

- Planned production time = 480 min
- Two breakdowns: 35 min and 25 min
- One changeover: 20 min
- Ideal cycle time per piece = 1.20 min
- Total pieces produced = 320
- Defective pieces = 18

Calculate: **(a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE, (e) Comment on world-class status.**

### **Solution ŌĆö Exhaustive Step-by-Step**

**Step 1: Compute Operating Time (T_op).**

Operating Time = Planned Production Time ŌłÆ All Downtime (breakdowns + changeovers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{op} &= 480 - (35 + 25 + 20) \\
T_{op} &= 480 - 80 \\
T_{op} &= 400 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Availability (A).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{T_{op}}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{400}{480} \times 100 \\
A &= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Performance (P).**

Ideal production at design speed = Ideal Cycle Time ├Ś Total Pieces

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ideal} &= 1.20 \times 320 \\
T_{ideal} &= 384 \text{ minutes} \\
P &= \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{op}} \times 100 \\
P &= \frac{384}{400} \times 100 \\
P &= 96.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{320 - 18}{320} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{302}{320} \times 100 \\
Q &= 94.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \div 10000 \\
OEE &= \frac{83.33 \times 96.00 \times 94.38}{10000} \times 100 \\
OEE &= 75.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class OEE = 85%.** This machine is operating at **75.50%**, indicating substantial improvement potential. The lowest pillar is **Availability (83.33%)**, suggesting that **breakdown and changeover losses** must be attacked via Pillar 2 (Kobetsu Kaizen) and Pillar 3 (Planned Maintenance).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical ŌĆö MTBF, MTTR, and Inherent Availability

A packaging machine operated for **5,000 hours** in one quarter and experienced **6 failures**, with cumulative repair time of **30 hours**.

### **Solution**

**Step 1: MTBF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
MTBF &= \frac{5000}{6} \\
MTBF &= 833.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: MTTR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
MTTR &= \frac{30}{6} \\
MTTR &= 5.00 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Inherent Availability (AI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_I &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A_I &= \frac{833.33}{833.33 + 5.00} \\
A_I &= \frac{833.33}{838.33} \\
A_I &= 0.9940 \text{ or } 99.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** High MTBF (833 hrs) and low MTTR (5 hrs) indicate the equipment is both **highly reliable** and **easily maintainable**. The very high AI of 99.40% reflects excellent design + maintenance culture.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right TPM Pillar

| Symptom Observed on Shop-Floor | Primary Pillar to Deploy | Supporting Tool |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequent unplanned stoppages | Pillar 3 ŌĆō Planned Maintenance | MTBF, MTTR tracking |
| Operators wait for technicians | Pillar 1 ŌĆō Jishu Hozen | CILT, Tag boards |
| High scrap at start-up of shift | Pillar 4 ŌĆō Quality Maintenance | Poka-Yoke, 5-Why |
| Slow model changeovers (SMED) | Pillar 2 ŌĆō Kobetsu Kaizen | SMED, VSM |
| New machine design is hard to maintain | Pillar 5 ŌĆō Early Equipment Mgmt. | Maintainability indices |
| Operators lack technical depth | Pillar 6 ŌĆō Training & Education | OJT matrix |
| Unsafe working conditions | Pillar 7 ŌĆō SHE | Risk assessment |
| Office delays bottleneck production | Pillar 8 ŌĆō Admin TPM | Value Stream Mapping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# ­¤ōĢ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram ŌĆö The 8 Pillars of TPM Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **hierarchical block-level architecture** of TPM, showing how each pillar links to either *Equipment Effectiveness* (OEE) or *Zero-Defect / Safe Culture*.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Total Productive Maintenance TPM] --> goal1[Five Zero Goals]
    rootA --> goal2[Maximum OEE]
    rootA --> goal3[Operator Ownership]

    rootA --> pilA[Pillar 1 Autonomous Maintenance]
    rootA --> pilB[Pillar 2 Kobetsu Kaizen]
    rootA --> pilC[Pillar 3 Planned Maintenance]
    rootA --> pilD[Pillar 4 Quality Maintenance]
    rootA --> pilE[Pillar 5 Early Equipment Management]
    rootA --> pilF[Pillar 6 Training and Education]
    rootA --> pilG[Pillar 7 Safety Health Environment]
    rootA --> pilH[Pillar 8 TPM in Administration]

    pilA --> toolA1[CILT Activities]
    pilA --> toolA2[Tag Board and One Point Lessons]
    pilB --> toolB1[PDCA and DMAIC]
    pilB --> toolB2[SMED Single Minute Exchange of Die]
    pilC --> toolC1[Time Based Maintenance]
    pilC --> toolC2[Predictive Maintenance]
    pilD --> toolD1[Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing]
    pilD --> toolD2[Five Why Analysis]
    pilE --> toolE1[Maintainability Design]
    pilE --> toolE2[Reliability Engineering]
    pilF --> toolF1[Multi Skill Matrix]
    pilF --> toolF2[OJT and Classroom Modules]
    pilG --> toolG1[Zero Accident Culture]
    pilG --> toolG2[Environmental Compliance]
    pilH --> toolH1[Office VSM]
    pilH --> toolH2[Stores and Supply Chain TPM]

    toolC2 -.Uses.-> predA[Vibration Analysis]
    toolC2 -.Uses.-> predB[Thermal Imaging]
    toolC2 -.Uses.-> predC[Oil Analysis]
```

## 4.2 The Six Big Losses ŌĆö Loss-to-Pillar Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    oeeRoot[OEE Multiplier Chain] --> avL[Availability]
    oeeRoot --> pfL[Performance]
    oeeRoot --> qaL[Quality]

    avL --> lossA1[Loss 1 Breakdown Failure]
    avL --> lossA2[Loss 2 Setup and Adjustment]

    pfL --> lossP1[Loss 3 Small Stops and Idling]
    pfL --> lossP2[Loss 4 Reduced Speed]

    qaL --> lossQ1[Loss 5 Defect and Rework]
    qaL --> lossQ2[Loss 6 Startup and Reduced Yield]

    lossA1 --> actA1[Deploy Pillar 3 Planned Maintenance]
    lossA2 --> actA2[Deploy Pillar 2 SMED Kobetsu Kaizen]
    lossP1 --> actP1[Deploy Pillar 1 Jishu Hozen]
    lossP2 --> actP2[Deploy Pillar 5 Early Equipment Management]
    lossQ1 --> actQ1[Deploy Pillar 4 Quality Maintenance with Poka Yoke]
    lossQ2 --> actQ2[Deploy Pillar 6 Training and Standard Work]
```

## 4.3 Jishu Hozen 7-Step Implementation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Step 1 Initial Cleaning] --> step2[Step 2 Counter Measures for Sources]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Tentative TPM Standards]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 General Inspection]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Autonomous Inspection Standards]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Autonomous Maintenance Management]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Independent Maintenance Rollout]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and list its 5 Zero goals.**

**Model Answer:**
TPM is a company-wide, proactive maintenance philosophy that maximizes OEE by involving **all employees** from operators to top management in maintaining equipment. The **5 Zero Goals** are:
1. **Zero** Defects (Quality)
2. **Zero** Breakdowns (Equipment)
3. **Zero** Accidents (Safety)
4. **Zero** Pollution (Environment)
5. **Zero** Waste (Efficiency)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "less defects" or "minimum breakdowns." Use the **exact word "Zero"** ŌĆö examiners award the mark only when zero-loss language is used.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Total Productive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Equipment only | Entire organization (including office) |
| Participation | Maintenance department only | All employees (operators included) |
| Focus | Avoiding breakdown | Maximizing OEE and achieving 5 Zero |
| Tools | Schedule, lubrication | CILT, OEE, Poka-Yoke, SMED |
| Origin | US Industry | Japan, 1971 (Nippondenso) |

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulation**, every Module Part B offers **internal choice**. Attempt ANY ONE of the two options.

---

### Ō£ģ **OPTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks**

#### **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]**
